topics.title: Loads and Moments Acting on Ship Structures in Still Water and in Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Loads and Moments Acting on Ship Structures in Still Water and in Waves**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In ship structural design, a vessel is treated as a **box girder** floating in two distinct environments: a perfectly calm fluid (still water) and a disturbed fluid (waves). The forces and moments that act on the hull girder are the primary inputs for determining scantlings (plate thickness, stiffener sizes, frame spacing, etc.).

- **Still Water Loads (SWL)** are the static, time-invariant forces and moments arising from the ship's own weight, buoyancy, and cargo distribution when floating in calm water.
- **Wave Loads (Dynamic Loads)** are the time-varying forces and moments induced by ocean waves, including hydrodynamic pressure, slamming, and added mass effects.
- **Still Water Bending Moment (SWBM)** is the internal longitudinal bending moment produced by the difference between the weight curve and the buoyancy curve in calm water.
- **Wave Bending Moment (WBM)** is the additional longitudinal bending moment induced by wave action, superimposed on the still water condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** The total design bending moment for hull girder strength is the **vector sum** of SWBM and WBM, evaluated at the most severe wave position (typically the wave length equal to ship length, $\lambda = L$).

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

Imagine standing a long, hollow cardboard tube vertically and pressing down on it from the top. The tube bends. Now imagine a long, thin ship floating on water. Just like the cardboard tube, gravity pulls it down, water pushes it up, and the **mismatch between weight and buoyancy distribution** along the length creates internal forces.

- **Still Water Analogy:** Think of a wooden plank laid across two scales (a bow scale and a stern scale) with weights placed unevenly. The scales read different values because the plank bends slightly between the supports. That bending is the **still water bending moment**.
- **Wave Analogy:** Now imagine gently rocking the plank on a sea swell. The wave crests push up at the bow and stern while the trough sucks water away at midship (or vice versa). This adds a *dynamic, oscillating* bending on top of the static one â€” the **wave bending moment**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in This Module:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Sea water density: $\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$ (in SI) or $\rho = 64 \text{ lb/ft}^3$ (in Imperial)
> - Gravitational constant for weight: $\gamma = \rho g = 10.055 \text{ kN/m}^3$

### 1.3 Why Are These Loads Critical?

| Load Type | Design Consequence |
|---|---|
| **Vertical Shear Force ($V$)** | Determines web thickness of side shell, deck, and bottom plating |
| **Vertical Bending Moment ($M$)** | Determines section modulus of hull girder, longitudinal strength |
| **Hogging Moment ($+M$)** | Tensile stress on deck, compressive stress on bottom |
| **Sagging Moment ($-M$)** | Tensile stress on bottom, compressive stress on deck |
| **Torsional Moment ($M_T$)** | Requires twisting strength, especially for asymmetric sections |
| **Slamming Force ($F_s$)** | Local bottom plate strength near forward sections |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, questions on this topic frequently test the derivation of pressure distribution and its integration to yield bending moment â€” focus on the **trochoidal wave derivation**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Loads on Ship Structures

Ship hull loads are broadly classified along two axes: **direction of action** and **nature (static/dynamic)**.

#### A. By Direction

1. **Vertical Loads (Longitudinal Direction):**
   - Weight (gravity, cargo, fuel, machinery, outfit)
   - Buoyancy (hydrostatic + hydrodynamic pressure on the underside)
   - Net result: Shear force $V(x)$ and Bending moment $M(x)$ along the length.

2. **Horizontal Loads (Transverse / Lateral):**
   - Lateral wave pressure on side shell
   - Wind heeling moments
   - Centrifugal force in turns

3. **Torsional Loads:**
   - Asymmetric wave loading when ship is oblique to waves
   - Cargo shift moments
   - Torque on the hull girder

#### B. By Nature

1. **Still Water Loads:** Quasi-static, time-independent, depend on loading condition.
2. **Wave Loads:** Time-dependent, periodic, depend on wave height, length, and heading.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Load Mechanism

**Step 1 â€” Weight Curve $w(x)$:**
The longitudinal distribution of weight per unit length. Constructed by integrating the weights of all components (steel, outfit, machinery, cargo) along the ship's length.

$$w(x) = \frac{dW(x)}{dx} \quad [\text{kN/m}]$$

**Step 2 â€” Buoyancy Curve $b(x)$:**
The longitudinal distribution of buoyancy force per unit length, equal to the integral of hydrostatic pressure over the girth at station $x$:

$$b(x) = \int_{wet} p(x,z) \, dA \quad [\text{kN/m}]$$

**Step 3 â€” Load Curve (Net Force per unit length):**
The difference $q(x) = b(x) - w(x)$ represents the unbalanced load per unit length. It is **zero** in pure still water equilibrium (Archimedes).

**Step 4 â€” Shear Force from Load Curve:**
$$V(x) = \int_0^x q(\xi) \, d\xi$$

**Step 5 â€” Bending Moment from Shear Force:**
$$M(x) = \int_0^x V(\xi) \, d\xi = \int_0^x \int_0^\xi q(\eta) \, d\eta \, d\xi$$

**Step 6 â€” Wave Adjustment:**
The pressure distribution $p(x,z)$ is modified by the wave surface displacement $\eta(x,t)$:

$$p(x,z,t) = p_{static} + p_{dynamic}$$

### 2.3 The Two Critical Wave Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hogging and Sagging** are the two extreme design cases. In KTU problems, students must clearly identify which condition applies to the given wave-ship geometry.

- **Hogging (Positive Bending Moment, $+M$):** Wave crest at bow and stern, trough at midship. The ship is "bent upward" like a U-shape. The **deck is in tension**, the **bottom is in compression**.
- **Sagging (Negative Bending Moment, $-M$):** Wave crest at midship, troughs at bow and stern. The ship "sags" downward. The **bottom is in tension**, the **deck is in compression**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | $p = \rho g h$ | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | $h$ measured from free surface |
| 2 | Total displacement | $\Delta = \rho g \nabla$ | $\text{kN}$ | $\nabla$ is displaced volume in $\text{m}^3$ |
| 3 | Weight curve integral | $W = \int_0^L w(x) \, dx$ | $\text{kN}$ | Must equal $\Delta$ in equilibrium |
| 4 | Shear force | $V(x) = \int_0^x [b(\xi) - w(\xi)] \, d\xi$ | $\text{kN}$ | $V(0) = V(L) = 0$ in equilibrium |
| 5 | Bending moment | $M(x) = \int_0^x V(\xi) \, d\xi$ | $\text{kNآ·m}$ | $M(0) = M(L) = 0$ |
| 6 | Trochoidal wave surface | $\eta(x,t) = \frac{H}{2} \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi x}{\lambda} - \sigma t\right)$ | $\text{m}$ | $H$=wave height, $\lambda$=wavelength |
| 7 | Trochoidal wave pressure (deep) | $p(x,z,t) = \rho g \!\left[\eta - z + \frac{H \pi}{\lambda} e^{-2\pi z/\lambda} \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi x}{\lambda} - \sigma t\right)\right]$ | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | Below mean water level |
| 8 | Wave bending moment (rule of thumb) | $M_w = 0.0405 \, W \, L \, C_b$ (sagging) | $\text{kNآ·m}$ | Empirical, head/following seas |
| 9 | Wave bending moment (hogging) | $M_w = 0.0423 \, W \, L \, C_b$ | $\text{kNآ·m}$ | Empirical, $\lambda \approx L$ |
| 10 | Smith's rule slamming force | $F_s = k_s \, A^{1.5} \, V_s^2$ | $\text{kN}$ | $k_s = 22$ (units specific) |
| 11 | Section modulus requirement | $Z = \dfrac{M_{total}}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | $\text{m}^3$ | $M_{total} = M_{sw} + M_{wave}$ |
| 12 | Shear stress | $\tau = \dfrac{V \, Q}{I \, t}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | Standard beam shear formula |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These equations form the backbone of longitudinal strength assessment required by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS). They are used to determine the required section modulus $Z$ of the hull girder, which directly dictates the structural arrangement of decks, side shells, and bottom longitudinals.

### 2.5 Boundary Conditions Recap

For a free-free hull girder in equilibrium (no support reactions):

$$V(0) = V(L) = 0$$
$$M(0) = M(L) = 0$$
$$M(0) \;\text{slope is finite},\quad V(0)\;\text{is finite}$$

> [!TIP]
> These boundary conditions are the **primary checkpoints** in any KTU 14-mark derivation. Failing to state them explicitly costs at least 1 mark.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure Distribution

**Step 1.** Consider a point on the hull at depth $h$ below the free water surface. The hydrostatic pressure is the weight of the water column above this point per unit area.

**Step 2.** The pressure at depth $h$:

$$p = \frac{\text{Weight of water column above}}{\text{Area}} = \frac{\rho g h \cdot A}{A} = \rho g h$$

**Step 3.** The total force on an area $A$ submerged at constant depth $h$:

$$F = p \cdot A = \rho g h A \quad [\text{kN}]$$

> **Engineering Note:** In ship design, the *girth* of the ship at a transverse section is the area on which hydrostatic pressure acts. Integrating this pressure over the girth yields the **buoyancy curve** $b(x)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Trochoidal Wave Pressure (Havelock's Source Method)

We model the wave as a **2D irrotational, incompressible, inviscid** flow. The velocity potential $\phi(x,z,t)$ satisfies Laplace's equation:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

**Step 1.** The free-surface boundary conditions (linearized) are:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} \quad \text{at } z = 0$$

$$\eta = -\frac{1}{g} \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \quad \text{(dynamic)}$$

**Step 2.** Substituting the kinematic BC into the dynamic BC and applying separation of variables, the potential takes the form:

$$\phi(x,z,t) = A \, e^{kz} \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

where $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the wave number and $\sigma = 2\pi/T$ is the angular frequency.

**Step 3.** Substituting into Laplace's equation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} = -A k^2 e^{kz} \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = A k^2 e^{kz} \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

Sum: $-A k^2 + A k^2 = 0$ âœ“

**Step 4.** From the dispersion relation for deep-water trochoidal waves:

$$\sigma^2 = g k \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{\sigma^2}{g}$$

**Step 5.** The wave surface elevation is:

$$\eta(x,t) = \frac{H}{2} \cos(kx - \sigma t)$$

**Step 6.** Apply the linearized Bernoulli equation for the pressure:

$$p(x,z,t) = -\rho \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$$

**Step 7.** Substituting $\phi$:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = -A \sigma \, e^{kz} \cos(kx - \sigma t)$$

**Step 8.** Apply the linearized dynamic free-surface condition to determine $A$:

At $z = 0$, the pressure must equal $\rho g \eta$:

$$-\rho \left. \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \right|_{z=0} = \rho g \eta$$

$$\rho A \sigma \cos(kx - \sigma t) = \rho g \cdot \frac{H}{2} \cos(kx - \sigma t)$$

$$A = \frac{gH}{2\sigma}$$

**Step 9.** Therefore, the **general pressure equation at any point** $(x, z)$ below the mean water level ($z < 0$):

$$p(x,z,t) = \rho g \left[ \frac{H}{2} e^{kz} \cos(kx - \sigma t) - z \right]$$

where $z$ is measured **positive upward** from the mean free surface, and the term $e^{kz}$ is the **attenuation factor** that decays exponentially with depth.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Bending Moment from Pressure Distribution

The vertical force (buoyancy) at section $x$ is the integral of pressure over the wetted girth:

**Step 1.** Bending moment contribution at midship from element $dA$ at distance $\xi$ forward of midship:

$$dM = p(x,z) \cdot (x - \xi) \, dA$$

**Step 2.** Total bending moment:

$$M(x) = \int_{wetted} p(x,z) \cdot (x - \xi) \, dA$$

**Step 3.** Substituting the wave pressure and integrating over the hull girth (an involved computation using the ship form coefficients $C_b$, $C_p$, etc.), the final closed-form expression for the **wave bending moment at midship** for a ship of length $L$, beam $B$, draft $T$, and block coefficient $C_b$ in head seas with $\lambda = L$ is:

$$\boxed{M_w = 0.0405 \, W \, L \, C_b \quad \text{(sagging)}}$$

$$\boxed{M_w = 0.0423 \, W \, L \, C_b \quad \text{(hogging)}}$$

where $W = \Delta g$ is the displacement in kN, $L$ is the length between perpendiculars in meters, and $C_b$ is the block coefficient.

> [!NOTE]
> These empirical coefficients (0.0405 and 0.0423) are the **classical KTU-style results** for the standard $\lambda = L$ wave condition. They are derived from the full integration of the trochoidal pressure over a prismatic ship form. Always state the **standard condition** ($\lambda = L$, head seas, $H/L = 1/20$ typically) when using these.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hogging vs Sagging Summary

| Parameter | Hogging ($+M$) | Sagging ($-M$) |
|---|---|---|
| Wave crest position | Bow and stern | Midship |
| Wave trough position | Midship | Bow and stern |
| Hull shape | U-shape (concave up) | Inverted U (concave down) |
| Deck stress | **Tensile** | Compressive |
| Bottom stress | Compressive | **Tensile** |
| Critical section | Midship | Often 0.4L from AP/FP |
| Empir. coefficient | 0.0423 | 0.0405 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Ship Loads (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LOADS ON SHIP HULL GIRDER] --> B[By Direction]
    A --> C[By Nature]
    A --> D[By Time Dependence]
    B --> B1[Vertical Loads]
    B --> B2[Horizontal Loads]
    B --> B3[Torsional Loads]
    C --> C1[Hydrostatic]
    C --> C2[Hydrodynamic]
    C --> C3[Static Weight]
    D --> D1[Still Water SW]
    D --> D2[Wave Induced W]
    B1 --> B1a[Shear Force V]
    B1 --> B1b[Bending Moment M]
    B2 --> B2a[Lateral Pressure]
    B2 --> B2b[Wind Heeling]
    B3 --> B3a[Asymmetric Buoyancy]
    B3 --> B3b[Cargo Shift]
    C1 --> C1a[Pressure rho g h]
    C2 --> C2a[Trochoidal Pressure]
    C2 --> C2b[Slamming Force]
    D1 --> D1a[SWBM Msw]
    D1 --> D1b[SWSF Vsw]
    D2 --> D2a[WBM Mw]
    D2 --> D2b[WSF Vw]
    D1a --> E[Total M = Msw + Mw]
    D2a --> E
    D1b --> F[Total V = Vsw + Vw]
    D2b --> F
    E --> G[Section Modulus Z]
    F --> H[Shear Stress tau]
    G --> I[Hull Scantlings]
    H --> I
```

### 4.2 Wave Bending Moment Computation Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In1[Ship Data: L B T Cb] --> Step1
    In2[Wave Data: H lambda heading] --> Step1
    In3[Loading Condition: SWBM] --> Step1
    Step1[Compute Pressure p at each section x, z] --> Step2
    Step2[Integrate p over girth] --> Step3
    Step3[Bcurve b of x] --> Step4
    In3 --> Step4
    Step4[Load curve q of x] --> Step5
    Step5[Shear V of x integration] --> Step6
    Step6[Moment M of x integration] --> Step7
    Step7[Apply Boundary Conditions] --> Step8
    Step8[Identify Max Hogging and Sagging] --> Out1[Mtotal Msw plus Mw]
    Out1 --> Out2[Section Modulus Z]
    Out2 --> Out3[Scantling Decisions]
```

### 4.3 Coordinate System and Load Directions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CS[Ship Coordinate System OXYZ] --> X[OX Longitudinal: Bow positive]
    CS --> Y[OY Transverse: Port to starboard]
    CS --> Z[OZ Vertical: Upward positive]
    X --> L1[Weight W of x: acts downward Z negative]
    Y --> L2[Lateral F of y: side pressure]
    Z --> L3[Buoyancy B of x: acts upward Z positive]
    L1 --> M1[Vertical BM Mxx]
    L3 --> M1
    L1 --> M2[Shear Force Vx]
    L3 --> M2
    L2 --> M3[Torsional Moment Mxy]
```

### 4.4 Hogging and Sagging Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Wave Profile lambda equal to L] --> HOG[Hogging: Crest at bow and stern]
    W --> SAG[Sagging: Crest at midship]
    HOG --> H1[Ship bent concave up]
    HOG --> H2[Deck in tension]
    HOG --> H3[Bottom in compression]
    SAG --> S1[Ship bent concave down]
    SAG --> S2[Deck in compression]
    SAG --> S3[Bottom in tension]
    H1 --> HS[Mmax positive at midship]
    S1 --> SS[Mmax negative at midship]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams Instead of Physical Drawings?** Physical drawings of a ship in a trochoidal wave are complex, multi-curved figures that cannot be reliably rendered in Mermaid syntax. The block-level functional architecture above maps the **flow of computation** and the **logical classification** of loads, which is the analytical equivalent â€” and is exactly what KTU examiners expect in a derivation-based answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam â€“ May 2024 Style]

**Q: Define Still Water Bending Moment (SWBM) and Wave Bending Moment (WBM). State the boundary conditions for the bending moment of a free-free hull girder in still water.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** **SWBM:** The internal longitudinal bending moment produced along the hull girder due to the difference between the longitudinal distribution of weight $w(x)$ and the buoyancy $b(x)$ of the ship floating in perfectly calm (still) water. Mathematically, $M_{sw}(x) = \int_0^x \int_0^\xi [b(\eta) - w(\eta)] \, d\eta \, d\xi$.
- **(1 mark)** **WBM:** The additional longitudinal bending moment induced by hydrodynamic pressure from surface waves, evaluated at the most critical wave length and heading, superimposed on the SWBM.
- **(1 mark)** **Boundary conditions:** $M(0) = 0$, $M(L) = 0$, and the shear $V(0) = V(L) = 0$ for a free-free hull girder in equilibrium.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam â€“ Dec 2023 Style]

**Q: Distinguish between hogging and sagging conditions of a ship floating in waves. Which stress condition arises on the deck plating in each case?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** **Hogging:** Occurs when wave crests are at the bow and stern with a trough at midship, causing the hull to bend in a U-shape (concave upward).
- **(1 mark)** **Sagging:** Occurs when a wave crest is at midship with troughs at bow and stern, causing the hull to bend in an inverted U-shape (concave downward).
- **(1 mark)** **Stress on deck plating:** Deck experiences **tensile stress in hogging** and **compressive stress in sagging**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each â€” Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam â€“ Model Question]

**Q: A container ship of length $L = 200$ m, beam $B = 30$ m, draft $T = 12$ m, and block coefficient $C_b = 0.65$ is floating in head seas with wave length equal to ship length. The ship displacement is $\Delta = 50,000$ tonnes. Compute:**
- **(a) (7 marks)** The wave bending moment (WBM) at midship for both hogging and sagging conditions, using the standard empirical relations.
- **(b) (7 marks)** The required section modulus $Z$ at midship assuming an allowable stress of $\sigma_{allow} = 175$ MPa, given that the still water bending moment $M_{sw} = 200$ MNآ·m (hogging) at midship.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) â€” Wave Bending Moment:**

**Step 1.** Compute ship displacement in kN (weight):

$$W = \Delta \cdot g = 50{,}000 \text{ t} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 = 490{,}500 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2.** Apply the empirical formula for **sagging** ($\lambda = L$):

$$M_{w,sag} = 0.0405 \times W \times L \times C_b$$

$$M_{w,sag} = 0.0405 \times 490{,}500 \times 200 \times 0.65$$

$$M_{w,sag} = 2{,}581{,}601.25 \text{ kNآ·m} = 2581.6 \text{ MNآ·m}$$

**Step 3.** Apply the empirical formula for **hogging**:

$$M_{w,hog} = 0.0423 \times W \times L \times C_b$$

$$M_{w,hog} = 0.0423 \times 490{,}500 \times 200 \times 0.65$$

$$M_{w,hog} = 2{,}696{,}287.95 \text{ kNآ·m} = 2696.3 \text{ MNآ·m}$$

> **Answer (a):** $M_{w,sag} \approx 2581.6$ MNآ·m, $M_{w,hog} \approx 2696.3$ MNآ·m.

**Part (b) â€” Section Modulus:**

**Step 1.** Total bending moment for hogging condition (since $M_{sw}$ is given as hogging):

$$M_{total} = M_{sw} + M_{w,hog}$$

$$M_{total} = 200 + 2696.3 = 2896.3 \text{ MNآ·m} = 2896.3 \times 10^6 \text{ Nآ·m}$$

**Step 2.** Convert to Nآ·mm for compatibility with MPa:

$$M_{total} = 2896.3 \times 10^6 \text{ Nآ·m} \times 10^3 \text{ mm/m} = 2.8963 \times 10^{12} \text{ Nآ·mm}$$

**Step 3.** Apply the bending equation $M = \sigma Z$:

$$Z = \frac{M_{total}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{2.8963 \times 10^{12} \text{ Nآ·mm}}{175 \text{ N/mm}^2}$$

$$Z = 1.655 \times 10^{10} \text{ mm}^3 = 16.55 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Answer (b):** Required section modulus $Z \approx 16.55$ mآ³.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam â€“ Model Question, Alternative]

**Q: (a) (7 marks)** Starting from the linearized Bernoulli equation and the irrotational flow assumption, derive the expression for the pressure distribution $p(x, z, t)$ in a deep-water trochoidal wave of height $H$ and wavelength $\lambda$.

**(b) (7 marks)** A ship of length $L = 150$ m, block coefficient $C_b = 0.70$, and displacement $\Delta = 25{,}000$ t is floating in head seas with $\lambda = L$. Compute the maximum sagging and hogging wave bending moments and identify which governs the design.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) â€” Pressure Distribution Derivation:**

**Step 1.** State the governing equation: For an ideal, incompressible, irrotational fluid, the velocity potential $\phi(x,z,t)$ satisfies Laplace's equation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

**Step 2.** Assume a separable solution (wave propagating in $+x$ direction, decaying with depth):

$$\phi(x,z,t) = A e^{kz} \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

**Step 3.** Substituting into Laplace:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} = -A k^2 e^{kz} \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$
$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = A k^2 e^{kz} \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

Sum equals zero, satisfying Laplace's equation.

**Step 4.** Dispersion relation: $\sigma^2 = gk$, so $k = 2\pi/\lambda$, $\sigma = 2\pi/T$.

**Step 5.** Apply linearized dynamic free-surface boundary condition at $z=0$:

$$-\rho \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}\bigg|_{z=0} = \rho g \eta(x,t)$$

**Step 6.** From the kinematic condition, the wave surface is:

$$\eta(x,t) = \frac{H}{2} \cos(kx - \sigma t)$$

**Step 7.** Compute $\partial\phi/\partial t$:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = -A\sigma e^{kz} \cos(kx - \sigma t)$$

**Step 8.** Apply the BC:

$$\rho A \sigma \cos(kx - \sigma t) = \rho g \cdot \frac{H}{2} \cos(kx - \sigma t)$$

$$A = \frac{gH}{2\sigma}$$

**Step 9.** Apply linearized Bernoulli at general depth $z$ (below the mean water level, $z < 0$):

$$p(x,z,t) = -\rho g z + \rho g \cdot \frac{H}{2} e^{kz} \cos(kx - \sigma t)$$

> **Answer (a):**
> $$\boxed{p(x,z,t) = \rho g \left[ \frac{H}{2} e^{kz} \cos(kx - \sigma t) - z \right]}$$
> where $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ and $z$ is measured positive upward from mean free surface.

**Part (b) â€” Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1.** Compute weight in kN:

$$W = 25{,}000 \times 9.81 = 245{,}250 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2.** Sagging WBM:

$$M_{w,sag} = 0.0405 \times 245{,}250 \times 150 \times 0.70 = 1042.7 \text{ MNآ·m}$$

**Step 3.** Hogging WBM:

$$M_{w,hog} = 0.0423 \times 245{,}250 \times 150 \times 0.70 = 1089.0 \text{ MNآ·m}$$

**Step 4.** Comparison:

$$M_{w,hog} > M_{w,sag} \quad (1089.0 > 1042.7 \text{ MNآ·m})$$

> **Answer (b):** $M_{w,sag} \approx 1042.7$ MNآ·m, $M_{w,hog} \approx 1089.0$ MNآ·m. **Hogging governs the design** since it produces the larger bending moment, requiring greater deck reinforcement for tensile stress.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Loss of up to 4 marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting boundary conditions:** Always state $M(0) = M(L) = 0$ in the still-water derivation. Examiners explicitly check for this â€” losing 1 mark here is very common.
> 2. **Confusing hogging and sagging coefficients:** 0.0423 (hogging) and 0.0405 (sagging) are easily swapped. Remember: **hogging coefficient is larger** because the bowed-up condition produces higher curvature at the bow/stern.
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** WBM comes out in kNآ·m, but section modulus calculations require Nآ·mm and MPa. **Always convert consistently** before plugging into $Z = M/\sigma$.
> 4. **Skipping the assumption statement:** In derivations, explicitly state: "linearized theory, deep water, irrotational, inviscid, $\lambda = L$, head seas." This single sentence can earn 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to add SWBM + WBM:** Many students compute only the WBM and forget the SWBM when computing the total design moment. $M_{total} = M_{sw} + M_w$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- [ ] **SWBM** arises only from weightâ€“buoyancy mismatch; $M_{sw}(0) = M_{sw}(L) = 0$.
- [ ] **WBM** requires the trochoidal wave pressure integrated over the wetted hull girth.
- [ ] **Hogging** = wave crest at bow + stern â†’ deck tension, bottom compression. Coefficient = **0.0423**.
- [ ] **Sagging** = wave crest at midship â†’ deck compression, bottom tension. Coefficient = **0.0405**.
- [ ] **Standard condition** for empirical formula: head/following seas, $\lambda = L$.
- [ ] **Trochoidal wave pressure** has an exponential depth-decay factor $e^{kz}$ â€” **the deeper the point, the smaller the wave-induced pressure**.
- [ ] **Laplace's equation** governs the velocity potential; substitute and verify before applying BCs.
- [ ] **Dispersion relation:** $\sigma^2 = gk$ for deep-water trochoidal waves.
- [ ] **Total design moment:** $M_{total} = M_{sw} + M_w$ (algebraic sum with sign).
- [ ] **Section modulus:** $Z = M_{total}/\sigma_{allow}$; convert units to Nآ·mm and N/mmآ².
- [ ] **Smith's rule** gives slamming force on the forward bottom for high-speed vessels.
- [ ] **Free-free hull** boundary conditions: $V$ and $M$ are zero at both ends (aft and forward perpendiculars).
- [ ] **Density of sea water:** $\rho = 1.025$ t/mآ³; **$g = 9.81$ m/sآ²** in all KTU numerical problems.
- [ ] **Hogging governs design** when 0.0423 > 0.0405 (typical for most ships).
- [ ] **Linearized theory** is assumed in the trochoidal pressure derivation; always state this assumption in the answer.
- [ ] **Torsional moment** is significant in oblique seas (beam seas) but is *not* part of the standard SWBM + WBM (head/following) calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modes of Failure in Sagging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modes of Failure in Sagging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 â€” Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Sagging** is the longitudinal hull-girder bending condition in which the deflection of the midship region is **downward** relative to the ends, producing a curvature that is *concave upward* (a "smile" geometry). In this state:

- The **deck plating and deck longitudinals are subjected to longitudinal COMPRESSION**.
- The **bottom shell plating and bottom longitudinals are subjected to longitudinal TENSION**.
- The **neutral axis** lies between the bottom shell and the deck, typically closer to the bottom for a typical ship with the centre of gravity below the centre of buoyancy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sagging occurs in two principal scenarios:**
> 1. **Still-water loading:** Heavy cargo concentrated amidships (e.g., bulk carriers in alternate holds, tankers in loaded condition) â€” weight exceeds buoyancy amidships, depressing the middle.
> 2. **Wave loading:** A **wave trough amidships** (negative synchronous wave) â€” buoyancy shifts to the ends, weight dominates amidships, deflecting the middle downward.

The reverse condition (deck in tension, bottom in compression) is called **Hogging**.

> [!NOTE]
> Per IACS UR S11 and common KTU 2024 Scheme references, the design hull-girder bending moment in sagging $M_{sag}$ governs the **upper-deck compression design** and **bottom-shell tension design** of the midship section.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a long, thin steel ruler horizontally with both palms at its two ends and pressing **downward with your thumbs at the centre**. The ruler bends into a "U" shape (concave up) â€” this is **sagging**.

Now map this analogy to the ship:

| Ruler Element | Ship Equivalent | Stress State in Sagging |
|---|---|---|
| Top surface of ruler (concave-up curve) | **Deck plating** of the ship | **COMPRESSION** |
| Bottom surface of ruler (stretched) | **Bottom shell plating** | **TENSION** |
| Middle layer (no stress change) | Neutral axis of hull section | Zero longitudinal stress |

A piece of paper folded into a "U" will crumple at the **top (inner) surface** but stay intact at the **bottom (outer) surface** â€” exactly mirroring how a ship hull's **deck plating collapses by buckling** while the **bottom plating simply elongates (yields in tension)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because buckling is a *stability* problem (sudden, catastrophic, mode-jump), while yielding is a *strength* problem (gradual, ductile), **the deck (compression side) is the critical region in sagging**, not the bottom.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following values are **standard ship-structural design constants** used throughout the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus:

- **Young's modulus for mild steel:** $E = 2.06 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^2$ (= **206 GPa**)
- **Yield stress of mild shipbuilding steel (Grade A):** $\sigma_y = 235 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- **Higher-tensile steel (Grade AH36):** $\sigma_y = 355 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- **Poisson's ratio for steel:** $\nu = 0.3$
- **Permissible bending stress (compression, sagging, IACS):** $\sigma_{perm} \approx 0.7 \sigma_y$ for ultimate strength evaluation
- **Ship slenderness ratio L/D:** typically **10 to 20** for merchant vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 â€” Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Failure Modes in Sagging

In the sagging condition, failure may occur by any one (or a combination) of the following mechanisms, listed in decreasing order of criticality for a typical merchant ship:

### Mode 1 â€” Elastic / Inelastic Buckling of Deck Plating (Plate Panel)
The deck plating is subdivided into rectangular **plate panels** by the longitudinal stiffeners (typically spaced 600â€“900 mm apart in modern designs). Each panel is essentially a thin rectangular plate loaded by:
- In-plane longitudinal compression (from hull-girder bending), and
- In-plane shear (from transverse loading on deck).

The panel collapses by **edge compression buckling** when the compressive stress reaches the elastic critical buckling stress. For long, simply-supported plates under uniform compression, this is a **classical Euler-type plate buckling** problem.

### Mode 2 â€” Beam-Column Buckling of Deck Longitudinals
Each longitudinal stiffener acts as a **column** supported laterally by the plating (which acts as a continuous elastic foundation). Failure occurs when:
- The longitudinal buckles as a column between transverse frames (overall mode), or
- The longitudinal undergoes **tripping** (rotational instability about the plateâ€“stiffener junction), or
- The stiffener combined with attached plating buckles in a **stiffened-panel mode**.

### Mode 3 â€” Buckling of Side Shell Plating (Above Neutral Axis)
The side shell above the neutral axis is in **compression** during sagging. For fine forms with flared bows or pronounced tumblehome, the side shell panels may collapse before the deck does. This is especially critical in **large hatch openings** where the side shell forms the primary load path.

### Mode 4 â€” Yielding (Plastic Collapse) of Bottom Shell Plating
Since the bottom shell is in **tension**, its primary failure mode is **material yielding** â€” a ductile, gradual, non-catastrophic failure. Bottom plating is therefore not usually the *governing* failure mode in sagging, but it determines the **ultimate bending moment capacity** $M_u$ of the hull girder.

### Mode 5 â€” Failure at Hatch Corners
In container ships and bulk carriers with large hatches, **slamming stresses** and stress concentrations at the corners of hatch openings can cause **fatigue cracking** or **local plastic collapse**. Hatch corners are second-order stress features that combine hull-girder bending with local transverse bending.

### Mode 6 â€” Overall Hull-Girder Plastic Collapse
When $M_{sag}$ reaches the **ultimate hull-girder bending moment** $M_u$, the entire cross-section yields (plastic hinges form at deck and bottom). The midship section has zero reserve capacity â€” failure of the hull girder.

## 2.2 Why the Deck Governs the Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Buckling stress is **proportional to the square of the slenderness ratio** (panel width / thickness)آ². Yield stress is independent of geometry. Therefore, for thin plating typical of shipbuilding, **buckling always governs on the compression side**, and **yielding governs on the tension side**.

In sagging:
- **Compression side (deck):** thin plating â†’ low critical buckling stress â†’ failure is by **buckling**.
- **Tension side (bottom):** cannot buckle â†’ failure is by **yielding**.
- **Asymmetric consequence:** the *neutral axis shifts toward the tension side* (bottom) when local buckling reduces the effective deck section.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every equation required for KTU 2024 ESE problems on Modes of Failure in Sagging. The notation `vert` is used in place of the vertical pipe to keep table syntax safe.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bending stress at distance y from N.A. | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | $M$ in Nآ·mm, $y$ in mm, $I$ in mmâپ´, $\sigma_b$ in N/mmآ² | Hull-girder primary stress |
| 2 | Section modulus | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | $I$ in mmâپ´, $y_{max}$ in mm, $Z$ in mmآ³ | Capacity of hull section |
| 3 | Bending moment capacity | $M = Z \cdot \sigma_{perm}$ | $M$ in Nآ·mm, $Z$ in mmآ³, $\sigma$ in N/mmآ² | Direct design check |
| 4 | Slenderness of plate panel | $\beta = \dfrac{b}{t} \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}$ | $b,t$ in mm, dimensionless | Plate classification |
| 5 | Elastic plate buckling stress (long plate, simply supported, k = 4.0) | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \,\pi^{2} E}{12(1 - \nu^{2})} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$ | N/mmآ², $b$ = panel breadth, $t$ = thickness | Plate buckling in deck |
| 6 | Reduced slenderness (plate) | $\lambda_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{cr}}}$ | dimensionless | Plate strength curve input |
| 7 | Johnson-type inelastic plate stress | $\sigma_u = \sigma_y \left(1 - \dfrac{\sigma_y}{4\sigma_{cr}}\right)$ when $\sigma_{cr} > 0.5\sigma_y$ | N/mmآ² | Transition between elastic and plastic buckling |
| 8 | Effective width factor (Faulkner) | $\dfrac{b_e}{b} = \dfrac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_y} \left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{cr}}\right)} - 1 \right]$ | dimensionless | Replace buckled plate with effective strip |
| 9 | Euler column buckling stress (longitudinal stiffener) | $\sigma_E = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{(L_e / r)^{2}}$ | $L_e$ effective length, $r$ radius of gyration | Tripping / column buckling of longitudinals |
| 10 | Perry-Robertson column formula | $\dfrac{\sigma_u}{\sigma_y} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_E}\right)\left[\eta - \eta^{2} + \sqrt{(\eta - \eta^{2})^{2} + 4\dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_E}}\right]$ | $\eta = \dfrac{L_e}{1000 r} + 0.0035$ | Real column with imperfection |
| 11 | Hull-girder ultimate moment | $M_u = \sigma_y \cdot Z_{plastic}$ | Nآ·mm | Reserve strength check |
| 12 | Minimum deck plate thickness (empirical) | $t_{deck} \approx \dfrac{s \sqrt{h}}{285} + 1.5$ (mm) | $s$ = stiffener spacing (mm), $h$ = design head (m) | Rule-of-thumb (BV/GL style) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding these failure modes is essential for:

1. **Structural design offices** of shipyards (e.g., Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, Hyundai Heavy Industries) â€” they determine plate thickness, stiffener spacing, and scantlings.
2. **Classification societies** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS) â€” they publish the **Common Structural Rules (CSR)** with explicit buckling checks.
3. **Offshore and naval architecture consultancies** â€” for failure investigation after accidents (e.g., hull-girder collapse of *MV Kowloon Bridge*, 1986).
4. **Research on ultimate strength** using progressive-collapse analysis (Smith's method, ISUM plate elements).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 â€” Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 â€” Bending Stress Distribution in the Hull Girder

We begin from first principles using Eulerâ€“Bernoulli beam theory applied to the ship hull as a box girder.

**Step 1: Assumption of plane sections.** Cross-sections that are plane before bending remain plane after bending. Therefore, longitudinal strain $\epsilon_x$ varies linearly with distance $y$ from the neutral axis:

$$
\epsilon_x = \dfrac{y}{\rho}
$$

where $\rho$ is the radius of curvature.

**Step 2: Hooke's Law in the longitudinal direction.** For uniaxial stress in the hull-girder:

$$
\sigma_x = E \, \epsilon_x = E \, \dfrac{y}{\rho}
$$

**Step 3: Resultant moment equals applied bending moment.** The internal bending moment about the neutral axis must equal the externally applied hull-girder moment $M$:

$$
M = \int_A \sigma_x \, y \, dA = \dfrac{E}{\rho} \int_A y^{2} \, dA = \dfrac{E I}{\rho}
$$

where $I = \int_A y^{2} dA$ is the **second moment of area** of the hull cross-section about the neutral axis.

**Step 4: Eliminate the curvature.** Divide (Step 2) by (Step 3) to eliminate $E/\rho$:

$$
\sigma_x = \dfrac{M \, y}{I}
$$

This is the **Navier bending formula** â€” fundamental to all hull-girder analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> In sagging, $M > 0$ (sagging-positive convention). Therefore $\sigma_x > 0$ (compression) when $y > 0$ (deck), and $\sigma_x < 0$ (tension) when $y < 0$ (bottom).

**Step 5: Maximum stress at extreme fibre.** Setting $y = y_{max}$ gives the maximum fibre stress:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}
$$

where the **section modulus** is $Z = I / y_{max}$. This is the design check used by every classification society.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 â€” Critical Buckling Stress of a Deck Plate Panel

A deck plate panel between two longitudinal stiffeners is idealised as a **long rectangular plate** (length $a$, breadth $b$, thickness $t$) under uniform in-plane compression $\sigma_x$, simply supported on all four edges.

**Step 1: Governing differential equation.** For thin-plate flexure, the equilibrium equation under in-plane compression is:

$$
D \left(\dfrac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{4}} + 2\dfrac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{2}\partial y^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial y^{4}}\right) + \sigma_x \, t \, \dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} = 0
$$

where $D$ is the **flexural rigidity** of the plate:

$$
D = \dfrac{E \, t^{3}}{12(1 - \nu^{2})}
$$

**Step 2: Buckled shape assumption (double sine series).** Assume the deflection field is:

$$
w(x, y) = w_0 \, \sin\!\left(\dfrac{m \pi x}{a}\right) \sin\!\left(\dfrac{n \pi y}{b}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the plate equation.** After substitution and applying the Rayleighâ€“Ritz energy method:

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D}{t \, b^{2}} \left(\dfrac{m b}{a} + \dfrac{n^{2} a}{m b}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 4: Find the buckling mode.** For a **long plate** ($a \gg b$, i.e., $a/b > \sqrt{2}$), the minimum occurs at $m = 1, n = 1$, giving:

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D}{t \, b^{2}} \left(\dfrac{b}{a} + \dfrac{a}{b}\right)^{2} \to \dfrac{4 \pi^{2} D}{t \, b^{2}} \quad \text{as } \dfrac{a}{b} \to \infty
$$

**Step 5: Substitute $D$.** The final classical plate-buckling formula:

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \, \pi^{2} E}{12(1 - \nu^{2})} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}\;}
$$

where $k = 4.0$ for the long simply-supported plate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For $\nu = 0.3$, the constant $\dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{12(1-\nu^{2})} \approx 3.615 E$. With $E = 2.06 \times 10^{5}$ N/mmآ², this becomes approximately $7.45 \times 10^{5}$ N/mmآ². Thus:
> $\sigma_{cr} \approx 2.98 \times 10^{6} (t/b)^{2}$ N/mmآ² for $k = 4$.

**Step 6: Real-plate correction (Johnson parabola).** For stocky plates where $\sigma_{cr}$ exceeds $\sigma_y/2$, the **inelastic buckling** stress is given by the empirical Johnson parabola:

$$
\sigma_u = \sigma_y \left[1 - \dfrac{\sigma_y}{4 \sigma_{cr}}\right] \quad \text{for } \sigma_{cr} > 0.5 \sigma_y
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 â€” Effective Width Concept (Faulkner)

When a plate panel buckles, the post-buckling load is carried by the **edge strips** of width $b_e/2$ on each side. The effective width is found by equating the area under the actual stress distribution to a uniform rectangular block at yield:

**Step 1: Equate area under actual stress distribution to yield stress أ— effective width.**

$$
\sigma_y \, b_e = \int_0^{b} \sigma_x(y) \, dy
$$

**Step 2: For a long plate, the central deflection profile is parabolic.** Integration yields:

$$
\dfrac{b_e}{b} = \dfrac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_y} \left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{cr}}\right)} - 1\right]
$$

**Step 3: Limiting cases.**
- When $\sigma_{cr} \gg \sigma_y$ (very slender plate): $b_e/b \to 1$ (plate is fully effective â€” fails elastically).
- When $\sigma_{cr} \ll \sigma_y$ (very stocky plate): $b_e/b \to 0$ (most of the plate carries nothing â€” heavy post-buckling degradation).

This is the basis of the **effective width method** used by IACS Common Structural Rules.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A deck plate panel has breadth $b = 750$ mm, thickness $t = 12$ mm. The material is mild steel with $E = 2.06 \times 10^{5}$ N/mmآ², $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y = 235$ N/mmآ². The hull-girder sagging moment produces a longitudinal compressive stress $\sigma_x = 180$ N/mmآ² in the deck. Check whether the plate is safe.

**Step 1: Compute the slenderness.**

$$
\beta = \dfrac{b}{t} \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}} = \dfrac{750}{12}\sqrt{\dfrac{235}{2.06\times 10^{5}}} = 62.5 \times 0.0338 = 2.11
$$

Since $\beta > 1.0$, the plate is in the **slender range** â€” elastic buckling governs.

**Step 2: Compute critical buckling stress.**

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \times \pi^{2} \times 2.06 \times 10^{5}}{12(1 - 0.3^{2})} \times \left(\dfrac{12}{750}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 9.8696 \times 2.06 \times 10^{5}}{10.92} \times (0.016)^{2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{cr} = 7.45 \times 10^{5} \times 2.56 \times 10^{-4} = 190.7 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 3: Compare to applied stress.** Applied $\sigma_x = 180$ N/mmآ² < $\sigma_{cr} = 190.7$ N/mmآ². 

**Step 4: Apply safety factor.** Using IACS permissible $\sigma_{perm} = \sigma_{cr} / S.F.$ with $S.F. = 1.5$:

$$
\sigma_{perm} = \dfrac{190.7}{1.5} = 127.1 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

Since $180 > 127.1$, the plate **is NOT safe** â€” must either **increase thickness** (try $t = 14$ mm â†’ $\sigma_{cr} = 259.4$ N/mmآ² â†’ $\sigma_{perm} = 172.9$ â†’ still fails) or **reduce stiffener spacing**.

**Step 5: Conclusion for the examiner.** The deck plate is unsafe at the given $b/t$ ratio under the applied sagging stress. Recommendation: reduce stiffener spacing to $b = 600$ mm or increase thickness to $t = 16$ mm.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 â€” Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart â€” Failure-Mode Decision Tree in Sagging

The following decision tree summarises how a design engineer classifies the failure path of a deck structure under a sagging bending moment. The block-level functional architecture maps the logical flow from load input to failure-mode identification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sagging Hull Girder Moment Msag] --> B{Deck Plating Stress State}
    B -- Compression --> C[Slenderness beta = b/t sqrt sigma_y / E]
    C -- beta less than 1.0 --> D[Stocky Plate]
    C -- beta between 1 and 3 --> E[Slender Plate]
    C -- beta greater than 3 --> F[Very Slender Plate]
    D -- Compute --> G[Yielding at sigma_y]
    E -- Compute sigma_cr --> H[Elastic Plate Buckling]
    F -- Compute sigma_cr --> I[Elastic Plate Buckling at low sigma_cr]
    H --> J{Effective Width be}
    I --> J
    G --> K[Deck Longitudinals Check]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Beam Column Euler Buckling sigma_E]
    L --> M{Tripping Mode}
    M -- Yes --> N[Stiffener Rotation Collapse]
    M -- No --> O[Overall Stiffened Panel Collapse]
    H --> P[Side Shell Above Neutral Axis Check]
    P --> Q[Compression Buckling of Side Panels]
    G --> R[Bottom Shell Tension Check]
    R -- Yielding --> S[Ultimate Plastic Collapse of Section]
    S --> T[Mu Reached Hull Girder Failure]
    O --> T
    N --> T
    Q --> T
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each terminal node (D, H, N, S, etc.) represents a distinct **Mode of Failure in Sagging** listed in Section 2.1. The block-level functional architecture shows how the failure mode is identified by working down the tree from the applied moment to the local structural element.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram â€” Stress Distribution in Sagging

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOAD[Applied Loading]
        Msag[Sagging Moment Msag]
    end
    subgraph STRESS[Stress Distribution Through Depth]
        NA[Neutral Axis]
        Deck[Deck Plating - sigma_x Positive - Compression]
        UpperSide[Upper Side Shell - sigma_x Positive - Compression]
        LowerSide[Lower Side Shell - sigma_x Negative - Tension]
        Bottom[Bottom Shell - sigma_x Negative - Tension]
    end
    subgraph FAIL[Governing Failure Modes]
        F1[Mode 1 Deck Plate Buckling]
        F2[Mode 2 Longitudinal Column Buckling]
        F3[Mode 3 Side Shell Buckling Above NA]
        F4[Mode 4 Bottom Yielding]
        F5[Mode 5 Hatch Corner Collapse]
    end
    Msag --> NA
    NA --> Deck
    NA --> UpperSide
    NA --> LowerSide
    NA --> Bottom
    Deck --> F1
    Deck --> F2
    UpperSide --> F3
    Bottom --> F4
    Deck --> F5
```

## 4.3 ASCII Stress Profile (Complementary Visual Aid)

For students requiring an immediate mental picture of the bending-stress profile:

```
   SAG (deckside: COMPRESSION)
    â†‘ +دƒ
    |     ___________
    |    /           |  دƒ_max  (deck)
    |   /            |
    |  /             |  â†گ deck plating (compression)
    | /              |
    +/_______________|________________
    |                |  â†گ Neutral Axis (N.A.)
    |                |
    |\               |  â†گ side shell (tension below N.A.)
    | \              |
    |  \             |  â†گ bottom plating (tension)
    |   \____________|
    |                |  دƒ_max  (bottom)
    â†“ âˆ’دƒ
   SAG (bottomside: TENSION)
```

- Above the N.A.: $\sigma_x > 0$ â†’ **Compression** (deck, upper side shell) â†’ buckling modes.
- Below the N.A.: $\sigma_x < 0$ â†’ **Tension** (lower side shell, bottom) â†’ yielding mode.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 â€” KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A â€” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam â€“ May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the term "Sagging" as applied to a ship hull girder. Which part of the hull section is most critical in sagging and why?

**Model Answer:**

Sagging is the longitudinal bending condition of the hull girder in which the midship region deflects **downward** relative to the ends, producing a concave-up curvature. It arises when weight exceeds buoyancy amidships (cargo concentrated amidships or wave-trough amidships).

In sagging, the **deck plating** is in longitudinal **compression** and is the most critical part of the section because:
1. Compression can cause **buckling**, which is a stability failure occurring at stresses much below yield.
2. The bottom shell, being in tension, only fails by **yielding** â€” a gradual, ductile mode.
3. Therefore, **buckling of the deck governs the design** in sagging.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam â€“ Dec 2023]`

**Q:** List any **four modes of failure** that may occur in a ship hull in the sagging condition.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Elastic / inelastic buckling of deck plating** between longitudinal stiffeners.
2. **Beam-column buckling of deck longitudinals** (overall or tripping mode).
3. **Buckling of side shell plating** above the neutral axis.
4. **Yielding of bottom shell plating** in tension.
5. *(Optional â€” for credit)* **Failure at hatch corners** due to stress concentration.
6. *(Optional â€” for credit)* **Overall hull-girder plastic collapse**.

---

## 5.2 PART B â€” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **forget the Poisson factor** $(1-\nu^2)$ in plate buckling â€” loss of up to **3 marks**.
> 2. Confusing **hogging and sagging** sign convention â€” deck is in **compression for sagging**, not tension. Loss of **2 marks**.
> 3. Failing to convert units (mm â†” m) â€” silently producing answers off by 1000أ—. Loss of **2 marks**.
> 4. Not specifying whether the plate is **long or short** (i.e., the $a/b$ ratio) â€” affects whether $k=4.0$ or $k>4.0$. Loss of **2 marks**.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam â€“ May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **stress distribution** in a hull-girder cross-section under sagging moment. Mark the neutral axis and indicate which parts are in tension and compression. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **deck plate panel** has breadth $b = 800$ mm and thickness $t = 10$ mm. It is made of mild steel with $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/mmآ², $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y = 240$ N/mmآ². The plate is long and simply supported on all edges. Determine:
  (i) the elastic critical buckling stress, and
  (ii) whether the plate can safely carry a hull-girder compressive stress of $160$ N/mmآ². **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution â€” Part (a):**

Refer to the ASCII stress profile diagram in Section 4.3. Key points:

1. The bending stress is given by $\sigma_x = \dfrac{M y}{I}$, where $y$ is measured from the neutral axis.
2. The neutral axis passes through the centroid of the effective hull section (accounting for stiffeners).
3. Above the N.A. (toward the deck) â€” **compression** (positive in sagging).
4. Below the N.A. (toward the bottom) â€” **tension** (negative in sagging).
5. Maximum fibre stresses occur at the deck and bottom extremes.
6. In sagging, the deck governs the design because compression leads to buckling, while tension at the bottom only yields.

**Model Solution â€” Part (b):**

Given: $b = 800$ mm, $t = 10$ mm, $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/mmآ², $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y = 240$ N/mmآ², applied $\sigma_x = 160$ N/mmآ².

**(i) Elastic critical buckling stress.**

For a long simply-supported plate, $k = 4.0$. The plate-buckling formula:

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \, \pi^{2} E}{12(1 - \nu^{2})} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}
$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \times \pi^{2} \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{12(1 - 0.09)} \times \left(\dfrac{10}{800}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 9.8696 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{10.92} \times (0.0125)^{2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{cr} = 7.229 \times 10^{5} \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{cr} \approx 113.0 \text{ N/mm}^{2}}
$$

**(ii) Safety check.**

Apply the safety factor $S.F. = 1.5$ (IACS permissible stress criterion):

$$
\sigma_{perm} = \dfrac{\sigma_{cr}}{S.F.} = \dfrac{113.0}{1.5} \approx 75.3 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

Compare applied stress: $\sigma_x = 160$ N/mmآ² $> \sigma_{perm} = 75.3$ N/mmآ².

**Conclusion:** The plate **fails** the safety check. Required remedial action: increase thickness or reduce stiffener spacing. Re-trying with $t = 14$ mm:

$$
\sigma_{cr,\,new} = 113.0 \times \left(\dfrac{14}{10}\right)^{2} = 221.5 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{perm,\,new} = 147.7 \text{ N/mm}^{2} < 160 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \quad \text{(still fails slightly)}
$$

Try $t = 15$ mm:

$$
\sigma_{cr} = 113.0 \times 2.25 = 254.3 \text{ N/mm}^{2}, \quad \sigma_{perm} = 169.5 \text{ N/mm}^{2} > 160
$$

Hence **$t = 15$ mm is the minimum safe deck plate thickness** at the given stiffener spacing.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam â€“ Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Discuss the **modes of failure of a deck longitudinal stiffener** in the sagging condition, distinguishing clearly between **column buckling**, **tripping**, and **stiffened-panel collapse**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **deck longitudinal** is a flat-bar $120 \times 12$ mm. It is supported laterally by transverse frames spaced at $L = 2500$ mm. The effective lateral support by the plating may be modelled as **pin-ended**. The steel has $E = 2.06 \times 10^{5}$ N/mmآ², $\sigma_y = 235$ N/mmآ². Compute the **Euler critical stress** and comment on the mode of failure. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution â€” Part (a):**

The deck longitudinal is essentially a column built into the deck plating. It may fail in three distinct ways:

**1. Column buckling (overall mode):** The stiffener buckles laterally as a column between transverse frames, with the plating acting as a continuous elastic foundation. The Euler critical stress is:

$$
\sigma_E = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{(L_e / r)^{2}}
$$

**2. Tripping (rotational mode):** The stiffener rotates about its web-to-plate junction, twisting out of plane. This is governed by the **torsional rigidity** of the stiffener and the lateral restraint of the plating. It is favoured by **shallow stiffeners** (low torsional constant) and is the **dominant mode in modern flat-bar longitudinals** because they have low torsional stiffness.

**3. Stiffened-panel collapse (overall panel mode):** The longitudinal and the attached plating buckle **together** as a single orthotropic plate. The plate provides lateral support to the stiffener, but the stiffener enforces a nodal line, reducing the buckling wavelength. The critical stress depends on the **relative stiffness parameter** $\gamma$:

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{E I_{s}}{b D}
$$

where $I_s$ is the second moment of area of the stiffener about the plate junction, and $D$ is the plate flexural rigidity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The actual mode that occurs depends on the relative stiffness of the longitudinal and the plate. **For weak longitudinals (small $\gamma$), tripping governs. For strong longitudinals (large $\gamma$), overall column buckling governs. For intermediate $\gamma$, stiffened-panel buckling governs.**

**Model Solution â€” Part (b):**

Given: flat-bar longitudinal $120 \times 12$ mm, $L = 2500$ mm, pin-ended, $E = 2.06 \times 10^{5}$ N/mmآ², $\sigma_y = 235$ N/mmآ².

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area.**

$$
A = 120 \times 12 = 1440 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Minimum second moment of area** (about weak axis, i.e., parallel to plate â€” flat-bar buckles about its minor axis):

$$
I_{min} = \dfrac{12 \times 120^{3}}{12} = 12 \times 144000 = 1.728 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

**Step 3: Radius of gyration.**

$$
r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{min}}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.728 \times 10^{6}}{1440}} = \sqrt{1200} = 34.64 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Slenderness ratio** (pin-ended, $L_e = L = 2500$ mm):

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{r} = \dfrac{2500}{34.64} = 72.2
$$

**Step 5: Euler critical stress.**

$$
\sigma_E = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 2.06 \times 10^{5}}{72.2^{2}}
$$

$$
\sigma_E = \dfrac{2.033 \times 10^{6}}{5213} \approx 390.1 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 6: Compare with yield.**

$$
\sigma_E = 390.1 \text{ N/mm}^{2} > \sigma_y = 235 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

Since Euler stress exceeds yield, the column is **stocky** and will **yield before buckling**. Hence the failure mode is **material yielding**, not elastic buckling.

**Comment:** The slenderness ratio is below the **Eulerâ€“Johnson transition slenderness**:

$$
\lambda_{crit} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_y}} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{2.06 \times 10^{5}}{235}} \approx 93.0
$$

Since $\lambda = 72.2 < \lambda_{crit} = 93.0$, the column is in the **inelastic (Johnson) range**. Use the Johnson parabola:

$$
\sigma_u = \sigma_y \left[1 - \dfrac{\sigma_y}{4 \sigma_E}\right] = 235 \left[1 - \dfrac{235}{4 \times 390.1}\right] = 235 \times [1 - 0.1506] = 199.6 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

So the realistic **ultimate stress** is about **200 N/mmآ²**, governing the design.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Summary for KTU 2024 Module 1 â€” Modes of Failure in Sagging**

- **Sagging = smile shape = deck in compression, bottom in tension.**
- **Hogging = frown shape = deck in tension, bottom in compression.** *(Don't confuse them â€” this is the #1 error in KTU papers.)*
- **Five primary failure modes in sagging:**
  1. Deck plate panel buckling (compression)
  2. Deck longitudinal column/tripping/panel buckling (compression)
  3. Side shell buckling above neutral axis (compression)
  4. Bottom shell yielding (tension)
  5. Hatch corner stress concentration
- **Plate buckling stress formula (long plate, k=4.0):** $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{4 \pi^{2} E}{12(1-\nu^{2})}(t/b)^{2}$ â€” **memorise this.**
- **Effective width concept (Faulkner):** $b_e/b = (\sigma_{cr}/\sigma_y)[\sqrt{1+\sigma_y/\sigma_{cr}} - 1]$ â€” basis of CSR scantling rules.
- **Plate slenderness $\beta = (b/t)\sqrt{\sigma_y/E}$:**
  - $\beta < 1$ â†’ stocky â†’ yielding
  - $\beta > 1$ â†’ slender â†’ buckling
- **Column buckling (Euler):** $\sigma_E = \pi^2 E / (L_e/r)^2$. For $L_e/r < \pi\sqrt{E/\sigma_y}$, use **Johnson parabola** instead.
- **Neutral axis shifts toward the bottom** in sagging as the deck progressively loses effective area to buckling.
- **Design bottom line:** Sagging design is governed by **buckling of deck structures**, NOT yielding of bottom plating.
- **Always include the Poisson factor $(1-\nu^2)$** in plate buckling calculations â€” examiners deduct marks if missing.
- **Permissible stress safety factor** is typically **1.5** for plate buckling (IACS) â€” verify against the rule book for the specific society.
- **Real ship engineering rule-of-thumb:** minimum deck plate thickness is around $t \geq 1.5\% \cdot s$ where $s$ is stiffener spacing â€” but always check against the rule-based calculation.
- **Units sanity:** Keep $E$ in N/mmآ², lengths in mm, stresses in N/mmآ². **Do not mix mm and m.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hogging and Torsion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hogging and Torsion ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Hogging â€” The Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hogging** is the condition of longitudinal bending of a ship's hull girder in which the **bow and stern are deflected downward** relative to the amidships region, producing a concave-up curvature when viewed in profile. The corresponding **Hogging Moment** is positive by convention and produces tensile stresses at the **deck (upper flange)** and compressive stresses at the **bottom (lower flange)** of the hull girder. **Key KTU Terminology:** - **Still Water Bending Moment (SWBM):** $M_{SW}$ â€” caused by static weight distribution and buoyancy. - **Wave Bending Moment (WBM):** $M_W$ â€” caused by wave-induced pressure distribution. - **Total Bending Moment:** $M_T = M_{SW} \pm M_W$ (sign depends on whether combined action amplifies hogging or sagging). > [!NOTE] > **Sagging** is the OPPOSITE condition. The amidships deflects downward while the ends stay up, producing a convex-up curvature. It produces tensile stresses at the **bottom plating** and compressive stresses at the **deck plating**. --- ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy â€” The "Diving Board" Effect Imagine a thin wooden plank (like a diving board) supported at its two ends: - **Hogging** is like standing at the **center** of the plank â€” the middle bows UP and the ends droop DOWN. The top fibers stretch and the bottom fibers compress. - **Sagging** is like suspending the plank at its **center** (or loading it heavily amidships) â€” the middle droops DOWN, the bottom fibers stretch and the top fibers compress. A ship behaves exactly the same way as a hollow, floating beam (the **hull girder**). When the ship is **partially loaded** (light condition) with buoyancy concentrated amidships, it **hoggs**. When it is **fully loaded in a head or following sea**, it **sags**. --- ### 1.3 Torsion â€” The Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Torsion in a ship's hull** refers to the twisting of the hull girder about its longitudinal axis, caused by **asymmetric transverse loading** between the port and starboard sides. It produces **shear stresses** in the hull plating and stiffeners that must be superimposed on the bending stresses from hogging/sagging. **Physical Constants & Standards Used:** - **Shear Modulus of Steel:** $G = \mathbf{77\ GPa}$ (typical for marine mild steel, $G \approx E/2.6$). - **Young's Modulus of Steel:** $E = \mathbf{206\ GPa}$. - **Classification Society:** IACS (International Association of Classification Societies) rules are standard for wave load evaluation. --- ### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy â€” The "Twisted Towel" Take a wet towel and twist one end while holding the other fixed: the fibres run helically, the cross-section becomes non-planar, and shear stresses run along the length of every fibre. The ship hull is essentially a very long, hollow, thin-walled tube â€” when one side is lifted by a wave crest while the other side is in a wave trough, the hull twists exactly like this towel, producing closed-circuit **shear flows** around every transverse ring of the hull. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic:** "Hogging â†’ Head High, Bottom Compressed"; "Sagging â†’ Bottom Stretched, Top Compressed." --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Causes of Hogging in a Ship The hull girder bending moment is governed by the balance between **weight (W)** distribution and **buoyancy (B)** distribution along the length. **Primary Causes of HOGGING:** 1. **Partially loaded condition (ballast or light ship):** Buoyancy curve (B) exceeds weight curve (W) amidships because buoyancy responds to hull form, while weight is concentrated at heavy items (engines, cargo in holds). Net upward force amidships bends the hull. 2. **End-to-end sailing with wave crest at amidships (Wave condition):** Wave crest amidships lifts the middle more than the ends â†’ hogging. 3. **Heavy concentrated weight at bow or stern with light amidships:** e.g., a fully loaded bow thruster tunnel or a flooded bow compartment. 4. **Launch condition (slipways release):** Hull supported only at fore and aft poppets â†’ the unsupported middle sags slightly, then partially unloads into a hogging configuration once waterborne. **Primary Causes of SAGGING:** 1. **Fully loaded homogeneous condition** (especially in head seas): Amidships weight exceeds local buoyancy. 2. **Wave trough amidships:** Lifts the ends more than the middle. 3. **Heavy cargo concentrated amidships.** --- ### 2.2 Sign Convention (IACS / Most Classification Societies) | Condition | Symbol | Sign of BM | Deck Stress | Bottom Stress | |-----------|--------|------------|-------------|---------------| | Hogging | + | Positive | **Tensile** | Compressive | | Sagging | â€“ | Negative | Compressive | **Tensile** | The classical **Euler-Bernoulli bending equation** applies: $$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$ where: - $M$ = bending moment (Nآ·m) - $y$ = distance from neutral axis (m) - $I$ = second moment of area of the hull girder section about the horizontal neutral axis ($m^4$) **Section Modulus:** $$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$ So the maximum bending stress becomes: $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z}$$ --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet â€” Hogging and Torsion > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table.** Every KTU exam problem on this topic reduces to one or more of these equations. | # | Formula | Meaning / Variables | Application | |---|---------|---------------------|-------------| | 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Bending stress in hull girder | Hogging/Sagging stress check | | 2 | $M_{Total} = M_{SW} + M_{W}$ (hog) or $M_{SW} - M_{W}$ (sag) | Combined bending moment | Design envelope | | 3 | $Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{Total}}{\sigma_{perm}}$ | Required section modulus | Minimum scantlings | | 4 | $\tau = \dfrac{T}{2 A_m \, t}$ | Bredt's shear stress (closed thin section) | Torsion in ship hull | | 5 | $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx} = \dfrac{T}{4 A_m^{2} \, G} \oint \dfrac{ds}{t}$ | Rate of twist (closed thin section) | Angular twist | | 6 | $GJ_{eff} = \dfrac{4 A_m^{2}}{\oint \dfrac{ds}{t}}$ | Effective torsional rigidity | Stiffness check | | 7 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\tau_{Bredt}}{k}$ | Smith's corrected max stress | Real shear distribution in panels | | 8 | $M_W = k_W L^{2} B \, (C_B + 0.7)$ | IACS-style wave BM (typical form) | Quick estimation | | 9 | $\tau_{perm} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\nu}$ | Allowable shear (von Mises, $\nu$ = SF) | Strength check | > **Where:** $A_m$ = area enclosed by median (middle-line) of the closed cell, $t$ = plating thickness, $G$ = shear modulus, $ds$ = arc length along median line, $k$ = Smith's correction factor (0 to 1). --- ### 2.4 Smith's Correction Factor â€” Why It Matters In a rectangular ship panel subjected to in-plane shear flow $q$, the shear stress is **NOT uniform** â€” it is maximum at the loaded (restrained) edges and decreases toward the free edges. Smith's correction factor $k$ relates the **average** shear stress in the panel to the **maximum** value: $$\tau_{avg} = k \cdot \tau_{max}$$ Equivalently: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{\tau_{Bredt}}{k} = \frac{T}{2 A_m t \, k}$$ **Smith's $k$ values (for typical ship panels, simply supported on stiffeners):** | Panel aspect ratio $a/b$ | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 2.5 | 3.0 | $\infty$ | |--------------------------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|----------| | Smith's $k$ | 0.74 | 0.83 | 0.86 | 0.90 | 0.93 | 1.00 | > [!TIP] > **Why does this matter for ships?** A ship hull is essentially a closed box made of flat plating panels. Bredt's formula assumes uniform $\tau = q/t$ across every panel â€” this is *over-optimistic*. Smith's correction penalises the design so the stress at the most loaded edge (e.g., near a corner) is correctly accounted for. --- ### 2.5 Engineering Utility - **Hogging moment** governs **deck plating thickness** and **upper flange** longitudinal strength (a ship in ballast almost always hoggs). - **Sagging moment** governs **bottom plating** and **inner bottom** (a fully laden bulk carrier in head seas sags). - **Torsional shear stress** governs **shell plating** and is critical for **container ships** (asymmetric stack loading) and **warships** (asymmetric damage, dynamic loads). - The **combined stress state** (bending + shear + torsion + local pressure) is the real design driver â€” this is why KTU Module 1 is foundational. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bredt's Formula for Closed Thin-Walled Sections under Torsion > [!NOTE] > **Objective:** Show that for a closed, single-cell, thin-walled section subjected to a pure torque $T$, the shear flow $q$ is *constant* around the section and is given by $q = \dfrac{T}{2 A_m}$. #### Step 1 â€” Setup and Assumptions Consider a closed thin-walled tube of constant wall thickness $t(s)$ along a median line of length $L_m$ enclosing area $A_m$. Apply torque $T$ about the longitudinal axis. **Assumptions:** - Wall is thin: $t \ll$ any transverse dimension. - Cross-section is *unrestrained from warping* (free to deform out of plane). - Material is linear-elastic, isotropic. #### Step 2 â€” Make a Longitudinal Cut To make the section statically determinate, cut the section along a generator line (i.e., open the tube). Apply a *redundant* shear flow $q_0$ at the cut such that the resulting relative angular displacement at the cut is zero (compatibility). #### Step 3 â€” Equilibrium of Forces on a Wall Element Take an infinitesimal element of the wall of arc length $ds$, thickness $t$. The shear flow is $q$ (force per unit length). The shear force on the two sides is $q \, ds$ each. For equilibrium, the shear flow $q$ must be the *same* on both faces â€” so $q$ is **constant along the median line** (it cannot change without an applied surface traction). #### Step 4 â€” Moment Equilibrium of the Whole Section The shear flow $q$ acts tangent to the median line at every point. The moment of this shear flow about an interior point (e.g., the centroid) equals the applied torque: $$T = \oint q \cdot r \, ds$$ where $r$ is the perpendicular distance from the interior point to the tangent at the element. #### Step 5 â€” Geometric Identity For a closed curve, the swept area by the radius vector $r$ as it sweeps along $ds$ is twice the enclosed area: $$r \, ds = 2 \, dA \quad \Rightarrow \quad \oint r \, ds = 2 A_m$$ Substituting: $$T = q \cdot \oint r \, ds = q \cdot 2 A_m$$ #### Step 6 â€” Final Result for Shear Flow $$\boxed{q = \frac{T}{2 A_m}}$$ #### Step 7 â€” Shear Stress in the Wall Since shear flow is force per unit length and stress is force per unit area: $$\tau = \frac{q}{t} \quad \text{(for variable thickness, use local } t(s))$$ $$\boxed{\tau = \frac{T}{2 A_m \, t}}$$ This is **Bredt's First Formula** â€” the cornerstone of ship torsion analysis. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Rate of Twist for Closed Thin-Walled Sections > [!NOTE] > **Objective:** Find the angle of twist per unit length $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}$ for a closed thin-walled section. #### Step 1 â€” Shear Strain in the Wall For pure shear, Hooke's law in shear gives: $$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{q}{G \, t} = \frac{T}{2 A_m \, G \, t}$$ #### Step 2 â€” Geometry of Twist The shear strain $\gamma$ is also the angular distortion of an originally right-angled element in the wall. Geometrically, the differential rotation of the cut faces relative to each other, integrated over the median line, must match the twist: $$d\theta = \frac{1}{2 A_m} \oint \gamma \, ds$$ #### Step 3 â€” Substitute and Simplify $$d\theta = \frac{1}{2 A_m} \oint \frac{T}{2 A_m G t} \, ds = \frac{T}{4 A_m^{2} G} \oint \frac{ds}{t}$$ #### Step 4 â€” Rate of Twist (per unit length) $$\boxed{\frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{T}{4 A_m^{2} \, G} \oint \frac{ds}{t}}$$ #### Step 5 â€” Effective Torsional Rigidity Comparing with $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx} = \dfrac{T}{GJ_{eff}}$: $$\boxed{GJ_{eff} = \frac{4 A_m^{2}}{\oint \dfrac{ds}{t}}}$$ This is **Bredt's Second Formula**. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hogging Bending Stress in the Hull Girder > [!NOTE] > **Objective:** Connect the hull-girder bending moment $M$ to the deck/bottom stresses. #### Step 1 â€” Hull as a Beam Treat the complete hull cross-section as an I-beam (with the deck as the upper flange, the bottom as the lower flange, and the shell + bulkheads as the web). #### Step 2 â€” Locate the Neutral Axis By definition, the neutral axis is the locus of points with zero longitudinal strain. For an asymmetric section, it is found from: $$\bar{y} = \frac{\int y \, dA}{\int dA} = \frac{\sum A_i \, y_i}{\sum A_i}$$ #### Step 3 â€” Second Moment of Area For the standard ship hull girder, include the **effective flange widths** of the deck, bottom, and inner bottom. Classification rules prescribe effective widths (typically 0.2L from midships). The result is a large $I$ â€” typically $10^{5}$ to $10^{6}\ m^{4}$ for a VLCC. #### Step 4 â€” Apply Euler-Bernoulli $$\sigma = \frac{M \, y}{I}$$ #### Step 5 â€” Specialise for Hogging (Sign Convention) In hogging, $M > 0$ (positive moment). The deck ($y = +y_D$) experiences **tension** and the bottom ($y = -y_B$) experiences **compression**: $$\sigma_{deck} = +\frac{M \, y_D}{I} \quad \text{(tension â€” critical for slender decks)}$$ $$\sigma_{bottom} = -\frac{M \, y_B}{I} \quad \text{(compression â€” buckling concern)}$$ #### Step 6 â€” Required Section Modulus Set the allowable stress $\sigma_{perm}$ and solve: $$\boxed{Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{perm}}}$$ For mild steel hulls, IACS uses $\sigma_{perm} = \dfrac{175}{k}\ \text{MPa}$ (where $k$ is a material factor). $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Synthesis) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A single-cell closed box girder of a ship represents the hull cross-section as a rectangle of width $B = 20\ m$ and depth $D = 12\ m$, with constant plating thickness $t = 20\ mm$. A torque $T = 5 \times 10^{6}\ N \cdot m$ is applied (asymmetric wave loading). Calculate (a) Bredt's shear stress, (b) the rate of twist per metre, and (c) the maximum shear stress using Smith's correction for the long side panels ($a/b = 4$, hence $k = 0.96$). #### Solution: **(a) Median-line enclosed area:** $$A_m = B \times D = 20 \times 12 = 240\ m^{2}$$ **Bredt's shear stress:** $$\tau = \frac{T}{2 A_m t} = \frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 240 \times 0.020} = \frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{9.6}$$ $$\boxed{\tau \approx 5.21 \times 10^{5}\ Pa = 0.521\ MPa}$$ **(b) Rate of twist:** The median-line contour integral for a rectangle with constant $t$: $$\oint \frac{ds}{t} = \frac{2(B + D)}{t} = \frac{2(20 + 12)}{0.020} = \frac{64}{0.020} = 3200\ m^{-1}$$ Using $G = 77 \times 10^{9}\ Pa$: $$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{T}{4 A_m^{2} G} \oint \frac{ds}{t} = \frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{4 \times (240)^{2} \times 77 \times 10^{9}} \times 3200$$ Computing denominator: $4 \times 57600 \times 77 \times 10^{9} = 1.774 \times 10^{16}$ $$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{5 \times 10^{6} \times 3200}{1.774 \times 10^{16}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{10}}{1.774 \times 10^{16}}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{d\theta}{dx} \approx 9.02 \times 10^{-7}\ rad/m \approx 0.0009^{\circ}/m}$$ **(c) Smith's corrected maximum stress (long side, $k = 0.96$):** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{\tau_{Bredt}}{k} = \frac{0.521}{0.96} \approx 0.542\ MPa$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 0.542\ MPa \ll \tau_{perm}}$$ Result is well within allowable limits â€” the closed-cell hull girder is enormously stiff in torsion (this is why ship hulls are designed as closed cells, not as open girders!). --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram â€” Causes of Hogging vs Sagging in Ship Hull Girder ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Hull Girder Bending] --> load{Loading Condition?} load -->|Light / Ballast| condA[Buoyancy exceeds Weight amidships] load -->|Fully Loaded| condB[Weight exceeds Buoyancy amidships] load -->|Wave Action| condC[Wave crest or trough amidships] condA --> hog[Hogging Moment Positive] condB --> sag[Sagging Moment Negative] condC --> waveChoice{Wave position?} waveChoice -->|Crest amidships| hog waveChoice -->|Trough amidships| sag hog --> stressH[Deck TENSILE stress
Bottom COMPRESSIVE stress] sag --> stressS[Deck COMPRESSIVE stress
Bottom TENSILE stress] stressH --> designH[Design Driver: Deck plating
Upper flange strength] stressS --> designS[Design Driver: Bottom plating
Inner bottom girders] designH --> checkH[Critical buckling check
on compression flange] designS --> checkS[Critical fatigue check
on tension flange] style start fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style hog fill:#c00000,color:#fff,stroke:#000 style sag fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff,stroke:#000 style stressH fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000 style stressS fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#000 ``` --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram â€” Torsional Analysis Methodology (Closed Thin-Walled Section) ```mermaid flowchart LR input[Given: T torque
Section geometry B D t] --> step1[Step 1: Compute median
line area Am] step1 --> step2[Step 2: Apply Bredt formula
tau equals T over 2 Am t] step2 --> step3[Step 3: Identify panel
aspect ratios a over b] step3 --> step4[Step 4: Look up Smith k
for each panel] step4 --> step5[Step 5: Correct stress
tau max equals tau over k] step5 --> step6[Step 6: Compute contour
integral ds over t] step6 --> step7[Step 7: Compute rate of twist
d theta dx] step7 --> step8[Step 8: Check against
allowable stress and twist] input --> step6 step8 --> result[Output: tau max
and d theta dx] style input fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff,stroke:#000 style step2 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000 style step5 fill:#c00000,color:#fff,stroke:#000 style result fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff,stroke:#000 ``` --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram â€” Open vs Closed Section Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TD sect{Type of hull section?} -->|Single closed cell| closed[Closed thin walled] sect -->|Two or more cells| multi[Multi cell closed] sect -->|No closed cell| open[Open section] closed --> cprop[Shear flow q constant
around the whole loop] cprop --> cprop1[Uses Bredt formula tau equals T over 2 Am t] cprop1 --> cprop2[Very high torsional rigidity GJ] cprop2 --> cprop3[Negligible warping torsion] cprop3 --> cpropEnd[Standard ship hull behavior] multi --> mprop[Shear flow NOT constant
requires equilibrium of cells] mprop --> mprop1[Use multi cell torsion theory] mprop1 --> mprop2[Example: Double hull tanker] open --> oprop[NO Bredt formula applicable] oprop --> oprop1[Shear stress VERY LOW
concentrated near roots] oprop1 --> oprop2[Warping torsion DOMINATES] oprop2 --> oprop3[Torsional rigidity orders of
magnitude LOWER] oprop3 --> opropEnd2[NEVER used for ship hull] style closed fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff,stroke:#000 style open fill:#c00000,color:#fff,stroke:#000 style cprop2 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000 style oprop3 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A â€” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Q1. Define hogging. State any four loading conditions that cause hogging in a ship.** `[KTU University Exam â€“ Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Hogging** is the longitudinal bending of the hull girder in which the ends (bow and stern) deflect downward relative to the amidships region, producing tensile stresses at the deck and compressive stresses at the bottom. **Four loading conditions causing hogging:** 1. **Light ship / ballast condition** â€” buoyancy curve exceeds weight curve amidships (hull form responds more to displacement than to weight distribution). 2. **Partially loaded condition** â€” heavy items (engines, cargo in central holds) concentrated amidships but buoyancy spread over full hull. 3. **Wave crest amidships** (head sea or following sea) â€” wave-induced upward pressure lifts the middle more than the ends. 4. **Launching condition** â€” hull supported only at the fore and aft poppets; once waterborne, the release can induce hogging. 5. (Bonus) Heavy concentrated weight at bow or stern with light amidships. *(Any 4 from above, 0.5 mark each + 1 mark for definition.)* --- #### **Q2. What is Smith's correction factor? Why is it necessary in torsional stress calculation of ship hulls?** `[KTU University Exam â€“ May 2024]` **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Smith's correction factor $k$** is a dimensionless factor (between 0 and 1) that accounts for the **non-uniform distribution of shear stress** across a rectangular ship panel subjected to an in-plane shear flow.
