It is defined as: > $$\tau_{avg} = k \cdot \tau_{max}$$ **Necessity:** - Bredt's formula assumes a **uniform** shear stress $\tau = q/t$ across the wall thickness â€” this is true only if the panel aspect ratio $a/b \to \infty$. - In a real ship hull panel, shear stress is **maximum at the restrained edges** (e.g., at stiffeners or corners) and **lower in the middle** of the panel. - Without Smith's correction, Bredt's formula **underestimates** the actual peak stress, leading to unsafe design. - $k$ depends on the panel aspect ratio $a/b$; typical values range from **0.74** (square panel) to **1.00** (very long slender panel). *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for necessity and explanation.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B â€” Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A â€” On Torsion (Bredt + Smith)** > **(a)** Derive Bredt's formula $\tau = \dfrac{T}{2 A_m t}$ for the shear stress in a thin-walled closed single-cell section subjected to pure torsion. State all assumptions clearly. **[7 marks]** > > **(b)** A warship hull cross-section is approximated as a closed rectangular box girder of breadth $B = 18\ m$, depth $D = 10\ m$, and uniform plating thickness $t = 18\ mm$. Due to asymmetric wave loading, the hull experiences a torque $T = 8 \times 10^{6}\ N \cdot m$. The longer side panels have aspect ratio $a/b = 3$ (Smith's $k = 0.93$) and the shorter side panels have $a/b = 1.5$ (Smith's $k = 0.83$). Calculate: > - (i) Bredt's shear stress > - (ii) Smith's corrected maximum stress in the longer side panels > - (iii) Smith's corrected maximum stress in the shorter side panels > - (iv) Rate of twist per metre, given $G = 77\ GPa$. **[7 marks]** #### **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** See the full step-by-step derivation in **Section 3.1** above. Key checkpoints for valuation: - Statement of assumptions (thin wall, free warping, linear elastic) â€” **1 mark** - Cut-section free body diagram and identification of constant $q$ â€” **2 marks** - Moment equilibrium leading to $T = q \cdot 2 A_m$ â€” **2 marks** - Final form $\tau = T / (2 A_m t)$ â€” **2 marks** **Part (b):** **(i) Median-line enclosed area:** $$A_m = B \times D = 18 \times 10 = 180\ m^{2}$$ **Bredt's shear stress:** $$\tau_{Bredt} = \frac{T}{2 A_m t} = \frac{8 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 180 \times 0.018} = \frac{8 \times 10^{6}}{6.48}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{Bredt} \approx 1.235\ MPa}$$ **(ii) Smith's corrected max stress â€” longer side ($k = 0.93$):** $$\tau_{max,L} = \frac{\tau_{Bredt}}{k_L} = \frac{1.235}{0.93} \approx 1.328\ MPa$$ **(iii) Smith's corrected max stress â€” shorter side ($k = 0.83$):** $$\tau_{max,S} = \frac{\tau_{Bredt}}{k_S} = \frac{1.235}{0.83} \approx 1.488\ MPa$$ **(iv) Rate of twist:** The contour integral for a rectangle with constant thickness: $$\oint \frac{ds}{t} = \frac{2(B + D)}{t} = \frac{2(18 + 10)}{0.018} = \frac{56}{0.018} \approx 3111.11\ m^{-1}$$ $$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{T}{4 A_m^{2} G} \oint \frac{ds}{t} = \frac{8 \times 10^{6}}{4 \times (180)^{2} \times 77 \times 10^{9}} \times 3111.11$$ Denominator: $4 \times 32400 \times 77 \times 10^{9} = 9.979 \times 10^{15}$ $$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{8 \times 10^{6} \times 3111.11}{9.979 \times 10^{15}} = \frac{2.489 \times 10^{10}}{9.979 \times 10^{15}}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{d\theta}{dx} \approx 2.49 \times 10^{-6}\ rad/m \approx 0.00143^{\circ}/m}$$ **Conclusion:** The shorter side panels govern the design (higher corrected stress). All values are well within allowable limits for a warship hull. --- ### **Question B â€” On Hogging (Wave BM + Section Modulus)** > **(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the phenomenon of hogging and sagging in a ship's hull girder. Explain how the weight and buoyancy curves lead to these conditions, and describe the resulting stress distribution. **[7 marks]** > > **(b)** A bulk carrier of length $L = 200\ m$, breadth $B = 32\ m$, and draft $T = 14.5\ m$ has block coefficient $C_B = 0.82$. Using the IACS standard wave bending moment formula $M_W = k_W L^{2} B (C_B + 0.7)$ with $k_W = 0.055$ (hogging positive): > - (i) Calculate the wave bending moment at midships. > - (ii) If the still-water bending moment is $M_{SW} = 1.5 \times 10^{8}\ N \cdot m$ (hogging), determine the total design hogging moment. > - (iii) The neutral axis of the hull girder is at midships depth, with distances $y_{deck} = 8\ m$ and $y_{bottom} = 6.5\ m$. The second moment of area is $I = 1.2 \times 10^{6}\ m^{4}$. Calculate the maximum deck and bottom stresses. > - (iv) If the permissible stress is $\sigma_{perm} = 175\ MPa$, comment on whether the section is adequate. **[7 marks]** #### **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** *(Narrative + sketch description for valuation)* The hull behaves as a **beam** supported (in a hydrodynamic sense) by a distributed buoyancy force and loaded by its own distributed weight. - **Weight curve $w(x)$:** Generally uniform-ish in a fully loaded ship; peaks where heavy items (engine room, cargo holds) are located. - **Buoyancy curve $b(x)$:** Mirrors the underwater hull form; peaks where the hull is widest (amidships). **Hogging** occurs when $b(x) > w(x)$ in the middle and $b(x) < w(x)$ at the ends â†’ net upward force amidships â†’ ends droop, middle lifts â†’ deck stretches (tension), bottom compresses. **Sagging** occurs in the opposite sense. The **net force curve** $f(x) = b(x) - w(x)$ is integrated twice (with $M(x) = \int f(x)\,dx^2$ with appropriate signs) to give the bending moment diagram. | Marks | Content expected | |-------|------------------| | 2 | Sketch showing hull curvature in hog and sag, with deck/bottom stresses marked | | 2 | Explanation of weight and buoyancy curves | | 2 | Derivation of net force â†’ bending moment | | 1 | Conclusion on stress distribution | **Part (b):** **(i) Wave bending moment:** $$M_W = 0.055 \times (200)^{2} \times 32 \times (0.82 + 0.7)$$ $$M_W = 0.055 \times 40000 \times 32 \times 1.52$$ $$M_W = 0.055 \times 40000 \times 48.64$$ $$\boxed{M_W \approx 1.070 \times 10^{8}\ N \cdot m}$$ **(ii) Total design hogging moment (both act in same direction):** $$M_{Total} = M_{SW} + M_W = 1.5 \times 10^{8} + 1.070 \times 10^{8}$$ $$\boxed{M_{Total} \approx 2.570 \times 10^{8}\ N \cdot m}$$ **(iii) Maximum stresses:** Deck (tension in hogging): $$\sigma_{deck} = \frac{M_{Total} \cdot y_{deck}}{I} = \frac{2.570 \times 10^{8} \times 8}{1.2 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$\sigma_{deck} = \frac{2.056 \times 10^{9}}{1.2 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1.713 \times 10^{3}\ \text{Pa} \cdot 10^{3}$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_{deck} \approx 171.3\ MPa\ \text{(Tension)}}$$ Bottom (compression in hogging): $$\sigma_{bottom} = \frac{M_{Total} \cdot y_{bottom}}{I} = \frac{2.570 \times 10^{8} \times 6.5}{1.2 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_{bottom} \approx 139.2\ MPa\ \text{(Compression)}}$$ **(iv) Adequacy check:** - Deck stress $171.3\ MPa < 175\ MPa$ â€” **MARGINALLY OK** (within 2% â€” design is *just* acceptable, but no real safety margin). - Bottom stress $139.2\ MPa < 175\ MPa$ â€” OK. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign errors are the #1 cause of mark loss.** In hogging, deck is in TENSION (positive $M$, positive $y$). Do NOT write "deck compression" â€” that is for sagging. > 2. **Wave BM and SWBM must be ADDED for hogging** (both positive) and **SUBTRACTED for sagging** if their signs oppose. Reading the question carefully is critical. > 3. **Smith's correction divisor vs multiplier:** Some textbooks define $k$ as $\tau_{max} / \tau_{avg}$. Verify the definition in your specific module. Here we used the conservative IACS form: $\tau_{max} = \tau_{Bredt}/k$. > 4. **Forgetting units:** $t$ must be in METRES (not mm!) when computing stress in Pa. Slip-ups here lose 1 mark immediately. > 5. **In multi-cell torsion**, Bredt's formula in its simple form DOES NOT apply. The shear flow varies between cells. Always check if the section is single-cell before applying Bredt. > 6. **Allowable stress in shear** = $\sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$ by von Mises, NOT $\sigma_y$. For $\sigma_y = 235\ MPa$, $\tau_{perm} \approx 136\ MPa$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist â€” Print This Before Exam** **Hogging & Sagging (Longitudinal Bending):** - Hogging: ends down, middle up â†’ **deck in tension, bottom in compression**. - Sagging: middle down, ends up â†’ **deck in compression, bottom in tension**. - Caused by mismatch between weight and buoyancy distributions. - Bending stress: $\sigma = My/I = M/Z$. - IACS wave BM: $M_W = k_W L^{2} B (C_B + 0.7)$, $k_W \approx 0.055$ for hog. - Required $Z = M_{max}/\sigma_{perm}$. **Torsion in Ship Hull (Closed Thin-Walled Section):** - Hull is a closed box â†’ shear flow $q$ is constant around median line. - **Bredt 1st formula:** $\tau = T/(2 A_m t)$. - **Bredt 2nd formula:** $GJ_{eff} = 4 A_m^{2}/\oint (ds/t)$. - **Rate of twist:** $d\theta/dx = T/(4 A_m^{2} G) \oint (ds/t)$. - Use $G = 77\ GPa$ for marine steel. **Smith's Correction (Crucial for Real Ship Panels):** - $\tau_{max} = \tau_{Bredt}/k$, where $k \in [0.74, 1.0]$. - $k$ depends on panel aspect ratio $a/b$. - $k \approx 0.74$ (square), $k \to 1$ (long slender). - **Always apply** Smith's correction in ship torsion problems. **Open vs Closed Section:** - Closed: extremely stiff in torsion (uses Bredt). - Open: very weak in torsion (warping torsion dominates, NOT Bredt). - **Ships are ALWAYS designed as closed sections** for this reason. **Critical Pitfalls:** - Don't confuse absolute value $\vert x \vert$ â€” write $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX. - Always check sign of $M$ (hog +ve, sag âˆ’ve). - Smith divisor vs multiplier â€” verify definition. - $t$ in metres, not mm. - Multi-cell section â‰  simple Bredt. **Key Constants to Memorise:** - $E_{steel} = 206\ GPa$ - $G_{steel} = 77\ GPa$ (or $E/2.6$) - $\sigma_{y,mild\ steel} = 235\ MPa$ - $\tau_{allow,shear} = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$ --- **End of Module 1 Notes â€” Hogging and Torsion** *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme â€” PCNST601 Ship Structures II*

topics.title: Weight and Weight Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Weight and Weight Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Weight (W)** of a ship is defined as the total gravitational force exerted on the vessel by all its constituent components, measured in **tonnes (t)** or **kilonewtons (kN)**, with the standard conversion factor being **1 tonne = 9.81 kN**.

In naval architecture, ship weight is broadly classified into two principal categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lightship Weight (W_LS)** â€” The weight of the complete ship in operating condition *without* cargo, consumables, fuel, lubricating oil, ballast water, fresh water, feed water, stores, crew, or any variable loads. It is essentially the "empty" weight of the ship as it leaves the shipyard after construction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deadweight (DWT or DW)** â€” The total variable load a ship can carry, including cargo, fuel, fresh water, lubricating oil, ballast, stores, crew, and passengers. It represents the *earning capacity* of a merchant vessel.

The **Displacement (خ”)** of a ship is the total weight of water displaced by the hull, given by Archimedes' principle:

$$\Delta = W_{LS} + DWT = \rho \cdot \nabla$$

where $\rho$ is the **density of seawater (1.025 t/mآ³)** and $\nabla$ is the **volumetric displacement**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a giant floating office building:
- **Lightship weight** = the empty building itself â€” steel structure, electrical wiring, plumbing, elevators, fixed furniture, machinery (HVAC, generators), paint, and glass.
- **Deadweight** = everything you put *into* the building to make it functional â€” the people working, the documents, the food in the cafeteria, the diesel for the backup generator, the water in the tanks.

A ship's "weight distribution" is like knowing *exactly* where on each floor the heavy stuff is placed â€” a 500-tonne engine in the middle of deck 3, fuel tanks low in the hull, cargo stacked forward, and a bridge full of electronics on top. The **center of gravity (G)** is the single point where, if you could balance the entire ship on a pin, it would tip perfectly.

> [!NOTE]
> In ship design, knowing **where** the weight is, is just as critical as knowing **how much** weight exists. A well-distributed ship floats level; a poorly distributed one lists or trims dangerously.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in KTU/Naval Architecture

- **Density of seawater:** **1.025 t/mآ³**
- **Density of freshwater:** **1.000 t/mآ³**
- **Standard conversion:** **1 tonne = 9.81 kN = 1000 kgf**
- **Block Coefficient (C_B):** Ratio of ship volume to its bounding block
- **Form coefficients:** C_B, C_P (prismatic), C_M (midship)

### 1.4 Components of Lightship Weight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Lightship weight ($W_{LS}$) = **Steel Weight** + **Outfit Weight** + **Machinery Weight**

| Sub-Component | Description | Typical % of W_LS |
|---|---|---|
| Steel Weight | Hull plating, frames, stiffeners, superstructure | 60â€“70% |
| Outfit Weight | Paint, joinery, electrical, navigation, lifesaving | 20â€“25% |
| Machinery Weight | Main engine, auxiliaries, boilers, shafting | 10â€“15% |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Ship Weight

The complete ship weight tree is structured as follows:

**Level 1 â€” Total Displacement (خ”):**
- **Lightship Weight (W_LS):** Fixed, constant
  - Hull Steel Weight ($W_H$)
  - Outfit Weight ($W_O$)
  - Machinery Weight ($W_M$)
- **Deadweight (DWT):** Variable, cargo-earning
  - Cargo Weight ($W_{CGO}$)
  - Fuel Oil ($W_{FO}$)
  - Diesel Oil ($W_{DO}$)
  - Lubricating Oil ($W_{LO}$)
  - Fresh Water ($W_{FW}$)
  - Ballast Water ($W_{BW}$)
  - Stores & Provisions ($W_{ST}$)
  - Crew & Effects ($W_{CR}$)

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Component

- **Steel weight** dominates because the hull must withstand hydrostatic, hydrodynamic, and wave-induced loads over the ship's 25â€“30 year service life.
- **Outfit weight** is the "second ship" â€” without it, a steel hull is just an empty can.
- **Deadweight** is what the shipowner actually *sells* in the freight market; therefore maximising DWT is a primary design objective (subject to stability and strength constraints).

### 2.3 Weight Distribution and the Center of Gravity

A ship is a 3-D rigid body, so its **Center of Gravity (G)** requires three coordinates relative to a reference frame:

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Frame Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - **X-axis:** Longitudinal, measured from **AP (Aft Perpendicular)** or **midship (+ve forward)**
> - **Y-axis:** Transverse, measured from **centerline (+ve to port or starboard)**
> - **Z-axis:** Vertical, measured from **baseline (keel) or waterline**

The three coordinates of the center of gravity are:

$$x_G = \frac{\sum (w_i \cdot x_i)}{\sum w_i} \quad \text{(LCG â€” Longitudinal Center of Gravity)}$$

$$y_G = \frac{\sum (w_i \cdot y_i)}{\sum w_i} \quad \text{(TCG â€” Transverse Center of Gravity)}$$

$$z_G = \frac{\sum (w_i \cdot z_i)}{\sum w_i} \quad \text{(VCG â€” Vertical Center of Gravity)}$$

### 2.4 Weight Distribution Curves

A **weight distribution curve** plots the cumulative weight or weight-per-unit-length against the ship's length. The first moment of this curve gives the LCG. There are three principal representations:

1. **Loading Curve (Weight per Unit Length vs. Station):** Shows the "load density" â€” peaks correspond to machinery, superstructure, or concentrated cargo.
2. **Cumulative Weight Curve:** Integral of the loading curve, rising from AP to FP.
3. **Shear Force & Bending Moment Curves:** Derivatives/integrals derived from the weight distribution â€” critical for structural design.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta = W_{LS} + DWT$ | Total displacement | tonnes (t) |
| 2 | $\nabla = \Delta / \rho$ | Volumetric displacement | mآ³ |
| 3 | $x_G = \dfrac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}$ | LCG (longitudinal CG) | m from AP |
| 4 | $z_G = \dfrac{\sum w_i z_i}{\sum w_i}$ | VCG (vertical CG) | m above keel |
| 5 | $M_L = \sum w_i \cdot x_i$ | Longitudinal moment | tآ·m |
| 6 | $M_V = \sum w_i \cdot z_i$ | Vertical moment | tآ·m |
| 7 | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient | dimensionless |
| 8 | $W_{LS} = W_H + W_O + W_M$ | Lightship weight sum | t |
| 9 | $DWT = W_{CGO} + W_{FO} + W_{FW} + \ldots$ | Deadweight sum | t |
| 10 | $Trim = T_{FP} - T_{AP}$ | Trim (positive = bow down) | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** In all KTU numerical problems, always use a **consistent reference point** (AP, midship, or FP) for ALL x-coordinates. Mixing references is the #1 cause of wrong LCG answers.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Weight distribution analysis is the foundation for:

- **Stability analysis** â€” GZ curves, GGM, free surface effect
- **Strength analysis** â€” Still-water bending moment (SWBM), wave bending moment
- **Trim optimization** â€” Minimising resistance via optimal trim
- **Loading computer design** â€” Real-time cargo monitoring on modern ships
- **Classification society compliance** â€” DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS rules

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Center of Gravity Coordinates

Consider a ship comprising **n** discrete weight elements $w_1, w_2, w_3, \ldots, w_n$ located at positions $(x_1, y_1, z_1), (x_2, y_2, z_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n, z_n)$.

**Step 1 â€” Total Weight**
The total weight is simply the algebraic sum of all individual weights:

$$W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + \ldots + w_n$$

**Step 2 â€” Moment about a Reference Plane (e.g., Transverse plane through AP)**
By the principle of moments (Varignon's theorem), the moment of the total weight about any plane equals the sum of moments of individual weights about the same plane:

$$M = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i$$

This is the **longitudinal moment** when measured about a transverse plane.

**Step 3 â€” Equating to the CG Location**
The CG is defined as the point where the *entire weight W* may be assumed concentrated to produce the *same moment* about the reference plane as the actual distributed weight system:

$$W \cdot x_G = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i$$

**Step 4 â€” Solving for LCG**
Rearranging by dividing both sides by W:

$$x_G = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$

The identical derivation applies to $y_G$ (about centerline) and $z_G$ (about baseline).

**Step 5 â€” Application: Combined Loading**
If a ship is loaded in two stages (e.g., lightship + cargo), the **combined LCG** follows the moment-addition rule:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{G,combined} &= \frac{W_{LS} \cdot x_{G,LS} + DWT \cdot x_{G,DWT}}{W_{LS} + DWT} \\
&= \frac{W_{LS} \cdot x_{G,LS} + DWT \cdot x_{G,DWT}}{\Delta}
\end{aligned}$$

The vertical moment similarly:

$$z_{G,combined} = \frac{W_{LS} \cdot z_{G,LS} + DWT \cdot z_{G,DWT}}{\Delta}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** A bulk carrier has the following loading condition. Compute the displacement, LCG (from AP), and VCG (above keel).

| Item | Weight (t) | LCG from AP (m) | VCG above keel (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steel + Outfit + Machinery | 8,000 | 75.0 | 6.5 |
| Cargo Hold 1 | 5,000 | 30.0 | 8.0 |
| Cargo Hold 3 | 7,000 | 90.0 | 8.0 |
| Fuel Oil | 600 | 95.0 | 2.0 |
| Fresh Water | 300 | 85.0 | 4.0 |
| Stores & Crew | 200 | 110.0 | 14.0 |

**Solution:**

**Step 1 â€” Total Displacement**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 8000 + 5000 + 7000 + 600 + 300 + 200 \\
&= 21{,}100 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 â€” Total Longitudinal Moment (about AP)**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_L &= (8000 \times 75.0) + (5000 \times 30.0) + (7000 \times 90.0) \\
&\quad + (600 \times 95.0) + (300 \times 85.0) + (200 \times 110.0) \\
&= 600{,}000 + 150{,}000 + 630{,}000 + 57{,}000 + 25{,}500 + 22{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}484{,}500 \text{ tآ·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 â€” LCG**

$$x_G = \frac{1{,}484{,}500}{21{,}100} = 70.36 \text{ m from AP}$$

**Step 4 â€” Total Vertical Moment (about keel)**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_V &= (8000 \times 6.5) + (5000 \times 8.0) + (7000 \times 8.0) \\
&\quad + (600 \times 2.0) + (300 \times 4.0) + (200 \times 14.0) \\
&= 52{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 56{,}000 + 1{,}200 + 1{,}200 + 2{,}800 \\
&= 153{,}200 \text{ tآ·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 â€” VCG**

$$z_G = \frac{153{,}200}{21{,}100} = 7.26 \text{ m above keel}$$

**Step 6 â€” Sanity Check**
The VCG of 7.26 m is reasonable for a bulk carrier (typical 6â€“9 m). The LCG of 70.36 m is approximately amidships for a 140 m vessel, which is balanced.

### 3.3 Key Analytical Relationships

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Lever Rule (KTU Favourite):** For two weight groups A and B, the CG of the system divides the line AB in the inverse ratio of the weights: $\overline{AG} / \overline{GB} = W_B / W_A$.

This is a direct geometric consequence of the moment equation and is the fastest way to solve two-component problems in exams.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
