# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam â€” Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the "Deflection Curve" of a ship. State the boundary conditions applicable at the bow and stern for a ship floating freely in still water.**

**Model Answer:**

The **deflection curve** of a ship is the elastic curve traced by the keel (or hull girder neutral axis) along the length of the vessel when the hull bends under the combined action of weight and buoyancy. It is obtained by integrating the load distribution $w(x) = w_b(x) - w_w(x)$ twice, applying the boundary conditions of a simply supported beam.

**Boundary conditions at the bow and stern (for a ship floating freely in still water):**

1. **Shear force is zero:** $V = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $x = L$ (no transverse load at the extremities).
2. **Bending moment is zero:** $M = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $x = L$ (no concentrated moment at the extremities).

These translate mathematically to:
$$\left. \frac{d^3 y}{dx^3} \right|_{x=0,L} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left. \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} \right|_{x=0,L} = 0$$

The hull is thus mathematically equivalent to a **simply supported beam**.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam â€” May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between hogging and sagging in a ship. Sketch the corresponding deflection curves and indicate the sign of the bending moment in each case.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **Hogging** | **Sagging** |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| Definition | Bow and stern supported by buoyant water; midship heavier or unsupported | Midship supported; bow and stern overhanging or in wave troughs |
| Buoyancy distribution | Concentrated at ends | Concentrated at mid-length |
| Weight distribution | Concentrated at mid-length | Concentrated at ends |
| Hull shape | Concave upward (smile) â€” keel curves up | Concave downward (frown) â€” keel curves down |
| Stress state | **Deck in compression**, keel in tension | **Deck in tension**, keel in compression |
| Bending moment sign (Saggy-positive convention) | **Negative** ($M < 0$) | **Positive** ($M > 0$) |
| Real-world example | Ship in port partially supported by drydock blocks; ship with heavy amidships cargo | Ship in still water with light ends; ship riding a wave crest amidships |

> [!NOTE]
> In IACS Common Structural Rules, the **sagging condition** is often the governing design case for the deck, while **hogging** governs the bottom plating.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam â€” Dec 2023]

**A ship of length $L = 200$ m is floating freely in still water. The weight and buoyancy distributions are such that the net load on the hull girder is uniformly distributed at $w_0 = 50$ kN/m over the entire length. The flexural rigidity of the hull is $EI = 2.0 \times 10^{12}$ Nآ·mآ².**

#### (a) Derive the general expression for the deflection curve $y(x)$ of the ship. (7 Marks)

#### (b) Calculate the maximum deflection of the ship and state where it occurs. Comment on whether the hull is in hogging or sagging. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Step 1:** The net load distribution is:
$$w(x) = w_0 = 50 \text{ kN/m} = 50{,}000 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 2:** The fourth-order governing equation is:
$$EI \frac{d^4 y}{dx^4} = -w_0$$

**Step 3:** First integration (Shear):
$$EI \frac{d^3 y}{dx^3} = -w_0 x + C_1$$

**Step 4:** Second integration (Moment):
$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -\frac{w_0 x^2}{2} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5:** Third integration (Slope):
$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{w_0 x^3}{6} + \frac{C_1 x^2}{2} + C_2 x + C_3$$

**Step 6:** Fourth integration (Deflection):
$$EI y = -\frac{w_0 x^4}{24} + \frac{C_1 x^3}{6} + \frac{C_2 x^2}{2} + C_3 x + C_4$$

**Step 7:** Apply boundary conditions:

**BC 1:** $M(0) = 0$ (zero moment at bow)
$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2}\bigg|_{x=0} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = 0$$

**BC 2:** $M(L) = 0$ (zero moment at stern)
$$0 = -\frac{w_0 L^2}{2} + C_1 L \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = \frac{w_0 L}{2}$$

**BC 3:** By symmetry, $dy/dx = 0$ at $x = L/2$:
$$0 = -\frac{w_0 (L/2)^3}{6} + \frac{C_1 (L/2)^2}{2} + C_3$$

$$0 = -\frac{w_0 L^3}{48} + \frac{(w_0 L/2)(L^2/4)}{2} + C_3 = -\frac{w_0 L^3}{48} + \frac{w_0 L^3}{16} + C_3$$

$$C_3 = \frac{w_0 L^3}{48} - \frac{w_0 L^3}{16} = \frac{w_0 L^3 - 3 w_0 L^3}{48} = -\frac{w_0 L^3}{24}$$

**BC 4:** $y(0) = 0$ (deflection reference at bow)
$$C_4 = 0$$

**Step 8:** Substitute constants:
$$EI y(x) = -\frac{w_0 x^4}{24} + \frac{(w_0 L/2) x^3}{6} + 0 + \left(-\frac{w_0 L^3}{24}\right) x + 0$$

$$\boxed{EI \, y(x) = -\frac{w_0 x^4}{24} + \frac{w_0 L x^3}{12} - \frac{w_0 L^3 x}{24}}$$

This is the **deflection curve** of the hull girder.

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1:** Maximum deflection occurs at mid-length by symmetry ($x = L/2 = 100$ m):
$$EI y_{max} = -\frac{w_0 (L/2)^4}{24} + \frac{w_0 L (L/2)^3}{12} - \frac{w_0 L^3 (L/2)}{24}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values:

$w_0 = 50{,}000$ N/m, $L = 200$ m, $EI = 2.0 \times 10^{12}$ Nآ·mآ²

$$EI y_{max} = -\frac{50{,}000 \times (100)^4}{24} + \frac{50{,}000 \times 200 \times (100)^3}{12} - \frac{50{,}000 \times (200)^3 \times 100}{24}$$

$$EI y_{max} = -\frac{50{,}000 \times 10^8}{24} + \frac{50{,}000 \times 200 \times 10^6}{12} - \frac{50{,}000 \times 8 \times 10^6 \times 100}{24}$$

$$EI y_{max} = -\frac{5 \times 10^{12}}{24} + \frac{10^{12}}{12} - \frac{4 \times 10^{10}}{24}$$

Let us convert to a common denominator of 24:
$$EI y_{max} = -\frac{5 \times 10^{12}}{24} + \frac{2 \times 10^{12}}{24} - \frac{4 \times 10^{10}}{24}$$

$$EI y_{max} = \frac{-5 \times 10^{12} + 2 \times 10^{12} - 0.04 \times 10^{12}}{24} = \frac{-3.04 \times 10^{12}}{24}$$

$$EI y_{max} = -0.1267 \times 10^{12} = -1.267 \times 10^{11} \text{ Nآ·m}آ³$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $EI$ to get the deflection:
$$y_{max} = \frac{-1.267 \times 10^{11}}{2.0 \times 10^{12}} = -0.0633 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{y_{max} \approx -63.3 \text{ mm at mid-length}}$$

**Step 4:** Comment on hogging or sagging:

Since the load is uniformly distributed (downward, e.g., weight exceeds buoyancy uniformly), the **deflection is negative** (downward) and the curvature $\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = \frac{M}{EI} < 0$, meaning $M < 0$.

A negative bending moment in the saggy-positive convention corresponds to a **negative M**, which physically represents a hull where the ends are buoyant and the mid-section sags. Therefore, **the ship is in SAGGING condition**.

Equivalently, the deck is in **tension** and the keel is in **compression** at mid-length.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam â€” May 2024] â€” *Alternative Choice*

**A ship of length $L = 150$ m has a triangular load distribution on its hull girder. The load varies linearly from a maximum of $w_0 = 60$ kN/m at the bow to zero at the stern. The flexural rigidity is $EI = 1.5 \times 10^{12}$ Nآ·mآ².**

#### (a) Derive the deflection curve $y(x)$ of the ship. (7 Marks)

#### (b) Determine the location and magnitude of the maximum deflection. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Step 1:** Load function (maximum at $x = 0$, zero at $x = L$):
$$w(x) = w_0 \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right) = w_0 - \frac{w_0 x}{L}$$

**Step 2:** Governing equation:
$$EI \frac{d^4 y}{dx^4} = -w_0 + \frac{w_0 x}{L}$$

**Step 3:** First integration (Shear):
$$EI \frac{d^3 y}{dx^3} = -w_0 x + \frac{w_0 x^2}{2L} + C_1$$

**Step 4:** Second integration (Moment):
$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -\frac{w_0 x^2}{2} + \frac{w_0 x^3}{6L} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5:** Third integration (Slope):
$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{w_0 x^3}{6} + \frac{w_0 x^4}{24L} + \frac{C_1 x^2}{2} + C_2 x + C_3$$

**Step 6:** Fourth integration (Deflection):
$$EI y = -\frac{w_0 x^4}{24} + \frac{w_0 x^5}{120L} + \frac{C_1 x^3}{6} + \frac{C_2 x^2}{2} + C_3 x + C_4$$

**Step 7:** Apply boundary conditions:

**BC 1:** $M(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$

**BC 2:** $V(L) = 0$:
$$0 = -w_0 L + \frac{w_0 L}{2} + C_1 = -\frac{w_0 L}{2} + C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = \frac{w_0 L}{2}$$

**BC 3:** $M(L) = 0$ (verify and check consistency):
$$0 = -\frac{w_0 L^2}{2} + \frac{w_0 L^2}{6} + \frac{w_0 L}{2} \cdot L = -\frac{w_0 L^2}{2} + \frac{w_0 L^2}{6} + \frac{w_0 L^2}{2} = \frac{w_0 L^2}{6}$$

This is inconsistent â€” the boundary conditions are over-determined for a triangular load. The actual condition to use is that **the integral of the slope from 0 to L must equal zero** (since $y(0) = y(L) = 0$):

$$\int_0^L \frac{dy}{dx} dx = y(L) - y(0) = 0$$

Multiplying by $EI$:
$$0 = \int_0^L \left[ -\frac{w_0 x^3}{6} + \frac{w_0 x^4}{24L} + \frac{w_0 L x^2}{4} + C_3 \right] dx$$

$$0 = -\frac{w_0 L^4}{24} + \frac{w_0 L^4}{120} + \frac{w_0 L^4}{12} + C_3 L$$

Converting to common denominator 120:
$$0 = -\frac{5 w_0 L^4}{120} + \frac{w_0 L^4}{120} + \frac{10 w_0 L^4}{120} + C_3 L = \frac{6 w_0 L^4}{120} + C_3 L = \frac{w_0 L^4}{20} + C_3 L$$

$$C_3 = -\frac{w_0 L^3}{20}$$

**BC 4:** $y(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_4 = 0$

**Step 8:** Substitute constants:
$$EI y(x) = -\frac{w_0 x^4}{24} + \frac{w_0 x^5}{120 L} + \frac{w_0 L x^3}{12} - \frac{w_0 L^3 x}{20}$$

$$\boxed{EI \, y(x) = -\frac{w_0 x^4}{24} + \frac{w_0 x^5}{120 L} + \frac{w_0 L x^3}{12} - \frac{w_0 L^3 x}{20}}$$

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1:** Maximum deflection occurs where $dy/dx = 0$:
$$0 = -\frac{w_0 x^3}{6} + \frac{w_0 x^4}{24 L} + \frac{w_0 L x^2}{4} - \frac{w_0 L^3}{20}$$

Divide by $w_0$ and let $u = x/L$:
$$0 = -\frac{L^3 u^3}{6} + \frac{L^3 u^4}{24} + \frac{L^3 u^2}{4} - \frac{L^3}{20}$$

$$0 = -\frac{u^3}{6} + \frac{u^4}{24} + \frac{u^2}{4} - \frac{1}{20}$$

Multiplying by 120 (LCM of 6, 24, 4, 20):
$$0 = -20 u^3 + 5 u^4 + 30 u^2 - 6$$

$$5 u^4 - 20 u^3 + 30 u^2 - 6 = 0$$

**Step 2:** Solve this quartic numerically. Using Newton's method with initial guess $u_0 = 0.5$:

Let $f(u) = 5u^4 - 20u^3 + 30u^2 - 6$
$f'(u) = 20u^3 - 60u^2 + 60u$

At $u = 0.5$: $f(0.5) = 5(0.0625) - 20(0.125) + 30(0.25) - 6 = 0.3125 - 2.5 + 7.5 - 6 = -0.6875$
$f'(0.5) = 20(0.125) - 60(0.25) + 60(0.5) = 2.5 - 15 + 30 = 17.5$
$u_1 = 0.5 - (-0.6875)/17.5 = 0.5 + 0.0393 = 0.5393$

At $u = 0.5393$: $f(0.5393) = 5(0.0846) - 20(0.1569) + 30(0.2908) - 6 = 0.423 - 3.138 + 8.724 - 6 = 0.009 \approx 0$

Therefore, the critical point is at:
$$\boxed{u \approx 0.5393 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_{max} \approx 0.5393 \times 150 = 80.9 \text{ m from the bow}}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the maximum deflection. Substitute $x = 80.9$ m into the deflection equation:

$w_0 = 60{,}000$ N/m, $L = 150$ m, $EI = 1.5 \times 10^{12}$ Nآ·mآ²

$x = 80.9$ m, $x^2 = 6545$, $x^3 = 529{,}470$, $x^4 = 4.28 \times 10^7$, $x^5 = 3.46 \times 10^9$

$EI y_{max} = -\frac{60{,}000 \times 4.28 \times 10^7}{24} + \frac{60{,}000 \times 3.46 \times 10^9}{120 \times 150} + \frac{60{,}000 \times 150 \times 529{,}470}{12} - \frac{60{,}000 \times (150)^3 \times 80.9}{20}$

Term 1: $-\frac{60{,}000 \times 4.28 \times 10^7}{24} = -\frac{2.568 \times 10^{12}}{24} = -1.07 \times 10^{11}$

Term 2: $\frac{60{,}000 \times 3.46 \times 10^9}{18{,}000} = \frac{2.076 \times 10^{14}}{18{,}000} = 1.153 \times 10^{10}$

Term 3: $\frac{60{,}000 \times 150 \times 529{,}470}{12} = \frac{4.765 \times 10^{12}}{12} = 3.971 \times 10^{11}$

Term 4: $\frac{60{,}000 \times 3.375 \times 10^6 \times 80.9}{20} = \frac{1.638 \times 10^{13}}{20} = 8.19 \times 10^{11}$

$EI y_{max} = -1.07 \times 10^{11} + 1.153 \times 10^{10} + 3.971 \times 10^{11} - 8.19 \times 10^{11}$

$EI y_{max} = (-1.07 + 0.1153 + 3.971 - 8.19) \times 10^{11} = -5.174 \times 10^{11}$ Nآ·mآ³

**Step 4:** Compute the deflection:
$$y_{max} = \frac{-5.174 \times 10^{11}}{1.5 \times 10^{12}} = -0.345 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{y_{max} \approx -345 \text{ mm at } x \approx 80.9 \text{ m from the bow}}$$

**Note:** This is an unusually large deflection because the assumed $w_0$ and $EI$ values represent an extreme scenario. In practice, the load would be smaller relative to the stiffness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the symmetry condition:** When the load is symmetric (uniform, parabolic, or central point load), you MUST use $dy/dx = 0$ at $x = L/2$. Marks are deducted for ignoring this.
> 2. **Wrong BC for asymmetric load:** For triangular loads, you cannot impose $dy/dx = 0$ at the mid-length. Instead, use the integral constraint $\int (dy/dx) dx = 0$.
> 3. **Sign convention errors:** Be consistent. If $y$ is positive upward, then a downward load gives a negative deflection. Mismatch of signs = 2â€“3 marks lost.
> 4. **Wrong location of maximum:** For triangular loads, $y_{max}$ is **NOT** at $x = L/2$. The KTU examiner specifically tests this trap.
> 5. **Units mismatch:** $E$ in N/mآ², $I$ in mâپ´, $w_0$ in N/m, $L$ in m â†’ deflection in metres. Mixing mm and m is a common error.
> 6. **Skipping the $C_2 = 0$ step:** Always show explicitly that the moment boundary condition at $x=0$ gives $C_2 = 0$. The examiner wants to see this step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist â€” Deflection Curve**

- **Definition:** The deflection curve of a ship is the elastic curve of the hull girder plotted along the length under longitudinal bending loads (weight âˆ’ buoyancy).
- **Idealization:** A ship floating freely in still water behaves as a **simply supported beam** of length $L$ with flexural rigidity $EI$.
- **Boundary Conditions (Bow and Stern):** $V = 0$ and $M = 0$ at both $x = 0$ and $x = L$.
- **Differential Relations:** $EI y'' = M$, $EI y''' = V$, $EI y^{(4)} = -w$.
- **Symmetry Trick:** For symmetric loads, $dy/dx = 0$ at $x = L/2$ â€” use this to find one of the four constants.
- **Asymmetry Trick:** For triangular/asymmetric loads, use the integral constraint $\int_0^L (dy/dx) dx = 0$ to find $C_3$.
- **Standard Cases â€” Memorize:**
  - **Uniform load:** $y_{max} = \dfrac{5 w_0 L^4}{384 \, EI}$ at $x = L/2$
  - **Central point load:** $y_{max} = \dfrac{P L^3}{48 \, EI}$ at $x = L/2$
  - **Triangular load:** $y_{max} \approx \dfrac{0.00652 \, w_0 L^4}{EI}$ at $x \approx 0.5193 L$ (NOT at mid-length)
- **Hogging vs. Sagging:**
  - **Hogging:** $M < 0$ (saggy-positive convention), deck in compression, keel in tension.
  - **Sagging:** $M > 0$, deck in tension, keel in compression.
- **Material Constant:** $E \approx 2.1 \times 10^{11}$ N/mآ² (210 GPa) for shipbuilding steel.
- **Block Coefficient Connection:** Uniform load â†’ $C_B = 1$; Parabolic â†’ $C_B = 2/3$; Triangular â†’ $C_B = 1/2$.
- **Physical Check:** The deflection of a 200 m ship in still water is typically **a few cm to tens of cm** â€” not metres. If your answer is in metres for a real ship, check your units.
- **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** "Derive the deflection curve for a ship under [load type] and find the maximum deflection." â€” Practice all three cases.
- **Common Examiner Trap:** Asking for the location of $y_{max}$ in an asymmetric load case to test whether you blindly substitute $x = L/2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Thermal Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect of Thermal Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 â€” Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Load Definition (KTU Board Examiner Standard):**
> A **thermal load** is an internal mechanical action induced in a structural member when a change in temperature ($\Delta T$) produces a strain that is either fully constrained, partially constrained, or accompanied by a thermal gradient through the cross-section. It is not an externally applied force, but an **internally generated self-equilibrating stress** resulting from the tendency of every material to undergo **free thermal expansion** governed by its **Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion ($\alpha$)**.

In the context of **Ship Structures II**, thermal loads arise in:

1. **Engine room structures** (exposed to high heat from main/aux. engines, exhaust uptakes, boiler casings).
2. **Cargo tank regions of oil/chemical tankers** carrying heated cargo or undergoing hot tank cleaning ($60^\circ\text{C} - 80^\circ\text{C}$).
3. **LNG/LPG carrier secondary barriers** exposed to cryogenic temperatures ($-163^\circ\text{C}$).
4. **Hull plating** exposed to sun-induced temperature differentials between internal and external surfaces.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy â€” The "Railway Track" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine a steel railway track laid on a hot summer day without expansion gaps. As the sun heats the rail, the steel *wants* to grow longer (this is called **free thermal expansion**). Because the rails are bolted to sleepers and constrained at the ends, they cannot actually grow. This internal "frustration" generates a massive compressive force along the rail â€” sometimes so large that the track buckles sideways (the famous "sun kink"). This compressive force, generated purely from temperature, is exactly what naval architects call a **thermal stress**.

In a ship:
- The "rail" = a stiffened panel, bulkhead, or shell plate.
- The "sun" = hot cargo, engine heat, or sun radiation.
- The "sleepers" = surrounding stiff structure that prevents free expansion.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Shipbuilding Material Property:**
> For **mild steel (shipbuilding grade)**:
> - **Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion:** $\alpha = \mathbf{12 \times 10^{-6}} \text{ per } ^\circ\text{C}$
> - **Young's Modulus:** $E = \mathbf{2.1 \times 10^{5}} \text{ MPa}$
> - **Poisson's ratio:** $\nu = \mathbf{0.30}$

> [!NOTE]
> The value $E \cdot \alpha$ (the **thermal stress modulus**) for shipbuilding steel is approximately $E\alpha \approx \mathbf{2.5 \text{ MPa}/^\circ\text{C}}$, meaning every **$1^\circ\text{C}$** of restrained temperature change produces **2.5 MPa** of stress. This is a board-exam favorite number to remember.

## 1.4 Classification of Thermal Effects in Ships

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Canonical Cases (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> 1. **Free Expansion** â€” No restraint, only strain, zero stress.
> 2. **Fully Restrained Expansion** â€” No strain allowed, only stress, $\sigma = E\alpha\Delta T$.
> 3. **Partially Restrained / Thermal Gradient** â€” Both strain AND stress develop, often producing **thermal bowing** (bending).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 â€” Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Theoretical Framework

### Step 1 â€” Free Thermal Strain
When a uniform bar of length $L$ experiences a temperature rise $\Delta T$ with **no mechanical restraint**, it simply elongates by:

$$\delta_T = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$

The corresponding **free thermal strain** is purely geometric:

$$\varepsilon_T = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Why:** The atomic lattice vibrates more vigorously with heat; the mean inter-atomic spacing increases proportionally to $\alpha$. No internal force is generated because atoms can rearrange freely.

### Step 2 â€” Mechanical Strain from Restraint
If the bar is **fully prevented** from expanding by rigid walls, a **compressive reaction force $P$** must push the bar back to its original length. By superposition, the **mechanical strain** required to undo the free expansion is:

$$\varepsilon_M = -\alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

The **mechanical (Hookean) stress** is therefore:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon_M = -E \alpha \Delta T$$

**How:** The negative sign indicates compression for a temperature rise (the bar pushes against the wall) and tension for a temperature drop (the bar pulls on the wall while trying to contract).

### Step 3 â€” Composite / Bi-Metallic Bars (Bimetallic Strip Analogy)
When two materials with different $\alpha$ values are rigidly bonded (e.g., a stainless steel liner welded to a mild steel bulkhead, or a copper-nickel pipe on a steel tank), a temperature change forces a **common strain**, generating **unequal stresses** in each material.

### Step 4 â€” Thermal Gradient Across Thickness (Thermal Bowing)
If the temperature varies linearly through plate thickness $t$ from $T_1$ (one face) to $T_2$ (other face), the plate bends into a circular arc. This is the same mechanism behind a **bimetallic thermostat**.

**Curvature induced:**

$$\kappa = \frac{\alpha (\Delta T)}{t}$$

where $\Delta T = T_1 - T_2$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas in this table are board-exam essentials. Memorize the symbol, units, and physical meaning.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application in Ships |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\delta_T = \alpha L \Delta T$ | Free elongation | mm, m | Shell plate expansion length |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_T = \alpha \Delta T$ | Free thermal strain | dimensionless | Strain gauge correction |
| 3 | $\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T$ | Fully restrained thermal stress | MPa, N/mmآ² | Bulkhead between hot/cold tanks |
| 4 | $\sigma = E(\varepsilon_{total} - \alpha \Delta T)$ | General thermal-mechanical stress | MPa | Combined loading |
| 5 | $\sigma_1 \cdot A_1 = \sigma_2 \cdot A_2$ | Force equilibrium in composite bars | N | Bi-metallic connections |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2$ | Compatibility in bonded bars | â€” | Liner-shell compatibility |
| 7 | $\sigma_T = \dfrac{P}{A} - E\alpha\Delta T$ | Thermal + axial mechanical load | MPa | Tie rods in hot tanks |
| 8 | $\kappa = \dfrac{\alpha \Delta T}{t}$ | Thermal curvature of a plate | 1/m | Bimetallic strip / sun-side plating |
| 9 | $M_T = \dfrac{E I \alpha \Delta T}{t}$ | Thermal bending moment | Nآ·m | Gradient-induced panel bending |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E\alpha}$ | Temperature rise to yield | آ°C | Limit-state design check |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Pipe Conflict Resolution:** In formulas above, the modulus expression $E \cdot \alpha$ is written as the product $E\alpha$ (no pipe character). Always avoid the literal `|` symbol inside a table cell â€” use the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` if a divisor is unavoidable.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Naval Architecture

- **Engine Room Casings:** Exhaust uptakes operate at $300^\circ\text{C} - 500^\circ\text{C}$. The thermal expansion of the uptake shell relative to the cooler surrounding deckhouse generates shear at welded connections â€” designers must use **expansion bellows** or **slip joints**.
- **LNG Carrier Membrane Tanks:** Inner membrane at $-163^\circ\text{C}$, outer hull at ambient $+20^\circ\text{C}$ â€” a $183^\circ\text{C}$ differential produces enormous restrained thermal stress if continuity is not broken by insulation.
- **Hot Oil Cargo:** During loading, tank plating can rise $40^\circ\text{C}$ - $50^\circ\text{C}$ above the un-loaded state; longitudinal expansion of the double bottom must be absorbed by **sliding saddles** or **flexible supports**.
- **Sun-Absorbed Plating:** Black-painted topside plating can reach $60^\circ\text{C}$ on a tropical day while the shaded inner side is $30^\circ\text{C}$, inducing **out-of-plane thermal bowing** of plate panels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 â€” Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 â€” Fully Restrained Thermal Stress in a Bar Between Rigid Walls

**Given:**
- Bar of mild steel, length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$
- Coefficient of thermal expansion: $\alpha$
- Young's Modulus: $E$
- Uniform temperature rise: $\Delta T$
- Bar clamped between two immovable rigid walls

**Find:** Internal stress $\sigma$ and reaction force $P$.

**Step 1 â€” Free thermal elongation (no restraint):**

$$\delta_T = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 â€” Recognize the restraint condition.** The walls enforce:

$$\delta_{actual} = 0$$

**Step 3 â€” Decompose using superposition (thermal + mechanical):**

$$\delta_{actual} = \delta_{thermal} + \delta_{mechanical} = 0$$

**Step 4 â€” Express mechanical deformation in terms of unknown reaction $P$:**

$$\delta_{mechanical} = -\frac{P L}{A E}$$

(Negative sign because the wall pushes back, compressing the bar.)

**Step 5 â€” Substitute into the superposition equation:**

$$\alpha L \Delta T - \frac{P L}{A E} = 0$$

**Step 6 â€” Solve algebraically for $P$:**

$$\frac{P L}{A E} = \alpha L \Delta T \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{P}{A E} = \alpha \Delta T$$

**Step 7 â€” Multiply both sides by $E$ to obtain stress:**

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = E \alpha \Delta T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (boxed for memory):**
>
> $$\boxed{\sigma_{thermal} = E \alpha \Delta T}$$
>
> This is the **single most important equation** in the entire thermal-load module. It appears in nearly every KTU board question on this topic.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 â€” Composite Bar (Two Materials Bonded Together)

**Given:**
- Material 1 (e.g., stainless steel liner): $A_1$, $E_1$, $\alpha_1$
- Material 2 (e.g., mild steel backing): $A_2$, $E_2$, $\alpha_2$
- Common temperature rise: $\Delta T$
- Rigid supports at both ends, bonded at the interface

**Find:** Stress in each material and the interface force.

**Step 1 â€” Force equilibrium.** The bar is externally unloaded, so the internal forces must balance:

$$P_1 + P_2 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P_1 = -P_2$$

**Step 2 â€” Total elongation is zero (rigid walls):**

$$\delta_1 + \delta_2 = 0$$

**Step 3 â€” Express each elongation as (thermal + mechanical):**

$$\delta_1 = \alpha_1 L \Delta T + \frac{P_1 L}{A_1 E_1}$$

$$\delta_2 = \alpha_2 L \Delta T + \frac{P_2 L}{A_2 E_2}$$

**Step 4 â€” Substitute equilibrium $P_2 = -P_1$:**

$$\alpha_1 L \Delta T + \frac{P_1 L}{A_1 E_1} + \alpha_2 L \Delta T - \frac{P_1 L}{A_2 E_2} = 0$$

**Step 5 â€” Divide by $L$ and group the $P_1$ terms:**

$$P_1 \left[ \frac{1}{A_1 E_1} - \frac{1}{A_2 E_2} \right] = -(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) \Delta T$$

**Step 6 â€” Solve for $P_1$:**

$$P_1 = \frac{-(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{A_1 E_1} - \dfrac{1}{A_2 E_2}}$$

**Step 7 â€” Stresses:**

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P_1}{A_1}, \qquad \sigma_2 = -\frac{P_1}{A_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The material with the **higher** $\alpha$ (typically the stainless liner with $\alpha_{ss} \approx 17 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ\text{C}$) wants to expand more than the mild steel ($\alpha_{ms} \approx 12 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ\text{C}$). Since it cannot, it ends up in **compression**, while the mild steel ends up in **tension**. The interface carries a shear flow equal to $P_1/L$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 â€” Thermal Curvature of a Plate with Through-Thickness Gradient

**Given:**
- Plate thickness $t$, faces at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$
- Linear gradient, $\alpha$ constant

**Find:** Induced curvature $\kappa$ and bending moment $M$.

**Step 1 â€” Define the neutral surface** as the mid-plane of the plate (assumed to remain unstrained in pure thermal bending).

**Step 2 â€” At a distance $z$ from the mid-plane ($-t/2 \le z \le t/2$), the local temperature is:**

$$T(z) = T_{mid} + \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{t} \cdot z$$

**Step 3 â€” Free thermal strain at level $z$:**

$$\varepsilon_T(z) = \alpha \left[ T_{mid} + \frac{\Delta T}{t} z \right]$$

**Step 4 â€” Because the plate bends as a unit, the actual strain is:**

$$\varepsilon(z) = \varepsilon_0 + \kappa z$$

**Step 5 â€” Equate thermal to mechanical strain (no external load, only stress-free axial force assumed):**

$$\varepsilon_0 + \kappa z = \alpha T_{mid} + \frac{\alpha \Delta T}{t} z$$

**Step 6 â€” Match coefficients of $z$ (the linear part) to obtain curvature:**

$$\boxed{\kappa = \frac{\alpha \Delta T}{t}}$$

**Step 7 â€” Internal bending moment** (force أ— moment arm, integrated):

$$M = \int_{-t/2}^{t/2} E \, \varepsilon_{mech}(z) \cdot z \, b \, dz$$

For a strip of width $b$ and moment of inertia $I = bt^3/12$:

$$\boxed{M_T = \frac{E I \alpha \Delta T}{t}}$$

This is the **thermal bending moment** that must be resisted by the panel's flexural rigidity.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix â€” Comparison of Three Thermal Cases

| Case | Restraint | Strain Produced | Stress Produced | Real Ship Example |
|------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Free Expansion** | None | $\varepsilon = \alpha \Delta T$ | $\sigma = 0$ | Freely sliding tank dome |
| **Fully Restrained** | Total ($\delta = 0$) | $\varepsilon = 0$ | $\sigma = E \alpha \Delta T$ | Bulkhead welded between two fixed tanks |
| **Thermal Gradient** | Bending only | $\varepsilon(z) = \kappa z$ | $\sigma(z) = E \kappa z$ | Sun-heated topside plating |
| **Composite Bar** | Bonded but free axially at ends | Common $\varepsilon$ | Unequal $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ | Stainless-lined cofferdam bulkhead |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 â€” Structural Diagrams & Schematics

flowchart TD

    C -- "Composite Bar" --> F["BIMETALLIC STRIP\nدƒâ‚پ and دƒâ‚‚ unequal\nInterface Shear"]

# SECTION 5 â€” KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A â€” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`**

### **Q1. Define thermal stress. Derive an expression for the thermal stress developed in a bar fully restrained against expansion.**
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Thermal stress is the internal stress induced in a structural member when a temperature change causes a tendency to expand or contract, but the member is prevented from doing so by external restraints.

**Expression (2 Marks):**
For a bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha$, and Young's modulus $E$, subjected to a uniform temperature rise $\Delta T$:

- Free thermal elongation: $\delta_T = \alpha L \Delta T$
- For full restraint, total deformation = 0, so mechanical deformation must cancel thermal deformation.
- The compressive reaction force $P$ satisfies: $\dfrac{PL}{AE} = \alpha L \Delta T$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{th} = \frac{P}{A} = E \alpha \Delta T}$$

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`**

### **Q2. Explain the phenomenon of thermal bowing in ship plating. Give the expression for thermal curvature.**
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

When one face of a plate is at a different temperature from the other (a through-thickness gradient), the hotter face expands more than the cooler face. Because the faces are bonded together through the plate thickness, this differential expansion forces the plate to bend into a cylindrical arc. This is **thermal bowing**.

For a plate of thickness $t$ with face temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$\boxed{\kappa = \frac{\alpha (T_1 - T_2)}{t}}$$

In ships, this commonly affects topside plating on hot sunny days and the boundary between heated cargo tanks and the surrounding structure.

---

## 5.2 Part B â€” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **MODULE INTERNAL CHOICE â€” ANSWER EITHER Q(A) OR Q(B)**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`**

A mild steel bulkhead of length $L = 4 \text{ m}$, cross-sectional area $A = 2400 \text{ mm}^2$, is rigidly clamped between two immovable supports. The temperature of the bulkhead rises uniformly by $\Delta T = 60^\circ\text{C}$.

**Given:**
- Coefficient of thermal expansion, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per } ^\circ\text{C}$
- Young's modulus, $E = 2.1 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}$
- Yield stress of mild steel, $\sigma_y = 250 \text{ MPa}$

**(a) Determine the thermal stress and the reaction force developed in the bulkhead. Also check whether the bulkhead yields.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b) If a circular hole of diameter 25 mm is drilled through the bulkhead to relieve stress, by what percentage is the reaction force reduced? Comment on whether drilling a hole is a sound design solution.** **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Q(A)(a)**

**Step 1 â€” Identify the case: Fully restrained thermal loading.**

$$\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$$

**Step 2 â€” Substitute numerical values:**

$$\sigma_{th} = (2.1 \times 10^5) \times (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 60$$

$$\sigma_{th} = 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 7.2 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{th} = 151.2 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3 â€” Reaction force:**

$$P = \sigma_{th} \times A = 151.2 \times 2400 = 362{,}880 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{P = 362.88 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4 â€” Yield check:**

$$\sigma_{th} = 151.2 \text{ MPa} \;<\; \sigma_y = 250 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

The bulkhead does **not** yield, with a safety margin of $\frac{250 - 151.2}{250} \times 100 = 39.5\%$.

---

#### **Solution to Q(A)(b)**

**Step 1 â€” Reduced area after drilling:**

$$A_{new} = A - \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = 2400 - \frac{\pi (25)^2}{4}$$

$$A_{new} = 2400 - 490.87 = 1909.13 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 â€” Percentage reduction in area:**

$$\%\text{ reduction} = \frac{490.87}{2400} \times 100 = 20.45\%$$

**Step 3 â€” New thermal stress (still fully restrained):**

$$\sigma_{th,\,new} = 151.2 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(unchanged â€” stress depends only on } E, \alpha, \Delta T\text{)}$$

**Step 4 â€” New reaction force:**

$$P_{new} = 151.2 \times 1909.13 = 288{,}660 \text{ N} = 288.66 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 â€” Percentage reduction in force:**

$$\%\text{ reduction in P} = \frac{362.88 - 288.66}{362.88} \times 100 = 20.45\%$$

This matches the percentage reduction in area, as expected.

**Comment (Design Criticism):** Drilling a hole is **not** a sound engineering solution. Although it reduces the reaction force on the supports, it:
1. Concentrates stress around the hole (stress concentration factor $K_t \approx 3$ for a small circular hole), causing local yielding and fatigue cracking.
2. Reduces the cross-section available to carry other loads (hydrostatic, cargo, sea pressure).
3. The thermal stress in the remaining material is unchanged.

A proper solution is to introduce an **expansion joint**, **slip connection**, or **corrugated section** that allows controlled movement.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**
> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`**

A ship's deck plating of thickness $t = 20 \text{ mm}$ is exposed to direct sunlight, causing the upper face to reach $T_1 = 55^\circ\text{C}$ while the lower (shaded) face remains at $T_2 = 28^\circ\text{C}$. Take $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ\text{C}$ and $E = 2.1 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}$.

**(a) Calculate the induced thermal curvature $\kappa$ and the radius of curvature $R$ of the deformed plate. Comment on the magnitude.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b) For a 1-metre-wide strip of this plate, compute the thermal bending moment per unit width and the maximum bending stress at the outer fibre.** **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Q(B)(a)**

**Step 1 â€” Temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = T_1 - T_2 = 55 - 28 = 27^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2 â€” Curvature formula:**

$$\kappa = \frac{\alpha \Delta T}{t} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-6} \times 27}{20}$$

$$\kappa = \frac{3.24 \times 10^{-4}}{20} = 1.62 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 â€” Radius of curvature:**

$$R = \frac{1}{\kappa} = \frac{1}{1.62 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 61{,}728 \text{ m} \approx 61.7 \text{ km}}$$

**Step 4 â€” Comment:**

The radius of curvature is extremely large â€” the plate bows very gently, but over a large ship-length panel the accumulated out-of-plane deflection can be of the order of **several millimetres to centimetres**, which is enough to cause visible hull-girder distortion and contribute to fatigue in welded longitudinals.

---

#### **Solution to Q(B)(b)**

**Step 1 â€” Section modulus per metre width:**

For a plate strip of width $b = 1000 \text{ mm}$ and thickness $t = 20 \text{ mm}$:

$$I = \frac{b t^3}{12} = \frac{1000 \times (20)^3}{12} = \frac{1000 \times 8000}{12} = 666{,}667 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$Z = \frac{I}{t/2} = \frac{666{,}667}{10} = 66{,}667 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 2 â€” Thermal bending moment per unit width:**

$$m_T = \frac{E \alpha \Delta T \cdot t}{6} \quad \text{(per unit width, simplified)}$$

$$m_T = \frac{2.1 \times 10^5 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 27 \times 20}{6}$$

$$m_T = \frac{2.1 \times 10^5 \times 6.48 \times 10^{-3}}{6} = \frac{1360.8}{6}$$

$$m_T \approx 226.8 \text{ Nآ·m/m}$$

**Step 3 â€” Alternative verification using full-strip formula:**

$$M_T = \frac{E I \alpha \Delta T}{t} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^5 \times 666{,}667 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 27}{20}$$

$$M_T = 2.268 \times 10^5 \text{ Nآ·mm} = 226.8 \text{ Nآ·m} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 â€” Maximum bending stress at outer fibre:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_T}{Z} = \frac{2.268 \times 10^5}{66{,}667}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} \approx 3.4 \text{ MPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This stress is small compared to yield ($\sigma_y = 250 \text{ MPa}$), so a single thermal gradient event is not immediately critical. However, **cyclic daily gradients** (dayâ€“night, tropicalâ€“polar crossings) induce **low-cycle thermal fatigue** in welded details â€” a major design concern in service-life assessment.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Board Examiner Pitfalls):**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in $\sigma = E\alpha\Delta T$:** Students often forget whether the stress is tensile or compressive. For a **temperature rise**, the bar wants to expand but is held back, so it ends up in **compression** (negative by convention). For a **temperature drop**, it ends up in **tension**.
>
> 2. **Confusing "strain" with "stress":** Free thermal expansion gives **strain only**, not stress. Many students write $\sigma = \alpha \Delta T$ â€” this is wrong. Stress requires **restraint**.
>
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** $\alpha$ must be in $^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ or $\text{K}^{-1}$, NOT $^\circ\text{F}^{-1}$. Mixing SI and imperial loses full marks.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Compatibility in Composite Bars:** When two materials are bonded, students often write only force equilibrium and forget the **compatibility condition** $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2$. Both equations are required.
>
> 5. **Treating Gradient as a Uniform Temperature Rise:** A through-thickness gradient produces **bending**, not uniform axial stress. Do not apply $\sigma = E\alpha\Delta T$ â€” use the curvature formula instead.
>
> 6. **Arithmetic in $E\alpha$ Product:** Many lose 1â€“2 marks by writing $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ and $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ but forgetting to multiply â€” yielding a wrong answer by orders of magnitude.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Bullet Density):**

- **Master Equation (must memorize):** $\sigma_{thermal} = E\alpha\Delta T$ â€” applies only when the member is **fully restrained**.
- **Free Expansion Equation:** $\delta_T = \alpha L \Delta T$ â€” applies when the member is **unrestrained**.
- **General Form (most general):** $\sigma = E(\varepsilon_{total} - \alpha \Delta T)$ â€” applies in **all** cases; combine with equilibrium and compatibility for partially restrained problems.
- **Composite / Bimetallic Bars:** Two equations â€” **force equilibrium** ($P_1 = -P_2$) and **strain compatibility** ($\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2$). Solve simultaneously.
- **Through-Thickness Gradient:** Produces **bending**, not axial stress. Use $\kappa = \alpha\Delta T/t$ and $M_T = EI\alpha\Delta T/t$.
- **Material Constants for Mild Steel (shipbuilding standard):**
  - $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ\text{C}$
  - $E = 2.1 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}$
  - $E\alpha \approx 2.5 \text{ MPa}/^\circ\text{C}$ (memorize this product)
- **Limit Temperature Rise to Yield:** $\Delta T_{crit} = \sigma_y / (E\alpha) = 250 / 2.5 = 100^\circ\text{C}$ for mild steel. Beyond this, fully restrained steel yields.
- **Ship Applications to Remember:**
  1. Engine room uptakes â€” use expansion bellows.
  2. Hot oil cargo tanks â€” sliding supports.
  3. LNG containment â€” insulation and secondary barrier.
  4. Sun-exposed topside plating â€” thermal bowing of panels.
- **Board Exam Triggers (KTU 2024 Pattern):** Any combination of "fully restrained bar," "composite bar," "thermal gradient," or "verify yielding" is a likely 14-mark question stem.
- **Sign Convention Reminder:** Temperature rise + full restraint â†’ **compressive** stress; temperature drop + full restraint â†’ **tensile** stress.
- **Avoid the Pipe-Character Trap:** Always write $E\alpha$ or $E \cdot \alpha$, never $E|\alpha$ or anything with `|` that breaks table formatting.
- **Three Canonical Cases â€” Free, Restrained, Composite, Gradient:** Identify which case applies **before** writing any formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loads Imposed by Regular Waves and Oblique Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Loads Imposed by Regular Waves and Oblique Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 â€” Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are "Loads Imposed by Regular Waves"?

A **regular wave** is a long-crested, monochromatic (single-frequency), sinusoidal water wave whose amplitude, wavelength, period, and direction remain constant in time and space. The "loads" that such a wave imposes on a floating ship are the **time-varying hydrodynamic forces and moments** that cause the hull to oscillate in **heave (vertical translation), pitch (rotation about transverse axis), roll (rotation about longitudinal axis), sway (lateral translation), yaw (rotation about vertical axis), and surge (longitudinal translation)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The hydrodynamic loads on a ship advancing in regular waves are the superposition of three physically distinct components:
> 1. **Froudeâ€“Krylov Force** â€” pressure of the undisturbed incident wave field acting on the instantaneous wetted surface of the hull.
> 2. **Diffraction Force** â€” pressure of the wave field scattered (diffracted) by the presence of the hull.
> 3. **Radiation Force** â€” pressure of the waves generated by the oscillating hull itself (in-phase component = **added mass**; out-of-phase component = **damping**).

Mathematically:
$$F_{total}(t) = F_{FK}(t) + F_{D}(t) + F_{R}(t)$$

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy â€” "The Three Pushes"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy â€” Swimming in a Wave Pool**
> Imagine you stand still in a wave pool (a stationary observer). You feel three distinct pushes:
> 1. **The wave itself pushing you** (this is the *Froudeâ€“Krylov force* â€” the wave's pressure field pressing on your body).
> 2. **The wave bending around you** â€” water has to go around your body, and the way it bends creates an extra pressure imbalance (*diffraction force*).
> 3. **The splash-back from your own body moving** â€” if you bob up and down, you push water aside, and that water pushes back on you. The part that depends on how fast you accelerate is *added mass*, and the part that depends on how fast you move is *damping*.
>
> A ship in waves feels exactly these three effects â€” that is the foundation of **potential-flow wave-load theory**.

## 1.2 What Are "Oblique Waves"?

**Oblique waves** are regular waves whose direction of propagation makes an angle $\beta \neq 0آ°$ or $90آ°$ or $180آ°$ with the ship's heading. Conventionally:

| Symbol | Sea State | Angle $\beta$ (wave-to-heading) |
|---|---|---|
| Head sea | Wave coming directly against bow | $\beta = 180آ°$ |
| Following sea | Wave chasing the stern | $\beta = 0آ°$ |
| Beam sea | Wave hitting broadside | $\beta = 90آ°$ |
| Oblique sea | Anything in between | $0آ° < \beta < 180آ°$, $\beta \neq 90آ°$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **oblique seas**, the wave crest reaches the **port and starboard sides of the hull at different times**, producing a *phase-lag* between pressures on opposite sides. This phase lag is the origin of **roll excitation**, **lateral drift forces**, and the famous **Smith correction**.

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy â€” "The Tilting Bus"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy â€” A Bus in Cross-Wind / Cross-Rain**
> If rain falls straight down (head sea equivalent), the bus windshield gets equal drops everywhere. If rain comes at an angle (oblique), the front-left window gets hit *before* the front-right window. The bus driver feels a **sideways push** and a **twisting moment** â€” this is exactly how an oblique wave produces roll and sway forces on a ship.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following appear in *every* KTU 2024 numerical on this topic â€” they must be memorized:

- Acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$**
- Density of seawater: **$\rho = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$**
- Density of fresh water: **$\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$**
- Wave numberâ€“frequency relation (deep water): **$k = \omega^2 / g$**
- Encounter frequency parameter: **$\mu = \omega V / g$** (dimensionless)

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Ship Structural Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 2 explicitly links **wave loads** to **structural scantlings** (plate thickness, stiffener spacing, frame sizes). The loads computed here directly enter the equations for:
> - **Bending moments** (still-water + wave) â†’ used in **Section Modulus** calculation.
> - **Shear forces** â†’ used for **web plate** design.
> - **Local pressures** (e.g., **slamming**, **green water**) â†’ used for local panel scantlings.
>
> Therefore, mastering wave-load theory is the *prerequisite* for every Module 3 (Longitudinal Strength) problem.

# SECTION 2 â€” Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Wave-Induced Hydrodynamic Forces

A ship advancing at speed $V$ in regular waves of amplitude $\zeta_a$, frequency $\omega$, wave number $k$, and heading angle $\beta$ experiences **six degrees of motion**. In the **frequency domain** (linear theory), each motion $x_j$ satisfies:

$$\left[-\omega_e^2(M_{jj} + a_{jj}(\omega_e)) + i\omega_e b_{jj}(\omega_e) + c_{jj}\right] X_j(\omega_e) = F_j(\omega_e)$$

The three blocks on the left are:

| Symbol | Meaning | Physical Origin |
|---|---|---|
| $M_{jj}$ | Rigid-body mass / moment of inertia | Ship's own mass distribution |
| $a_{jj}(\omega_e)$ | **Added mass** coefficient | Water accelerated by moving hull |
| $b_{jj}(\omega_e)$ | **Damping** coefficient | Energy radiated as ship-induced waves |
| $c_{jj}$ | Hydrostatic restoring | Gravity + buoyancy (e.g., $c_{33} = \rho g A_{WP}$) |

The right-hand side $F_j(\omega_e)$ is the **exciting force/moment**.

### 2.1.1 Why Each Term Matters (the "Why")

- **Added mass $a_{jj}$**: Because the ship has to push a large mass of water when it moves, its *effective inertia* is greater than its *dry mass*. For a circular cylinder in heave, added mass equals the displaced water mass.
- **Damping $b_{jj}$**: The ship, while oscillating, continuously generates waves that carry energy away. This energy loss appears as a force opposing motion â€” *radiation damping*.
- **Restoring $c_{jj}$**: When the ship moves down (heave), it pushes more water aside, buoyancy increases, pulling it back up. In roll, the metacenter provides the righting arm. In pitch, $c_{55} = \rho g \overline{GM}_L I_{WL}$ where $I_{WL}$ is the second moment of waterplane area.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for KTU ESE Module 2 questions.** Commit every row to memory.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Special Cases |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wave number (deep water) | $k = \omega^2 / g$ | $\text{rad/m}$ | From dispersion relation $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$, simplifies in deep water |
| 2 | Wavelength | $\lambda = 2\pi / k = g T^2 / (2\pi)$ | m | Used in head-sea bending moment calculations |
| 3 | **Encounter frequency** | $\omega_e = \omega - kV\cos\beta = \omega\!\left(1 - \dfrac{\omega V}{g}\cos\beta\right)$ | rad/s | Crucial â€” ALL RAOs are plotted vs $\omega_e$ |
| 4 | Special: head sea $\beta=180آ°$ | $\omega_e = \omega + \omega^2 V / g$ | rad/s | $\cos(180آ°) = -1$ |
| 5 | Special: following sea $\beta=0آ°$ | $\omega_e = \omega - \omega^2 V / g$ | rad/s | Can be zero (zero-encounter, $\omega = g/V$) |
| 6 | Special: beam sea $\beta=90آ°$ | $\omega_e = \omega$ | rad/s | Speed does not change frequency |
| 7 | Froudeâ€“Krylov heave force (2D strip) | $dF_{FK,3}/dx = -\rho g\, b(x)\, \zeta_e(x,t)$ | N/m | Where $b(x)$ = section beam |
| 8 | Froudeâ€“Krylov pitch moment | $dM_{FK,5}/dx = x\, dF_{FK,3}/dx$ | Nآ·m/m | Integration: $x$ measured from midship |
| 9 | Hydrostatic stiffness (heave) | $c_{33} = \rho g A_{WP}$ | N/m | $A_{WP}$ = waterplane area |
| 10 | Hydrostatic stiffness (pitch) | $c_{55} = \rho g \overline{GM}_L I_{WL}$ | Nآ·m/rad | $I_{WL} = \int b^3(x)\, dx \,/\, 12$ (approx) |
| 11 | **RAO definition** | $\text{RAO}_j(\omega_e) = \vert X_j \vert / \zeta_a$ | m/m or rad/m | Dimensionless ratio of response to wave amp |
| 12 | **Smith correction factor** (heave/pitch in oblique head seas) | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\left[1 - (kL/2)^2\sin^2\beta\right]^3}}$ (for certain integrals) | â€” | Multiplies the heave or pitch force |
| 13 | Smith effective wave slope | $\alpha = (kL/2)\sin\beta$ | â€” | Critical parameter â€” appears in $J_0(\alpha)$ Bessel functions |
| 14 | Bessel-function Smith correction | $C_s = J_0(\alpha) + 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(-1)^n \dfrac{J_{2n}(\alpha)}{(2n)^2-1}$ | â€” | For roll excitation by beam seas |
| 15 | Total roll exciting moment (beam sea, Smith) | $M_4^{Smith} = \dfrac{\rho g \zeta_a k^2 L}{2}\, I_{TL}\, \sin\beta$ | Nآ·m | $I_{TL}$ = transverse moment of waterplane |

> [!WARNING]

Use $\lvert X_j \rvert$ or $\left\vert X_j \right\vert$ instead. This is enforced by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of This Framework

The formulae above are not academic â€” they are used directly by:

- **Classification Societies** (IRS, DNV, Lloyd's Register) to compute **design wave bending moments**.
- **Naval architects** during **contract design** to size midship section modulus.
- **Sea-keeping analysts** to predict crew comfort and operability limits.
- **Structural analysts** running **FEA** for fatigue loads (Modules 4 and 5 of PCNST601).

In particular, the **encounter frequency $\omega_e$** is the *master variable* â€” every RAO curve plotted by classification societies is plotted versus $\omega_e$ (or its non-dimensional cousin $\mu = \omega_e V / g$).

# SECTION 3 â€” Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 â€” Encounter Frequency $\omega_e$

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a ship moving with **speed $V$** along a straight course. The wave propagates at **phase speed $C = \omega/k$** in a direction making an angle $\beta$ with the ship's heading. We wish to find the frequency $\omega_e$ at which the ship "sees" the wave crests pass by.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric setup:** Let the ship's position at time $t$ be the origin of a moving coordinate system. The wave propagates along its own direction $\hat{e}_w$. The component of the ship's velocity along the wave direction is $V\cos\beta$.

The phase of the incident wave at a *fixed point* in space is:
$$\phi_{wave}(x_w, t) = k x_w - \omega t$$

The phase *experienced by the ship* is the phase evaluated at the ship's instantaneous position. Choosing coordinates so that the ship is at the origin at $t=0$:

$$x_w(t) = V t \cos\beta$$

Substituting into the wave phase:
$$\phi_{ship}(t) = k V t \cos\beta - \omega t = -(\omega - k V \cos\beta) t$$

The rate of change of phase experienced by the ship is, by definition, the **encounter frequency**:
$$\omega_e = -\frac{d\phi_{ship}}{dt} = \omega - k V \cos\beta$$

Using the deep-water dispersion relation $\omega^2 = gk \Rightarrow k = \omega^2 / g$:

$$\omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 V}{g}\cos\beta = \omega\!\left(1 - \frac{\omega V}{g}\cos\beta\right)$$

We can also express it as a ratio:
$$\frac{\omega_e}{\omega} = 1 - \frac{\omega V}{g}\cos\beta$$

### 3.1.3 Special Cases

| Sea State | $\beta$ | $\cos\beta$ | $\omega_e$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Following | $0آ°$ | $1$ | $\omega_e = \omega\!\left(1 - \dfrac{\omega V}{g}\right)$ |
| Beam | $90آ°$ | $0$ | $\omega_e = \omega$ |
| Head | $180آ°$ | $-1$ | $\omega_e = \omega\!\left(1 + \dfrac{\omega V}{g}\right)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical insight:** In **head seas**, the ship *runs into* wave crests, so it experiences them more frequently ($\omega_e > \omega$). In **following seas**, it may outrun them ($\omega_e < \omega$), and at the critical speed $V_c = g/\omega$, the encounter frequency becomes **zero** â€” the ship sits in the same wave trough forever, producing the dangerous **zero-encounter resonance in roll**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 â€” Froudeâ€“Krylov Heave Force on a 2D Strip

### 3.2.1 Setup

A ship section (strip) at longitudinal position $x$ has beam $b(x)$ and is in undisturbed water of depth $d$. The wave has local surface elevation:
$$\zeta_e(x,t) = \zeta_a \cos(k x \cos\beta - \omega_e t)$$

The Froudeâ€“Krylov hypothesis says: *treat the wave pressure field as if the hull were not there* â€” compute the integrated pressure on the instantaneous wetted surface of the hull.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

The **unsteady Bernoulli pressure** (linearised, deep water, regular wave) at depth $z$ below the mean waterline is:
$$p(x,z,t) = -\rho g z + \rho g \zeta_e(x,t) e^{k z}$$

At the still waterline ($z=0$):
$$p(x,0,t) = \rho g \zeta_e(x,t)$$

For a *narrow* strip at position $x$ with instantaneous heave displacement $\eta_3(x,t)$, the wetted surface of the strip changes. Integrating the pressure over the (instantaneous) wetted surface, to leading order:

$$dF_{FK,3} = -p(x, 0, t) \cdot b(x) \cdot dx = -\rho g\, b(x)\, \zeta_e(x,t)\, dx$$

Substituting the wave elevation:
$$\frac{dF_{FK,3}}{dx} = -\rho g\, b(x)\, \zeta_a \cos(k x \cos\beta - \omega_e t)$$

Total heave force (integrate from $x=-L/2$ to $x=+L/2$):
$$F_{FK,3}(t) = -\rho g \zeta_a \cos(\omega_e t) \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} b(x) \cos(k x \cos\beta)\, dx$$

If $b(x)$ is even (symmetric hull, $b(-x)=b(x)$):
$$F_{FK,3}(t) = -\rho g \zeta_a \cos(\omega_e t) \int_{0}^{L/2} 2 b(x) \cos(k x \cos\beta)\, dx$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical insight:** The Froudeâ€“Krylov force is **proportional to waterplane area** (in the long-wavelength limit, $kx \to 0$, $\cos(kx\cos\beta) \to 1$), giving $F_{FK,3} \approx -\rho g A_{WP} \zeta_a \cos(\omega_e t)$. For long waves, this equals the hydrostatic restoring force $\rho g A_{WP} \eta_3$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 â€” Smith Correction Factor for Oblique Head Seas

### 3.3.1 Setup

When a regular wave meets the hull at an oblique angle $\beta$, the wave crests strike the **port side and starboard side at different times**. Specifically, the phase difference between the wave reaching midship and the wave reaching the ends of the ship is:

$$\Delta\phi = k (L/2) \sin\beta$$

This phase lag modifies the **effective wave slope** experienced by the hull, and therefore the heaving/pitching/rolling moments. **Smith (1969)** proposed a correction factor that captures this.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

The **effective wave slope** at midship in oblique seas is $\zeta_a k \sin\beta$ (since only the wave-slope component transverse to the hull drives roll). The heaving force per unit length on a strip includes a term proportional to this slope times section beam squared:

$$\frac{dF_3^{slope}}{dx} = -\rho g b(x) \zeta_a k \sin\beta \cdot \frac{b(x)}{2}$$

The factor $b(x)/2$ represents the lever arm from the centerline to the waterline. To account for the phase lag along the hull, Smith multiplied this by a Bessel-function correction. The dominant term is:

$$C_s(\alpha) = J_0(\alpha) - 2 J_2(\alpha) + \ldots$$

where $\alpha = k(L/2)\sin\beta$ is the **Smith parameter**.

For **pitch moment**, the integrated effect becomes:
$$M_5^{Smith} = M_5^{head} \cdot \frac{J_0(\alpha)}{1 + 2\alpha^2/3}$$

A widely used simplified closed-form (cited in many KTU textbooks) for the **pitch excitation** in oblique head seas is:

$$M_5(\beta) = M_5(180آ°) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\left[1 - (kL/2)^2 \sin^2\beta\right]^3}}$$

This is the **Smith pitch correction**. Note that as $\beta \to 0آ°$ (head sea), the factor reduces to $1$. As $\beta \to 90آ°$ (beam sea), the denominator becomes large and the pitch excitation drops â€” physically correct because beam seas excite roll, not pitch.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 â€” Strip Theory Equation of Motion (Heave + Pitch Coupling)

### 3.4.1 Setup

Consider a ship in head seas ($\beta = 180آ°$). Let the section at position $x$ be a 2D floating cylinder. Its section motions are heave $\eta_3(x,t)$ and pitch about the ship center of gravity $G$. The strip theory (also called **Lewis form theory** or **2D+t theory**) assumes:

- Sections do not interact hydrodynamically (no cross-coupling between strips).
- 3D effects are recovered by integrating 2D solutions over the length.

### 3.4.2 Section Equations of Motion

For each strip at $x$, the 2D heave equation is:

$$\left(m + a_{33}(x)\right) \ddot{\eta}_3(x,t) + b_{33}(x) \dot{\eta}_3(x,t) + c_{33}(x)\, \eta_3(x,t) = f_3^{FK}(x,t)$$

where:
- $m$ = section mass = $\rho A(x) \cdot dx$ (mass of displaced water per strip).
- $a_{33}(x), b_{33}(x)$ = 2D added mass and damping per strip.
- $c_{33}(x) = \rho g b(x)$ = hydrostatic stiffness per unit length.
- $f_3^{FK}(x,t) = \rho g b(x) \zeta_e(x,t)$ = Froudeâ€“Krylov excitation.

In frequency domain, $\eta_3(x,t) = \text{Re}\{H_3(x,\omega_e) e^{i\omega_e t}\}$, and:

$$\left[-\omega_e^2 (m + a_{33}) + i\omega_e b_{33} + c_{33}\right] H_3(x) = \rho g b(x) \zeta_a e^{ikx}$$

### 3.4.3 Rigid-Body Heave and Pitch Recovery

Integrating across the ship length, the **total heave** is:
$$\eta_3(t) = \frac{1}{L}\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \eta_3(x,t)\, dx$$

The **total pitch angle** $\theta_5$ (about midship, for simplicity) is recovered from the slope:
$$\theta_5(t) \approx \frac{1}{L}\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \frac{\partial \eta_3(x,t)}{\partial x}\, dx$$

The **pitch moment** about midship:
$$M_5(t) = -\rho g \zeta_a \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} x\, b(x)\, e^{ikx}\, dx$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU result:** Strip theory assumes no cross-coupling between strips in the **vertical plane**. This is accurate for slender ships in **head seas**, but breaks down for **oblique and beam seas** because the wave diffraction couples port and starboard strips. **For oblique waves, a 3D diffraction theory (Haskind / Newman) or panel methods must be used.**

# SECTION 4 â€” Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Physical ship-hull drawings are too complex for Mermaid syntax. The following **block-level functional architecture flows** capture the *engineering logic* of wave-load computation â€” which is what the KTU examiner tests.

## 4.1 Diagram A â€” Decomposition of Hydrodynamic Force

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Regular Wave Field] --> B[Incident Wave Pressure p_inc]
    A --> C[Wave Scattering by Hull]
    B --> D[Froude-Krylov Force F_FK]
    C --> E[Diffraction Force F_D]
    F[Hull Oscillation Motion x_j] --> G[Ship-Generated Waves]
    G --> H[Radiation Force F_R]
    H --> H1[Added Mass Component a_ij]
    H --> H2[Damping Component b_ij]
    D --> I[Total Wave-Exciting Force F_total]
    E --> I
    I --> J[Equation of Motion in Frequency Domain]
    H1 --> J
    H2 --> J
    J --> K[Rigid-Body Mass plus Hydrostatic Stiffness]
    K --> L[Response Amplitude Operator RAO]
    L --> M[Midship Bending Moment M_BM]
```

## 4.2 Diagram B â€” Strip Theory (Lewis Form) Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[3D Ship Hull Geometry] --> B[Discretize into N transverse strips]
    B --> C[Each strip at position x_i]
    C --> D[2D diffraction problem per strip]
    D --> E[Compute strip added mass a_33_x_i]
    D --> F[Compute strip damping b_33_x_i]
    D --> G[Compute strip Froude-Krylov force f_FK_x_i]
    E --> H[Integrate over ship length]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[3D Rigid-Body Heave eta_3]
    H --> J[3D Rigid-Body Pitch theta_5]
    H --> K[3D Rigid-Body Roll theta_4]
    I --> L[Coupled 6-DOF Motion RAOs]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.3 Diagram C â€” Oblique Wave Encounter Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Heading Direction] --> B[Angle beta between ship heading and wave propagation]
    B --> C{Compute Encounter Frequency}
    C -->|beta equals 180 deg| D[Head Sea omega_e greater than omega]
    C -->|beta equals 90 deg| E[Beam Sea omega_e equals omega]
    C -->|beta equals 0 deg| F[Following Sea omega_e less than omega]
    C -->|0 less than beta less than 180| G[Oblique Sea Smith Correction Active]
    G --> H[Smith Parameter alpha equals k times L over 2 times sin beta]
    H --> I[Compute Smith Factor C_s of alpha]
    I --> J[Multiply Heave Force by C_s]
    I --> K[Multiply Pitch Moment by C_s]
    I --> L[Multiply Roll Moment by J_0 of alpha]
```

## 4.4 Diagram D â€” Motion Equation Frequency-Domain Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wave Elevation zeta_a] --> B[Froude-Krylov plus Diffraction Excitation F_exc of omega_e]
    B --> C[Sum Block]
    D[Inertia M plus a of omega_e] --> C
    E[Damping b of omega_e times i omega_e] --> C
    F[Stiffness c] --> C
    C --> G[Response X_j of omega_e]
    G --> H[RAO equals magnitude X_j over zeta_a]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The blocks labelled $a(\omega_e)$, $b(\omega_e)$, $c$ are *frequency-dependent hydrodynamic coefficients*. They are not constants â€” they are **computed from 2D strip diffraction solutions** (for slender ships) or **3D panel-method solutions** (for general heading). This is the heart of any ship-motion computer program (e.g., **WAMIT**, **NLOAD**, **VERES**).
