# SECTION 5 â€” KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A â€” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam â€” May 2024]`
**Define the Froudeâ€“Krylov force. Why is the Froudeâ€“Krylov hypothesis a useful engineering approximation even though it neglects wave scattering?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Froudeâ€“Krylov force is the hydrodynamic force on a ship obtained by integrating the **undisturbed incident wave pressure field** over the **instantaneous wetted surface** of the hull, *as if the hull were transparent to the waves* (3 marks distributed: 1 mark definition, 1 mark on instantaneous wetted surface concept, 1 mark on practical utility).

It is useful because (i) for **slender, sharp-ended ships** in **head seas** the scattered field is small relative to the incident field; (ii) it correctly captures the **buoyancy-restoring** physics for low-frequency long waves; and (iii) it gives a **closed-form analytical** result that can be checked against experiment. For blunt hulls or beam/oblique seas, the diffraction term must be added.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam â€” Dec 2023]`
**State the expression for the encounter frequency of a ship moving with speed $V$ in regular oblique waves. What is its significance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$\omega_e = \omega - \dfrac{\omega^2 V}{g}\cos\beta = \omega\!\left(1 - \dfrac{\omega V}{g}\cos\beta\right)$$

Significance (1 mark): The encounter frequency is the **frequency at which the ship physically meets successive wave crests**. It is the correct independent variable for plotting **RAOs**, **bending moment spectra**, and **fatigue damage rates**. For head seas ($\beta = 180آ°$), $\omega_e > \omega$; for following seas ($\beta = 0آ°$), $\omega_e < \omega$, and at the **critical speed** $V_c = g/\omega$ the encounter frequency becomes zero â€” the ship is locked into the same wave trough, producing dangerous roll resonance.

---

## Part B â€” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation**, every Module question in PCNST601 offers an **internal choice**. The two questions below are presented as alternatives. Either one may appear in the examination.

---

### Part B â€” Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam â€” Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **encounter frequency** $\omega_e$ of a ship advancing at speed $V$ in regular waves of frequency $\omega$ and angle $\beta$. Discuss the special cases of head, following, and beam seas. Mention the physical significance of the critical speed $V_c = g/\omega$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **strip theory**, derive the equation of motion for the **heave** of a ship in regular head waves. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution â€” Part A(a) â€” Encounter Frequency

**Step 1: Define geometry.**
Let the ship travel with velocity $V$ along its heading. The regular wave of frequency $\omega$ and wave number $k$ propagates in a direction making an angle $\beta$ with the ship's heading.

**Step 2: Wave phase at a fixed point.**
$$\phi = k\, x_w - \omega\, t$$
where $x_w$ is the coordinate along the wave-propagation direction.

**Step 3: Position of the ship along wave direction.**
With the ship at the origin at $t=0$:
$$x_w(t) = V t \cos\beta$$

**Step 4: Phase experienced by the ship.**
$$\phi_{ship}(t) = k V t \cos\beta - \omega t = -(\omega - k V \cos\beta)\, t$$

**Step 5: Encounter frequency.**
$$\omega_e = -\frac{d\phi_{ship}}{dt} = \omega - k V \cos\beta$$

**Step 6: Substitute deep-water dispersion** $k = \omega^2/g$:
$$\omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 V}{g}\cos\beta = \omega\!\left(1 - \frac{\omega V}{g}\cos\beta\right)$$

**Step 7: Special cases.**

| Sea State | $\beta$ | $\cos\beta$ | $\omega_e$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Head sea | $180آ°$ | $-1$ | $\omega(1 + \omega V/g)$ |
| Following sea | $0آ°$ | $1$ | $\omega(1 - \omega V/g)$ |
| Beam sea | $90آ°$ | $0$ | $\omega$ |

**Step 8: Critical speed.** In following seas, $\omega_e = 0$ when:
$$\omega - \frac{\omega^2 V_c}{g} = 0 \Rightarrow V_c = \frac{g}{\omega}$$
At this speed, the ship is *trapped* in a single wave trough â€” the wave never passes the ship. Roll resonance can persist for long durations.

> **Mark allocation:** 1 mark for setup, 2 marks for algebraic derivation, 2 marks for special cases, 2 marks for $V_c$ discussion.

---

#### Model Solution â€” Part A(b) â€” Strip Theory Heave Equation

**Step 1: Assumption statement.**
Strip theory assumes the ship can be sliced into $N$ independent transverse 2D sections, each behaving as a floating cylinder oscillating in heave (and pitch). No cross-coupling between strips.

**Step 2: Heave equilibrium for one strip at position $x$:**
$$\left[m(x) + a_{33}(x, \omega_e)\right]\ddot{\eta}_3 + b_{33}(x, \omega_e)\dot{\eta}_3 + c_{33}(x)\,\eta_3 = f_3^{FK}(x, t)$$

**Step 3: Section quantities.**
- $m(x) = \rho A(x)\, dx$ (mass of displaced water per strip).
- $a_{33}(x, \omega_e)$ = 2D added mass per strip length (from Lewis-form 2D diffraction).
- $b_{33}(x, \omega_e)$ = 2D damping per strip length.
- $c_{33}(x) = \rho g\, b(x)$ (hydrostatic stiffness).
- $f_3^{FK}(x,t) = \rho g b(x) \zeta_a \cos(kx - \omega_e t)$ (Froudeâ€“Krylov excitation in head sea).

**Step 4: Transfer to frequency domain.**
Assume $\eta_3(x,t) = \text{Re}\{H_3(x) e^{i\omega_e t}\}$, then:
$$\left[-\omega_e^2(m + a_{33}) + i\omega_e b_{33} + c_{33}\right] H_3(x) = \rho g b(x) \zeta_a e^{ikx}$$

**Step 5: Solve for strip transfer function.**
$$H_3(x) = \frac{\rho g\, b(x)\, \zeta_a\, e^{ikx}}{-\omega_e^2(m + a_{33}) + i\omega_e b_{33} + c_{33}}$$

**Step 6: Total 3D heave motion.**
$$\eta_3(t) = \frac{1}{L}\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \eta_3(x,t)\, dx = \frac{1}{L}\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} H_3(x)\, e^{i\omega_e t}\, dx$$

**Step 7: Statement of validity.** Strip theory is accurate for **slender hulls** (high length-to-beam ratio) in **head seas** where the wave crests are aligned along the ship length. It is **less accurate** for **beam and oblique seas**, where 3D diffraction effects become dominant.

> **Mark allocation:** 2 marks for assumptions, 3 marks for equation derivation, 2 marks for integration to 3D heave.

---

### Part B â€” Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam â€” Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the components of hydrodynamic forces on a ship in regular waves. Discuss the **Froudeâ€“Krylov hypothesis** and its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are **oblique waves**? With a neat sketch description, derive the **Smith correction factor** for the heave force in oblique head seas. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution â€” Part B(a) â€” Components of Hydrodynamic Forces

**Step 1: Total force decomposition.**
$$F_{total}(t) = F_{FK}(t) + F_D(t) + F_R(t)$$

**Step 2: Froudeâ€“Krylov force $F_{FK}$.**
Computed by integrating the *undisturbed* incident wave pressure $p_{inc}$ over the instantaneous wetted surface $S_w(t)$:
$$F_{FK} = -\iint_{S_w(t)} p_{inc}\, \hat{n}\, dS$$
**Limitation:** Ignores wave scattering by the hull â€” accurate only when hull is *transparent* or *slender*.

**Step 3: Diffraction force $F_D$.**
Computed by integrating the *scattered* wave pressure $p_{sc}$ (i.e., the wave field that satisfies the hull boundary condition but is *not* the incident field) over the mean wetted surface:
$$F_D = -\iint_{S} p_{sc}\, \hat{n}\, dS$$
**Role:** Important for *blunt* hulls, *beam seas*, and *short waves*.

**Step 4: Radiation force $F_R$.**
Computed by integrating the *radiated* wave pressure (waves generated by the hull's own oscillation) over the mean wetted surface:
$$F_R = -\iint_S p_{rad}\, \hat{n}\, dS$$
**Decomposition:**
- In-phase with acceleration $\Rightarrow$ **Added mass** $a_{ij}$.
- In-phase with velocity $\Rightarrow$ **Damping** $b_{ij}$.

**Step 5: Limitation of Froudeâ€“Krylov hypothesis.**
- Fails for blunt hull forms (tugs, SWATHs, semi-submersibles).
- Fails at resonance when radiation dominates.
- Fails in oblique / beam seas.
- Cannot capture viscous roll damping.

**Step 6: Practical engineering use.**
Despite limitations, FK is widely used in preliminary design because it gives **closed-form answers**, **scales with waterplane area** (a key design parameter), and is correct in the **long-wave limit** which governs most sea states.

> **Mark allocation:** 1 mark decomposition, 2 marks each on FK, diffraction, radiation, 2 marks on limitations.

---

#### Model Solution â€” Part B(b) â€” Smith Correction Factor for Oblique Head Seas

**Step 1: Define oblique wave geometry.**
A regular wave of frequency $\omega$ propagates in a direction making angle $\beta$ ($0 < \beta < 180آ°$) with the ship heading. The phase at midship is zero; the phase at a position $x$ along the ship length is $k x \sin\beta$ (resolved component of wave vector along ship).

**Step 2: Phase lag along hull.**
The wave crest reaches **port midship** and **starboard midship** simultaneously (in head seas $\beta = 180آ°$, or any oblique sea, the lateral phase difference is zero at any given $x$ â€” but the *longitudinal* phase varies along $x$). The critical phase lag between bow ($x = L/2$) and midship is:
$$\Delta\phi = k \cdot (L/2) \cdot \sin\beta$$

**Step 3: Smith parameter.**
Define the **Smith parameter**:
$$\alpha = \frac{k L}{2}\sin\beta$$

**Step 4: Effective wave-slope contribution to heave force.**
For a strip at $x$, the local wave-slope contribution to heave is:
$$\frac{dF_3^{slope}}{dx} = -\rho g\, b(x)\, \zeta_a\, (k\sin\beta)\, \frac{b(x)}{2}\, \cos(k x \sin\beta - \omega_e t)$$

**Step 5: Integrate along the hull.**
$$F_3^{slope}(t) = -\frac{\rho g\, \zeta_a\, k\sin\beta}{2}\cos(\omega_e t) \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} b^2(x)\cos(k x\sin\beta)\, dx$$

If $b(x) \approx b_0$ (uniform):
$$\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} b^2\cos(kx\sin\beta)\, dx = b_0^2 \cdot \frac{2\sin(\alpha)}{\alpha}$$

**Step 6: Form the Smith correction factor.**
The ratio of oblique-sea heave slope force to head-sea heave slope force is:
$$C_s(\alpha) = \frac{\sin(\alpha)}{\alpha}$$

The full Smith correction (accounting for higher-order terms from hull asymmetry) is:
$$\boxed{C_s(\alpha) = J_0(\alpha) - 2J_2(\alpha) + 2J_4(\alpha) - \ldots}$$

or, in closed form for parabolic sections:
$$C_s = \frac{1}{\sqrt{[1 - (kL/2)^2\sin^2\beta]^3}}$$

**Step 7: Physical interpretation.**
As $\beta \to 0$ (head sea), $\alpha \to 0$, $\sin\alpha/\alpha \to 1$, no correction. As $\beta \to 90آ°$ (beam sea), $\alpha \to kL/2$, and the correction factor reduces to a small value â€” slope-induced heave nearly vanishes in beam seas, which is correct.

> **Mark allocation:** 2 marks geometry, 2 marks for Smith parameter derivation, 3 marks for correction factor derivation and closed-form.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top reasons students lose marks on Module 2 questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\omega$ and $\omega_e$** â€” RAOs are plotted against $\omega_e$, not $\omega$. Using $\omega$ in the strip equation gives the *wrong motion amplitude*.
> 2. **Forgetting the sign of $\cos\beta$** â€” In head seas $\cos(180آ°) = -1$, *not* $+1$. This single sign error flips head-sea and following-sea answers.
> 3. **Confusing $k$ with $\omega/c$** â€” In deep water $k = \omega^2/g$, but in finite depth $k$ satisfies $\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$. KTU may test both.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2 in the Bessel Smith factor** â€” The series $C_s = J_0 - 2J_2 + 2J_4 - \ldots$ is *not* just $J_0$.
> 5. **Treating added mass as constant** â€” It is **strongly frequency-dependent**, especially near roll resonance.
> 6. **Drawing RAO vs $\omega$ instead of RAO vs $\omega_e$** â€” Automatic 1-mark penalty for axis mislabeling.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2:**

- [ ] The **three force components**: Froudeâ€“Krylov + Diffraction + Radiation.
- [ ] **Froudeâ€“Krylov force** = pressure of undisturbed wave field on instantaneous wetted surface.
- [ ] **Diffraction force** = pressure of wave field scattered by the hull.
- [ ] **Radiation force** decomposes into **added mass** (in-phase with acceleration) + **damping** (in-phase with velocity).
- [ ] **Encounter frequency** $\omega_e = \omega(1 - (\omega V/g)\cos\beta)$.
- [ ] **Head sea** $\to$ $\omega_e > \omega$; **Following sea** $\to$ $\omega_e < \omega$; **Beam sea** $\to$ $\omega_e = \omega$.
- [ ] **Critical speed** $V_c = g/\omega$ gives zero encounter frequency in following seas.
- [ ] **RAO** = magnitude of response divided by wave amplitude â€” dimensionless.
- [ ] **Strip theory** (Lewis form) = 2D+t approximation; valid for slender hulls in head seas.
- [ ] **Oblique waves** create phase lag $\Delta\phi = k(L/2)\sin\beta$ between bow and midship.
- [ ] **Smith parameter** $\alpha = (kL/2)\sin\beta$.
- [ ] **Smith correction factor** for heave in oblique head seas is $\sin\alpha/\alpha$ (simplest form) or $J_0(\alpha) - 2J_2(\alpha) + \ldots$ (full Bessel form).
- [ ] **Long-wave limit**: FK heave force $\approx \rho g A_{WP} \zeta_a$.
- [ ] **Pitch moment** $M_5 = \int x\, dF_3$ integrated over ship length.
- [ ] **Hydrostatic heave stiffness** $c_{33} = \rho g A_{WP}$.
- [ ] **Hydrostatic pitch stiffness** $c_{55} = \rho g \overline{GM}_L I_{WL}$.
- [ ] **Bending moments** from wave loads feed directly into Module 3 (Longitudinal Strength).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representation of Irregular Seaway
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Representation of Irregular Seaway

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

An **irregular seaway** (also termed a *real sea state* or *random sea*) is a mathematical representation of the actual ocean surface â€” a superposition of an infinite continuum of infinitesimal regular (sinusoidal) wave components, each with its own frequency, amplitude, phase, and direction of propagation. Because the ocean never produces a single pure sinusoidal wave, every realistic ship-structure loading problem begins with the formal statistical description of this random wave field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An irregular seaway is treated as a stationary, ergodic, Gaussian random process. The wave surface elevation $\eta(t)$ at a fixed point is the sum of N sinusoidal components:
> $$\eta(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_{i} \cos(\omega_{i} t + \varepsilon_{i})$$
> where $a_{i}$ is amplitude, $\omega_{i}$ is angular frequency, and $\varepsilon_{i}$ is a uniformly distributed random phase in $[0, 2\pi]$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy â€” The Symphony Orchestra

Imagine an orchestra playing in a concert hall:

| Concept | Orchestra Analogy |
|---|---|
| Regular wave (single sinusoid) | A single musician playing one note |
| Irregular seaway | The full orchestra â€” every instrument playing simultaneously |
| Wave amplitude $a_i$ | Loudness (volume) of each instrument |
| Wave frequency $\omega_i$ | Pitch of each note |
| Random phase $\varepsilon_i$ | The exact moment each instrument begins playing |
| **Wave Spectrum $S(\omega)$** | The **score sheet** â€” it tells you how much energy exists at every pitch |

Just as a conductor cannot predict the exact instantaneous volume (because each musician plays at slightly different timings) but can perfectly characterise the *energy distribution across frequencies* (the score), an engineer cannot predict the exact instantaneous wave height but can perfectly characterise the **wave energy spectrum** $S(\omega)$.

---

## 1.3 Why Regular Waves are Insufficient

> [!NOTE]
> In **Ship Structures II**, structural loads (bending moments, shear forces, whipping responses) depend on the *time history* of wave-induced motions. Realistic fatigue and ultimate strength assessments **must** use an irregular seaway representation.

**Limitations of Regular Wave Theory:**
1. Ocean is fundamentally stochastic, not deterministic.
2. Resonance and broad-band excitation cannot be captured by single frequency.
3. Fatigue damage requires realistic frequency content.
4. Ship-operating environment (Beaufort scale) is described statistically.
5. Statistical wave parameters (e.g., significant wave height $H_s$) are only meaningful in irregular seas.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics (Standard Nomenclature)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise these symbols â€” they appear in every KTU numerical and theory question.**

- $\mathbf{H_s}$ â€” Significant wave height (average height of highest 1/3 of waves) â€” typically equals **4 دƒ_خ·**.
- $\mathbf{T_z}$ â€” Mean zero-upcrossing period.
- $\mathbf{T_1}$ â€” Mean period between successive crests.
- $\mathbf{\omega_p}$ â€” Spectral peak frequency.
- $\mathbf{S(\omega)}$ â€” Spectral density function (units: mآ²آ·s/rad).
- $\mathbf{m_n}$ â€” nth spectral moment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation â€” Superposition Principle

The sea surface elevation $\eta(x, t)$ at a fixed point is modelled as a **stationary Gaussian random process**. By Fourier-Stieltjes representation:

$$\eta(t) = \int_{0}^{\infty} \cos(\omega t) \, dA(\omega) + \int_{0}^{\infty} \sin(\omega t) \, dB(\omega)$$

For practical (discrete) computational purposes, this becomes the finite summation already given in آ§1.1.

---

## 2.2 The Wave Energy Spectrum $S(\omega)$

The spectral density function $S(\omega)$ is defined such that the variance of the surface elevation is:

$$\sigma_{\eta}^{2} = m_{0} = \int_{0}^{\infty} S(\omega) \, d\omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of $S(\omega)$:** It is the *energy per unit water-plane area per unit angular frequency*. The area under the spectral curve over any frequency band equals the contribution of that band to total wave variance.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta(t) = \sum a_i \cos(\omega_i t + \varepsilon_i)$ | Surface elevation (irregular seaway) | m |
| 2 | $m_n = \int_{0}^{\infty} \omega^{n} S(\omega) \, d\omega$ | nth spectral moment | varies |
| 3 | $m_0 = \sigma_{\eta}^{2}$ | Zero moment = variance of $\eta$ | mآ² |
| 4 | $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0} = 4\sigma_{\eta}$ | Significant wave height | m |
| 5 | $T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0 \, / \, m_2}$ | Mean zero-upcrossing period | s |
| 6 | $T_1 = 2\pi \, m_0 \, / \, m_1$ | Mean crest-to-crest period (approx.) | s |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \sqrt{1 - m_2^{2}/(m_0 m_4)}$ | Spectral bandwidth parameter | dimensionless |
| 8 | $f(H) = (2H/\bar{H}^{2})\exp[-H^{2}/\bar{H}^{2}]$ | Rayleigh PDF of wave heights | 1/m |
| 9 | $\bar{H} = \sqrt{2\pi} \, \sigma_{\eta} \approx 0.625 H_s$ | Mean wave height | m |
| 10 | $S(\omega) = (A/\omega^{5})\exp(-B/\omega^{4})$ | Piersonâ€“Moskowitz spectrum | mآ²آ·s/rad |
| 11 | $A = 0.0081 g^{2}$ | PM coefficient (fully developed) | mآ²/sâپ´ |
| 12 | $B = 0.74(g/U_{19.5})^{4}$ | PM decay coefficient | radâپ´/sâپ´ |
| 13 | $S_{J}(\omega) = S_{PM}(\omega) \cdot \gamma^{\exp[-( \omega-\omega_p)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2}\omega_p^{2})]}$ | JONSWAP spectrum | mآ²آ·s/rad |
| 14 | $\gamma = 3.3$; $\sigma_a = 0.07$; $\sigma_b = 0.09$ | JONSWAP peak/shape constants | â€” |

---

## 2.4 Standard Wave Spectra Explained

### (a) Piersonâ€“Moskowitz (PM) Spectrum â€” Fully Developed Sea

Derived empirically from North Atlantic measurements (1964). Assumes **infinite fetch and duration**.

$$S_{PM}(\omega) = \frac{0.0081 \, g^{2}}{\omega^{5}} \exp\!\left[-1.25\left(\frac{\omega_p}{\omega}\right)^{4}\right]$$

Peak frequency: $\omega_p = 0.84 \, g / U_{19.5}$ (where $U_{19.5}$ is wind speed at 19.5 m height).

### (b) JONSWAP Spectrum â€” Fetch-Limited Sea

Developed from the **JOint North Sea Wave Project** (1973). Modifies PM by a peak-enhancement factor $\gamma$:

$$S_{J}(\omega) = S_{PM}(\omega) \cdot \gamma^{\exp\left[-\frac{(\omega - \omega_p)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}\omega_p^{2}}\right]}$$

with:
- $\sigma = \sigma_a = 0.07$ for $\omega \le \omega_p$
- $\sigma = \sigma_b = 0.09$ for $\omega > \omega_p$

### (c) ISSC / ITTC Two-Parameter Spectrum (Bretschneider)

$$S(\omega) = \frac{5}{8} \cdot \frac{H_s^{2}}{\omega_p^{5}} \, \omega^{-5} \exp\!\left[-\frac{5}{4}\left(\frac{\omega_p}{\omega}\right)^{4}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU numerical problems, the **JONSWAP spectrum** is the most frequently tested because Indian Ocean/Arabian Sea conditions are fetch-limited, not fully developed.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| Ship Structural Design | Wave-induced bending moment prediction (rule-based + spectral) |
| Offshore Platforms | Fatigue damage accumulation from spectral density |
| Naval Architecture | Time-domain simulation of ship motions in random seas |
| Mooring System Design | Slow-drift forces derived from second-order wave spectra |
| Classification Societies | DNV, ABS, BV spectral fatigue assessment procedures |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I â€” Rayleigh Distribution of Wave Heights

### Assumption (Narrow-Band Spectrum)

The wave spectrum is *narrow-banded* â€” all wave energy concentrated near $\omega_p$. Under this assumption, the surface elevation envelope follows a **Rayleigh distribution**.

### Step 1: Envelope of a Narrow-Band Gaussian Process

For a stationary Gaussian process $\eta(t)$ with zero mean and standard deviation $\sigma_\eta$, the joint PDF of envelope $R$ and phase $\phi$ is:

$$f_{R,\phi}(r, \phi) = \frac{r}{2\pi\sigma_{\eta}^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^{2}}{2\sigma_{\eta}^{2}}\right)$$

### Step 2: Marginal PDF of Envelope

Integrate over $\phi \in [0, 2\pi]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{R}(r) &= \int_{0}^{2\pi} f_{R,\phi}(r, \phi) \, d\phi \\
&= \int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{r}{2\pi\sigma_{\eta}^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^{2}}{2\sigma_{\eta}^{2}}\right) d\phi \\
&= \frac{r}{\sigma_{\eta}^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^{2}}{2\sigma_{\eta}^{2}}\right), \quad r \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Convert Envelope to Wave Height

Wave height = $H = 2R$ (crest-to-trough distance for narrow-band wave). Substitute $R = H/2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f(H) &= f_{R}(H/2) \cdot \left\vert \frac{dR}{dH} \right\vert \\
&= \frac{H/2}{\sigma_{\eta}^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^{2}}{8\sigma_{\eta}^{2}}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

Simplify by defining $\bar{H} = \sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_{\eta}$ (mean wave height â€” proven in Step 4):

$$f(H) = \frac{2H}{\bar{H}^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^{2}}{\bar{H}^{2}}\right)$$

> This is the **Rayleigh PDF** of wave heights â€” a foundational result for KTU problems.

### Step 4: Proof that $\bar{H} = \sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_\eta$

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{H} &= \int_{0}^{\infty} H \cdot f(H) \, dH \\
&= \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2H^{2}}{\bar{H}^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^{2}}{\bar{H}^{2}}\right) dH
\end{aligned}$$

Let $u = H/\bar{H}$, so $dH = \bar{H}\,du$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{H} &= 2\bar{H} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{2} e^{-u^{2}} du \\
&= 2\bar{H} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{4} \quad \text{(standard Gaussian integral)} \\
&= \bar{H} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

This equation is self-consistent. Solving for the integral yields the relation:

$$\boxed{\bar{H} = \sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_\eta \approx 2.507\,\sigma_\eta}$$

### Step 5: Derivation of Significant Wave Height

By definition, $H_s$ = mean of the highest 1/3 of waves. From the Rayleigh CDF:

$$F(H_0) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{H_0^{2}}{\bar{H}^{2}}\right) = \frac{2}{3}$$

Solving: $H_0 = \bar{H}\sqrt{\ln(3/2)}$. The mean of waves above $H_0$ is:

$$H_s = \frac{3}{\bar{H}^{2}} \int_{H_0}^{\infty} H^{2} f(H) \, dH = \bar{H} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\ln 3}}{1 - (3/2)^{-1/2}} \cdot \text{constant}$$

Evaluating numerically gives the celebrated result:

$$\boxed{H_s \approx 4.004\,\sigma_\eta \approx 4\sqrt{m_0}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation II â€” Mean Zero-Crossing Period $T_z$

### Step 1: Expected Number of Zero Upcrossings

For a stationary Gaussian process, the expected number of zero upcrossings per unit time is:

$$\nu_{0}^{+} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{m_2}{m_0}}$$

### Step 2: Define Zero-Crossing Period

$$T_z = \frac{1}{\nu_{0}^{+}}$$

### Step 3: Substitute

$$\boxed{T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}}}$$

This is one of the most commonly tested formulas â€” **memorise it**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III â€” Spectral Moments of PM Spectrum

For the Piersonâ€“Moskowitz spectrum:

$$S_{PM}(\omega) = \frac{A}{\omega^{5}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{B}{\omega^{4}}\right)$$

### Step 1: Zero Moment $m_0$

$$m_0 = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{A}{\omega^{5}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{B}{\omega^{4}}\right) d\omega$$

Substitute $u = B/\omega^{4}$, so $du = -4B/\omega^{5} \, d\omega$:

$$\begin{aligned}
m_0 &= A \cdot \frac{1}{4B} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{-1/4} e^{-u} \cdot \frac{1}{u} \, du \quad \text{(after careful substitution)} \\
&= \frac{A}{4B} \, \Gamma(1/4) \\
&\approx \frac{A}{4B} \cdot 3.6256
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Result in Terms of $H_s$

Using $A = 0.0081 g^2$ and $B = 0.74(g/U)^{4}$, and noting $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$:

$$\boxed{m_0 = \frac{H_s^{2}}{16} \implies H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}}$$

The PM spectrum can therefore be rewritten purely in terms of $H_s$ and $\omega_p$:

$$S_{PM}(\omega) = \frac{H_s^{2}}{16\,\omega_p^{4}} \cdot \frac{1}{\omega^{5}} \cdot \omega_p^{4} \cdot \exp\!\left[-1.25\left(\frac{\omega_p}{\omega}\right)^{4}\right]$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow Architecture â€” Spectral Wave Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ocean Sea State Real World] --> B[Measured Wave Record eta of t]
    B --> C[Stationarity Check]
    C --> D[FFT Spectral Estimation]
    D --> E[Raw Spectral Density S omega]
    E --> F[Spectrum Fitting]
    F --> G1[Pierson Moskowitz PM]
    F --> G2[JONSWAP J]
    F --> G3[Bretschneider ISSC]
    G1 --> H[Spectral Moments Computation]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I1[m0 equals Hs squared over 16]
    H --> I2[m1 m2 m4]
    I1 --> J[Statistical Wave Parameters]
    I2 --> J
    J --> K1[Hs Significant Wave Height]
    J --> K2[Tz Zero Crossing Period]
    J --> K3[T1 Mean Period]
    J --> K4[Epsilon Bandwidth]
    K1 --> L[Ship Structural Response RAO]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    L --> M[Bending Moment Spectrum]
    M --> N[Fatigue and ULS Assessment]
```

**Figure 1:** Block-level architecture of the spectral representation workflow. The irregular sea is measured, decomposed into a spectrum, fitted to a standard model, summarised by spectral moments, and finally used to predict ship structural responses.

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Spectrum Diagram (Schematic)

```
S(د‰)   â–²
       â”‚           â•±â•²
       â”‚          â•±  â•²
       â”‚         â•±    â•²  â†گ Peak at د‰ = د‰p
       â”‚        â•±      â•²
       â”‚       â•±        â•²
       â”‚      â•±          â•²___
       â”‚     â•±               â•²___
       â”‚    â•±                     â•²___

       â”‚ â•±                                       â•²___â†’
       â””â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â–؛ د‰
        0           د‰p                          
        
        â†گâ”€â”€ Low Freq â”€â”€â†’â†گâ”€â”€ Mid Freq â”€â”€â†’â†گâ”€â”€ High Freq â”€â”€â†’
         (Swell)           (Wind Sea)        (Capillary)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the spectrum:** Total wave energy $\propto \int S(\omega)d\omega$. Most energy is concentrated near $\omega_p$. The tail (high $\omega$) is small but important for slamming and springing.

---

## 4.3 Narrow-Band vs Broad-Band Spectrum

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Narrow Band S omega] --> B[Epsilon small near 0]
    C[Broad Band S omega] --> D[Epsilon large near 1]
    B --> E[Rayleigh distribution valid]
    D --> F[Non Rayleigh correction needed]
    E --> G[Hs equals 4 sigma eta]
    F --> H[Use Longuet Higgins correction]
```

**Figure 2:** Comparison of narrow-band and broad-band spectra. The spectral width parameter $\varepsilon$ controls whether the Rayleigh distribution is a valid approximation for wave heights.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A â€” 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam â€“ May 2024]`
**Define: (i) Significant wave height $H_s$, (ii) Mean zero-crossing period $T_z$, (iii) Wave energy spectrum $S(\omega)$.**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Significant Wave Height $H_s$:** The mean height of the highest one-third of waves observed in a sea state, approximated as $H_s \approx 4\sigma_\eta = 4\sqrt{m_0}$, where $m_0$ is the zeroth spectral moment.
>
> **(ii) Mean Zero-Crossing Period $T_z$:** The average time interval between successive upward crossings of the mean water level, given by $T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0/m_2}$.
>
> **(iii) Wave Energy Spectrum $S(\omega)$:** A function describing the distribution of wave variance (energy) across angular frequencies $\omega$, with units of mآ²آ·s/rad. The total wave variance equals $m_0 = \int_0^\infty S(\omega)\,d\omega$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam â€“ Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Piersonâ€“Moskowitz and JONSWAP spectra.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Piersonâ€“Moskowitz | JONSWAP |
|---|---|---|
| Sea Type | Fully developed (infinite fetch & duration) | Fetch-limited (developing sea) |
| Origin | North Atlantic measurements (1964) | Joint North Sea Wave Project (1973) |
| Peak Sharpness | Smooth, single peak | Sharper peak ($\gamma$ enhancement) |
| Parameters | $H_s$, wind speed $U$ | $H_s$, $\omega_p$, $\gamma$, $\sigma_a$, $\sigma_b$ |
| Applicability | Open ocean, long-duration storms | Coastal, finite fetch regions |

---

## PART B â€” 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam â€“ June 2024]` â€” Choose A or B

**(a)** Derive the Rayleigh probability distribution of wave heights for a narrow-band irregular seaway. State clearly the underlying assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sea state has the following spectral moments: $m_0 = 1.96 \text{ m}^2$, $m_1 = 1.40 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, $m_2 = 1.20 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$, $m_4 = 2.50 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^4$. Compute $H_s$, $T_z$, and the spectral bandwidth parameter $\varepsilon$. Comment on whether the narrow-band assumption holds. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A(a)

**Assumptions:**
1. Sea surface elevation $\eta(t)$ is a stationary, ergodic Gaussian random process with zero mean.
2. Wave spectrum is **narrow-banded** (energy concentrated near peak frequency).
3. Linear wave theory (Airy) applies â€” no wave-wave interaction.

**Derivation (Step-by-step):**

**Step 1:** Joint PDF of envelope $R$ and phase $\phi$:

$$f_{R,\phi}(r,\phi) = \frac{r}{2\pi\sigma_\eta^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^{2}}{2\sigma_\eta^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Marginal PDF of $R$ (integrate over $\phi$):

$$f_{R}(r) = \frac{r}{\sigma_\eta^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^{2}}{2\sigma_\eta^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $H = 2R$:

$$f(H) = \frac{H}{4\sigma_\eta^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^{2}}{8\sigma_\eta^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Mean wave height $\bar{H} = \sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_\eta$. Define $H_{rms} = \sqrt{8}\,\sigma_\eta$, so $8\sigma_\eta^{2} = H_{rms}^{2}$ and $4\sigma_\eta^{2} = \bar{H}^{2}/\pi$. After rearrangement:

$$\boxed{f(H) = \frac{2H}{\bar{H}^{2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{H^{2}}{\bar{H}^{2}}\right)}$$

**Step 5:** Significant wave height from Rayleigh CDF:

Setting $F(H_s) = 2/3$ gives the highest one-third threshold $H_0 = \bar{H}\sqrt{\ln(3/2)}$. The mean of waves above this threshold yields:

$$H_s = 4.004\,\sigma_\eta \approx 4\sqrt{m_0}$$

---

### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Given:** $m_0 = 1.96$ mآ², $m_1 = 1.40$ mآ²/s, $m_2 = 1.20$ mآ²/sآ², $m_4 = 2.50$ mآ²/sâپ´.

**Step 1: Significant Wave Height**

$$H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0} = 4\sqrt{1.96} = 4 \times 1.4 = 5.6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Mean Zero-Crossing Period**

$$T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{1.96}{1.20}} = 2\pi \times 1.278 = 8.03 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Spectral Bandwidth Parameter**

$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{1 - \frac{m_2^{2}}{m_0 \cdot m_4}} = \sqrt{1 - \frac{(1.20)^{2}}{1.96 \times 2.50}}$$

$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{1 - \frac{1.44}{4.90}} = \sqrt{1 - 0.2939} = \sqrt{0.7061} = 0.840$$

**Step 4: Comment on Narrow-Band Assumption**

For a narrow-band spectrum, $\varepsilon \to 0$. Here $\varepsilon = 0.84$, indicating a **broad-band** spectrum. The Rayleigh distribution must be **corrected** (e.g., Longuet-Higgins distribution).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam â€“ Dec 2022]` â€” Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the physical significance of the wave energy spectrum $S(\omega)$. Discuss how $H_s$ and $T_z$ are obtained from $S(\omega)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a JONSWAP spectrum, the peak enhancement factor is $\gamma = 3.3$, peak frequency $\omega_p = 0.6$ rad/s, and significant wave height $H_s = 4$ m. Compute the spectral density at $\omega = 0.6$ rad/s and at $\omega = 1.2$ rad/s. Use the approximation $S_{PM}(\omega) = (H_s^{2}/16\omega_p^{4}) \cdot \omega_p/\omega^{5} \cdot \exp[-1.25(\omega_p/\omega)^{4}]$. Take $g = 9.81$ m/sآ². **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B(a)

**Physical Significance of $S(\omega)$:**
- $S(\omega)\,d\omega$ represents the contribution to wave variance from frequency components between $\omega$ and $\omega + d\omega$.
- Plotting $S(\omega)$ versus $\omega$ shows the **energy distribution** of the irregular sea.
- Area under curve = total variance $m_0 = \sigma_\eta^{2}$.
- Peak frequency $\omega_p$ indicates the dominant wave frequency.

**Obtaining $H_s$ and $T_z$:**

$$H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0} = 4\sqrt{\int_0^\infty S(\omega)\,d\omega}$$

$$T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{\int_0^\infty S(\omega)\,d\omega}{\int_0^\infty \omega^{2} S(\omega)\,d\omega}}$$

The procedure: integrate spectrum to find $m_0$, multiply by $\omega^2$ and integrate to find $m_2$, then combine.

---

### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Given:** $H_s = 4$ m, $\omega_p = 0.6$ rad/s, $\gamma = 3.3$.

**Compute the prefactor:**

$$C = \frac{H_s^{2}}{16\,\omega_p^{4}} = \frac{(4)^{2}}{16 \times (0.6)^{4}} = \frac{16}{16 \times 0.1296} = 7.716 \text{ m}^2\text{s}^4/\text{rad}^5$$

Wait â€” recompute: $\omega_p^4 = 0.1296$, so $C = 16/(16 \times 0.1296) = 1/0.1296 = 7.716$ mآ²آ·sâپ´/radâپµ. Re-checking units: dimensionally $S(\omega)$ has mآ²آ·s/rad, and $C$ has mآ²آ·sâپ´/radâپµ â€” multiplied by $1/\omega^5$ (radâپ»âپµآ·sâپµ) yields mآ²آ·s/rad âœ“.

**Case 1: $\omega = \omega_p = 0.6$ rad/s**

The JONSWAP peak enhancement factor: $\gamma^{\exp[-(0.6 - 0.6)^2/(2\sigma^2 \cdot 0.36)]} = \gamma^{1} = 3.3$

The exponent in PM: $-1.25 \times (0.6/0.6)^4 = -1.25$

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{PM}(0.6) &= 7.716 \times \frac{1}{(0.6)^{5}} \times e^{-1.25} \\
&= 7.716 \times \frac{1}{0.07776} \times 0.2865 \\
&= 99.24 \times 0.2865 \\
&= 28.43 \text{ m}^2\text{s/rad}
\end{aligned}$$

$$S_J(0.6) = 3.3 \times 28.43 = 93.83 \text{ m}^2\text{s/rad}$$

**Case 2: $\omega = 1.2$ rad/s**

JONSWAP exponent: $\exp[-((1.2 - 0.6)^2/(2 \times (0.09)^2 \times 0.36))] = \exp[-0.36/(0.005832)] = \exp(-61.73) \approx 0$

So enhancement factor $\approx 1$ (spectrum equals PM at far frequency).

PM exponent: $-1.25 \times (0.6/1.2)^4 = -1.25 \times (0.5)^4 = -1.25 \times 0.0625 = -0.0781$

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{PM}(1.2) &= 7.716 \times \frac{1}{(1.2)^{5}} \times e^{-0.0781} \\
&= 7.716 \times \frac{1}{2.488} \times 0.9249 \\
&= 3.101 \times 0.9249 \\
&= 2.868 \text{ m}^2\text{s/rad}
\end{aligned}$$

$$S_J(1.2) \approx 2.868 \text{ m}^2\text{s/rad}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout â€” Common Mark Losses:**
> 1. **Units of $S(\omega)$:** Students write mآ² instead of mآ²آ·s/rad and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Frequency vs Angular Frequency:** Confusing $f$ (Hz) with $\omega = 2\pi f$ â€” a single mark deduction per error.
> 3. **Rayleigh Assumption Validity:** Always check $\varepsilon$ before applying Rayleigh. Failure = 1â€“2 mark loss.
> 4. **PM vs JONSWAP factor:** Forgetting $\gamma^{\exp[...]}$ factor when $\omega \approx \omega_p$ â€” major 3-mark loss.
> 5. **Showing Integration Steps:** For derivations, write substitution $u = B/\omega^4$ explicitly. Skipping = 2 marks lost.
> 6. **Standard Value $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$:** Use the exact value $4.004\sqrt{m_0}$ if precision is required; otherwise $4\sqrt{m_0}$ is accepted.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist â€” Must Memorise Before Exam:**

### Definitions
- [x] Irregular seaway = superposition of regular waves with random phases
- [x] $S(\omega)$ = wave energy spectral density
- [x] $H_s$ = significant wave height = highest 1/3 of waves â‰ˆ $4\sqrt{m_0}$

### Key Formulas (Recap)
- [x] $m_n = \int_0^\infty \omega^{n} S(\omega)\,d\omega$
- [x] $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$
- [x] $T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0/m_2}$
- [x] $\varepsilon = \sqrt{1 - m_2^2/(m_0 m_4)}$
- [x] Rayleigh PDF: $f(H) = (2H/\bar{H}^{2})\exp(-H^{2}/\bar{H}^{2})$
- [x] PM Spectrum: $S(\omega) = (A/\omega^{5})\exp(-B/\omega^{4})$
- [x] JONSWAP Spectrum: $S_{J} = S_{PM} \cdot \gamma^{\exp[\cdots]}$

### Important Numerical Values
- [x] $\gamma = 3.3$, $\sigma_a = 0.07$, $\sigma_b = 0.09$
- [x] $A = 0.0081 g^{2}$, $B = 0.74(g/U)^{4}$
- [x] $\bar{H} = 0.625 H_s$, $H_{rms} = 0.706 H_s$

### Physical Reasoning
- [x] Narrow-band $\Rightarrow \varepsilon \approx 0 \Rightarrow$ Rayleigh valid
- [x] Broad-band $\Rightarrow \varepsilon \to 1 \Rightarrow$ Longuet-Higgins correction
- [x] Higher $H_s$ shifts spectral peak to lower frequency (longer waves)
- [x] JONSWAP sharper peak than PM due to fetch-limited conditions
- [x] Real ocean waves are NEVER purely sinusoidal

### Units Vigilance
- [x] $S(\omega)$: **mآ²آ·s/rad** (not just mآ²)
- [x] $\omega$: **rad/s** (not Hz)
- [x] $H_s$, $\bar{H}$: **m**
- [x] $T_z$, $T_1$: **s**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short Term and Long Term Distribution of Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Short Term and Long Term Distribution of Loads ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Short-term distribution of loads** refers to the statistical description of still water and wave-induced loads acting on a ship over a **short, fixed duration (typically 3 hours)** during which the sea state (characterized by significant wave height $H_s$ and zero-crossing period $T_z$) remains statistically stationary. **Long-term distribution of loads** refers to the statistical description of the **extreme (maximum) loads** experienced by the ship over its **entire operational life (typically 20â€“25 years)**, accounting for the fact that the vessel will encounter a wide variety of sea states during its service. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The classification societies (IACS S26, DNV, LR) require the **long-term extreme value distribution** for the determination of the **design hull girder bending moment** used in yield (ULS) and ultimate (ALS) strength checks. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are measuring how much **rainfall** hits a single small town over different time scales: - **Short-term (3-hour weather forecast)**: "In the next 3 hours, there will be a steady drizzle averaging 5 mm, with a standard deviation of 2 mm." â†’ This is **short-term distribution** of loads (Gaussian around a mean = SWBM). - **Long-term (50-year climate record)**: "Over 50 years, this town has experienced rainfall amounts exceeding 250 mm, with a return period of 100 years." â†’ This is **long-term distribution** of extreme loads. In ship design: - **Short-term = "Today's weather"** for a specific sea state. - **Long-term = "Lifetime climate"** combining all sea states the ship will ever face. ### 1.3 Components of Ship Loads $$M_{total} = M_{sw} + M_{w}$$ where: - $M_{sw}$ = Still Water Bending Moment (SWBM) â€” quasi-static, depends on **loading condition**. - $M_{w}$ = Wave-Induced Bending Moment (WBM) â€” dynamic, depends on **wave environment**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric (IACS S26, 2024):** Design life $T_L = \mathbf{25}$ years. Service life exposure $N_{cycles} \approx \mathbf{10^8}$ wave encounters. Probability of exceedance for yield check: $Q = 10^{-8}$. For ultimate strength: $Q = 10^{-4}$. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Logic **Step 1 â€” Identify the Two Load Components** - *Static* (Still Water): deterministic, from weight and buoyancy distribution at a given draft. - *Dynamic* (Wave): stochastic, from ocean waves. **Step 2 â€” Choose the Time Window of Interest** - *Short-term window*: 3 hours (10,000 wave cycles). Sea state is stationary. - *Long-term window*: 20â€“25 years (ship's entire service life). **Step 3 â€” Apply the Appropriate Probability Model** - *Short-term*: **Gaussian (Normal) distribution** because the wave-induced bending moment is a sum of many small harmonic contributions (Central Limit Theorem). - *Long-term*: **Convolution/integration** of short-term distributions weighted by the **wave scatter diagram** probability of occurrence. **Step 4 â€” Combine to Find the Design Extreme Load** - The extreme value in $N$ cycles is governed by the **Most Probable Maximum (MPM)** formula. - The extreme value over the ship's life is governed by the **return period** concept. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | |---|---------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | Short-term PDF (Gaussian) | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\mu = M_{sw}$ [kNآ·m], $\sigma$ = std. dev. of WBM | | 2 | Total moment mean | $\mu = M_{sw} + M_{w,mean}$ | [kNآ·m] | | 3 | Spectral variance (WBM) | $\sigma^2 = \int_0^\infty S_{BM}(\omega)\,d\omega$ | $S_{BM}$ = response spectrum $[\text{kNآ·m}]^2 \cdot s$ | | 4 | Zero-crossing period | $T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0/m_2}$ | $m_n = \int \omega^n S(\omega)\,d\omega$ | | 5 | Upcrossing rate (Rice) | $\nu^+(x) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{m_2}{m_0}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | upcrossings per second of level $x$ | | 6 | Most Probable Maximum in $N$ cycles | $x_{mpm} = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N}$ | dimensionless for zero-mean process | | 7 | Long-term exceedance probability | $Q_{LT}(x) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-T_L \!\iint \nu^+(x\mid H_s,T_z) \, p(H_s,T_z)\,dH_s\,dT_z\right)$ | $p(\cdot)$ = scatter density | | 8 | Return period | $T_R(x) = \dfrac{-T_L}{\ln(1-Q_{LT}(x))} \approx \dfrac{1}{N_a \cdot Q_{LT}(x)}$ | $N_a$ = encounters/year | | 9 | IACS S26 design level (yield) | $Q = 10^{-8}$ over 25 years | design bending moment $M_{yd}$ | | 10 | IACS S26 design level (ultimate) | $Q = 10^{-4}$ over 25 years | ultimate bending moment $M_{ud}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The short-term distribution is essential for **fatigue damage** (Miner's rule) assessments. The long-term distribution is essential for **scantling design** (yield and ultimate strength) of the hull girder. ### 2.3 Why Two Different Distributions? â€” The "Why" and "How" **Why short-term Gaussian?** A ship's wave-induced bending moment in a fixed sea state is a narrow-band stochastic process well-modelled as the **sum of hundreds of wave components** (D'Alembert superposition). By the **Central Limit Theorem**, this sum is Gaussian. A zero-mean Gaussian *narrow-band* process in $N$ cycles yields a **Rayleigh-distributed envelope**, which in turn gives the MPM formula. **How long-term is built:** For each sea state $(H_s, T_z)$ in the IACS wave scatter diagram (typically 8 أ— 10 = 80 cells for North Atlantic), compute the short-term variance. Then **integrate over all sea states** weighted by their probability of occurrence to obtain the long-term distribution. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Most Probable Maximum (MPM) in a Short-Term Sea State We consider a **zero-mean, narrow-band Gaussian process** $X(t)$ with variance $\sigma^2$. We seek the most probable value of the maximum of $X$ over $N$ zero-crossing cycles. **Step 1 â€” CDF of the Maximum in N Cycles** The probability that the maximum of $N$ independent samples is less than $x$ is: $$F_{max}(x) = [F_X(x)]^N$$ where $F_X(x) = \Phi(x/\sigma)$ is the standard Gaussian CDF. **Step 2 â€” Asymptotic Expansion for Large $x/\sigma$** For large $x/\sigma$ (rare events), use the asymptotic form of the standard normal tail: $$F_X(x) \approx 1 - \phi\!\left(\frac{x}{\sigma}\right) = 1 - \frac{\sigma}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ **Step 3 â€” Take the Natural Logarithm of the Survival Function** $$1 - F_{max}(x) \approx 1 - \left[1 - \frac{\sigma}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\right]^N$$ Using the approximation $\ln(1-u) \approx -u$ for small $u$: $$-\ln(1 - F_{max}) = -\ln(1 - [1-\epsilon]^N) \approx N\epsilon = \frac{N\sigma}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ **Step 4 â€” Differentiate and Set to Zero to Find the Mode (MPM)** Let $g(x) = -\ln(1 - F_{max}(x))$. The MPM is where $dg/dx = 0$: $$\frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{N\sigma}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\right] = 0$$ This yields the condition $\left(1/x - x/\sigma^2\right) = 0$, hence: $$x^2 = \sigma^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_{mpm} = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N}$$ **Final MPM Formula:** $$\boxed{\;x_{mpm} = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N}\;}$$ This is the **most probable amplitude** of the largest peak in $N$ cycles. ### 3.2 Derivation: Long-Term Exceedance Probability In a single short-term sea state, the rate of upcrossings of level $x$ (mean zero) is given by the **Rice formula**: $$\nu^+(x) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{m_2}{m_0}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) = \frac{1}{T_z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ In a small time $dt$, the probability of exceeding $x$ at least once is $1 - \exp(-\nu^+ \, dt) \approx \nu^+\,dt$. Over the ship's life $T_L$, in a *single* sea state $(H_s, T_z)$: $$Q_1(x \mid H_s,T_z) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{T_L}{T_z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2(H_s,T_z)}\right)\right)$$ The **long-term** probability integrates over all sea states using the scatter density $p(H_s, T_z)$: $$\boxed{\;Q_{LT}(x) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-T_L \iint \frac{p(H_s,T_z)}{T_z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2(H_s,T_z)}\right) dH_s\,dT_z\right)\;}$$ **In discrete form (using IACS scatter diagram)**: $$Q_{LT}(x) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-T_L \sum_{i}\sum_{j} \frac{p_{ij}}{T_{z,j}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2_{ij}}\right)\right)$$ where $p_{ij}$ is the probability of sea state cell $(H_{s,i}, T_{z,j})$ and $\sigma^2_{ij}$ is the corresponding WBM variance. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix â€” Short-Term vs Long-Term | Property | Short-Term | Long-Term | |----------|------------|-----------| | Time scale | **3 hours** (or one sea state) | **20â€“25 years** (ship life) | | Random variable | Instantaneous bending moment $M(t)$ | Lifetime maximum $M_{max,L}$ | | Distribution type | **Gaussian** (Narrow-band) | **Weibull-like** (extreme value) | | Parameters | $\mu = M_{sw}$, $\sigma = \sigma(H_s,T_z)$ | Scale + shape from scatter diagram | | Physical input | One sea state $(H_s, T_z)$ | Full IACS scatter diagram | | Output use | Fatigue, seakeeping, local strength | Hull girder yield & ultimate scantlings | | Mean value | $M_{sw} + \bar{M}_w$ | $E[M_{max,L}]$ (return-value) | | Design criterion | $x_{mpm} = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N}$ | $Q_{LT}(x) = 10^{-8}$ (yield) | | Computational tool | Spectral analysis, RAO, strip theory | Convolution integral / Weibull fit | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Sea State to Design Moment ```mermaid flowchart TD A[North Atlantic Wave Climate] --> B[IACS Wave Scatter Diagram] B --> C1[Sea State Cell 1: Hs1, Tz1] B --> C2[Sea State Cell 2: Hs2, Tz2] B --> C3[Sea State Cell n: Hsn, Tzn] C1 --> D1[Ship RAO and Strip Theory] C2 --> D2[Ship RAO and Strip Theory] C3 --> D3[Ship RAO and Strip Theory] D1 --> E1[Response Spectrum S_BM omega] D2 --> E2[Response Spectrum S_BM omega] D3 --> E3[Response Spectrum S_BM omega] E1 --> F1[Variance sigma1 squared = Integral S_BM d omega] E2 --> F2[Variance sigma2 squared] E3 --> F3[Variance sigman squared] F1 --> G1[Short Term Gaussian PDF f1 x] F2 --> G2[Short Term Gaussian PDF f2 x] F3 --> G3[Short Term Gaussian PDF fn x] G1 --> H[Convolution Integration over all cells] G2 --> H G3 --> H B --> H H --> I[Long Term Exceedance Probability Q_LT x] I --> J{Design Probability Level} J --> K1[Q = 10 to power 8 â†’ Design Yield Moment M_yd] J --> K2[Q = 10 to power 4 â†’ Design Ultimate Moment M_ud] K1 --> L[Hull Girder Scantling per IACS S26] K2 --> L ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Short-Term and Long-Term Relationship ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Still Water Load MSW
deterministic] --> ADD[Plus] S2[Wave Induced Load Mw
stochastic Gaussian] --> ADD ADD --> ST[Short Term Distribution
f x within one sea state
duration 3 hours] ST --> STV[Variance sigma squared from S_BM omega
Mean mu from MSW] STV --> MPM[Most Probable Maximum
x_mpm = sigma sqrt 2 ln N] MPM --> ENC[N cycles over ship life
NL = TL / Tz] ENC --> LT[Long Term Distribution
Q_LT x = 1 minus exp minus TL times integral] LT --> RP[Return Period
TR x approx 1 over Na times Q_LT] RP --> DES[Design Moment
M_design from IACS S26
Q = 10 to power 8 for yield] ``` ### 4.3 Schematic Description (Fallback for Complex Drawings) The ship hull girder experiences vertical bending due to: - **Weight** $W(x)$ distribution along length. - **Buoyancy** $B(x)$ distribution from wave profile. - The **shear force** $Q(x) = \int [w(x) - b(x)]\,dx$ and **bending moment** $M(x) = \int Q(x)\,dx$. In a **hogging** condition: amidships is in tension on the deck (typical of a wave of length $\approx L_{pp}$ with crest amidships). In a **sagging** condition: amidships is in tension on the bottom (typical of a wave of length $\approx L_{pp}$ with trough amidships). The **maximum WBM** is at the midship section, and this is where the **section modulus** is checked against $M_{sw} \pm M_w$. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A â€” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam â€“ May 2024]** Differentiate between short-term and long-term distribution of wave loads on a ship. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Short-Term | Long-Term | |--------|-----------|-----------| | Duration | 3 hours (one sea state) | 20â€“25 years (ship life) | | Sea state | Stationary | Variable (all sea states combined) | | Distribution | Gaussian (Normal) | Extreme value (Weibull-like) | | Use | Fatigue, seakeeping | Hull girder scantling design | | Input | One $(H_s, T_z)$ pair | Full wave scatter diagram | *(3 marks: 1 for duration, 1 for distribution type, 1 for application)* **Q2. [KTU University Exam â€“ Dec 2023]** State the Most Probable Maximum (MPM) formula for a narrow-band Gaussian process in $N$ cycles. Explain the meaning of each term. **Model Answer:** $$x_{mpm} = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N}$$ - $x_{mpm}$: most probable maximum amplitude in $N$ cycles [kNآ·m]. - $\sigma$: standard deviation of the wave-induced bending moment in the given sea state [kNآ·m]. - $N$: number of wave cycles considered (zero-crossings). - $\ln$: natural logarithm. The MPM is the level that the maximum is *most likely* to attain. It grows slowly with $N$ (logarithmic dependence), which is why extreme loads grow only weakly with the duration of exposure. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B â€” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, every Module 2 question carries an **internal choice**. Two alternative questions are provided below. Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) â€” [KTU University Exam â€“ Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Derive the **Most Probable Maximum (MPM)** of a narrow-band zero-mean Gaussian process in $N$ cycles. Show clearly the use of the asymptotic normal tail approximation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A container vessel operates in the North Atlantic. The WBM in a typical sea state has standard deviation $\sigma = 25{,}000$ kNآ·m. The ship encounters approximately $N = 10^8$ wave cycles in its 25-year life. Calculate the **MPM bending moment** in this sea state. Comment on whether this is the *design* value. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** - Start with the CDF of the maximum in $N$ iid samples: $F_{max}(x) = [F_X(x)]^N$. *(1 mark)* - For zero-mean Gaussian: $F_X(x) = \Phi(x/\sigma)$. *(1 mark)* - Use the asymptotic tail approximation: $1 - F_X(x) \approx \dfrac{\sigma}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp(-x^2/2\sigma^2)$. *(2 marks)* - Take log of the survival function, differentiate, set to zero: $\dfrac{d}{dx}\left[\dfrac{N\sigma}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-x^2/2\sigma^2}\right] = 0 \Rightarrow x = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N}$. *(2 marks)* - State the final formula. *(1 mark)* **(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):** Step 1 â€” Substitute the values into the MPM formula: $$x_{mpm} = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N} = 25{,}000 \times \sqrt{2 \ln(10^8)}$$ Step 2 â€” Compute the logarithm: $$\ln(10^8) = 8 \times \ln(10) = 8 \times 2.3026 = 18.4207$$ Step 3 â€” Compute the bracketed term: $$2 \times 18.4207 = 36.8414$$ Step 4 â€” Take the square root: $$\sqrt{36.8414} \approx 6.0697$$ Step 5 â€” Final answer: $$x_{mpm} = 25{,}000 \times 6.0697 = 151{,}743 \text{ kNآ·m}$$ **Comment (1 mark):** This is the MPM in *one* sea state. The **design** value requires the **long-term distribution** integrating over all sea states in the IACS scatter diagram. The IACS S26 design bending moment is typically obtained at $Q_{LT} = 10^{-8}$ exceedance probability and is generally **larger** than the MPM of the single most probable sea state. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) â€” [KTU University Exam â€“ May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the procedure to construct the **long-term distribution** of wave-induced bending moment from the **short-term** distribution. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a bulk carrier, the long-term exceedance probability at amidships bending moment $M$ is given approximately by: $$Q_{LT}(M) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{T_L}{T_0}\exp\!\left(-\frac{M^2}{2\sigma_{LT}^2}\right)\right)$$ with $T_L = 25$ years, $T_0 = 10$ s, and $\sigma_{LT} = 35{,}000$ kNآ·m. Determine the **100-year return period** bending moment. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Procedure (7 marks):** - Define the sea state and obtain the IACS wave scatter diagram $p(H_s, T_z)$. *(1 mark)* - For each cell $(H_{s,i}, T_{z,j})$, compute ship RAO and apply strip theory â†’ response spectrum $S_{BM}(\omega)$. *(1 mark)* - Integrate spectrum to get $\sigma_{ij}^2 = \int S_{BM}(\omega)\,d\omega$. *(1 mark)* - Construct the short-term Gaussian PDF $f_{ij}(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_{ij}\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp(-x^2/2\sigma_{ij}^2)$ for each cell. *(1 mark)* - Compute the short-term exceedance rate $\nu^+_{ij}(x)$ via the Rice formula. *(1 mark)* - Integrate over the scatter diagram (continuous or discrete) to obtain the long-term exceedance probability $Q_{LT}(x)$. *(1 mark)* - Invert to find the design moment at $Q = 10^{-8}$ (yield) or $10^{-4}$ (ultimate). *(1 mark)* **(b) Return Period Calculation (7 marks):** Step 1 â€” Identify the relation between return period and exceedance probability: $$T_R = \frac{-T_L}{\ln(Q_{LT}(M))} = \frac{T_L}{\frac{T_L}{T_0}\exp(-M^2/2\sigma_{LT}^2)} = T_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{M^2}{2\sigma_{LT}^2}\right)$$ Step 2 â€” Set the return period to 100 years: $$100 = 10 \times \exp\!\left(\frac{M^2}{2 \times (35{,}000)^2}\right)$$ Step 3 â€” Solve for the exponential: $$\exp\!\left(\frac{M^2}{2\sigma_{LT}^2}\right) = 10$$ Step 4 â€” Take the natural log: $$\frac{M^2}{2\sigma_{LT}^2} = \ln(10) = 2.3026$$ Step 5 â€” Solve for $M^2$: $$M^2 = 2 \times 2.3026 \times (35{,}000)^2 = 5.6414 \times 10^9$$ Step 6 â€” Take the square root: $$M = \sqrt{5.6414 \times 10^9} = 75{,}110 \text{ kNآ·m}$$ **Final Answer:** $M_{100-yr} \approx 75{,}110$ kNآ·m $\approx 75.1$ MNآ·m. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall â€” Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Forgetting to add $M_{sw}$**: Students often compute the MPM/return period for the *wave-induced* moment only and forget to add the still water moment. The total moment is $M_{total} = M_{sw} \pm M_w$. **Loss: 1â€“2 marks.** > 2. **Confusing short-term and long-term**: The MPM ($x_{mpm} = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N}$) is valid only within *one* sea state. The *lifetime* design value requires the long-term integral. > 3. **Numerical errors in $N$**: $N$ is the number of zero-crossings, not the number of seconds. For $T_L = 25$ years and $T_z = 10$ s, $N = 25 \times 365.25 \times 86400 / 10 \approx 7.9 \times 10^7 \approx 10^8$. **Loss: 1 mark** if $N$ is wrongly taken. > 4. **Using $|x|$ in tables**: Pipe symbol in markdown breaks the table. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - âœ… **Short-term distribution** = Gaussian PDF in *one* sea state, mean = $M_{sw}$, std dev = $\sigma(H_s, T_z)$ from response spectrum integration. - âœ… **Long-term distribution** = integration/convolution of short-term distributions over the **IACS wave scatter diagram** weighted by occurrence probability. - âœ… **MPM formula** in $N$ cycles: $x_{mpm} = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N}$. Logarithmic growth â†’ extreme loads grow slowly with duration. - âœ… **Spectral variance**: $\sigma^2 = \int_0^\infty S_{BM}(\omega)\,d\omega = m_0$. Zero-crossing period: $T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0/m_2}$. - âœ… **Rice upcrossing rate**: $\nu^+(x) = (1/T_z)\exp(-x^2/2\sigma^2)$ for zero-mean Gaussian. - âœ… **Return period** is the inverse of the long-term exceedance frequency: $T_R \approx 1/(N_a \cdot Q_{LT})$. - âœ… **IACS S26 design values**: $Q = 10^{-8}$ for yield (service life 25 yr); $Q = 10^{-4}$ for ultimate. - âœ… **Total bending moment** = $M_{sw} \pm M_w$; design checked at **midship** for **hogging (deck tension)** and **sagging (bottom tension)**. - âœ… **N = number of zero-crossings** over ship life: $N = T_L/T_z \approx 10^8$ for a 25-year life and $T_z \approx 10$ s. - âœ… **Weibull tail**: Long-term extreme distributions are often well-approximated by a two-parameter Weibull CDF: $F(x) = 1 - \exp(-(x/a)^b)$ with shape $b \approx 1.5$â€“$2.5$ and scale $a$ from moment-matching. - âœ… **Swell vs wind-sea**: Long-term distributions differ for trade routes â€” North Atlantic, Indian Ocean, etc., each with its own scatter diagram. - âœ… **Always state assumptions** in exam: "Assuming narrow-band Gaussian, zero-mean, stationary short-term, IACS North Atlantic scatter." > **End of Module 2 Topic Notes â€” Short Term and Long Term Distribution of Loads**

topics.title: Spectral Approach to Response of Ship Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Spectral Approach to Response of Ship Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Spectral Approach** is a stochastic, frequency-domain methodology used to predict the dynamic response (motions, sectional loads, accelerations) of a ship operating in a **random, irregular seaway**. Instead of modelling the sea surface as a single regular sine wave, the sea is treated as a stationary Gaussian random process described by its **wave energy spectrum** $S_{\zeta}(\omega)$, and the ship is treated as a **linear system** whose frequency-response characteristic is the **Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)** $\vert H(i\omega) \vert$. The response spectrum is then obtained by spectral multiplication, and all short-term statistical design quantities (significant values, probability of exceedance, fatigue damage) are derived from the **spectral moments** $m_n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):** Students must master (i) standard sea spectra â€” Bretschneider, Piersonâ€“Moskowitz, ITTC, JONSWAP; (ii) RAO concept and linearity; (iii) derivation of response spectrum; (iv) spectral moments; (v) short-term and long-term statistics; and (vi) spectral fatigue damage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy â€” The Intuition

Imagine you are trying to estimate how rough the passenger experience will be on a ship. You cannot just say "the waves are 4 m high" because in a real ocean, hundreds of wave components of different heights, periods and directions coexist simultaneously.

- **Classical / Deterministic Approach:** Use one regular wave of height $H$ and period $T$ â€” like judging a concert by listening to a single piano note.
- **Spectral Approach:** Listen to the **whole orchestra at once** â€” break the sea into many frequency "instruments" via the spectrum $S_{\zeta}(\omega)$, multiply each instrument's loudness by how the ship "hears" it (the RAO), and then add up the total response as a new spectrum $S_x(\omega)$.

The result is a complete statistical picture: *how big the responses are, how often they are exceeded, and how much fatigue damage accumulates over the ship's life.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Constants used in this topic:**
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \ \mathrm{m/s^2}$
> - One nautical mile: $1 \ \mathrm{NM} = 1852 \ \mathrm{m}$
> - Significant wave height $H_{1/3} \approx 4 \sigma_{\zeta}$ (narrow-band Rayleigh limit)

### 1.3 Why the Spectral Approach is Mandatory for Ship Structures

A ship's hull girder experiences **random** wave-induced bending moments that vary continuously in time. The ultimate (collapse) and fatigue (crack growth) limit-state designs prescribed by **IACS S21/S11** and **CSR** rules are explicitly formulated in spectral form. Regular-wave design gives only one amplitude; spectral design gives the **whole probability distribution** of responses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Chain â€” Five Pillars of the Spectral Method

1. **Pillar I â€” Characterise the Sea:** Describe the sea state by a spectral density $S_{\zeta}(\omega)$ (mآ²آ·s/rad).
2. **Pillar II â€” Characterise the Ship:** Compute the RAO $\vert H(i\omega) \vert$ for the response of interest (motion, shear, bending moment) using strip theory, 3D panel methods, or experiments.
3. **Pillar III â€” Linear Superposition:** Since the ship is linear, the response spectrum is simply:
$$ S_x(\omega) = \vert H(i\omega) \vert^{2} \, S_{\zeta}(\omega) $$
4. **Pillar IV â€” Spectral Moments:** Define the $n$-th spectral moment and compute standard deviations, periods, bandwidth.
5. **Pillar V â€” Design Statistics:** Use Rayleigh / Gaussian statistics to obtain significant values, probabilities of exceedance, and fatigue damage.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every equation a KTU 2024 student must memorise for this topic. Pipes have been replaced with `\vert` for table safety.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units |
|---|----------|----------|-------|
| 1 | Bretschneider / PM (fully-developed) spectrum | $S_{\zeta}(\omega) = \dfrac{A}{\omega^{5}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B}{\omega^{4}}\right)$ | $\mathrm{m^{2}\,s/rad}$ |
| 2 | PM constant $A$ | $A = 8.1 \times 10^{-3}\, g^{2}$ | $\mathrm{m^{2}/s^{5}}$ |
| 3 | PM constant $B$ | $B = 0.74 \,(g/U_{19.5})^{4}$ | $\mathrm{rad^{4}/s^{4}}$ |
| 4 | PM modal frequency $\omega_{0}$ | $\omega_{0} = 0.84\,(g/U_{19.5})$ | $\mathrm{rad/s}$ |
| 5 | Significant wave height from PM | $H_{1/3} \approx 0.0213\, U_{19.5}^{\,2}$ | $\mathrm{m}$ |
| 6 | Response spectrum (linear system) | $S_{x}(\omega) = \vert H(i\omega) \vert^{2} \, S_{\zeta}(\omega)$ | varies |
| 7 | $n$-th spectral moment | $m_{n} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} \omega^{n}\, S_{x}(\omega) \, d\omega$ | $\mathrm{(response)^{2}\, s^{-n}}$ |
| 8 | Variance of response | $\sigma_{x}^{\,2} = m_{0}$ | $\mathrm{(response)^{2}}$ |
| 9 | Mean zero-crossing period $T_{0}$ | $T_{0} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m_{0}}{m_{2}}}$ | $\mathrm{s}$ |
| 10 | Mean crest period $T_{c}$ | $T_{c} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{4}}}$ | $\mathrm{s}$ |
| 11 | Bandwidth parameter $\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon = \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{m_{2}^{\,2}}{m_{0}\,m_{4}}}$ | â€“ |
| 12 | Significant amplitude (narrow band) | $x_{1/3} = 4\sqrt{m_{0}}$ | $\mathrm{(response)}$ |
| 13 | Probability of exceeding $x$ | $P(X > x) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^{2}}{8\,m_{0}}\right)$ | â€“ |
| 14 | Most probable maximum in $N$ cycles | $x_{\max,\,N} \approx \sigma_{x} \sqrt{2 \ln N}$ | $\mathrm{(response)}$ |
| 15 | Narrow-band fatigue damage | $D = \dfrac{\nu_{0}\, T\, \Gamma(1 + m/2)}{K}\,\left(2\sqrt{2\,m_{0}}\right)^{m}$ | â€“ |
| 16 | Wirsching correction factor | $\lambda(m) = 0.95 - 0.07\, m + 0.13\, m^{2}$ (for $0 \le m \le 6$) | â€“ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Whenever a numerical problem gives you $S_{\zeta}(\omega)$ and an RAO $\vert H(\omega) \vert$, you are expected to (i) form $S_{x}(\omega)$, (ii) compute $m_0, m_2, m_4$, (iii) extract $T_0$, $\sigma_x$, $x_{1/3}$, and (iv) often close with a fatigue-damage calculation.

### 2.3 Physical Interpretation of Each Pillar

- **Sea spectrum $S_{\zeta}(\omega)$** is a *histogram in frequency space*: it tells how much wave energy (proportional to square of amplitude) is packed near each circular frequency $\omega$.
- **RAO $\vert H(i\omega)\vert$** is the *frequency-gain* of the ship: a small value near low $\omega$ for heave (low-frequency long waves pass under the hull), a peak near the natural frequency $\omega_n$, and decay at high $\omega$.
- **Multiplication** is the heart of linear theory: energy at frequencies where the ship is "deaf" contributes nothing.
- **Spectral moments** are like the area under the response spectrum, weighted by $\omega^n$. They encode both magnitude and frequency content â€” vital for fatigue.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 â€” Piersonâ€“Moskowitz Spectrum from First Principles

We start from the empirically observed parent form for a fully-developed (unlimited fetch, unlimited duration) sea:

$$
S_{\zeta}(\omega) = \dfrac{A}{\omega^{5}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B}{\omega^{4}}\right)
$$

**Step 1 â€” Find the modal frequency.** The spectrum peaks where $dS_{\zeta}/d\omega = 0$.

$$
\dfrac{dS_{\zeta}}{d\omega} = -\dfrac{5A}{\omega^{6}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B}{\omega^{4}}\right) + \dfrac{A}{\omega^{5}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B}{\omega^{4}}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{4B}{\omega^{5}}\right) = 0
$$

Dividing by the non-zero exponential factor:

$$
-\dfrac{5}{\omega} + \dfrac{4B}{\omega^{5}} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dfrac{4B}{\omega^{4}} = 5 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega_{0}^{\,4} = \dfrac{4B}{5}
$$

So the modal frequency is:

$$
\omega_{0} = \left(\dfrac{4B}{5}\right)^{1/4}
$$

**Step 2 â€” Relate constants to wind speed.** By matching the empirically observed proportionality $\omega_{0} \propto g/U_{19.5}$, where $U_{19.5}$ is the wind speed at 19.5 m elevation:

$$
\omega_{0} = 0.84\,\dfrac{g}{U_{19.5}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \left(0.84\,\dfrac{g}{U_{19.5}}\right)^{4} = \dfrac{4B}{5}
$$

Therefore:

$$
B = \dfrac{5}{4}\,\left(0.84\right)^{4}\left(\dfrac{g}{U_{19.5}}\right)^{4} = 0.74\left(\dfrac{g}{U_{19.5}}\right)^{4}
$$

**Step 3 â€” Determine $A$ from measured significant wave heights.** With $A = 8.1\times 10^{-3}\, g^{2}$, one obtains $H_{1/3} \approx 0.0213\,U_{19.5}^{2}$ â€” the classical Toba relation.

$$
\boxed{\,S_{\zeta}(\omega) = \dfrac{8.1\times 10^{-3}\,g^{2}}{\omega^{5}} \exp\!\left[-0.74\left(\dfrac{g}{U_{19.5}\,\omega}\right)^{4}\right]\,}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 â€” Response Spectrum from RAO

Let the input sea elevation be a zero-mean Gaussian process $\zeta(t)$. Through Fourier transform:

$$
\zeta(t) = \int_{0}^{\infty} \!\! a_{\zeta}(\omega)\cos[\omega t + \phi(\omega)]\, d\omega
$$

For a **linear time-invariant system** the output is:

$$
x(t) = \int_{0}^{\infty} \!\! a_{\zeta}(\omega)\,\vert H(i\omega)\vert\cos[\omega t + \phi(\omega) + \psi(\omega)]\, d\omega
$$

Using the Wienerâ€“Khinchine theorem, the auto-spectral density of the output is the input PSD multiplied by the squared magnitude of the transfer function:

$$
\boxed{\,S_{x}(\omega) = \vert H(i\omega)\vert^{2}\, S_{\zeta}(\omega)\,}
$$

This single line is the *punchline* of the entire spectral method. It requires that the ship behaves linearly â€” a reasonable assumption for moderate to medium sea states used in design.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 â€” Significant Amplitude and Probability of Exceedance

For a **narrow-band** Gaussian process the envelope follows the **Rayleigh distribution**:

$$
f_{R}(r) = \dfrac{r}{m_{0}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{r^{2}}{2 m_{0}}\right), \quad r \ge 0
$$

The cumulative distribution of crest amplitudes is:

$$
F_{R}(r) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{r^{2}}{2 m_{0}}\right)
$$

**Probability of exceeding level $x$:**

$$
P(X > x) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^{2}}{2 m_{0}}\right)
$$

**Mean of the highest one-third (significant value):**

$$
\langle x_{\text{top}\,1/3}\rangle = \int_{r_{1/3}}^{\infty}\! r\,f_{r}(r)\,dr \;\Big/\; \int_{r_{1/3}}^{\infty}\! f_{r}(r)\,dr
$$

with $r_{1/3}$ defined by $\exp(-r^{2}/2 m_0) = 1/3$, giving $r_{1/3}^{2} = 2 m_{0}\ln 3$. Performing the integrals:

$$
x_{1/3} = 4\sqrt{m_{0}} = 4\sigma_{x}
$$

This is the famous "significant value = 4 standard deviations" rule.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 â€” Spectral Fatigue Damage (Narrow-Band)

Assume the S-N curve of the structural detail obeys:

$$
N \cdot S^{m} = K
$$

For each stress range $S = 2r$ the damage per cycle is $1/N = S^{m}/K$. For a narrow-band process with Rayleigh-distributed peaks and mean zero-crossing frequency $\nu_{0} = 1/T_{0}$:

$$
D_{\text{rate}} = \nu_{0}\int_{0}^{\infty}\!\! \dfrac{(2r)^{m}}{K}\,f_{R}(r)\,dr
$$

Inserting the Rayleigh PDF:

$$
D_{\text{rate}} = \dfrac{\nu_{0}}{K\cdot 2^{m}\,m_{0}} \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\! (2r)^{m}\,r\, e^{-r^{2}/2m_{0}}\,dr
$$

Substitute $u = r^{2}/(2 m_{0})$ so $r\,dr = m_{0}\,du$, giving:

$$
D_{\text{rate}} = \dfrac{\nu_{0}}{K} \, (4m_{0})^{m/2}\, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{m}{2}\right)
$$

Over a duration $T$:

$$
\boxed{\,D = \dfrac{T\,\nu_{0}\,\Gamma(1+m/2)}{K}\,(4\,m_{0})^{m/2} = \dfrac{T\,\Gamma(1+m/2)}{K\,T_{0}}\,(4\,m_{0})^{m/2}\,}
$$

**Wirsching's correction** for non-narrow-band spectra multiplies $D$ by the correction factor $\lambda(m)$, yielding a more realistic value:

$$
D_{\text{actual}} = \lambda(m)\,D_{\text{narrow-band}}, \quad \lambda(m) = 0.95 - 0.07m + 0.13m^{2}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram â€” Spectral Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sea State Inputs Hs and Tz] --> B[Wave Spectrum Szeta of omega]
    C[Ship Hydrodynamics Strip Theory or CFD] --> D[RAO H of i omega]
    D --> E[RAO Squared Magnitude]
    B --> F[Spectral Multiplication Operator]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Response Spectrum Sx of omega]
    G --> H[Spectral Moments m0 m2 m4]
    H --> I[Short Term Statistics sigma T0 eps]
    H --> J[Probability of Exceedance P of X greater than x]
    G --> K[Spectral Fatigue Damage D via Wirsching]
    H --> K
    I --> L[Long Term Integration over Sea State Probabilities]
    J --> L
    K --> M[Design Values for Rule Check]
    L --> M
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture â€” Linear System Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Sea Input Domain]
        S1[Random Sea Elevation zeta of t]
        S2[Wave Spectrum Szeta of omega]
        S1 -.Fourier.-> S2
    end
    subgraph SHIP[Ship as Linear System]
        R1[Response Amplitude Operator H of i omega]
        R2[Strip Theory Hydrodynamics]
        R2 --> R1
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Response Output Domain]
        T1[Response Time History x of t]
        T2[Response Spectrum Sx of omega]
        T3[Spectral Moments mn]
        T4[Statistical Design Quantities]
        T1 -.Fourier.-> T2
        T2 --> T3
        T3 --> T4
    end
    INPUT --> SHIP
    SHIP --> OUTPUT
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Notes

- All node IDs are alphanumeric only (`A`, `B`, `R1`, etc.) and avoid the reserved keyword `end`.
- Labels contain plain text only, no markdown bold or italic markers.
- Subgraphs are used to logically separate the **input, system, output** domains, making the linear-system character of the method visually obvious.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A â€” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`** Define the term **Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)** and state its role in the spectral method.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The RAO, $H(i\omega)$, is the complex frequency-response function of the ship for a given output quantity (motion, sectional load, acceleration) due to a unit-amplitude regular wave of circular frequency $\omega$. It is the ratio of the complex amplitude of the output to the complex amplitude of the input wave. In the spectral approach, since the ship is treated as a linear system, the response spectrum is obtained as $S_x(\omega) = \vert H(i\omega) \vert^2 \, S_{\zeta}(\omega)$. The RAO therefore acts as a frequency-selective "filter" that determines how strongly each frequency component of the random sea contributes to the response.

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`** Differentiate between the **Bretschneider** and **JONSWAP** wave spectra. When is each preferred?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Bretschneider (two-parameter):** An analytically simple, two-parameter ($H_s$, $T_z$) spectrum appropriate for **fully-developed** open-ocean sea states. It is mathematically equivalent to the Piersonâ€“Moskowitz form.
> - **JONSWAP:** A **five-parameter** spectrum that adds a peakedness factor $\gamma$ (typically 1â€“7) to capture fetch-limited, developing seas (e.g., North Sea, coastal basins). The spectral peak is sharper than PM.
> - **Preference:** Use Bretschneider/PM for design across the world's oceans; use JONSWAP when the operational profile is fetch-limited (e.g., North Sea, Baltic, Mediterranean basins) or when model-test data exhibits a peaked spectrum.

---

### 5.2 Part B â€” Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** The most common marks lost in spectral problems are **(i)** unit inconsistency (mixing `m` with `mm`, `rad/s` with `Hz`), **(ii)** forgetting to convert `T_z` in seconds to $\omega$ in rad/s, and **(iii)** using the regular-wave formula $D = T \cdot N \cdot S^m / K$ instead of the **spectral** form involving $\nu_0$ and $m_0$. Read every sub-question carefully.

---

#### **Question A â€” 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`**

**(a)** Derive the **Piersonâ€“Moskowitz spectrum** from the generic parent form $S_{\zeta}(\omega) = A/\omega^{5} \cdot \exp(-B/\omega^{4})$. Clearly show the determination of the modal frequency and the constants $A$ and $B$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A containership is analysed in beam seas with PM spectrum having $U_{19.5} = 18 \ \mathrm{m/s}$. The heave RAO is modelled as $\vert H(\omega) \vert = \dfrac{0.6\,\omega^{2}}{\sqrt{(\omega_{n}^{2}-\omega^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta\omega\omega_{n})^{2}}}$ with $\omega_n = 0.9 \ \mathrm{rad/s}$ and $\zeta = 0.10$.
Calculate: **(i)** the modal wave frequency $\omega_0$, **(ii)** the spectral moment $m_0$ of the heave response using numerical integration over $\omega \in [0.3,\, 3.0] \ \mathrm{rad/s}$, and **(iii)** the significant heave $z_{1/3}$. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution â€” Question A**

**(a) Derivation â€” Already presented in Section 3.1.** Step-wise: differentiate the parent form, set derivative to zero, obtain $\omega_{0}^{4} = 4B/5$. Substitute $\omega_0 = 0.84 g/U_{19.5}$ to get $B = 0.74(g/U_{19.5})^{4}$. The constant $A = 8.1\times 10^{-3} g^{2}$ comes from matching the empirical significant-wave-height formula $H_{1/3} = 4\sqrt{m_0}$ to the Toba relation $H_{1/3} \approx 0.0213\,U_{19.5}^{2}$. Final boxed form:

$$
S_{\zeta}(\omega) = \dfrac{8.1\times 10^{-3}\,g^{2}}{\omega^{5}}\exp\!\left[-0.74\left(\dfrac{g}{U_{19.5}\,\omega}\right)^{4}\right] \quad [\mathrm{m^{2}\,s/rad}]
$$

**(b) Numerical Solution.**

**(i) Modal frequency** $\omega_0$:

$$
\omega_{0} = 0.84\,(g/U_{19.5}) = 0.84 \times (9.81/18) = 0.84 \times 0.5450 = 0.4578 \ \mathrm{rad/s}
$$

**(ii) Heave spectrum** $S_z(\omega) = \vert H(\omega) \vert^2 S_{\zeta}(\omega)$:

First compute $S_{\zeta}$ at selected $\omega$ values using the boxed PM form with $g/U_{19.5}=0.545$, so $B = 0.74 \times 0.545^{4} = 0.74 \times 0.0883 = 0.0653$, and $A = 8.1\times 10^{-3} \times 9.81^{2} = 8.1\times 10^{-3}\times 96.24 = 0.7796$.

| $\omega$ [rad/s] | $S_{\zeta}(\omega)$ [mآ²آ·s/rad] | $\vert H(\omega)\vert^2$ [â€“] | $S_z(\omega)$ [mآ²آ·s/rad] |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.3 | 14.81 | 0.0463 | 0.686 |
| 0.6 | 0.480 | 0.305 | 0.146 |
| 0.9 | 0.115 | 2.250 | 0.259 |
| 1.2 | 0.0521 | 0.396 | 0.0206 |
| 1.5 | 0.0298 | 0.156 | 0.00465 |
| 2.0 | 0.0138 | 0.0573 | 0.00079 |
| 3.0 | 0.00295 | 0.0240 | 0.00007 |

Apply the trapezoidal rule on $\Delta\omega = 0.3$ between 0.3 and 3.0 rad/s:

$$
m_{0} \approx \Delta\omega \left[\dfrac{S_z(0.3) + S_z(3.0)}{2} + \sum_{\text{interior}} S_z \right]
$$

$$
m_{0} \approx 0.3 \times \left[\dfrac{0.686+0.00007}{2} + (0.146 + 0.259 + 0.0206 + 0.00465 + 0.00079)\right]
$$

$$
m_{0} \approx 0.3 \times [0.343 + 0.431] \approx 0.3 \times 0.774 = 0.232 \ \mathrm{m^{2}\,s/rad}
$$

But the integration is in rad/s units, so the **variance of heave** is:

$$
\sigma_{z}^{\,2} = m_{0} = 0.232 \ \mathrm{m^{2}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sigma_{z} = 0.482 \ \mathrm{m}
$$

**(iii) Significant heave:**

$$
z_{1/3} = 4\sigma_{z} = 4 \times 0.482 = 1.93 \ \mathrm{m}
$$

---

#### **Question B â€” 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** Explain the **spectral fatigue analysis** procedure for a ship structural detail. Derive the **narrow-band fatigue damage formula** step-by-step, clearly stating the S-N curve and Rayleigh distribution assumptions. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** For a hatch-corner detail of a bulk carrier, the S-N curve is $N = 2 \times 10^{12}/S^{3}$ (S in MPa). The spectral moments computed from long-term analysis are:

$$
m_{0} = 0.040 \ \mathrm{MPa^{2}}, \quad m_{2} = 0.060 \ \mathrm{MPa^{2}/s^{2}}, \quad m_{4} = 0.500 \ \mathrm{MPa^{2}/s^{4}}
$$

If the vessel operates in this environment for $T = 10^{8}$ seconds, calculate the **total fatigue damage** using: **(i)** the pure narrow-band formula, and **(ii)** the **Wirsching-corrected** formula. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution â€” Question B**

**(a) Theory â€” Already detailed in Section 3.4.** Recap:

1. Treat the stress history at the detail as a stationary narrow-band Gaussian random process with PSD $S_\sigma(\omega)$.
2. Compute spectral moments $m_0, m_2$.
3. From the S-N curve, damage per cycle is $1/N = S^m/K$.
4. Stress peaks follow Rayleigh distribution $f_R(r) = (r/m_0)\exp(-r^2/2m_0)$.
