5. Integrate $D_{\text{rate}} = \nu_0 \int_0^\infty (2r)^m f_R(r)\,dr/K$ to obtain:

$$
D_{\text{rate}} = \dfrac{\nu_0\,\Gamma(1+m/2)}{K}(4\,m_0)^{m/2}
$$

6. Multiply by duration $T$ to obtain total damage $D$.

**(b) Numerical Solution.**

Given: $K = 2\times 10^{12}$, $m = 3$, $m_0 = 0.040 \ \mathrm{MPa^{2}}$, $m_2 = 0.060 \ \mathrm{MPa^{2}/s^{2}}$, $T = 10^{8} \ \mathrm{s}$.

**Step 1 â€” Mean zero-crossing frequency:**

$$
\nu_{0} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{0}}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{0.060}{0.040}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{1.5} = \dfrac{1.2247}{6.2832} = 0.1949 \ \mathrm{Hz}
$$

**Step 2 â€” Narrow-band damage:**

$$
D_{\text{NB}} = \dfrac{T\,\nu_{0}\,\Gamma(1 + m/2)}{K}(4\,m_{0})^{m/2}
$$

Compute each piece:

- $\Gamma(1 + 3/2) = \Gamma(2.5) = \dfrac{3\sqrt{\pi}}{4} = 1.3293$
- $(4\,m_0)^{m/2} = (4 \times 0.040)^{3/2} = (0.160)^{1.5} = 0.0640$
- Numerator: $T \cdot \nu_0 \cdot \Gamma = 10^{8} \times 0.1949 \times 1.3293 = 2.591 \times 10^{7}$

$$
D_{\text{NB}} = \dfrac{2.591 \times 10^{7} \times 0.0640}{2 \times 10^{12}} = \dfrac{1.658 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{12}} = 8.29 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 3 â€” Wirsching correction** with $m = 3$:

$$
\lambda(3) = 0.95 - 0.07(3) + 0.13(3)^{2} = 0.95 - 0.21 + 1.17 = 1.91
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that $\lambda > 1$ is mathematically possible for small $m$ because Wirsching's polynomial was fitted to specific datasets; some texts cap it at $\lambda \le 1$. For safety-critical KTU problems, students should clearly state the assumption used.

**Wirsching-corrected damage:**

$$
D_{\text{actual}} = \lambda(3)\cdot D_{\text{NB}} = 1.91 \times 8.29 \times 10^{-7} = 1.58 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Since $D \ll 1$, the detail has very large fatigue life at this stress level â€” a typical design conclusion for properly detailed hatch corners.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Spectral method = Linear system response + Sea spectrum + RAO + Statistics.**
- **PM spectrum** is the workhorse for fully-developed seas; **JONSWAP** for fetch-limited basins.
- **Response spectrum** equation: $S_x(\omega) = \vert H(i\omega)\vert^2 S_\zeta(\omega)$ â€” memorise verbatim.
- **Spectral moments** $m_0, m_2, m_4$ unlock variance, period, bandwidth, and fatigue damage.
- **Significant value = $4\sqrt{m_0}$** only in the narrow-band Rayleigh limit; for wide-band spectra a bandwidth correction is required.
- **Probability of exceedance** is **Rayleigh**: $P(X > x) = \exp(-x^2/2m_0)$.
- **Most probable maximum** in $N$ cycles: $x_{\max,N} \approx \sigma_x \sqrt{2\ln N}$.
- **Long-term** statistics require integration over the sea-state scatter diagram: $P_{\text{LT}}(X > x) = \sum_i P(X > x \mid H_{s,i})\, p_i$.
- **Narrow-band fatigue damage** formula must include $\nu_0 = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{m_2/m_0}$, **not** a single frequency value.
- **Wirsching correction** $\lambda(m) = 0.95 - 0.07m + 0.13m^2$ converts narrow-band damage to wide-band reality.
- **Unit discipline:** always keep $\omega$ in rad/s; $H_s$ in m; moments in proper $\mathrm{(response)^2 s^{-n}}$.
- **Common pitfall:** forgetting that $S_\zeta$ is in $\mathrm{m^2\,s/rad}$, so $m_0 = \int S_x\, d\omega$ has units of $\mathrm{(response)^2}$ only because $d\omega$ cancels the time unit implicitly stored in the spectrum's denominator.
- **KTU 2024 Module 2 Outcome:** students should be able to *derive*, *apply*, and *interpret* all the above for ship motions and hull-girder loads.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Slamming and Shipping of Green Seas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Effect of Slamming and Shipping of Green Seas # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Slamming? **Slamming** is a highly transient, impulsive hydrodynamic load that occurs when the forward sections of a ship (particularly the bow, shoulder, and forward bottom) re-enter the water surface after emerging into air during violent ship motions in heavy seas. The relative vertical velocity between the hull and the water surface can be extremely high, generating localized impact pressures of several hundred kilopascals over very short durations (typically **0.01 to 0.1 seconds**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCNST601 - Module 2):** > *Slamming refers to the severe, short-duration hydrodynamic impact loads experienced by the bow, forward shoulder, and bottom plating of a ship when these regions emerge from and re-enter the sea surface during heavy weather. These loads are critical for determining the local scantlings of the forward hull structure.* ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine jumping into a swimming pool from a height of 2 meters. The instant your feet hit the water, you feel a sharp, stinging impact on your soles â€” that is **slamming**. Now imagine that instead of a soft human foot, the water strikes a steel plate at the bottom of a 200-meter ship moving at 20 knots. The kinetic energy of the ship is enormous, and when the bow re-enters the sea, that energy must be dissipated in a fraction of a second over a small contact area. The result is a violent pressure spike that the hull plating must withstand without buckling or rupturing. > [!NOTE] > **Three Critical Slamming Regions on a Ship Hull:** > 1. **Bottom Slamming** â€“ occurs on the flat of the bottom in head seas. > 2. **Bow Flare Slamming** â€“ on the flared sections of the bow above the waterline. > 3. **Stern Slamming** â€“ emerging phenomenon in modern fine-form vessels, particularly in following/quartering seas. --- ## 1.2 What is "Shipping of Green Seas"? **Shipping of Green Seas** (also called **green water loading** or **deck wetness**) refers to the phenomenon where a wave crest breaks over the bow or forecastle of a ship and a substantial volume of seawater accumulates on the exposed weather deck. This causes: - A sudden **vertical force** (weight of accumulated water) acting on the deck. - A **dynamic horizontal impact** as the water mass surges along the deck. - Potential **destabilizing free-surface effects** in the superstructure or holds. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Picture a small open boat riding into a beach wave. Just before the wave breaks, the bow rises; the next moment, tons of foaming water cascade over the gunwale and slosh around the deck. That is **shipping green water**. For a large ship, the volume of water shipped can be **hundreds of tons** arriving in **1â€“3 seconds**, producing a massive transient load on the deck plating, hatch covers, and any lashed cargo. > [!IMPORTANT] > **IMO SOLAS & Classification Society Context:** > The *International Maritime Organization (IMO)* and classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS) require minimum bow height, forecastle design, and deck drainage to mitigate green sea shipping. The probability of deck wetness in head seas is a key design parameter. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Magnitude | |---|---| | Slamming impact pressure (peak) | **0.5 to 3.0 MPa** | | Slamming pressure rise time | **0.01 to 0.1 s** | | Slamming contact area | **0.5 to 5 mآ²** | | Relative vertical velocity (slamming) | **5 to 15 m/s** | | Green water mass shipped | **50 to 500 tonnes** | | Green water loading duration | **1 to 3 s** | | Design return period (slamming) | **20 to 25 years** (North Atlantic) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics of Slamming When a ship section re-enters the water, the impact is modeled as a **wedge entry problem**. The classical solution was developed independently by **Von Karman (1929)** and **Wagner (1932)**. The key physics are: 1. **Kinetic Energy Dissipation**: The vertical kinetic energy of the hull section striking the water must be absorbed. 2. **Added Mass Effect**: As the hull pushes water aside, it accelerates a large mass of water upward â€” this is the **added mass** of the system. 3. **Pressure Spike**: The momentum transfer occurs over a very small wetted area at the instant of impact, leading to extremely high local pressures. 4. **Air Entrapment**: Trapped air cushions under the hull can briefly reduce peak pressure but increase its duration. ### Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic - **Step 1 â€“ Relative Velocity**: Compute the vertical velocity $V_z$ of the hull section relative to the wave surface. - **Step 2 â€“ Wetted Area Growth**: The wetted area $A(t)$ grows as the hull penetrates, typically as $A \propto t^2$ for a wedge. - **Step 3 â€“ Added Mass**: The added mass $m_a(t)$ grows with wetted area. - **Step 4 â€“ Momentum Equation**: Equate the rate of change of momentum to the impact force $F(t) = \dfrac{d}{dt}[m_a V_z]$. - **Step 5 â€“ Pressure Distribution**: Convert the force to pressure by dividing by the instantaneous wetted area. --- ## 2.2 Von Karmanâ€“Wagner Slamming Theory The classical analytical framework treats the ship section as a **2D wedge of deadrise angle $\beta$** entering calm water at vertical velocity $V$. The peak impact pressure for a wedge of deadrise angle $\beta$ and instantaneous wetted half-length $l$ is: $$ P_{peak} = \frac{\rho \cdot V^2}{2} \cdot \cot^2\beta $$ > [!NOTE] > **Theoretical Insight:** The cotangent-squared term shows why **flared bows** ($\beta \to 90آ°$) experience very high slamming pressures. Flat-bottomed hulls ($\beta \to 0آ°$) theoretically give infinite pressure â€” a non-physical result that explains the use of **slenderness corrections**. --- ## 2.3 Ochi's Slamming Pressure Formula (Most Cited in Practice) For practical design, **Ochi (1964)** developed a widely-used empirical-theoretical formula that has been adopted by classification societies. For bottom slamming in head seas: $$ P_{slam} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_s \cdot V_{rel}^2 $$ Where: - $\rho$ = **1025 kg/mآ³** (sea water density). - $C_s$ = slamming pressure coefficient (function of hull form, deadrise angle, and forward speed). - $V_{rel}$ = relative vertical velocity at impact point (m/s). A more refined form widely used in modern naval architecture: $$ P_{slam} = K \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot V_{rel}^2}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{\nabla}{A_w}\right)^{1/3} $$ Where: - $K$ = empirical slamming coefficient (typically **0.4 to 0.6** for bottom slamming, **0.7 to 1.2** for bow flare). - $\nabla$ = ship displacement volume. - $A_w$ = waterplane area. --- ## 2.4 Green Water Loading â€” Key Formulas ### 2.4.1 Probability of Deck Wetness (Second-Order Wave Theory) The probability that the water surface at the bow exceeds the deck edge height $h_d$ in irregular head seas: $$ P_{wet} = \exp\left(-\frac{h_d^2}{2 m_0}\right) $$ Where $m_0$ is the **zeroth spectral moment** of the wave elevation spectrum at the bow. ### 2.4.2 Green Water Force (Buoyancy-Type Loading) The vertical force on the deck due to shipped water is treated as a **buoyancy-like** distributed load on the flooded volume $V_f$: $$ F_{gw} = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_f \cdot \eta_{dyn} $$ Where: - $\eta_{dyn}$ = dynamic amplification factor (**1.5 to 2.5** to account for water in motion). - $V_f$ = volume of water shipped onto the deck (mآ³). ### 2.4.3 De Kat & Paulling Green Water Force Model For a stationary observer at the bow, the dynamic force from a passing wave crest: $$ F_{gw}(t) = \rho \cdot g \cdot B(t) \cdot \int_{0}^{L_f(t)} \eta(x,t) \, dx $$ Where $B(t)$ is the instantaneous beam of the shipped water column and $L_f$ is the flooded length. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{peak} = \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2} \cot^2\beta$ | Von Karman wedge slamming pressure | Pa | | 2 | $P_{slam} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_s V_{rel}^2$ | Ochi slamming pressure (bottom) | Pa | | 3 | $P_{slam} = K \cdot \dfrac{\rho V_{rel}^2}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\nabla}{A_w}\right)^{1/3}$ | Refined slamming pressure (full ship) | Pa | | 4 | $V_{rel} = V_{heave} + \omega \cdot \xi_a \cdot e^{kT_d}$ | Relative vertical velocity at bow | m/s | | 5 | $P_{wet} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{h_d^2}{2 m_0}\right)$ | Deck wetness probability (Rayleigh) | â€” | | 6 | $F_{gw} = \rho g V_f \eta_{dyn}$ | Green water vertical force | N | | 7 | $P_{wave} = \rho g H \cdot e^{-kT}$ | Local wave pressure at depth T | Pa | | 8 | $F_{slam,total} = P_{slam} \cdot A_{wetted}$ | Total slamming force on impact area | N | | 9 | $\sigma_{slam} = \dfrac{P_{slam} \cdot a^2}{c \cdot t^2}$ | Plate stress from slamming (Roark) | Pa | | 10 | $P_{slenderness} = P_{slam} \cdot \left[1 - e^{-kL/\beta}\right]$ | Slenderness correction (Wagner) | Pa | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always use $\rho = 1025 \, kg/m^3$ for sea water and $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ unless otherwise specified. Mark distribution in numerical problems typically requires **derivation (4 marks) + substitution (2 marks) + result with units (1 mark)**. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Classification Society Rules**: DNV GL, Lloyd's Register, ABS, and BV publish explicit slamming design pressures for different ship types (container ships, tankers, naval vessels) used to determine plate thickness and stiffener spacing. - **FEA Validation**: Modern hull slamming analysis uses CFD (e.g., STAR-CCM+, OpenFOAM) coupled with explicit FEA (LS-DYNA, ABAQUS) to predict transient structural response. - **Accident Investigation**: Major incidents such as the *MV Cougar Ace* (2006) and several bulker losses have been attributed to slamming-induced fatigue cracks at the bow shoulder. - **Military Applications**: Slamming loads are critical for the survivability of **planing hulls, SWATHs, and small fast craft** operating in high sea states. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Von Karmanâ€“Wagner Slamming Pressure We derive the peak slamming pressure for a 2D wedge of deadrise angle $\beta$ entering water with vertical velocity $V$. The wedge geometry is shown in the next section. ### Step 1 â€” Setup and Assumptions Consider a wedge of half-angle $\beta$ (the angle between the wedge centerline and the water surface at the instant of impact). Let the instantaneous wetted half-length be $l(t)$, and assume the wedge has unit length (perpendicular to the page). The water is incompressible and inviscid; the splash is neglected. ### Step 2 â€” Geometry of Wetted Region At time $t$ after first contact, the vertex of the wedge has penetrated a depth: $$ h(t) = V \cdot t $$ The wetted half-length on one side of the centerline is: $$ l(t) = \frac{h(t)}{\tan\beta} = \frac{V t}{\tan\beta} $$ ### Step 3 â€” Added Mass of the Wedge For a wedge entering water, the added mass per unit span is proportional to $\rho l^2$. From potential flow theory, the added mass coefficient for a wedge is $\pi / (2 + \pi/\beta)$ approximately, but for a slender approximation (which gives the cleanest result and is classical): $$ m_a(t) = \frac{\pi}{2} \rho l^2(t) $$ ### Step 4 â€” Rate of Change of Added Mass Differentiate $m_a$ with respect to time: $$ \frac{dm_a}{dt} = \frac{\pi}{2} \rho \cdot 2 l \cdot \frac{dl}{dt} = \pi \rho l \cdot \frac{dl}{dt} $$ Since $\dfrac{dl}{dt} = \dfrac{V}{\tan\beta}$: $$ \frac{dm_a}{dt} = \pi \rho l \cdot \frac{V}{\tan\beta} $$ Substituting $l = Vt/\tan\beta$: $$ \frac{dm_a}{dt} = \pi \rho \cdot \frac{V t}{\tan\beta} \cdot \frac{V}{\tan\beta} = \frac{\pi \rho V^2 t}{\tan^2\beta} $$ ### Step 5 â€” Momentum Equation The rate of change of vertical momentum equals the net vertical force. The vertical momentum carried by the water per unit span is $m_a V$. So: $$ F(t) = \frac{d}{dt}[m_a V] = V \cdot \frac{dm_a}{dt} = \frac{\pi \rho V^3 t}{\tan^2\beta} $$ ### Step 6 â€” Convert Force to Pressure The wetted area per unit span at time $t$ is $l(t) = Vt/\tan\beta$. So the average pressure is: $$ P(t) = \frac{F(t)}{l(t)} = \frac{\pi \rho V^3 t / \tan^2\beta}{Vt/\tan\beta} = \frac{\pi \rho V^2}{\tan\beta} $$ ### Step 7 â€” Peak Pressure (Von Karman Result) The Von Karman classical result (accounting for the actual wedge coefficient rather than the slender approximation) is: $$ P_{peak} = \frac{\pi \rho V^2}{2 \tan\beta} \cdot \cot\beta \quad \text{(after refinement)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; P_{peak} = \frac{\rho V^2}{2} \cot^2 \beta $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Von Karmanâ€“Wagner Result:** > > $$ > \boxed{P_{peak} = \frac{\rho V^2}{2} \cot^2 \beta} > $$ This is the **most-cited classical slamming formula** and is regularly asked in KTU examinations as a 7-mark derivation. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A ship with a bow flare of $\beta = 30آ°$ deadrise strikes a wave crest with a relative vertical velocity of $V_{rel} = 8 \, m/s$. Compute: (a) The peak slamming pressure using the Von Karman formula. (b) The total slamming force on a contact area of $A = 2 \, m^2$. (c) The required minimum plate thickness for grade-A steel ($\sigma_y = 235 \, MPa$) using the simplified plate-bending formula $\sigma = \dfrac{6 M}{t^2}$ with $M = P \cdot L^2 / 10$, $L = 0.6 \, m$. ### Part (a) â€” Peak Slamming Pressure **Step 1:** Identify constants. - $\rho = 1025 \, kg/m^3$ - $V = 8 \, m/s$ - $\beta = 30آ° \Rightarrow \cot\beta = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the Von Karman formula. $$ P_{peak} = \frac{1025 \times (8)^2}{2} \times (1.732)^2 $$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the dynamic pressure term. $$ \frac{1025 \times 64}{2} = 32800 \, Pa = 32.8 \, kPa $$ **Step 4:** Evaluate the cotangent term. $$ (1.732)^2 = 3.0 $$ **Step 5:** Combine. $$ P_{peak} = 32800 \times 3.0 = 98400 \, Pa \approx 98.4 \, kPa $$ > **Result:** $P_{peak} \approx 98.4 \, kPa$ ### Part (b) â€” Total Slamming Force $$ F_{slam} = P_{peak} \cdot A = 98400 \times 2 = 196800 \, N $$ > **Result:** $F_{slam} \approx 196.8 \, kN$ ### Part (c) â€” Required Plate Thickness **Step 1:** Compute the bending moment. $$ M = \frac{P \cdot L^2}{10} = \frac{98400 \times (0.6)^2}{10} = \frac{98400 \times 0.36}{10} = 3542.4 \, N\cdot m $$ **Step 2:** Apply the plate-bending stress formula. $$ \sigma = \frac{6M}{t^2} \Rightarrow t^2 = \frac{6M}{\sigma_y} = \frac{6 \times 3542.4}{235 \times 10^6} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate. $$ t^2 = \frac{21254.4}{235 \times 10^6} = 9.043 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2 $$ **Step 4:** Take the square root. $$ t = \sqrt{9.043 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.51 \times 10^{-3} \, m = 9.51 \, mm $$ > **Result:** $t_{min} \approx 9.5 \, mm$ â€” round up to the next available standard thickness (e.g., **10 mm** in grade-A hull plate). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix â€” Comparison of Slamming Models | Aspect | Von Karmanâ€“Wagner | Ochi Formula | CFD/FEA (Modern) | |---|---|---|---| | Geometry | 2D wedge | 3D hull form | Full 3D geometry | | Peak Pressure | $\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \cot^2\beta$ | $\frac{1}{2}\rho C_s V^2$ | Spatially resolved | | Empirical? | No (analytical) | Semi-empirical | Numerical | | Includes air cushion? | No | Partially (via $C_s$) | Yes | | Computational cost | Negligible | Low | High (hours to days) | | KTU exam relevance | **Highest (full derivation)** | High (numerical) | Low (theory only) | | Validation against model tests | Fair | Good | Excellent | --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Green Water Probability For a Gaussian sea with wave elevation $\zeta(x,t)$ having zero mean and variance $m_0$, the probability that $\zeta$ exceeds a threshold $h_d$ (the bow freeboard) is the **Rayleigh exceedance probability**: $$ P(\zeta > h_d) = \int_{h_d}^{\infty} \frac{\zeta}{m_0} \exp\left(-\frac{\zeta^2}{2 m_0}\right) d\zeta $$ Evaluating this integral gives: $$ \boxed{P_{wet} = \exp\left(-\frac{h_d^2}{2 m_0}\right)} $$ This is the **fundamental green-sea probability formula** in KTU Module 2. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Wedge Entry Geometry (Slamming) The following Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional architecture** of the wedge slamming system, since a literal 2D physics sketch is not directly renderable. ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Ship Hull Section
2D Wedge Model] --> B1[Deadrise Angle beta] A1 --> C1[Vertical Velocity V at Impact] B1 --> D1[Geometry Block
l of t equal Vt over tan beta] C1 --> D1 D1 --> E1[Added Mass m_a of t
pi rho l squared over 2] E1 --> F1[Momentum Equation
F of t equal V dm_a by dt] F1 --> G1[Force per unit span
pi rho V cubed t over tan squared beta] G1 --> H1[Average Pressure
P of t equal F over l] H1 --> I1[Peak Pressure Result
P_peak = 0.5 rho V squared cot squared beta] I1 --> J1[Output to
Plate Thickness Calculation] J1 --> K1[Slamming Force on Wetted Area
F_slam = P_peak times A] K1 --> L1[Structural Scantlings
Local Hull Plate Design] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The flow shows how a **physical geometry (wedge with deadrise خ²)** and a **kinematic input (vertical velocity V)** propagate through a chain of analytical models (added mass â†’ momentum â†’ pressure) to yield the **design output (plate thickness)**. Each block corresponds to one stage of the KTU derivation in Section 3.1. --- ## 4.2 Green Sea Shipping â€” Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A2[Wave Climate
Sea State Hs, Tp] --> B2[Wave Spectrum
Pierson Moskowitz or JONSWAP] B2 --> C2[Ship Response
RAO in Heave and Pitch] C2 --> D2[Relative Motion
at Bow Location] D2 --> E2{Does Bow Elevation
Exceed Freeboard?} E2 -->|Yes| F2[Deck Wetness Event
P_wet = exp minus h_d squared over 2 m_0] E2 -->|No| G2[No Green Water
Safe State] F2 --> H2[Water Volume Shipped
V_f of t on Deck] H2 --> I2[Dynamic Amplification
eta_dyn 1.5 to 2.5] I2 --> J2[Vertical Force on Deck
F_gw = rho g V_f eta_dyn] J2 --> K2[Horizontal Surge Force
on Lashed Cargo] J2 --> L2[Structural Design Loads
for Hatch Covers and Deck Plating] K2 --> L2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpreting the diagram:** Green-sea loading is fundamentally a **probabilistic chain**. The flow starts from a **sea state** and ends at **structural design loads**. The decision diamond (block E2) is the critical probabilistic gate, governed by the Rayleigh exceedance formula derived in Section 3.4. --- ## 4.3 Slamming Region Map on Ship Hull ```mermaid flowchart LR A3[Bow Flare Region
beta near 60 to 80 deg] -->|High pressure
P proportional to cot squared beta| B3[Slamming Zone 1
Bow Shoulder] B3 --> C3[Forward Bottom
beta near 10 to 30 deg] C3 -->|Lower pressure
but larger area| D3[Slamming Zone 2
Flat of Bottom] D3 --> E3[Midship
Rarely slams] E3 --> F3[Stern Region
beta near 20 to 40 deg] F3 -->|Quartering seas| G3[Slamming Zone 3
Stern Flare] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Bow vs. Stern Slamming:** With modern fine-form hulls (e.g., container ships, frigates), stern slamming in following/quartering seas has become a critical design case. The 2002 IACS S21A unified requirement for container ships was a direct response to observed stern slamming damage. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A â€” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam â€“ June 2023, Set B]` **Define the term "slamming" in ship structural design. List any three regions of a ship hull where slamming is most critical.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Slamming is a transient, impulsive hydrodynamic load that occurs when a portion of a ship's hull (which has emerged into air due to wave-induced ship motions) re-enters the water surface with a significant relative vertical velocity. The impact generates very high local pressures over a small wetted area for a short duration (0.01â€“0.1 s). > > **Three critical regions (1.5 marks):** > 1. **Bow flare** â€” flared sections of the bow above the waterline. > 2. **Forward bottom (flat of bottom)** â€” in head seas at high speed. > 3. **Stern region** â€” in following or quartering seas, especially in fine-form vessels. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam â€“ Dec 2022, Model Paper]` **Explain the term "shipping of green seas" and state its consequences on ship structural integrity.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Shipping of green seas refers to the phenomenon in which a wave crest breaks over the bow (or forecastle) of a vessel and a substantial volume of seawater is shipped onto the exposed weather deck. The water can accumulate to depths of **1â€“2 m** on the deck in extreme cases. > > **Consequences on structural integrity (1.5 marks):** > 1. Sudden vertical and horizontal **dynamic loading** on deck plating, hatch covers, and lashing equipment. > 2. **Local buckling** of deck plates and stiffeners. > 3. Damage to **deck fittings, vents, and freeing ports**; potential progressive flooding. > 4. **Loss of stability** due to free-surface water effects and a high vertical centre of gravity. --- ## 5.2 Part B â€” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam â€“ Dec 2023, Module 2, Set A]`** **(a)** With neat sketches, derive an expression for the **peak slamming pressure** on a 2D wedge entering calm water with vertical velocity $V$, using the Von Karmanâ€“Wagner theory. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 250 m LOA container ship (displacement $\nabla = 45,000 \, m^3$, waterplane area $A_w = 2,800 \, m^2$) encounters bottom slamming at a relative vertical velocity of $V_{rel} = 6.5 \, m/s$. Using the **Ochi formula** with slamming coefficient $K = 0.5$, calculate: (i) The peak slamming pressure. **(3 marks)** (ii) The total slamming force on an impact area of $A = 3 \, m^2$. **(2 marks)** (iii) Comment on how the bow-flare coefficient $K$ changes the design pressure for the same ship in bow-flare slamming. **(2 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution A** #### Part (a) â€” Derivation (7 marks) **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. The hull section is modelled as a 2D wedge of deadrise angle $\beta$. 2. Water is incompressible, inviscid, and initially at rest. 3. Flow is irrotational (potential flow). 4. Air entrapment and splash are neglected. 5. The wedge enters with constant vertical velocity $V$ (gravity is neglected during the brief impact). 6. Per-unit-span analysis (2D). **Geometry of the wetted region (1 mark):** At time $t$ after first contact, the wedge tip has penetrated $h(t) = Vt$. The wetted half-length per side is: $$ l(t) = \frac{Vt}{\tan\beta} $$ **Added mass of the wedge (1.5 marks):** By potential flow theory for a 2D wedge, the added mass per unit span is: $$ m_a(t) = \frac{\pi}{2} \rho l^2(t) = \frac{\pi}{2} \rho \left(\frac{Vt}{\tan\beta}\right)^2 $$ **Momentum equation (1.5 marks):** The vertical force on the wedge equals the rate of change of vertical momentum of the surrounding water: $$ F(t) = V \cdot \frac{dm_a}{dt} = V \cdot \pi \rho l \cdot \frac{dl}{dt} = V \cdot \pi \rho \cdot \frac{Vt}{\tan\beta} \cdot \frac{V}{\tan\beta} $$ $$ F(t) = \frac{\pi \rho V^3 t}{\tan^2 \beta} $$ **Pressure conversion and peak result (2 marks):** The wetted area per unit span at time $t$ is $l(t) = Vt/\tan\beta$. Therefore the average pressure is: $$ P(t) = \frac{F(t)}{l(t)} = \frac{\pi \rho V^2}{\tan\beta} $$ For a finite wedge (Wagner's correction, accounting for the pile-up of water at the chine), the **peak pressure** becomes: $$ \boxed{P_{peak} = \frac{\rho V^2}{2} \cot^2 \beta} $$ This is the Von Karmanâ€“Wagner result. Peak pressure grows with **$V^2$** and **$\cot^2 \beta$**, which is why flat bottoms ($\beta \to 0$) and highly flared bows ($\beta \to 90آ°$) both experience extreme pressures. --- #### Part (b) â€” Numerical (7 marks) **(i) Peak slamming pressure (3 marks):** The Ochi formula in the refined form: $$ P_{slam} = K \cdot \frac{\rho V_{rel}^2}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{\nabla}{A_w}\right)^{1/3} $$ Substitute values: - $K = 0.5$ - $\rho = 1025 \, kg/m^3$ - $V_{rel} = 6.5 \, m/s$ - $\nabla = 45,000 \, m^3$ - $A_w = 2,800 \, m^2$ **Step 1:** Compute $\frac{\rho V^2}{2}$. $$ \frac{1025 \times 6.5^2}{2} = \frac{1025 \times 42.25}{2} = 21,653.1 \, Pa $$ **Step 2:** Compute $\left(\frac{\nabla}{A_w}\right)^{1/3}$. $$ \frac{\nabla}{A_w} = \frac{45,000}{2,800} = 16.07 \, m $$ $$ (16.07)^{1/3} \approx 2.52 $$ **Step 3:** Combine. $$ P_{slam} = 0.5 \times 21,653.1 \times 2.52 = 27,283 \, Pa \approx 27.3 \, kPa $$ > **Result:** $P_{slam} \approx 27.3 \, kPa$ for **bottom slamming** with $K = 0.5$. --- **(ii) Total slamming force (2 marks):** $$ F_{slam} = P_{slam} \times A = 27,283 \times 3 = 81,849 \, N \approx 81.85 \, kN $$ > **Result:** $F_{slam} \approx 81.85 \, kN$ --- **(iii) Effect of bow-flare coefficient (2 marks):** For **bow-flare slamming**, the empirical coefficient $K$ is typically in the range **0.7 to 1.2**, which is **1.4 to 2.4 times** the bottom-slamming value of 0.5. Thus the same ship, under bow-flare slamming, would experience a peak pressure of approximately: $$ P_{flare} = (0.7 \text{ to } 1.2) \times \frac{\rho V^2}{2} \times \left(\frac{\nabla}{A_w}\right)^{1/3} \approx 38.2 \text{ to } 65.5 \, kPa $$ The **forward regions (bow flare and bow shoulder)** therefore govern the design of the bow structural scantlings, particularly the plating thickness, because they are subject to the highest localized impact pressures. The forepeak structure of a container ship is sized primarily for bow-flare slamming, not bottom slamming. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) â€” Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam â€“ Dec 2023, Module 2, Set B]`** **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **shipping of green seas**. Derive the **probability of deck wetness** for a ship operating in irregular head seas, clearly defining all variables. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A naval frigate of length $L = 120 \, m$ and bow freeboard $h_d = 6.5 \, m$ operates in a sea state with significant wave height $H_s = 4 \, m$ and zero-upcrossing period $T_z = 8 \, s$. Using the **Piersonâ€“Moskowitz spectrum**, compute: (i) The zeroth spectral moment $m_0$ of the wave elevation. **(2 marks)** (ii) The probability of deck wetness in any given wave cycle. **(2 marks)** (iii) The expected number of wetness events per hour. **(3 marks)** **Given:** $m_0 = \dfrac{H_s^2}{16}$ for a narrow-band PM spectrum, and the wave encounter rate at the bow is approximately $\mu = \dfrac{1}{T_z}$ per second. --- ### **Model Solution B** #### Part (a) â€” Theory and Derivation (7 marks) **Phenomenon (2 marks):** Shipping of green seas (green water loading) is the phenomenon in which a breaking or near-breaking wave crest overtops the bulwark or bow rail of a ship and a substantial volume of seawater runs onto the exposed weather deck. It occurs primarily in **head or bow seas** when the ship is in following or head-on wave conditions at moderate to high sea states. The phenomenon is governed by: - The **relative vertical motion** between the bow and the instantaneous wave surface. - The **bow freeboard** $h_d$. - The **sea state** (spectral wave energy). - The **ship's speed and heading**. **Derivation of deck-wetness probability (5 marks):** **Step 1:** Assume the wave elevation $\zeta(t)$ at the bow is a stationary Gaussian random process with zero mean and variance $m_0 = E[\zeta^2]$. **Step 2:** The probability density function of wave crest heights (Rayleigh distribution) is: $$ f(\zeta) = \frac{\zeta}{m_0} \exp\left(-\frac{\zeta^2}{2 m_0}\right), \quad \zeta \geq 0 $$ **Step 3:** Deck wetness occurs whenever the wave elevation at the bow exceeds the bow freeboard $h_d$: $$ P_{wet} = P(\zeta > h_d) = \int_{h_d}^{\infty} f(\zeta) \, d\zeta $$ **Step 4:** Substitute and integrate. $$ P_{wet} = \int_{h_d}^{\infty} \frac{\zeta}{m_0} \exp\left(-\frac{\zeta^2}{2 m_0}\right) d\zeta $$ Let $u = \zeta^2/(2m_0)$, $du = \zeta \, d\zeta / m_0$: $$ P_{wet} = \int_{h_d^2/(2m_0)}^{\infty} e^{-u} du = \left[-e^{-u}\right]_{h_d^2/(2m_0)}^{\infty} = \exp\left(-\frac{h_d^2}{2 m_0}\right) $$ **Final result:** $$ \boxed{P_{wet} = \exp\left(-\frac{h_d^2}{2 m_0}\right)} $$ This is the **Rayleigh exceedance probability** and is the standard green-water probability used in KTU Module 2 derivations. --- #### Part (b) â€” Numerical (7 marks) **(i) Zeroth spectral moment (2 marks):** For a narrow-band Piersonâ€“Moskowitz spectrum: $$ m_0 = \frac{H_s^2}{16} = \frac{(4)^2}{16} = \frac{16}{16} = 1.0 \, m^2 $$ > **Result:** $m_0 = 1.0 \, m^2$ --- **(ii) Deck-wetness probability (2 marks):** $$ P_{wet} = \exp\left(-\frac{h_d^2}{2 m_0}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{(6.5)^2}{2 \times 1.0}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{42.25}{2}\right) = \exp(-21.125) $$ $$ P_{wet} = e^{-21.125} \approx 6.6 \times 10^{-10} $$ > **Result:** $P_{wet} \approx 6.6 \times 10^{-10}$ (very low â€” frigate bow freeboard of 6.5 m in a 4 m sea state is well-protected) --- **(iii) Expected wetness events per hour (3 marks):** The wave encounter rate at the bow is: $$ \mu = \frac{1}{T_z} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125 \, \text{waves/s} $$ The expected number of wetness events per second is: $$ \lambda = \mu \cdot P_{wet} = 0.125 \times 6.6 \times 10^{-10} = 8.25 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{events/s} $$ Per hour: $$ N = \lambda \times 3600 = 8.25 \times 10^{-11} \times 3600 = 2.97 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{events/h} $$ > **Result:** Approximately **3 أ— 10âپ»âپ· events per hour**, i.e., a green-water event is expected roughly **once every 3,800 years** of continuous operation in this sea state. This confirms that the design bow freeboard is adequate for a $H_s = 4$ m sea state. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Slamming & Green-Sea Questions:** > > 1. **Wrong density:** Students often use $\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$ (fresh water) instead of $1025 \, kg/m^3$ (sea water). This is an automatic **1-mark deduction** in numerical problems. > > 2. **Confusing $V$ and $V_{rel}$:** The relative vertical velocity at the bow is **NOT** the same as the heave velocity; it includes the wave vertical velocity at the encounter point. The correct expression is $V_{rel} = \dot{z}_{heave} - \dot{\zeta}_{encounter}$. > > 3. **Forgetting units in final answer:** A numerical answer without units gets **0.5 mark deducted** per KTU valuation norms. > > 4. **Mixing up bow-flare and bottom slamming coefficients:** Using $K = 0.5$ for bow flare (should be 0.7â€“1.2) or vice versa. This is a **2-mark error** in design questions. > > 5. **Skipping assumptions in derivations:** The Von Karmanâ€“Wagner derivation requires explicit assumptions (2D, inviscid, irrotational, etc.). Omitting them costs up to **1.5 marks**. > > 6. **Wrong cot vs tan:** The classical formula has $\cot^2 \beta$ (cotangent), **NOT** $\tan^2 \beta$. Forgetting this inversion is a frequent error. > > 7. **In green-water problems:** Students often forget that $m_0$ is the **variance of wave elevation**, not the significant wave height squared. Use $m_0 = H_s^2/16$ for PM spectrum. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist â€” Module 2: Slamming & Green Seas** - [x] **Slamming Definition:** Impulsive, transient hydrodynamic load when a hull section re-enters water at high relative velocity. - [x] **Three Critical Slamming Regions:** Bow flare, forward bottom, stern (in following seas). - [x] **Von Karmanâ€“Wagner Peak Pressure:** $P_{peak} = \frac{\rho V^2}{2} \cot^2 \beta$ â€” most important formula of the module. - [x] **Ochi Formula:** $P_{slam} = K \cdot \frac{\rho V_{rel}^2}{2} \cdot (\nabla/A_w)^{1/3}$ â€” practical for design. - [x] **Slamming Coefficient $K$:** 0.4â€“0.6 (bottom), 0.7â€“1.2 (bow flare). - [x] **Sea Water Density:** Always use $\rho = 1025 \, kg/m^3$ unless stated. - [x] **Green Seas Definition:** Sea water shipped onto the weather deck. - [x] **Deck-Wetness Probability:** $P_{wet} = \exp(-h_d^2 / 2m_0)$ â€” Rayleigh exceedance. - [x] **Green-Water Vertical Force:** $F_{gw} = \rho g V_f \eta_{dyn}$, with $\eta_{dyn} = 1.5$â€“$2.5$. - [x] **Dynamic Amplification:** Always include $\eta_{dyn}$ in green-water force calculations. - [x] **Design Return Period:** 20â€“25 years (North Atlantic) for slamming scantlings. - [x] **Plate-Thickness Rule of Thumb:** Bottom slamming typically requires **1.2أ— to 1.5أ—** the still-water plate thickness. - [x] **Stern Slamming in Fine-Form Ships:** Critical for container ships and naval combatants; IACS S21A addresses this. - [x] **Pressure-Spikes Are Local:** Slamming affects local scantlings (plating and stiffeners), not the primary hull girder. - [x] **CFD/FEA Validation:** Modern practice uses explicit FEA (LS-DYNA) coupled with CFD for slamming analysis. > **End of Topic Notes â€” Module 2: Effect of Slamming and Shipping of Green Seas**

topics.title: Torsional Loads Due to Oblique Waves and Cargo Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Torsional Loads Due to Oblique Waves and Cargo Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 â€” Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Torsional loading** in a ship is defined as the twisting moment (torque) acting about the ship's longitudinal axis (the x-axis or roll axis), which arises from **non-symmetric transverse loading** across the beam of the vessel. In the KTU 2024 framework, this topic is studied under two principal causal categories:

1. **Hydrodynamic Torsion** â€” caused by **oblique (quartering or following) waves** that generate asymmetric pressure distributions on the port and starboard sides of the hull.
2. **Static / Quasi-Static Torsion** â€” caused by **asymmetric cargo, ballast, or weight distribution** that shifts the ship's center of gravity off the centerline, generating a heeling moment equivalent to a torque.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** *Torsional moment in a ship hull girder is the integral of the product of transverse pressure difference (or transverse force offset) and its lever arm about the ship's centerline plane, integrated along the length of the vessel.*

The torsional moment about the longitudinal axis at any section x is given by the general expression:

$$T(x) = \int_{section} \left[ p_P(y,z) - p_S(y,z) \right] \cdot y \, dA$$

Where $p_P$ and $p_S$ are the local hydrostatic/hydrodynamic pressures on the port and starboard sides, and y is the transverse coordinate measured from the centerline plane.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy â€” The Twisted Beam Intuition

Imagine a **long, thin rectangular cardboard box** floating on a pond. If a wave comes directly from the side (beam seas), the wave pushes equally on both port and starboard sides, simply **tilting** (heeling) the box without twisting it â€” the box is treated as a rigid body rotating about its long axis, with no internal torque beyond hydrostatic reaction.

Now consider a **wave hitting the box at 45آ° (oblique seas)**. One corner of the box is hit harder by the wave crest while the opposite corner is in a trough. The box is no longer simply tilting â€” it is being **physically wrung out** like a wet towel. The two ends (port bow/stern quarter and starboard bow/stern quarter) experience different pressures. This differential loading, multiplied by the lever arm across the beam, creates a **torque** that tries to twist the box about its long axis. This is exactly what happens to a ship hull girder in oblique seas.

For the **cargo distribution** aspect, picture a **delivery truck** loaded with pallets all stacked on one side. The truck is being **twisted** by the weight imbalance â€” the suspension on the heavy side compresses, and the chassis experiences a permanent torsional stress even when standing still. The same effect happens in a ship: an off-center weight distribution creates a static torque, equivalent to applying a heeling moment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this KTU-high-yield?** Ship structural design in head and beam seas is governed by **vertical and horizontal bending moments**. In oblique seas, **torsional moments** become critical because they interact with bending to create **combined stresses** that often govern scantling design near amidships and in way of the engine room. Modern container ships and naval vessels with wide beams and fine forms are particularly susceptible.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and units are central to KTU 2024 board problems on this topic:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ship length between perpendiculars | $L_{BP}$ | 100 â€“ 400 | m |
| Ship breadth (moulded) | $B$ | 15 â€“ 60 | m |
| Ship draft (design) | $T$ | 6 â€“ 20 | m |
| Block coefficient | $C_B$ | 0.55 â€“ 0.85 | dimensionless |
| Wave heading angle | $\beta$ | 0آ° â€“ 180آ° | degrees |
| Density of sea water | $\rho$ | **1.025** | t/mآ³ |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/sآ² |
| Allowable shear stress (mild steel) | $\tau_{allow}$ | 100 â€“ 120 | N/mmآ² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** In the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination), students are expected to use $\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless explicitly specified. Off-by-one errors in unit conversion (e.g., kN vs. N) are a frequent cause of mark loss.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 â€” Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Oblique Waves Cause Torsion â€” The Physical Mechanism

When a ship navigates through waves that approach from a direction **other than head-on (خ² = 0آ°)** or **beam-on (خ² = 90آ°)**, the wave crests are not parallel to the ship's centerline. This creates a **phase difference** in wave elevation along the ship's length and a **differential pressure** between the port and starboard sides.

The key consequences are:

1. **Asymmetric wave elevation**: At a given instant, the port bow may be in a wave crest while the starboard quarter is in a trough, and vice versa. The instantaneous water surface relative to the ship varies across the beam.
2. **Asymmetric added pressure**: The dynamic pressure (added mass أ— acceleration) also varies across the beam, with higher values on the side facing the wave.
3. **Vertical center of gravity shifts dynamically**: The combined effect of asymmetric wave elevation and pressure creates a moment that is mathematically equivalent to a torque about the longitudinal axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The most critical oblique wave angle for torsional loading is typically **خ² â‰ˆ 30آ° to 60آ°** (quartering seas). At خ² = 0آ° (head seas) and خ² = 90آ° (beam seas), the loading is symmetric across the centerline, and torsional moment is theoretically zero. At خ² = 180آ° (following seas), symmetry is also restored.

## 2.2 Theory of Oblique Wave Encounter â€” Apparent Wave Slopes

The **encounter frequency** between a ship of speed V and a wave of frequency د‰ approaching at angle خ² is:

$$\omega_e = \omega - k V \cos\beta = \omega \left( 1 - \frac{V \omega \cos\beta}{g} \right)$$

Using the dispersion relation $k = \omega^2 / g$, we get:

$$\omega_e = \omega \left( 1 - \frac{\omega V \cos\beta}{g} \right)$$

The **apparent wave slope** in the oblique direction is amplified by the encounter effect. For a wave of slope خ± (where خ± = H/خ» أ— constant depending on theory), the apparent slope seen by the ship is:

$$\tan\alpha_e = \frac{\sin\beta}{\cos\beta - \dfrac{V}{V_c}} \cdot \tan\alpha$$

Where $V_c = g / \omega$ is the **wave celerity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $V\cos\beta \to V_c$ (the ship speed component along the wave direction approaches the wave speed), the denominator approaches zero, and the apparent slope becomes infinite. This is the **wave-blocking phenomenon** and is of great importance in KTU problems on ship resistance in oblique seas.

## 2.3 Hydrodynamic Pressure Distribution â€” Lewis Form and Strip Theory

The transverse pressure distribution on a 2D ship section (treated as a Lewis form) subjected to an oblique wave can be modeled using the **strip theory** combined with a **2D Lewis form approximation**. The pressure at a transverse coordinate y is:

$$p(y) = p_0 \left(1 - \frac{y^2}{B^2}\right) \cdot e^{-kT}$$

Where:
- $p_0$ = pressure at the centerline (depends on wave height, slope, and ship motion)
- $B$ = half-beam at the waterline
- $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ = wave number
- $T$ = ship draft

For **oblique waves**, the pressure distribution is rotated by angle خ². The effective transverse pressure asymmetry across the beam is then:

$$\Delta p(x, y) = p(x, y, \beta) - p(x, -y, \beta)$$

This asymmetry is what generates the torque.

## 2.4 Torsional Moment Calculation â€” The Integrated Approach

The **torsional moment per unit length** at station x due to the asymmetric pressure is:

$$t(x) = \int_{-B(x)/2}^{B(x)/2} \Delta p(x, y) \cdot y \, dy$$

And the **total torsional moment** at any longitudinal station is obtained by integrating along the length:

$$T(x) = \int_0^L t(\xi) \, d\xi$$

In practice, KTU 2024 board problems simplify this by using:
- A **uniform pressure distribution** (rectangular approximation)
- A **triangular distribution** (for finer approximation)
- The **effective lever arm** approach

For the **simplified rectangular distribution**:

$$T_{wave} = p_{eff} \cdot B \cdot L \cdot e_{eff}$$

Where $e_{eff}$ is the effective eccentricity of the resultant pressure force from the centerline.

## 2.5 Cargo-Induced Torsion â€” Static Loading Theory

When cargo (or ballast) is loaded asymmetrically, the **center of gravity (G)** shifts transversely by a distance $\text{TCG}$ (Transverse Center of Gravity) from the ship's centerline. The resulting **static heeling moment** is:

$$M_{heel} = W \cdot \text{TCG}$$

This moment is **equivalently** a torsional moment about the roll axis:

$$T_{cargo} = W \cdot \text{TCG}$$

For a ship with multiple cargo holds, the total cargo-induced torque is:

$$T_{cargo} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot y_i$$

Where $W_i$ is the weight of cargo in hold $i$ and $y_i$ is the transverse offset of its center of gravity from the ship's centerline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget that the **effective lever arm** for cargo-induced torsion is the **transverse offset of the center of gravity**, not the offset of the cargo itself, because the moment is taken about the ship's centerline (which passes through the keel). The KTU board expects the use of TCG (Transverse Center of Gravity) explicitly.

## 2.6 Combined Loading â€” Wave + Cargo

The **total torsional moment** acting on a ship in oblique seas with asymmetric cargo is:

$$T_{total} = T_{wave} + T_{cargo}$$

This must be combined with the vertical and horizontal bending moments to compute the **combined stress** in the hull girder:

$$\sigma_{combined} = \frac{M_V \cdot z}{I_V} + \frac{M_H \cdot y}{I_H} \pm \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$

Where:
- $M_V$ = vertical bending moment
- $M_H$ = horizontal bending moment
- $I_V, I_H$ = moments of inertia about horizontal and vertical axes
- $T$ = torsional moment
- $r$ = distance from shear center
- $J$ = torsional constant (polar moment of inertia for thin-walled closed section)

For thin-walled closed sections (like a ship's hull girder with deck), **Bredt's formula** applies:

$$\tau = \frac{T}{2 A_m t}$$

Where $A_m$ is the **mean enclosed area** and $t$ is the local wall thickness.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this table as a rapid revision guide. CRITICAL: All absolute value or norm expressions use `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.**

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Encounter frequency | $\omega_e = \omega \left( 1 - \frac{\omega V \cos\beta}{g} \right)$ | Frequency at which ship meets waves |
| Apparent wave slope | $\tan\alpha_e = \frac{\sin\beta}{\cos\beta - V/V_c} \cdot \tan\alpha$ | Effective slope in oblique encounter |
| Lewis pressure | $p(y) = p_0 \left(1 - \frac{y^2}{B^2}\right) e^{-kT}$ | Transverse pressure on 2D section |
| Torsional moment per unit length | $t(x) = \int_{-B/2}^{B/2} \Delta p(x,y) \cdot y \, dy$ | Local torque per unit length |
| Total torsional moment | $T(x) = \int_0^L t(\xi) \, d\xi$ | Integrated torque along hull |
| Simplified wave torque | $T_{wave} = p_{eff} \cdot B \cdot L \cdot e_{eff}$ | Board-friendly rectangular approximation |
| Cargo torque | $T_{cargo} = \sum W_i y_i$ | Sum of weight أ— transverse offset |
| Total torque | $T_{total} = T_{wave} + T_{cargo}$ | Combined loading |
| Bredt's shear stress | $\tau = \frac{\vert T \vert}{2 A_m t}$ | Torsional shear in closed section |
| Combined stress | $\sigma = \frac{M_V z}{I_V} \pm \frac{M_H y}{I_H} \pm \frac{T r}{J}$ | Total normal stress |
| Polar moment (thin rect) | $J = \frac{1}{3} \sum b_i t_i^3$ | For multi-cell thin-walled sections |
| Free surface correction | $\text{TCG}_{FS} = \frac{\rho A_{FS} \cdot \bar{y}}{W}$ | Effect of slack tanks on TCG |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Container Ships**: Highly susceptible to torsional failure in oblique seas due to wide beam and asymmetric stack weights. The 1990s saw several major structural failures attributed to combined wave + cargo torsion.
- **Naval Frigates and Destroyers**: Fine-form vessels operating at high speed in quartering seas experience peak torsional moments near the engine room and sonar dome.
- **LNG Carriers**: The free-surface effect in partially filled cargo tanks amplifies effective TCG and hence torsion.
- **Ro-Ro and Car Carriers**: Asymmetric loading from drive-through operations creates severe static torsion.
- **Offshore Vessels (PSV, AHTS)**: Asymmetric deck cargo and dynamic positioning operations generate continuous torsional cycling.

# SECTION 3 â€” Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive, step-by-step mathematical derivations** required for KTU 2024 board examination problems. No step is omitted.

## 3.1 Derivation I â€” Torsional Moment from Asymmetric Pressure Distribution

**Statement:** *Derive an expression for the torsional moment per unit length at a transverse section of a ship in oblique waves, assuming a Lewis-form pressure distribution.*

### Step 1 â€” Set up the coordinate system

Take the ship's centerline as the x-axis (longitudinal), the transverse direction as y, and the vertical (upward) as z. The origin is at midships on the centerline plane. The half-beam at the waterline at station x is $B(x)/2$.

### Step 2 â€” Define the Lewis form pressure

For a Lewis section (sharp-edged, prismatic approximation), the static + dynamic wave pressure is parabolic across the beam:

$$p(y) = p_0 \left( 1 - \frac{y^2}{B^2/4} \right)$$

Note: Some authors use $B$ as half-beam; here we use $B$ as full beam, and the denominator is $(B/2)^2 = B^2/4$.

### Step 3 â€” Introduce obliquity

When waves approach at angle خ², the wave crest line makes angle خ² with the ship's centerline. The pressure at transverse coordinate y is shifted in phase relative to y = 0. For small angles, the pressure becomes:

$$p(y) = p_0 \left( 1 - \frac{y^2}{B^2/4} \right) \cos\left( k y \sin\beta \right)$$

The cosine term introduces the **asymmetry** across the centerline. For a small angle expansion:

$$\cos(k y \sin\beta) \approx 1 - \frac{(k y \sin\beta)^2}{2}$$

### Step 4 â€” Decompose into symmetric and antisymmetric parts

The pressure $p(y)$ can be decomposed:

$$p(y) = p_{sym}(y) + p_{anti}(y)$$

Where:
- Symmetric: $p_{sym}(y) = p_0 \left( 1 - \frac{y^2}{B^2/4} \right)$ â€” does not contribute to torsion
- Antisymmetric: $p_{anti}(y) = p_0 \left( 1 - \frac{y^2}{B^2/4} \right) \cdot \left[ \cos(k y \sin\beta) - 1 \right]$

The antisymmetric part is the **source of torsion**.

### Step 5 â€” Compute the differential pressure

The pressure difference between port (y > 0) and starboard (y < 0) is:

$$\Delta p(y) = p(y) - p(-y) = 2 p_0 \left( 1 - \frac{y^2}{B^2/4} \right) \cdot \left[ \cos(k y \sin\beta) - 1 \right]$$

### Step 6 â€” Compute the torque per unit length

The torque per unit length about the roll axis is:

$$t(x) = \int_{-B/2}^{B/2} p(y) \cdot y \, dy$$

Substituting the antisymmetric part only (the symmetric part integrates to zero over symmetric limits):

$$t(x) = 2 p_0 \int_0^{B/2} \left( 1 - \frac{4y^2}{B^2} \right) \left[ \cos(k y \sin\beta) - 1 \right] y \, dy$$

### Step 7 â€” Evaluate the integral

Let $u = k y \sin\beta$, so $y = u / (k \sin\beta)$ and $dy = du / (k \sin\beta)$:

$$t(x) = 2 p_0 \int_0^{k(B/2)\sin\beta} \left( 1 - \frac{4 u^2}{B^2 k^2 \sin^2\beta} \right) [\cos u - 1] \cdot \frac{u}{k^2 \sin^2\beta} \, du$$

This integral is best evaluated numerically in practice. For **board problems**, the KTU examiner typically provides a **simplified form** assuming small خ² and small $kB$:

$$t(x) \approx -p_0 \cdot \frac{\pi (kB)^2 \sin^2\beta}{48} \cdot B$$

The negative sign indicates the direction of the torque. The **magnitude** is the key result for board purposes:

$$\boxed{\;t(x) \approx p_0 \cdot \frac{\pi (kB)^2 \sin^2\beta \cdot B}{48}\;}$$

### Step 8 â€” Total torque by integration along length

$$T(x_0) = \int_0^{L} t(\xi) \, d\xi = p_0 \cdot \frac{\pi (kB)^2 \sin^2\beta \cdot B \cdot L}{48}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the KTU-board-ready formula.** Note the proportionality: torque scales with $\sin^2\beta$ (zero at خ² = 0آ° and خ² = 180آ°), with $B^3$ (the cube of beam â€” wide-beam ships are most vulnerable), and with $L$ (longer ships experience more torque).

## 3.2 Derivation II â€” Apparent Wave Slope in Oblique Encounter

**Statement:** *Derive the expression for the apparent wave slope seen by a ship moving with speed V in waves approaching at angle خ².*

### Step 1 â€” Wave elevation in a fixed frame

The sea surface elevation in a fixed (earth) reference frame is:

$$\eta(x, t) = a \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

Where $a$ is wave amplitude, $k$ is wave number, $\omega$ is angular frequency.

### Step 2 â€” Transform to ship-fixed reference frame

The ship moves with velocity V in the x-direction. The position of a point on the ship in the earth frame is:

$$x = x_0 + V t$$

Where $x_0$ is the position in the ship frame. Substituting:

$$\eta(x_0, t) = a \sin(k(x_0 + Vt) - \omega t) = a \sin(k x_0 + (kV - \omega) t)$$

### Step 3 â€” For oblique waves

If the wave propagates at angle خ² to the ship's heading, the wave number vector has components:

$$\vec{k} = (k\cos\beta, k\sin\beta, 0)$$

So the phase at ship-fixed point $(x_0, y_0)$ is:

$$\phi = k \cos\beta \cdot x_0 + k \sin\beta \cdot y_0 - \omega t$$

The elevation becomes:

$$\eta = a \sin(k\cos\beta \cdot x_0 + k\sin\beta \cdot y_0 - \omega t)$$

### Step 4 â€” Encounter frequency

In the ship frame, the time-dependent phase is $(\omega - k V \cos\beta) t$. The encounter frequency is therefore:

$$\omega_e = \omega - kV\cos\beta = \omega \left( 1 - \frac{\omega V \cos\beta}{g} \right)$$

### Step 5 â€” Apparent wavelength

The wave number in the ship frame is unchanged in the direction of propagation but the relative phase velocity is $\omega_e / k$. The apparent wavelength (along the ship's centerline) is:

$$\lambda_{apparent} = \frac{2\pi}{k\cos\beta} = \frac{\lambda}{\cos\beta}$$

### Step 6 â€” Apparent wave slope

The wave slope in the original direction is $\tan\alpha = 2\pi a / \lambda = k a$. The component of the slope along the transverse direction is:

$$(\nabla \eta)_y = k a \sin\beta \cos(\phi)$$

The component along the centerline direction is:

$$(\nabla \eta)_x = k a \cos\beta \cos(\phi)$$

In the ship-fixed frame, the rate of change of elevation as the ship moves is:

$$\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} = -a\omega \cos(\phi)$$

The **apparent slope** felt by the ship in the transverse direction is the ratio of the transverse gradient to the longitudinal rate:

$$\tan\alpha_{e,y} = \frac{k a \sin\beta}{a\omega / V} = \frac{k V \sin\beta}{\omega} \tan\alpha$$

Using $V_c = g/\omega$ and $k = \omega^2/g$:

$$\tan\alpha_{e,y} = \frac{\omega V \sin\beta}{g} \tan\alpha = \frac{V \sin\beta}{V_c} \tan\alpha$$

Combining with the along-ship component and including the encounter correction:

$$\boxed{\;\tan\alpha_{app} = \frac{\sin\beta}{\cos\beta - V/V_c} \tan\alpha\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** The denominator $\cos\beta - V/V_c$ becomes zero when $V\cos\beta = V_c$ â€” this is the **critical speed** at which the wave appears stationary to the ship, and the apparent slope becomes infinite. KTU 2024 board problems often ask: *"At what ship speed does wave-blocking occur in head seas?"* Answer: $V = V_c = g/\omega$.

## 3.3 Derivation III â€” Bredt's Formula for Torsional Shear in a Closed Ship Hull Section

**Statement:** *Derive Bredt's formula for the torsional shear stress in a thin-walled closed section, and apply it to a ship hull girder.*

### Step 1 â€” Assumptions

The ship hull girder (hull + deck + bottom plating) is approximated as a **thin-walled closed section** with constant wall thickness t around its perimeter. The enclosed area is $A_m$.

### Step 2 â€” Shear flow assumption

For thin-walled closed sections under torsion, the shear flow $q$ (force per unit length) is **constant around the perimeter** for a single-cell section:

$$q = \frac{T}{2 A_m}$$

This is a consequence of **compatibility** (the rate of twist is uniform) and the assumption that the cross-section does not warp (Saint-Venant torsion).

### Step 3 â€” Shear stress

The shear stress at any point in the wall is:

$$\tau = \frac{q}{t} = \frac{T}{2 A_m t}$$

### Step 4 â€” Application to ship hull

For a typical merchant ship hull girder with rectangular cross-section approximation:
- Breadth: $B$ (transverse dimension)
- Depth: $D$ (vertical dimension from keel to deck)
- Mean enclosed area: $A_m \approx B \cdot D$
- Plating thickness: $t_{plate}$ (varies â€” side shell, bottom, deck)

The **maximum shear stress** occurs where the plating is thinnest. For a typical ship, this is the **side shell** away from the bilge:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\vert T \vert}{2 B D t_{side}}$$

### Step 5 â€” Combined stress check

At the deck edge (top corner of the closed section), the **combined normal + shear stress** is critical. Using von Mises:

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2} \leq \sigma_{allow}$$

Where:
- $\sigma = \dfrac{M_V \cdot (D/2)}{I_V}$ from vertical bending
- $\tau = \dfrac{\vert T \vert}{2 B D t_{side}}$ from torsion

### Step 6 â€” Worked numerical example (KTU 2024 style)

**Problem:** A 200 m long container ship with $B = 32$ m, $D = 19$ m, side shell thickness $t = 25$ mm is subjected to an oblique wave torque $T = 250$ MNآ·m. Find the maximum torsional shear stress.

**Solution:**

Step 1 â€” Mean enclosed area: $A_m = B \cdot D = 32 \times 19 = 608 \text{ m}^2$

Step 2 â€” Apply Bredt's formula:

$$\tau = \frac{T}{2 A_m t} = \frac{250 \times 10^6 \text{ Nآ·m}}{2 \times 608 \text{ m}^2 \times 0.025 \text{ m}}$$

Step 3 â€” Compute denominator: $2 \times 608 \times 0.025 = 30.4 \text{ m}^3$

Step 4 â€” Final answer:

$$\tau = \frac{250 \times 10^6}{30.4} = 8.22 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 = 8.22 \text{ MPa}$$

**Check against allowable:** $\tau_{allow} = 110$ MPa (mild steel). Since $8.22 \ll 110$, the design is safe against torsional shear alone. However, when combined with bending stress, the total may approach allowable. 

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The 8.22 MPa result is realistic. Container ships typically experience torsional shear stresses of 5â€“20 MPa in service, and combined stresses of 150â€“220 MPa â€” close to the allowable for high-tensile steel (AH36, DH36).

## 3.4 Derivation IV â€” Cargo-Induced Torsional Moment and Free Surface Effect

**Statement:** *Calculate the effective torsional moment due to cargo distribution including free surface effects in partially filled tanks.*

### Step 1 â€” Direct cargo moment

If cargo of total weight $W_c$ has its center of gravity offset by $y_c$ from the centerline, the direct heeling moment (which is the torsional moment about the roll axis) is:

$$T_{cargo} = W_c \cdot y_c$$

For multiple holds with weights $W_i$ and offsets $y_i$:

$$T_{cargo} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot y_i$$

### Step 2 â€” Free surface moment

A partially filled tank contains liquid that can shift freely. The center of gravity of the liquid moves as the ship rolls. The free surface correction to the **virtual TCG** is:

$$\Delta TCG_{FS} = \frac{\rho_{liquid} \cdot A_{FS} \cdot \bar{y}}{W_{displacement}}$$

Where:
- $A_{FS}$ = free surface area
- $\bar{y}$ = transverse distance from centerline to centroid of free surface
- $W_{displacement}$ = total ship displacement

### Step 3 â€” Total effective TCG

$$TCG_{eff} = TCG_{cargo} + \Delta TCG_{FS}$$

### Step 4 â€” Total static torsional moment

$$\boxed{\;T_{total,static} = W_{displacement} \cdot TCG_{eff}\;}$$

### Step 5 â€” Worked numerical example

**Problem:** A 15,000 DWT chemical tanker has:
- Total displacement: 18,000 t
- Cargo in No. 2 port tank: 2000 t at transverse offset of 8 m from centerline
- Partially filled No. 3 starboard tank: 1500 t, free surface area 200 mآ², centroid offset 6 m
- All other tanks empty

Calculate the total static torsional moment.

**Solution:**

Step 1 â€” Direct cargo moment:
$$T_{cargo} = 2000 \times 8 = 16{,}000 \text{ tآ·m (port side)} = 16{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 10^3 \text{ Nآ·m}$$
$$= 1.57 \times 10^8 \text{ Nآ·m} = 157 \text{ MNآ·m}$$

Step 2 â€” Free surface correction (assume liquid cargo density = 1.0 t/mآ³):
$$\Delta TCG_{FS} = \frac{1.0 \times 200 \times 6}{18{,}000} = 0.0667 \text{ m}$$

Step 3 â€” Free surface moment:
$$T_{FS} = 18{,}000 \times 0.0667 = 1200 \text{ tآ·m} = 11.8 \text{ MNآ·m}$$

Step 4 â€” Total static torque (both effects on same side â€” they add):
$$T_{total} = 157 + 11.8 = 168.8 \text{ MNآ·m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall â€” Sign Convention:** The KTU board expects students to clearly state the **direction** of the torque (port or starboard heeling). A common mistake is to compute the magnitude and forget the sign, leading to wrong stress direction in combined loading. Always state "heeling to port" or "heeling to starboard" explicitly.
