For the typical case where the superstructure is centered vertically above the centerline (so $z_s = 0$ at the CL but offset transversely):

$$T(x) = M_s \cdot g \cdot y_s \cdot \cos\phi$$

Wait â€” this is incomplete. The full torsional moment at any section x requires integration of distributed loads. For a concentrated weight:

$$\boxed{T(x) = M_s \cdot g \cdot y_s \cdot \cos\phi \quad \text{(concentrated torque at section x)}}$$

**Mark allocation:** FBD (1.5), Assumptions (1), Vector/cross-product setup (1.5), Final formula with roll angle (2), Free-free boundary note (1).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $A_0 = 280$ mآ²
- $t = 0.012$ m
- $M_s = 1200 \times 10^3$ kg
- $y_s = 4.0$ m
- $\phi = 20آ°$, $g = 9.81$ m/sآ²

**(i) Maximum torsional moment:**

$$T = M_s \cdot g \cdot y_s \cdot \cos\phi$$

$$T = 1200 \times 10^3 \times 9.81 \times 4.0 \times \cos(20آ°)$$

$$T = 1200 \times 10^3 \times 9.81 \times 4.0 \times 0.9397$$

$$T = 44.25 \times 10^6 \text{ Nآ·m} = 44.25 \text{ MNآ·m}$$

**(ii) Maximum shear stress (Bredt's formula):**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{2 \cdot A_0 \cdot t} = \frac{44.25 \times 10^6}{2 \times 280 \times 0.012}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{44.25 \times 10^6}{6.72} = 6.585 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 6.59 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Shear flow in the side shell:**

$$q = \frac{T}{2 A_0} = \frac{44.25 \times 10^6}{2 \times 280} = 79.0 \times 10^3 \text{ N/m} = 79.0 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Mark allocation:** Given data (1), Formula application (2), Calculation T (1), Calculation د„ (1.5), Calculation q (1.5).

---

#### QUESTION B â€” [KTU University Exam â€“ May 2023, Modified Model Paper Q4(B)]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the difference between **St. Venant torsion** and **warping (non-uniform) torsion** for ship hull cross-sections. Under what conditions does warping torsion become dominant in container ships?

**(b)** [7 Marks] A container ship hull has the following torsion section properties: St. Venant torsion constant $I_T = 12$ mâپ´, warping constant $I_w = 3.5 \times 10^5$ mâپ¶, length $L = 250$ m, Young's modulus $E = 206$ GPa, shear modulus $G = 79.3$ GPa. A concentrated torque $T_0 = 30$ MNآ·m is applied at midship. Calculate (i) the torsion parameter $k$, (ii) the characteristic decay length $\lambda$, and (iii) the maximum angle of twist at midship for the free-free boundary condition.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

| Aspect | St. Venant Torsion | Warping (Non-Uniform) Torsion |
|---|---|---|
| **Cross-section behavior** | Remains plane (no warping) | Cross-section warps out of plane |
| **Stress type** | Pure shear stress $\tau$ only | Shear stress + axial normal stress $\sigma_w$ |
| **Governing equation** | $T = G I_T \theta'$ | $T_w = E I_w \theta'''$ |
| **Applicable sections** | Fully closed circular tubes | Open sections (slit tubes, I-beams) |
| **Loading type** | Uniform torque distribution | Concentrated or varying torque |

In **St. Venant torsion**, every cross-section rotates rigidly about the X-axis, with shear stresses distributed in a pattern determined purely by the section geometry. The St. Venant torsion constant $I_T$ for a closed cell is $4A_0^2 / \oint(ds/t)$.

In **warping torsion**, the cross-section cannot remain plane (especially at boundaries or where torque changes rapidly). Axial warping displacements create **secondary axial stresses** (normal stresses $\sigma_w$ parallel to the longitudinal axis), which can be significant at locations of concentrated torque application.

**Why warping dominates in container ships:**
1. The large hatch openings effectively create a **slit** in the deck, making the cross-section behave as an open section.
2. The superstructure applies a **concentrated torque** at a specific location (not distributed), requiring abrupt change in $\theta'(x)$.
3. The free-free boundary condition permits warping at the ends, but transverse bulkheads and structural discontinuities locally restrain warping.

**Mark allocation:** Definitions (2), Stress types (1.5), Governing equations (1.5), Container ship conditions (2).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Torsion parameter $k$:**

$$k^2 = \frac{G \cdot I_T}{E \cdot I_w} = \frac{79.3 \times 10^9 \times 12}{206 \times 10^9 \times 3.5 \times 10^5}$$

$$k^2 = \frac{951.6 \times 10^9}{7.21 \times 10^{16}} = 1.320 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{-2}$$

$$k = \sqrt{1.320 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.633 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Characteristic decay length $\lambda$:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{k} = \frac{1}{3.633 \times 10^{-3}} = 275.3 \text{ m}$$

(Note: $\lambda > L/2 = 125$ m, indicating warping effects extend over the full hull length.)

**(iii) Maximum twist at midship:**

For a free-free beam with concentrated torque $T_0$ at $x = L/2$:

$$\theta_{max}(L/2) = \frac{T_0}{2 k G I_T} \left[1 - \frac{1}{\cosh(kL/2)}\right]$$

Compute $kL/2 = 3.633 \times 10^{-3} \times 125 = 0.4541$

$$\cosh(0.4541) = \frac{e^{0.4541} + e^{-0.4541}}{2} = \frac{1.5747 + 0.6350}{2} = 1.1049$$

$$\theta_{max} = \frac{30 \times 10^6}{2 \times 3.633 \times 10^{-3} \times 79.3 \times 10^9 \times 12} \left[1 - \frac{1}{1.1049}\right]$$

Denominator: $2 \times 3.633 \times 10^{-3} \times 79.3 \times 10^9 \times 12 = 6.913 \times 10^9$

$$\theta_{max} = \frac{30 \times 10^6}{6.913 \times 10^9} \times [1 - 0.9050]$$

$$\theta_{max} = 4.339 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.0950 = 4.122 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{max} \approx 4.12 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad} \approx 0.024آ°}$$

This is equivalent to a transverse displacement of $0.412$ mm/m of hull length â€” within acceptable limits.

**Mark allocation:** Formula identification (1), Calculation of k (1.5), Calculation of خ» (1), Cosine hyperbolic calculation (1.5), Final answer with units (2).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning â€” Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the cosد† factor** (or using sinد† for the lever arm incorrectly). The static torque arm is $y_s \cos\phi$, while the dynamic part involves $z_s \sin\phi$. Mixing these up costs **2â€“3 marks** in Part B.
> 2. **Using simply-supported boundary conditions instead of free-free.** The hull girder is free at both ends. This is a frequent mistake in derivations.
> 3. **Confusing St. Venant torsion constant $I_T$ for open vs. closed sections.** For an open section (slit), $I_T \approx \frac{1}{3} \sum b_i t_i^3$. For a closed cell, use the Bredt formula. Mismatching these gives wrong answers.
> 4. **Forgetting the bimoment/warping term** when asked about non-uniform torsion. Examiners specifically look for the $E I_w \theta'''$ term.
> 5. **Unit errors:** Stress in Pa vs. MPa, area in mآ² vs. cmآ². Always state units clearly.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** Every derivation should explicitly state: (a) small deflection theory, (b) elastic material, (c) free-free boundary, (d) thin-walled assumption.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST â€” Container Ship Torsion**

**Core Concepts (Must Know):**
- âکگ Torsion in container ships arises from **asymmetric transverse weight distribution** (superstructure offset + container stowage)
- âکگ Hull girder cross-section is **thin-walled**, behaving as a **closed cell** between hatches and an **open slit section** at hatch locations
- âکگ Hull girder is **free-free** at both ends (no physical supports)
- âکگ Roll angle $\phi$ amplifies the lever arm of asymmetric weights by $\cos\phi$ and introduces a dynamic lever arm of $z_s \sin\phi$

**Critical Formulas (Memorize):**
- âکگ **Bredt's formula:** $\tau = T / (2 A_0 t)$
- âکگ **Shear flow in closed cell:** $q = T / (2 A_0)$
- âکگ **St. Venant torsion constant (closed):** $I_T = 4 A_0^2 / \oint (ds/t)$
- âکگ **St. Venant torsion constant (open slit):** $I_T \approx \frac{1}{3} \sum b_i t_i^3$
- âکگ **Vlasov non-uniform torsion ODE:** $E I_w \theta'''' - G I_T \theta'' = -m_T(x)$
- âکگ **Torsion parameter:** $k = \sqrt{G I_T / (E I_w)}$
- âکگ **Total torque:** $T_{total} = G I_T \theta' + E I_w \theta'''$
- âکگ **Von Mises equivalent:** $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$

**Physical Constants to Remember:**
- âکگ $E_{steel} = 206$ GPa, $G_{steel} = 79.3$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$
- âکگ IACS S11A twist limit: $\theta_{max} \leq 5$ mm/m (approximately $2.86 \times 10^{-3}$ rad/m)
- âکگ Typical container ship block coefficient $C_B = 0.55 - 0.70$

**Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
- âکگ DO NOT use simply-supported BCs â€” use free-free
- âکگ DO NOT confuse $I_T$ formulas for open vs. closed sections
- âکگ DO NOT forget to combine bending and torsion in stress check
- âکگ DO NOT neglect the hatch corner stress concentration

**Design Practice Points:**
- âکگ Place superstructure as close to centerline as possible (modern designs use bridge wing extensions)
- âکگ Provide adequate **longitudinal stiffeners** at hatch side girders
- âکگ Use **finite element analysis (FEA)** for final design verification
- âکگ Check fatigue at hatch corners (DNV CSR-BC requirements)
- âکگ Apply IACS S11A twist deflection limits
- âکگ Conduct torsional strength assessment for both still water and wave conditions

**Connections to Other Modules:**
- âکگ This topic connects to **Module 2** (Longitudinal Strength â€” combined bending + torsion)
- âکگ Also related to **Module 4** (Hatch Covers and Corner Details â€” fatigue-prone regions)
- âکگ Foundation from **Module 1** (Hull Girder Equivalent Beam Theory) is essential
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear Stress Distribution Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Shear Stress Distribution Procedure ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In ship structural analysis, the **Shear Stress Distribution Procedure** refers to the systematic engineering method used to determine the magnitude and variation of transverse (vertical) shear stresses acting across the various plating and stiffener elements of a ship's hull girder cross-section when the vessel is subjected to a vertical bending moment and the associated shear force. For a ship hull acting as a **box girder** (the standard ship structural idealisation), the vertical shear force $V$ at any transverse section is resisted by shear stresses acting in the vertical (or transverse) plane across the various horizontal and vertical elements of the hull cross-section. The procedure involves the application of the classical **flexural shear formula** to the ship section, with the necessary modifications to account for the thin-walled, multi-material, multi-cell nature of the hull. The governing relationship is: $$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$ where: - $\tau$ = average shear stress at the level under consideration - $V$ = total transverse shear force at the section - $Q$ = first moment of area (about the neutral axis) of the portion of the cross-section lying beyond (above or below) the level at which stress is being calculated - $I$ = second moment of area of the **effective** cross-section about the neutral axis - $b$ = total **effective breadth** of the cross-section at the level being considered > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3, the procedure is taught in the context of the hull girder under still-water and wave-induced bending. Students are expected to be able to *execute* the complete procedure from a given section geometry to a plotted shear stress distribution curve. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation Imagine a **thick stack of books** resting on a table, and you push the top book horizontally. The top book tries to slide off the stack. This horizontal sliding tendency is transmitted down through the stack because of **friction (shear)** between adjacent books. The shear force is maximum where the total horizontal push is being transmitted â€” but the *stress* depends on how the cross-section is configured. Now replace the stack of books with the **ship hull cross-section**, and the horizontal push with the **vertical shear force $V$** caused by wave action. The deck, side shell, bottom shell, and longitudinal bulkheads act together to resist this shear â€” just like the books. The "stress" each "layer" carries depends on: 1. **How much area is beyond that layer** (because the further layers don't yet contribute to resisting the push), and 2. **How wide the layer is** at that level (wider means the shear is spread over a larger area, hence lower stress). Think of the **neutral axis** as the "balance point" â€” and at this level, the maximum amount of material lies beyond, so the shear stress is **highest at the neutral axis** and **zero at the extreme top and bottom fibres**. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Insight:** The maximum shear stress in a typical ship occurs at the neutral axis (usually near the mid-height of the hull). Designers pay close attention to this region because high shear combined with low bending moment can cause **shear failure** of the side shell plating. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation The shear stress distribution procedure rests on four engineering assumptions: 1. **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the deformed neutral axis** (Eulerâ€“Bernoulli beam theory). 2. **Material behaves linear-elastically** within the operating range. 3. **The hull section is treated as a thin-walled box girder** with flanges (deck, bottom) and webs (side shell, longitudinal bulkheads). 4. **The shear stress $\tau$ is assumed uniform across the breadth $b$** at any given horizontal level (this is exact for narrow rectangular elements and a very good approximation for thin plating). The shear stress is not actually uniform through the thickness of plating â€” it follows a parabolic variation from zero at the outer surface to a maximum at the centre plane â€” but the **average shear stress** in the plating is given by the formula. This average is what is used in ship design. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure The procedure, as expected in KTU examinations, follows this structured flow: **Step 1 â€” Compute Section Properties of the Effective Section:** - Identify all longitudinal strength elements: deck plating, bottom plating, side shell, longitudinal bulkheads, sheer strake, gunwale, etc. - Determine the **neutral axis position** $\bar{y}$ by taking moments of effective areas about a reference axis (usually the baseline or keel). - Compute the **second moment of area** $I_{NA}$ of the entire effective section about the neutral axis using the parallel axis theorem. **Step 2 â€” Handle Multi-Material Sections (Effective Breadth):** - If different materials are used (e.g., mild steel in bottom and high-tensile steel in deck), introduce the **modular ratio** $n = E_i / E_{ref}$. - Transform the actual breadth $b_i$ of each element to its **effective breadth** $b_{eff,i} = n \cdot b_i$. - All subsequent calculations use effective breadths. **Step 3 â€” Choose Discrete Levels for Computation:** - Select a series of horizontal levels (typically every 1 m, or at every change in section geometry, or at the neutral axis). - Compute $Q$ and $b$ at each level. **Step 4 â€” Compute $Q$ at Each Level:** $Q$ is the first moment of area of the portion of the section on *one side* (either above or below) of the chosen level, taken about the neutral axis. $$Q = \sum A_i \cdot \bar{y}_i$$ where the sum is over all effective elements lying entirely above (or below) the chosen level. **Step 5 â€” Compute $b$ at Each Level:** $b$ is the **total effective breadth** at the chosen level â€” the sum of effective breadths of all plating/stiffener elements intersected by the level. **Step 6 â€” Compute $\tau$ at Each Level:** $$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I_{NA} \cdot b}$$ **Step 7 â€” Plot the Distribution:** Plot $\tau$ versus the vertical position $z$ (height above baseline). This gives the classic parabolic-like curve, zero at the top and bottom, maximum at the neutral axis. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Rule | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Flexural shear stress (basic) | $\tau = VQ / (Ib)$ | $\tau$ in Pa, V in N, Q in mآ³, I in mâپ´, b in m | | 2 | Shear flow | $q = \tau \cdot b = VQ / I$ | N/m â€” useful for stiffener design | | 3 | First moment about NA | $Q = \sum A_i \cdot \bar{y}_i$ | Take area on one side of cut | | 4 | Effective breadth (multi-material) | $b_{eff,i} = (E_i / E_{ref}) \cdot b_i$ | $E_{ref}$ = reference Young's modulus | | 5 | Moment of inertia (parallel axis) | $I_{NA} = \sum \left[ I_{o,i} + A_i \cdot (\bar{y}_i - \bar{y})^2 \right]$ | About NA of the entire section | | 6 | Neutral axis position | $\bar{y} = \sum (A_i \cdot y_i) / \sum A_i$ | From a reference axis (keel/baseline) | | 7 | Modular ratio | $n = E_{material} / E_{reference}$ | Dimensionless | | 8 | Shear stress boundary condition | $\tau = 0$ at extreme fibres | Top of deck and bottom of bottom plating | | 9 | Stress in deck/bottom plating | $\tau_{plating} = V \cdot Q_{flange} / (I \cdot b_{plating})$ | Generally small due to large $b$ | | 10 | Stress in side shell at NA | $\tau_{max} = V \cdot Q_{max} / (I \cdot 2t_s)$ | Where $t_s$ = side shell thickness | | 11 | Equilibrium check | $\int \tau \, dA = V$ | Integral of shear stress over section = applied shear | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common marking scheme allocates marks for *each* of: NA location (2 marks), I calculation (3 marks), Q calculation at multiple levels (5 marks), د„ calculation (3 marks), and the distribution plot/interpretation (1 mark). Memorise the *order* of operations. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hull Scantling Design:** The shear stress procedure is used directly to determine the required thickness of side shell plating and longitudinal bulkheads near the neutral axis. - **Shear Failure Prevention:** Ships are most vulnerable to **shear failure** at sections of high shear force combined with low bending moment â€” typically near the quarters (about 0.25L from bow/stern). Classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV) impose minimum side shell thickness rules specifically derived from shear stress considerations. - **Stiffener Spacing:** The shear flow $q$ is used to size longitudinal stiffeners and determine their spacing in the side shell and bottom regions. - **Topside Structures:** When aluminium superstructures are fitted atop a steel hull, the effective breadth method (with $n = E_{Al}/E_{steel} \approx 0.33$) is essential to correctly account for the reduced stiffness contribution. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Shear Stress Formula $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$ The classical derivation for a ship hull girder is as follows. Consider two cross-sections of the hull separated by a small distance $dx$. Between them, consider an element of plating cut by a horizontal plane at a distance $z_1$ above the neutral axis. **Sign Convention & Setup:** - Let the bending moment at the left section be $M$ and at the right section be $M + dM$. - The corresponding shear force is $V = dM/dx$. - Take the origin at the neutral axis, with $z$ positive upward. **Equilibrium of the Element (above the cut):** The portion of the cross-section above the cut at $z_1$ is subjected to: 1. Normal stresses $\sigma = M \cdot z / I$ acting on the left section. 2. Normal stresses $\sigma' = (M + dM) \cdot z / I$ acting on the right section. 3. A shear force $F$ on the cut surface to maintain horizontal equilibrium. **Sum of horizontal forces = 0:** $$F = \int_{z_1}^{z_{top}} \frac{(M + dM) \cdot z}{I} \, dA - \int_{z_1}^{z_{top}} \frac{M \cdot z}{I} \, dA$$ $$F = \frac{dM}{I} \int_{z_1}^{z_{top}} z \, dA$$ **Recognising the Integrals as $Q$:** The integral $\int_{z_1}^{z_{top}} z \, dA$ is, by definition, the first moment of area of the portion of the cross-section above the cut about the neutral axis. This is precisely $Q$. Therefore: $$F = \frac{dM}{I} \cdot Q = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I}$$ **Average Shear Stress:** If the cut is at a level where the total breadth is $b$, then by assuming the shear stress is uniform across this breadth: $$\tau = \frac{F}{b} = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$ $$\boxed{\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}}$$ This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem Statement:** A ship hull cross-section is idealised as a rectangular box girder with the following effective dimensions (steel throughout, so $n = 1$): - Breadth $B = 24$ m - Depth $D = 12$ m - Bottom plating thickness: $t_b = 22$ mm $= 0.022$ m - Deck plating thickness: $t_d = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m - Side shell thickness (each side): $t_s = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m The section is subjected to a vertical shear force $V = 5000$ kN. Compute the shear stress at: 1. The neutral axis. 2. The top of the side shell (just below the deck). 3. The mid-height of the side shell. **Solution â€” Step A: Locate the Neutral Axis** Because of symmetry, the neutral axis lies at mid-depth: $$\bar{z} = D / 2 = 6 \text{ m above baseline}$$ **Solution â€” Step B: Calculate the Second Moment of Area $I_{NA}$** Using the parallel axis theorem for each element: Bottom plate: $$I_b = \frac{B \cdot t_b^3}{12} + (B \cdot t_b) \cdot \bar{z}^2$$ $$I_b \approx 0 + (24)(0.022)(6)^2 = 19.008 \text{ m}^4$$ Deck plate: $$I_d \approx (24)(0.020)(6)^2 = 17.280 \text{ m}^4$$ Side shells (both sides combined): $$I_s = 2 \times \left[ \frac{t_s \cdot D^3}{12} + (t_s \cdot D) \cdot (0)^2 \right] = 2 \times \frac{0.020 \times 12^3}{12} = 2 \times 2.880 = 5.760 \text{ m}^4$$ Note: The own moment of inertia of the plating about its own centroid is negligible compared with the parallel axis term â€” a useful shortcut in ship scantling work. Total second moment of area: $$I_{NA} = 19.008 + 17.280 + 5.760 = 42.048 \text{ m}^4$$ **Solution â€” Step C: Shear Stress at the Neutral Axis** At $z = 6$ m (neutral axis), calculate $Q$ from the portion above (or below) the cut. Using the portion **above**: The portion above NA consists of: - Upper half of side shell: Area $= 2 \times t_s \times (D/2) = 2 \times 0.020 \times 6 = 0.24$ mآ²; centroid at $\bar{y} = 3$ m above NA. - Deck plate: Area $= B \cdot t_d = 24 \times 0.020 = 0.48$ mآ²; centroid at $\bar{y} = 6$ m above NA. $$Q_{NA} = (0.24)(3) + (0.48)(6) = 0.72 + 2.88 = 3.60 \text{ m}^3$$ Total effective breadth at NA: $$b_{NA} = 2 \times t_s = 2 \times 0.020 = 0.040 \text{ m}$$ Shear stress at NA: $$\tau_{NA} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{NA}}{I_{NA} \cdot b_{NA}} = \frac{5000 \times 3.60}{42.048 \times 0.040}$$ $$\tau_{NA} = \frac{18000}{1.68192} = 10701.2 \text{ kN/m}^2 \approx 10.70 \text{ MPa}$$ **Solution â€” Step D: Shear Stress at the Top of the Side Shell (just below deck)** At $z = D = 12$ m, the portion above the cut is **only the deck plate**: $$Q_{top} = (B \cdot t_d) \cdot \bar{y} = (0.48)(6) = 2.88 \text{ m}^3$$ The effective breadth at this level is the deck plating thickness (the side shell ends here): $$b_{top} = t_d = 0.020 \text{ m}$$ Wait â€” at exactly the deck level, $b$ is the thickness of the deck plating. But conceptually, the *shear stress* in the deck plating just below its lower face is best calculated using $b = $ deck thickness: $$\tau_{top} = \frac{5000 \times 2.88}{42.048 \times 0.020} = \frac{14400}{0.84096} = 17123.7 \text{ kN/m}^2 \approx 17.12 \text{ MPa}$$ **Interpretation:** The shear stress in the deck plating is very high (because $b$ is only 20 mm) â€” but the deck is also thickest in shear and carries mainly axial (bending) stress. Designers typically check that this is below allowable. **Solution â€” Step E: Shear Stress at Mid-Height of Side Shell (z = 6, which is the NA â€” already done in Step C)** If instead we pick $z = 3$ m (quarter height): The portion above this level consists of: - Upper 3/6 of side shell: Area $= 2 \times 0.020 \times 3 = 0.12$ mآ²; centroid at $1.5$ m above NA. - Deck plate: Area $= 0.48$ mآ²; centroid at $6$ m above NA. $$Q_{z=3} = (0.12)(1.5) + (0.48)(6) = 0.18 + 2.88 = 3.06 \text{ m}^3$$ Effective breadth $b = 2 \times 0.020 = 0.040$ m. $$\tau_{z=3} = \frac{5000 \times 3.06}{42.048 \times 0.040} = \frac{15300}{1.68192} = 9096.4 \text{ kN/m}^2 \approx 9.10 \text{ MPa}$$ **Summary Table:** | Level (m above baseline) | $Q$ (mآ³) | $b$ (m) | $\tau$ (MPa) | |---|---|---|---| | 0.0 (bottom of bottom plate) | 0 | 0.022 | 0 | | 0.022 (top of bottom plate) | 2.64 | 0.040 | 7.85 | | 3.0 (quarter height) | 3.06 | 0.040 | 9.10 | | 6.0 (neutral axis) | 3.60 | 0.040 | **10.70** | | 9.0 (three-quarter height) | 3.06 | 0.040 | 9.10 | | 11.98 (bottom of deck) | 2.88 | 0.040 | 8.57 | | 12.0 (top of deck) | 0 | 0.020 | 0 | > [!NOTE] > **Key Observation:** The shear stress is **zero** at the extreme top and bottom fibres, rises smoothly through the side shell, **peaks at the neutral axis**, and is asymmetric between bottom and deck because the bottom plate is slightly thicker. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Shear Stress Distribution Flowchart (Mermaid) The following block-level architecture flow maps the complete shear stress distribution procedure used in KTU ship structural problems: ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start: Given Hull Section Geometry and V]) --> S1[Step 1: Identify all longitudinal strength elements] S1 --> S2[Step 2: Determine modular ratios n_i for different materials] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute effective breadths b_eff_i equals n_i times b_i] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Locate Neutral Axis using area moments about baseline] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute total second moment of area I_NA via parallel axis theorem] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Choose discrete horizontal levels z_k for computation] S6 --> S7[Step 7: For each level calculate Q_k equals sum of A_i times y_i above cut] S6 --> S8[Step 8: For each level calculate effective breadth b_k] S7 --> S9[Step 9: Apply shear stress formula tau equals V times Q divided by I times b] S8 --> S9 S9 --> S10[Step 10: Tabulate tau versus z for all levels] S10 --> S11[Step 11: Plot shear stress distribution curve] S11 --> S12[Step 12: Identify maximum tau location and value] S12 --> S13[Step 13: Verify equilibrium: integral of tau dA equals V] S13 --> End([End: Distribution Validated]) ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram â€” Hull Cross-Section Functional Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram represents the functional interaction of the major hull components that carry the shear flow: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HullCrossSection[Ship Hull Cross Section] Deck[Deck Plating
Carries bending and shear] SidePort[Port Side Shell
Carries maximum shear near NA] SideStar[Starboard Side Shell
Carries maximum shear near NA] Bottom[Bottom Plating
Carries bending and shear] BulkheadL[Longitudinal Bulkhead Port
Optional secondary web] BulkheadR[Longitudinal Bulkhead Starboard
Optional secondary web] end V([Vertical Shear Force V]) --> NA((Neutral Axis Level)) NA --> SidePort NA --> SideStar Deck --- NA Bottom --- NA BulkheadL --- NA BulkheadR --- NA ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram â€” Effective Breadth Transformation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ActualSection[Actual Multi-Material Section] A1[Steel Deck: E_s, b_d] A2[Steel Sides: E_s, b_s] A3[HT Steel Bottom: E_h, b_b] A4[Aluminium Superstructure: E_a, b_a] end A1 --> T1[Transform: b_eff equals b_d times E_s divided by E_ref] A2 --> T2[Transform: b_eff equals b_s times E_s divided by E_ref] A3 --> T3[Transform: b_eff equals b_b times E_h divided by E_ref] A4 --> T4[Transform: b_eff equals b_a times E_a divided by E_ref] T1 --> ES[Effective Homogeneous Section in E_ref] T2 --> ES T3 --> ES T4 --> ES ES --> SP[Standard Procedure: tau equals VQ divided by I times b_eff] ``` ### 4.4 Qualitative Shear Stress Distribution Curve ```mermaid xychart-beta title "Typical Shear Stress Distribution in a Ship Hull" x-axis "Height above baseline (m)" [0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12] y-axis "Shear stress tau (MPa)" 0 --> 12 line [0, 6.5, 9.5, 10.7, 9.5, 8.5, 0] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the curve:** The shear stress is **zero at the keel and at the top of the deck**, rises steadily through the side shell, and reaches its **maximum at the neutral axis** (mid-depth for a symmetric section). The shape is approximately parabolic in the side shell and the small discontinuities occur at material boundaries (e.g., at the deck-shell junction). --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q:** Define the term **"shear flow"** in a ship hull girder. How is it related to shear stress, and why is it a useful concept in ship structural design? **Model Answer:** Shear flow $q$ at any horizontal level of a ship's cross-section is defined as the shear force per unit length along that level. It is mathematically defined as: $$q = \tau \cdot b = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I}$$ where $\tau$ is the average shear stress at that level, $b$ is the effective breadth, $V$ is the total shear force, $Q$ is the first moment of area, and $I$ is the second moment of area of the section. Shear flow is useful in ship design because: 1. It enables the **proportioning of longitudinal stiffeners** â€” the load carried by each stiffener equals the shear flow acting over its tributary length. 2. It provides a clearer picture of how shear force is transmitted between flanges (deck, bottom) and webs (side shell, bulkheads) of the hull girder. 3. It is the natural quantity for **designing the connections** (e.g., bracket connections between side shell and deck) where shear flow is balanced by local panel shear. **(3 marks)** --- #### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** What is **effective breadth** in the context of ship hull girder analysis? Explain why it is used when the deck and bottom are constructed from different grades of steel. **Model Answer:** Effective breadth $b_{eff}$ of a longitudinal strength element is its actual breadth multiplied by the modular ratio $n = E_i / E_{ref}$, where $E_i$ is the Young's modulus of the material of that element and $E_{ref}$ is the modulus of the reference material (usually mild steel). It is used to **transform a section composed of multiple materials into an equivalent homogeneous section** that can be analysed using standard formulas. When high-tensile steel is used in the deck (for higher yield strength) and mild steel in the bottom (for ease of fabrication and weldability), the two materials have different Young's moduli ($E_{HT} > E_{MS}$ slightly) but more importantly different stiffness contributions. By replacing the actual breadth $b$ of each element with $b_{eff} = (E_i / E_{ref}) \cdot b$, the section properties ($I$, $Q$, NA location) become those of an equivalent homogeneous section. This procedure, called the **transformed section method**, ensures that the relative stiffness contributions of each element are correctly represented in the bending and shear stress calculations. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each â€” Internal Choice) --- #### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q:** A ship hull is idealised as a rectangular box girder of breadth $B = 20$ m and depth $D = 10$ m. The plating thicknesses are: - Bottom plating: $t_b = 25$ mm - Deck plating: $t_d = 20$ mm - Side shell (each side): $t_s = 22$ mm Assume a single material throughout (steel). The section is subjected to a vertical shear force $V = 4000$ kN. **(a)** Calculate the position of the neutral axis and the second moment of area of the effective section about the neutral axis. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Determine the shear stress distribution at the following levels: at the neutral axis, at the top of the side shell, at the bottom of the side shell, and at mid-height between the neutral axis and the deck. Present your results in a tabulated form. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Neutral Axis Position & $I_{NA}$:** Areas of each element: - $A_b = B \cdot t_b = 20 \times 0.025 = 0.500$ mآ², centroid at $y_b = t_b/2 = 0.0125$ m above baseline. - $A_d = B \cdot t_d = 20 \times 0.020 = 0.400$ mآ², centroid at $y_d = D - t_d/2 = 9.990$ m above baseline. - $A_s = 2 \times t_s \cdot D = 2 \times 0.022 \times 10 = 0.440$ mآ², centroid at $y_s = D/2 = 5.000$ m above baseline. Total area: $A = 0.500 + 0.400 + 0.440 = 1.340$ mآ². Neutral axis position (taking moments about baseline): $$\bar{y} = \frac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{(0.500)(0.0125) + (0.400)(9.990) + (0.440)(5.000)}{1.340}$$ $$\bar{y} = \frac{0.00625 + 3.996 + 2.200}{1.340} = \frac{6.20225}{1.340} = 4.628 \text{ m}$$ So the neutral axis is at **$\bar{y} = 4.628$ m above the baseline**. Second moment of area (parallel axis theorem, ignoring own moments of thin plating): $$I_{NA} = A_b (\bar{y} - y_b)^2 + A_d (\bar{y} - y_d)^2 + A_s (\bar{y} - y_s)^2$$ $$I_{NA} = (0.500)(4.628 - 0.0125)^2 + (0.400)(4.628 - 9.990)^2 + (0.440)(4.628 - 5.000)^2$$ $$I_{NA} = (0.500)(21.333) + (0.400)(28.762) + (0.440)(0.1384)$$ $$I_{NA} = 10.667 + 11.505 + 0.0609 = 22.233 \text{ m}^4$$ **$\boxed{I_{NA} = 22.233 \text{ m}^4}$** **(b) Shear Stress at Various Levels:** **Level 1: Neutral Axis ($z = 4.628$ m):** Area above NA: - Upper portion of side shell from NA to deck: 2 sides أ— $t_s$ أ— $(D - \bar{y})$ = $2 \times 0.022 \times (10 - 4.628)$ = $0.022 \times 5.372 = 0.2364$ mآ². Centroid at distance $(D - \bar{y})/2 = 2.686$ m above NA. - Deck plate: $A_d = 0.400$ mآ². Centroid at $D - \bar{y} - t_d/2 = 5.372 - 0.010 = 5.362$ m above NA. $$Q_{NA} = (0.2364)(2.686) + (0.400)(5.362) = 0.6349 + 2.1448 = 2.7797 \text{ m}^3$$ Effective breadth at NA: $b_{NA} = 2 \cdot t_s = 0.044$ m. $$\tau_{NA} = \frac{4000 \times 2.7797}{22.233 \times 0.044} = \frac{11118.8}{0.97825} = 11365 \text{ kN/m}^2 \approx 11.37 \text{ MPa}$$ **Level 2: Top of Side Shell ($z = 10$ m, bottom of deck):** Area above this level: just the deck plate. $$Q_{top} = A_d \cdot (D - \bar{y} - t_d/2) = (0.400)(5.362) = 2.1448 \text{ m}^3$$ Effective breadth: $b = t_d = 0.020$ m (just deck thickness) â€” but to compare with side shell, take $b = 2 t_s = 0.044$ m at the level just below deck. Actually, at exactly the deck level (in the deck plating), $b = t_d = 0.020$ m, giving: $$\tau_{deck} = \frac{4000 \times 2.1448}{22.233 \times 0.020} = \frac{8579.2}{0.44466} = 19294 \text{ kN/m}^2 \approx 19.29 \text{ MPa}$$ **Level 3: Bottom of Side Shell ($z = 0.025$ m, top of bottom plating):** Area above this level: side shells + deck. For the side shells, total area = $0.440$ mآ², centroid at $\bar{y}_s = 5.000$ m, distance from NA = $5.000 - 4.628 = 0.372$ m. For the deck, contribution as before = $(0.400)(5.362) = 2.1448$ mآ³. $$Q_{bot} = (0.440)(0.372) + 2.1448 = 0.1637 + 2.1448 = 2.3085 \text{ m}^3$$ Effective breadth at this level: $b = 2 t_s = 0.044$ m. $$\tau_{bot} = \frac{4000 \times 2.3085}{22.233 \times 0.044} = \frac{9234.0}{0.97825} = 9439.6 \text{ kN/m}^2 \approx 9.44 \text{ MPa}$$ **Level 4: Mid-height between NA and Deck ($z = (\bar{y} + D)/2 = 7.314$ m):** Area above this level: - Side shell from $7.314$ to $10$ m: $2 \times 0.022 \times 2.686 = 0.1182$ mآ². Centroid at $7.314 + 2.686/2 = 8.657$ m, distance from NA = $8.657 - 4.628 = 4.029$ m. - Deck plate: $(0.400)(5.362) = 2.1448$ mآ³. $$Q_{mid} = (0.1182)(4.029) + 2.1448 = 0.4763 + 2.1448 = 2.6211 \text{ m}^3$$ $b = 0.044$ m. $$\tau_{mid} = \frac{4000 \times 2.6211}{22.233 \times 0.044} = \frac{10484.4}{0.97825} = 10717.6 \text{ kN/m}^2 \approx 10.72 \text{ MPa}$$ **Summary Table:** | Level | Height $z$ (m) | $Q$ (mآ³) | $b$ (m) | $\tau$ (MPa) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Top of deck | 10.000 | 0 | 0.020 | 0 | | Bottom of deck | 9.990 | 2.1448 | 0.020 | 19.29 | | Top of side shell | 10.000 | 2.1448 | 0.044 | 9.74 | | Mid between NA and deck | 7.314 | 2.6211 | 0.044 | 10.72 | | Neutral axis | 4.628 | 2.7797 | 0.044 | **11.37** | | Bottom of side shell | 0.025 | 2.3085 | 0.044 | 9.44 | | Bottom of hull | 0 | 0 | 0.025 | 0 | **Conclusion:** Shear stress is maximum at the neutral axis (11.37 MPa), zero at the extreme fibres, and varies approximately parabolically through the side shell. The peak stress in the deck plating itself (19.29 MPa) is high due to the very small breadth (20 mm), but the deck is generally designed primarily for bending stress, with shear as a secondary check. **(14 marks)** --- #### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative) **Q:** A ship hull cross-section has the following effective longitudinal elements (after transformation to a single reference material with $E_{ref} = E_{steel}$): | Element | Effective breadth $b_{eff}$ (m) | Thickness $t$ (m) | Distance of centroid above baseline (m) | |---|---|---|---| | Bottom plate | 24.0 | 0.022 | 0.011 | | Side shell (combined) | 0.044 (treated as 2 plates each 0.022 thick over depth 12 m) | â€” | 6.000 | | Deck plate | 24.0 | 0.020 | 11.990 | | Aluminium superstructure (effective) | 8.0 | 0.012 | 12.500 | The hull depth (steel portion) is 12 m, and the superstructure rises 0.6 m above the deck. **(a)** Calculate the position of the neutral axis of the full effective section. Hence compute the second moment of area $I_{NA}$ of the effective section. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the section is subjected to a vertical shear force $V = 6000$ kN, calculate the shear stress at: (i) the neutral axis, (ii) the top of the steel deck, and (iii) at the mid-height of the steel hull. Present your results with units and physical interpretation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Neutral Axis Position:** Effective areas: - Bottom: $A_b = b_{eff} \cdot t = 24.0 \times 0.022 = 0.528$ mآ² (acting as a flange). - Side shell: $A_s = 0.044 \times 12 = 0.528$ mآ², centroid at 6.000 m. - Deck: $A_d = 24.0 \times 0.020 = 0.480$ mآ², centroid at 11.990 m. - Superstructure: $A_{ss} = 8.0 \times 0.012 = 0.096$ mآ², centroid at 12.500 m. Total effective area: $A_{tot} = 0.528 + 0.528 + 0.480 + 0.096 = 1.632$ mآ². Neutral axis (moments about baseline): $$\bar{y} = \frac{(0.528)(0.011) + (0.528)(6.000) + (0.480)(11.990) + (0.096)(12.500)}{1.632}$$ $$\bar{y} = \frac{0.005808 + 3.168 + 5.7552 + 1.200}{1.632} = \frac{10.1290}{1.632} = 6.207 \text{ m}$$ So **$\bar{y} = 6.207$ m above baseline** â€” note that the aluminium superstructure pulls the NA slightly upward (compared with 6.000 m for steel alone). Second moment of area (parallel axis, ignoring own moments of thin plating): $$I_{NA} = (0.528)(6.207 - 0.011)^2 + (0.528)(6.207 - 6.000)^2 + (0.480)(6.207 - 11.990)^2 + (0.096)(6.207 - 12.500)^2$$ $$I_{NA} = (0.528)(38.339) + (0.528)(0.0428) + (0.480)(33.451) + (0.096)(39.624)$$ $$I_{NA} = 20.243 + 0.0226 + 16.057 + 3.804 = 40.127 \text{ m}^4$$ **$I_{NA} = 40.127$ m$^4$** **(b) Shear Stresses:** **(i) At the neutral axis ($z = 6.207$ m):** Area above NA: - Upper portion of side shell: $0.044 \times (12 - 6.207) = 0.044 \times 5.793 = 0.2549$ mآ². Centroid at $6.207 + 5.793/2 = 9.104$ m, distance from NA = $2.897$ m. - Deck: $0.480$ mآ², centroid at $11.990$ m, distance from NA = $5.783$ m. - Superstructure: $0.096$ mآ², centroid at $12.500$ m, distance from NA = $6.293$ m. $$Q_{NA} = (0.2549)(2.897) + (0.480)(5.783) + (0.096)(6.293)$$ $$Q_{NA} = 0.7384 + 2.7758 + 0.6041 = 4.1183 \text{ m}^3$$ Effective breadth at NA (still in side shell): $b = 0.044$ m. $$\tau_{NA} = \frac{6000 \times 4.1183}{40.127 \times 0.044} = \frac{24710}{1.7656} = 13995 \text{ kN/m}^2 \approx 14.00 \text{ MPa}$$ **(ii) At top of steel deck ($z = 12.000$ m, just at the deck-shell junction):** Area above: just the deck and superstructure. - Deck: $(0.480)(5.783) = 2.776$ mآ³. - Superstructure: $(0.096)(6.293) = 0.604$ mآ³. $$Q_{topdeck} = 2.776 + 0.604 = 3.380 \text{ m}^3$$ $b = 0.044$ m (just below deck level). $$\tau_{topdeck} = \frac{6000 \times 3.380}{40.127 \times 0.044} = \frac{20280}{1.7656} = 11486 \text{ kN/m}^2 \approx 11.49 \text{ MPa}$$ **(iii) At mid-height of steel hull ($z = 6.000$ m):** This is *just below* the NA (which is at 6.207 m). The side shell here has effective breadth $b = 0.044$ m. Area above: - Upper portion of side shell from $z = 6.000$ to $z = 12$: $0.044 \times 6 = 0.264$ mآ². Centroid at $(6.000 + 12)/2 = 9.000$ m, distance from NA = $9.000 - 6.207 = 2.793$ m. - Deck: $(0.480)(5.783) = 2.776$ mآ³. - Superstructure: $(0.096)(6.293) = 0.604$ mآ³. $$Q_{mid} = (0.264)(2.793) + 2.776 + 0.604 = 0.7374 + 2.776 + 0.604 = 4.1174 \text{ m}^3$$ $$\tau_{mid} = \frac{6000 \times 4.1174}{40.127 \times 0.044} = \frac{24704}{1.7656} = 13991 \text{ kN/m}^2 \approx 13.99 \text{ MPa}$$ (Note: Very close to $\tau_{NA}$ as expected, since the side shell has near-uniform contribution just below the NA.) **Physical Interpretation:** The shear stress at the neutral axis (14.00 MPa) is the maximum in the steel hull section. The presence of the aluminium superstructure, despite its lower Young's modulus (which is why $b_{eff}$ is small at 8.0 m for what would be a wider actual breadth), contributes meaningfully to $Q$ because it is far from the NA. Designers must verify that the side shell plating and the side-shell-to-deck-girder connection can safely carry this stress. The high shear at the deck-shell junction (11.49 MPa) justifies the use of **bracket connections** and **gusseted transitions** at this critical location. **(14 marks)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Evaluations:** > 1. **Forgetting the Effective Breadth Transformation** â€” When the problem states multiple materials (e.g., mild steel + high-tensile steel + aluminium), failing to apply $b_{eff} = n \cdot b$ leads to **incorrect $I$ and NA**, and *all subsequent marks are forfeited*. This is the single largest cause of zero-mark answers. > 2. **Misidentifying $Q$** â€” Many students compute $Q$ using the portion *below* the cut instead of *above*, and forget to include the *own* first moment of partial plating elements (e.g., the half side-shell above NA). Always state explicitly which side you are using. > 3. **Using Wrong Breadth $b$** â€” At a level inside the side shell, $b = 2 t_s$ (both sides). At a level inside the deck or bottom plating, $b = t_d$ or $t_b$ (single plate). Mixing these up is a 2-mark error. > 4. **Sign Errors in Centroid Distance** â€” When using the parallel axis theorem, $(\bar{y} - y_i)^2$ must use the correct orientation. Distance is always positive (or squared), but the NA offset sign matters when computing $Q$ contributions. > 5. **Forgetting Units** â€” KTU answer scripts must show units throughout. Writing "$11.37$" without MPa or kN/mآ² attracts a 0.5-mark deduction per occurrence. > 6. **Skipping the Plot/Interpretation Step** â€” The procedure explicitly requires a shear stress distribution sketch. A neat sketch with the maximum location marked earns the final 1 mark â€” students frequently skip this. > 7. **No Equilibrium Check** â€” A strong answer includes the verification $\int \tau \, dA = V$ or an equivalent check. This signals mastery and earns bonus consideration in marginal cases. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Core Formula (Memorise):** $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$ â€” flexural shear stress in any beam/ship hull cross-section. - **Shear Flow Companion Formula:** $q = VQ/I$ â€” useful for stiffener design and connection proportioning. - **Procedure Sequence (7 Steps):** 1. Identify longitudinal elements. 2. Apply effective breadth transformation for multi-material sections. 3. Locate NA via $\bar{y} = \sum A_i y_i / \sum A_i$. 4. Compute $I_{NA}$ via parallel axis theorem. 5. At chosen levels, compute $Q$ (first moment of area on one side of cut). 6. Compute effective breadth $b$ at the same level. 7. Apply $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$. - **Boundary Condition:** $\tau = 0$ at the extreme fibres (top of deck and bottom of bottom plate). - **Maximum Location:** Maximum $\tau$ occurs at the neutral axis (for symmetric sections, at mid-depth). - **Side Shell Contribution:** The side shell (and any longitudinal bulkheads) carry the bulk of the shear flow because they have small $b$ (thickness only), giving high $\tau$. - **Deck and Bottom Plating:** Despite having large area, they have very large $b$ at the level of the plating, so the resulting $\tau$ in the plating is moderate. - **Multi-Material Sections:** Always use $b_{eff} = n \cdot b$ where $n = E_i / E_{ref}$. - **Units Discipline:** Use SI throughout â€” $V$ in N or kN, $Q$ in mآ³, $I$ in mâپ´, $b$ in m, $\tau$ in Pa or kPa or MPa. - **Asymmetric Sections:** The NA is *not* at mid-depth when the deck and bottom have different thicknesses or when the superstructure is asymmetric â€” always compute it. - **Engineering Judgement:** High shear at the deck-shell junction requires **bracket connections** and **gussets** in real ship design â€” your answer should reflect this understanding. - **Equilibrium Check:** A complete solution verifies $\int \tau \, dA = V$ or uses a tabular area-under-curve check. ---

topics.title: Limit States - Ultimate
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Limit States - Ultimate

# âڑ“ Limit States â€” Ultimate (ULS) in Ship Structural Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Aligned with IACS Common Structural Rules / CSR & ISO 12215):**
> The **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** of a ship structure is that state which, if exceeded, will lead to structural collapse, loss of the hull girder's load-carrying capacity, or endangerment of the vessel. It corresponds to the **maximum design load** the structure can sustain immediately before failure, where failure is defined by reaching the **yield strength** of the material, **buckling instability**, **rupture**, or **progressive collapse** (transformation of the structure into a mechanism).

### ًں”§ Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **paper clip** that you keep bending back and forth. Initially, it springs back perfectly â€” this is the **Serviceability Limit State (SLS)**, the structure is still "working as intended." Keep bending, and it starts to deform permanently â€” the **yield limit** is crossed. Eventually, the wire **snaps** â€” that is the **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)**.

In a ship:
- The **hull girder** is the "paper clip" of the vessel.
- The **still-water bending moment** is the gentle flexing.
- A **storm wave** may push the structure toward its ULS â€” yielding, buckling of plates, or even progressive collapse of the double bottom.
- ULS is therefore the **"point of no return"** â€” the last barrier before catastrophe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant / Standard Reference for ULS in Ship Design:**
> - **Material factor for steel yield (IACS CSR):** $\gamma_M = \mathbf{1.02}$
> - **Material factor for steel ultimate tensile (IACS CSR):** $\gamma_M = \mathbf{1.05}$
> - **Design yield stress for mild steel (AH36):** $R_{eH} = \mathbf{355\ \text{MPa}}$
> - **Young's Modulus of ship steel:** $E = \mathbf{2.06 \times 10^{5}\ \text{MPa}}$

### Limit State Hierarchy (SLS vs. ULS vs. FLS)

| Limit State | Trigger Condition | Design Philosophy |
|---|---|---|
| **SLS** â€” Serviceability | Excessive deflection, vibration, local buckling | Stiffness / function |
| **ULS** â€” Ultimate | Yielding, rupture, instability, mechanism | **Strength / collapse** |
| **FLS** â€” Fatigue | Cyclic crack initiation/growth | Durability / life |

The ULS is the *strictest* of the three because it deals with survival conditions such as extreme wave bending, slamming, green-water, and docking.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### ًں”¬ Conceptual Decomposition of ULS

A ship structural member is said to have reached its **Ultimate Limit State** when **any one** of the following physical conditions is attained:

1. **Material Yielding (Rupture Criterion)** â€” The equivalent stress (typically von Mises) reaches the design yield strength.
2. **Plate / Shell Buckling** â€” A plate or curved panel loses stability and snaps through to a new equilibrium shape, losing load capacity.
3. **Column / Stiffener Buckling (Euler or Inelastic)** â€” A longitudinal stiffener or web fails by overall or local instability under compression.
4. **Progressive Collapse (Hull Girder ULS)** â€” A damaged compartment floods; the residual cross-section cannot resist the still-water + wave bending moment; the hull girder breaks in two.
5. **Mechanism Formation** â€” Enough plastic hinges develop to convert the structure into a 4-bar linkage that can deform indefinitely at constant load.
6. **Fatigue Crack Reaching Critical Size** (sometimes ULS, more often FLS).

### ًں§  The Core "Why" â€” Why Do We Design for ULS?

Because the ship is a **floating engineering structure** subject to:
- **Quasi-static loads** (still water + wave-induced bending & shear),
- **Dynamic loads** (slamming, sloshing, whipping),
- **Accidental loads** (grounding, collision, flooding).

ULS design ensures that, under the **most unfavourable combination** of these loads (multiplied by partial safety factors), the **factored resistance** of the structure is still greater than the **factored effect of actions**.

> **Governing ULS Inequality:**
> $$E_d \le R_d$$
> where $E_d$ = design effect of actions, and $R_d$ = design resistance.

### ًں“ک KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Design effect of actions** | $E_d = \sum_{i} \gamma_{F,i}\,E_{k,i}$ | $\gamma_{F,i}$ = partial load factor |
| 2 | **Design resistance** | $R_d = \dfrac{R_k}{\gamma_M}$ | $\gamma_M$ = partial material factor |
| 3 | **ULS design equation** | $E_d \le R_d$ | Inequality form (must be satisfied) |
| 4 | **Partial load factor (still water)** | $\gamma_{F,sw} = \mathbf{1.0}$ | IACS CSR BC OT |
| 5 | **Partial load factor (wave)** | $\gamma_{F,w} = \mathbf{1.0\ \text{to}\ 1.3}$ | Depends on load case (e.g., 1.0 for sagging, 1.3 for harbour/squat) |
| 6 | **Partial load factor (dynamic / slamming)** | $\gamma_{F,dyn} = \mathbf{1.1}$ | Short-term dynamic |
| 7 | **Material factor (yield)** | $\gamma_{M0} = \mathbf{1.02}$ | IACS CSR, mild/high-tensile steel |
| 8 | **Material factor (ultimate)** | $\gamma_{M1} = \mathbf{1.05}$ | IACS CSR |
| 9 | **Von Mises yield criterion** | $\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$ | $E_d \le R_d$ condition |
| 10 | **Euler column buckling load** | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{(K L)^2}$ | $K$ = effective length factor |
| 11 | **Plate elastic buckling stress** | $\sigma_{cr,p} = \dfrac{k\, \pi^2 E}{12\,(1-\nu^2)} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$ | $k$ = buckling coefficient |
| 12 | **Ultimate bending moment capacity (rect. section)** | $M_u = Z_p \cdot f_y / \gamma_{M0}$ | $Z_p$ = plastic section modulus |
| 13 | **Hull girder ULS (progressive collapse)** | $M_{sw} + M_{wv}\,(1+\psi) \le M_{u,res}$ | Residual strength criterion |
| 14 | **Hogging moment sign** | Negative (deck in tension) | Convention varies |
| 15 | **Sagging moment sign** | Positive (keel in tension) | Convention varies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule Followed:** Vertical bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid` in table cells to avoid breaking the markdown table grid (e.g., $\gamma_{M0} = \mathbf{1.02}$ â€” no pipes used).

### ًںڈ—ï¸ڈ Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of ULS |
|---|---|
| **Bulk Carriers** | Hatch corner ULS against wave-induced wheeling loads |
| **Tankers** | Double-bottom ULS during accidental flooding |
| **Container Ships** | ULS of sheer strake under combined hogging + torsional moments |
| **Naval Frigates** | ULS under shock loading (UNDEX) |
| **Offshore Platforms** | ULS of tubular joints under wave slamming |
| **Yachts (ISO 12215-5)** | ULS scantling check for plate panels & stiffeners |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### ًں”چ Derivation 1 â€” Plastic Section Modulus of a Rectangular Plate Element (Used in ULS of Plate Panels)

We want the **ultimate bending moment** of a rectangular cross-section (width $b$, thickness $t$) about its neutral axis.

**Step 1 â€” Define the cross-section.**  
A rectangle of breadth $b$ and thickness $t$ bending about its horizontal centroidal axis.

**Step 2 â€” At ultimate state, the section is fully plastic.**  
The entire cross-section yields, and the stress distribution is two equal rectangular blocks:
- Top half (compression): $\sigma = +f_y$
- Bottom half (tension): $\sigma = -f_y$

**Step 3 â€” Compute the plastic neutral axis (PNA).**  
By symmetry, the PNA coincides with the centroidal axis.

**Step 4 â€” Compute the tension force in the bottom half.**  
$$F_T = f_y \cdot b \cdot \frac{t}{2}$$

**Step 5 â€” Compute the lever arm between the two force resultants.**  
The two rectangular blocks have centroids separated by:
$$z = \frac{t}{2} + \frac{t}{4} = \frac{3t}{4}$$

Wait â€” let us carefully compute: top block is from $+t/2$ to $0$ (centroid at $+t/4$); bottom block is from $0$ to $-t/2$ (centroid at $-t/4$).

Distance between centroids:
$$z = \frac{t}{4} - \left(-\frac{t}{4}\right) = \frac{t}{2}$$

**Step 6 â€” Ultimate moment of resistance.**  
Taking moment about the PNA:
$$M_{u} = F_T \cdot z = f_y \cdot b \cdot \frac{t}{2} \cdot \frac{t}{2}$$

Therefore:
$$M_{u} = \frac{f_y \, b \, t^2}{4}$$

Equivalently, the **plastic section modulus** for a rectangle about its strong axis is:
$$Z_p = \frac{b\,t^2}{4}$$

and therefore:
$$M_{u} = Z_p \cdot f_y$$

**Step 7 â€” Apply the partial safety factor (IACS CSR).**  
$$M_{Rd} = \dfrac{Z_p \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{M0}} = \dfrac{Z_p \cdot f_y}{1.02}$$

**Step 8 â€” Final ULS check.**  
$$\boxed{M_{Ed} = \gamma_F \cdot M_{Ek} \le M_{Rd} = \frac{Z_p f_y}{\gamma_{M0}}}$$

---

### ًں”چ Derivation 2 â€” Euler Buckling Load for a Ship's Longitudinal Stiffener (ULS of a Web-Stiffened Panel)

A longitudinal stiffener of length $l$ acts as a column supported by the plating (which acts as a continuous elastic foundation). For a pin-ended approximation:

**Step 1 â€” Define the Euler critical load.**  
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(K L)^2}$$

**Step 2 â€” For a stiffener built-in at both ends (T-section continuous over multiple spans), $K = 0.7$.**

**Step 3 â€” Substitute $K = 0.7$, $L = $ span between transverse web frames (typical: $L = 3\ \text{m}$ for a container ship).**

For a T-stiffener with:
- Web: $h_w = 500\ \text{mm}$, $t_w = 12\ \text{mm}$
- Flange: $b_f = 100\ \text{mm}$, $t_f = 16\ \text{mm}$
- $E = 2.06 \times 10^5\ \text{MPa}$

**Step 4 â€” Compute the second moment of area about the neutral axis of the T-section** (approximated for ULS prelim check):
$$I = \frac{t_w h_w^3}{12} + b_f t_f \left(\frac{h_w}{2} - \frac{t_f}{2}\right)^2 \approx 1.45 \times 10^8\ \text{mm}^4$$

**Step 5 â€” Compute $P_{cr}$.**  
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot (2.06 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.45 \times 10^8)}{(0.7 \cdot 3000)^2}$$

**Step 6 â€” Numerator evaluation.**  
$$\pi^2 \cdot 2.06 \times 10^5 \cdot 1.45 \times 10^8 \approx 2.948 \times 10^{14}$$

**Step 7 â€” Denominator evaluation.**  
$$(0.7 \cdot 3000)^2 = (2100)^2 = 4.41 \times 10^6$$

**Step 8 â€” Final value.**  
$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.948 \times 10^{14}}{4.41 \times 10^6} \approx 6.685 \times 10^7\ \text{N} \approx 66.85\ \text{MN}$$

**Step 9 â€” ULS check.**  
Applied factored axial load: $P_{Ed} = \gamma_F \cdot P_{Ek} = 1.1 \cdot 40\ \text{MN} = 44\ \text{MN}$.  
Since $P_{Ed} = 44\ \text{MN} \le 66.85\ \text{MN} = P_{cr}$, the ULS is satisfied. âœ…

---

### ًں”چ Derivation 3 â€” Hull Girder Progressive Collapse (Flooded ULS)

After a side collision, one compartment of a double-hull tanker is flooded. The **residual cross-section** must still resist the still-water + wave bending moment.

**Step 1 â€” Still water bending moment.**  
$$M_{sw} = C_w \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot (C_B + 0.7) \cdot 10^{-3}$$
(Empirical formula, longitudinal strength.)

**Step 2 â€” Wave bending moment amplitude (IACS).**  
$$M_{wv} = k \cdot L^{2} \cdot B \quad \text{[kNآ·m]}$$

**Step 3 â€” Total factored design moment in damaged condition.**  
$$M_{Ed,\text{dam}} = \gamma_{F,sw} M_{sw} + \gamma_{F,w} M_{wv}$$

**Step 4 â€” Residual ultimate moment of damaged section.**  
$$M_{Rd,\text{res}} = \dfrac{\sum_i Z_{p,i}\, f_{y,i}}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

**Step 5 â€” ULS check for the damaged hull girder.**  
$$\boxed{M_{Ed,\text{dam}} \le M_{Rd,\text{res}}}$$

If this inequality fails, the ship **progressively collapses** â€” the damaged compartment "unzips" the hull girder.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### ًں§­ Mermaid Block â€” Ultimate Limit State Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ULS Analysis Trigger] --> B[Load Combination Assembly]
    B --> C[Factored Loads F_d = gamma_F x F_k]
    C --> D[Global Hull Girder Check]
    C --> E[Local Plate Panel Check]
    C --> F[Local Stiffener Check]
    C --> G[Detail / Hot Spot Check]
    D --> D1[Yielding Criterion]
    D --> D2[Hull Girder Progressive Collapse]
    D --> D3[Von Mises Equivalent Stress]
    E --> E1[Plate Buckling sigma_cr]
    E --> E2[Plate Yielding sigma_y]
    E --> E3[Ultimate Plate Strength sigma_u]
    F --> F1[Euler Column Buckling P_cr]
    F --> F2[Inelastic Column Buckling]
    F --> F3[Stiffener Torsional Buckling]
    G --> G1[Bracket Toe Stress]
    G --> G2[Hatch Corner Stress]
    G1 --> H{Resistance >= Demand}
    G2 --> H
    D1 --> H
    D2 --> H
    D3 --> H
    E1 --> H
    E2 --> H
    E3 --> H
    F1 --> H
    F2 --> H
    F3 --> H
    H -->|YES| I[ULS Satisfied OK]
    H -->|NO| J[Redesign Increase Scantling]
    J --> B
    I --> K[Move to FLS Fatigue Check]
```

**Caption:** The flow above shows the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a ULS assessment in a ship. The system first assembles **factored loads**, then dispatches them to four parallel check levels: **Global (hull girder)**, **Plate Panel**, **Stiffener**, and **Detail**. Each level computes its own design resistance ($R_d$). All four must satisfy $E_d \le R_d$ before passing on to the **Fatigue Limit State (FLS)** check.

### ًںژ¯ Schematic â€” Hierarchy of ULS Failure Modes in a Ship

```mermaid
graph LR
    SHIP[Ship Structure] --> HG[Hull Girder]
    SHIP --> PL[Plating]
    SHIP --> ST[Stiffeners]
    HG --> HGF1[Progressive Collapse]
    HG --> HGF2[Yielding of Deck or Bottom]
    PL --> PLF1[Elastic Plate Buckling]
    PL --> PLF2[Plastic Plate Collapse]
    ST --> STF1[Column Buckling]
    ST --> STF2[Lateral Torsional Buckling]
    ST --> STF3[Web Shear Yielding]
```

**Caption:** Visualising the **three-tier hierarchy** of ULS in ship structures: **GLOBAL â†’ LOCAL â†’ DETAIL**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### ًں“ڑ Part A â€” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam â€” Dec 2023]**
> **Define the Ultimate Limit State (ULS) of a ship structure. How does it differ from the Serviceability Limit State (SLS)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Ultimate Limit State is the state beyond which the structure loses its ability to carry further load and approaches collapse. It corresponds to yielding, rupture, buckling, or mechanism formation.

**Difference Table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | ULS | SLS |
|---|---|---|
| Concern | Strength / collapse | Stiffness / function |
| Failure mode | Yield, buckle, rupture | Excessive deflection, vibration |
| Load level | Factored extreme loads | Service / working loads |
| Consequence | Loss of vessel | Loss of function |

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam â€” June 2024]**
> **State the general ULS design equation and explain the role of partial safety factors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Design equation (1 mark):** $E_d \le R_d$, where $E_d$ is the design effect of actions and $R_d$ is the design resistance.

**Partial safety factors (2 marks):**
- $\gamma_F > 1$ amplifies loads to account for **uncertainty in magnitude** (e.g., wave height, slamming pressure).
- $\gamma_M > 1$ reduces material strength to account for **uncertainty in quality** (e.g., yield variability, welding defects).
- Together they deliver a **target reliability index** $\beta \approx 3.8$ (IACS CSR target for ULS).

---

### ًں“‌ Part B â€” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Question A â€” [KTU University Exam â€” Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Module 3]**
> **(a)** Explain the **different failure modes** associated with the Ultimate Limit State of a **stiffened plate panel** in a ship's bottom plating. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A simply-supported **plate panel** of a ship's bottom is $a = 2400\ \text{mm}$ long, $b = 800\ \text{mm}$ wide, and $t = 16\ \text{mm}$ thick. The plate is made of mild steel with $f_y = 235\ \text{MPa}$, $E = 2.06 \times 10^5\ \text{MPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$, and is subjected to a uniformly distributed in-plane longitudinal compressive load $N_x = 1.2\ \text{MN/m}$. The buckling coefficient for the simply-supported plate is $k = 4.0$. Check the ULS of the plate using both **yielding** and **buckling** criteria. Use $\gamma_{F} = 1.1$ and $\gamma_{M0} = 1.02$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) â€” Failure modes (7 marks):**
1. **Material yielding** of the plate (1 mark)
2. **Elastic plate buckling** under in-plane compression (1 mark)
3. **Inelastic / post-buckling** behaviour (1 mark)
4. **Ultimate plate collapse** after post-buckling reserve (1 mark)
5. **Stiffener column (Euler) buckling** (1 mark)
6. **Stiffener lateral-torsional / tripping** (1 mark)
7. **Stiffener web / flange local buckling** (1 mark)

**Part (b) â€” Numerical ULS Check (7 marks):**

**Step 1 â€” Factored load (1 mark).**  
$$N_{Ed} = \gamma_F \cdot N_{Ek} = 1.1 \times 1.2 = 1.32\ \text{MN/m}$$

**Step 2 â€” Yielding criterion (1 mark).**  
$$\sigma_{Ed} = \frac{N_{Ed}}{t} = \frac{1.32 \times 10^3}{0.016} = 82{,}500\ \text{kPa} = 82.5\ \text{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{Rd} = \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{M0}} = \frac{235}{1.02} = 230.39\ \text{MPa}$$

Since $\sigma_{Ed} = 82.5\ \text{MPa} \le 230.39\ \text{MPa} = \sigma_{Rd}$, **yielding is safe.** âœ…

**Step 3 â€” Elastic plate buckling stress (2 marks).**  
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k\, \pi^2 E}{12\,(1-\nu^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$

Substitute:
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{4.0 \cdot \pi^2 \cdot (2.06 \times 10^5)}{12\,(1-0.3^2)} \cdot \left(\frac{16}{800}\right)^2$$

Numerator of coefficient: $4.0 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 2.06 \times 10^5 = 8.132 \times 10^6$

Denominator of coefficient: $12 \cdot 0.91 = 10.92$

$$= \frac{8.132 \times 10^6}{10.92} \cdot (0.02)^2 = 744{,}700 \cdot 0.0004 = 297.9\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 â€” Compare with yield (slenderness check) (1 mark).**  
$$\frac{f_y}{\sigma_{cr}} = \frac{235}{297.9} = 0.789$$

Since $f_y / \sigma_{cr} < 1$, the plate is **stocky** and the ultimate strength equals the yield strength: $\sigma_u = f_y$.

**Step 5 â€” ULS check by buckling criterion (1 mark).**  
$$\sigma_{Ed} = 82.5\ \text{MPa} \le \sigma_{Rd} = 230.39\ \text{MPa}$$

**ULS is satisfied** for both yielding and buckling. âœ…

**Step 6 â€” Conclusion (1 mark):**  
The plate has adequate ultimate strength; no thickening is required.

---

> **Question B â€” [KTU University Exam â€” June 2024, Model Paper 2, Module 3] (Internal Choice Alternative)**
> **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **progressive collapse mechanism** of a ship's hull girder under extreme hogging moment. Discuss the role of the **residual ultimate moment capacity**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A bulk carrier has a midship section whose effective plastic section modulus in intact condition is $Z_{p,\text{intact}} = 18.5\ \text{m}^3$. The yield stress is $f_y = 315\ \text{MPa}$ (AH32 steel). After a side collision, one side compartment is flooded, reducing the effective plastic section modulus to $Z_{p,\text{res}} = 11.0\ \text{m}^3$. The factored design bending moment in the damaged condition is $M_{Ed,\text{dam}} = 2.95 \times 10^6\ \text{kNآ·m}$. Using $\gamma_{M0} = 1.02$, check the **progressive collapse (ULS)** of the damaged hull girder. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) â€” Progressive Collapse Explanation (7 marks):**
1. Define hogging â€” **deck in tension, bottom in compression** (1 mark)
2. In extreme hogging, **bottom plating / inner bottom** yields first (1 mark)
3. As bottom yields, the **neutral axis migrates upward** (1 mark)
4. **Topside deck/bottom** loses contribution; **compressive structures** (bilge, side shell) carry more (1 mark)
5. After one compartment flood: cross-section weakened; **local instability** of side shell triggers (1 mark)
6. **Snap-through buckling** spreads â†’ plastic hinge spreads along length â†’ **mechanism** (1 mark)
7. **Residual ultimate moment $M_{u,\text{res}}$** must exceed applied moment, else collapse (1 mark)

**Part (b) â€” Numerical ULS Check (7 marks):**

**Step 1 â€” Compute ultimate moment capacity in damaged condition (2 marks).**  
$$M_{Rd,\text{res}} = \frac{Z_{p,\text{res}} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{M0}} = \frac{11.0 \times 315 \times 10^3}{1.02}\ \text{kNآ·m}$$

Wait â€” careful with units: $Z_{p,\text{res}} = 11.0\ \text{m}^3 = 11.0 \times 10^9\ \text{mm}^3$? No, keep consistent in **m, MPa**:

$$M_{Rd,\text{res}} = \frac{11.0\ \text{m}^3 \cdot 315 \times 10^3\ \text{kN/m}^2}{1.02}$$

$$= \frac{11.0 \cdot 315{,}000}{1.02} = \frac{3.465 \times 10^6}{1.02} = 3.397 \times 10^6\ \text{kNآ·m}$$

**Step 2 â€” Compare with design moment (2 marks).**  
$$M_{Ed,\text{dam}} = 2.95 \times 10^6\ \text{kNآ·m}$$

$$M_{Rd,\text{res}} - M_{Ed,\text{dam}} = 3.397 \times 10^6 - 2.95 \times 10^6 = 0.447 \times 10^6\ \text{kNآ·m}$$

**Step 3 â€” Reserve margin (1 mark).**  
$$\text{Margin} = \frac{0.447}{2.95} \times 100\% \approx 15.1\%$$

**Step 4 â€” ULS decision (1 mark).**  
Since $M_{Ed,\text{dam}} = 2.95 \times 10^6\ \text{kNآ·m} \le 3.397 \times 10^6\ \text{kNآ·m} = M_{Rd,\text{res}}$, **ULS is satisfied** with ~15% reserve. âœ…

**Step 5 â€” Engineering comment (1 mark):**  
The 15% margin is acceptable per IACS CSR (target $\beta \approx 3.0$ for damaged condition). However, if the flooded compartment were wider, the margin would shrink and additional stiffening of the side shell would be required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **ًںڑ¨ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse ULS with SLS** â€” many students mistakenly apply the *working* (un-factored) moment to a *factored* resistance. Always multiply the load by $\gamma_F$.
> 2. **Don't forget units** â€” converting between Nآ·m, kNآ·m, MPaآ·mآ³ is where most students lose 1â€“2 marks. Use the SI-mnemonic: **1 MPa أ— 1 mآ³ = 10آ³ kNآ·m**.
> 3. **For buckling problems, ALWAYS check slenderness** â€” if $\sigma_{cr} < f_y$, do not equate $\sigma_u$ to $f_y$. Use the **Winter / Johnson column curve** instead.
> 4. **For progressive collapse, the **damaged** condition uses a *lower* reliability target** (خ² â‰ˆ 3.0 vs 3.8) â€” students often over-design.
> 5. **Plastic section modulus â‰  elastic section modulus** â€” the difference is **the shape factor** $f = Z_p / Z_e$. For I-sections, $f \approx 1.14$.

---

### âœ… Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ًں”¹ **ULS** = state of incipient collapse â€” governed by strength, not stiffness.
- ًں”¹ **Core inequality:** $E_d \le R_d$.
- ًں”¹ **Partial safety factors** (IACS CSR): $\gamma_{M0} = 1.02$ (yield), $\gamma_{M1} = 1.05$ (ultimate), $\gamma_F = 1.0$â€“$1.3$ depending on load.
- ًں”¹ **Five canonical ULS failure modes** in ships: yielding, plate buckling, column buckling, progressive collapse, mechanism.
- ًں”¹ **Plate slenderness rule of thumb:** if $\beta = (b/t)\sqrt{f_y/E} \le 1.0$, plate is stocky and $\sigma_u = f_y$.
- ًں”¹ **Hull girder ULS** must be checked in **intact** AND **damaged (flooded)** conditions.
- ًں”¹ **Material:** mild steel (235 MPa), AH32 (315 MPa), AH36 (355 MPa), EH36 (355 MPa).
- ًں”¹ **Reference standards:** IACS CSR (Bulk Carriers & Oil Tankers), ISO 12215 (Yachts), Common Rules for Naval vessels.
- ًں”¹ **ULS comes after SLS, before FLS** in the design verification chain.
- ًں”¹ **Always include $\gamma_{M0}$ for yielding** and $\gamma_{M1}$ for buckling (when explicitly specified by rules).
- ًں”¹ **Progressive collapse margin** typically targeted at 10â€“20% above the design damaged-condition moment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Serviceability
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Serviceability

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the framework of **Limit State Design (LSD)** applied to ship structures, **Serviceability** refers to the **Serviceability Limit State (SLS)** â€” a condition in which a structural member, assembly, or the hull girder as a whole remains fit for its intended operational use, ensuring occupant comfort, functional operability of equipment, and an aesthetically/structurally acceptable appearance, *without* exhibiting damage, deterioration, or deformation beyond predefined engineering tolerances.

Mathematically, the Serviceability Limit State is the bound on structural response governed by:

$$S_d \leq C_{lim}$$

where $S_d$ is the **design serviceability action effect** (deflection, vibration amplitude, crack width, local stress under quasi-permanent loads) and $C_{lim}$ is the **prescribed serviceability limit** (e.g., $\delta_{lim}$, $a_{lim}$, $f_{n,min}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight â€” Module 3:**
> Serviceability in ship structures covers: (i) deflection criteria for plates, panels, beams, and bulkheads, (ii) vibration limits for human comfort and equipment operability, (iii) local damage tolerance (corrosion, fatigue cracking), and (iv) alignment with **IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR)** and **Classification Society** guidelines (DNV, LR, ABS, IRS).

## 1.2 Three Pillars of the Serviceability Limit State

| Pillar | Engineering Concern | Ship-Structure Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Functionality** | Structure performs its design role | Watertight hatch covers sealing properly, cargo gear operating |
| **Comfort** | Human acceptability of motion/noise | Crew accommodation vibration, bridge motion sickness |
| **Appearance** | Aesthetic / durability | Visible hull plate deflection, paint cracking, water ingress |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy â€” The "Serviceability = Daily Comfort" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine driving a car over a speed bump. The car must not only **not break** (that's the **Ultimate Limit State â€” ULS**, structural collapse / yielding) but also must **not bottom out, rattle violently, or throw passengers out of their seats** (that's **Serviceability â€” SLS**, deflection and vibration control).
>
> In a ship, the hull must not only resist breaking in heavy seas (ULS) but also must not **deflect so much that watertight doors jam**, must not **vibrate so severely that crew in the engine room become nauseous**, and must not **crack paint and coatings causing accelerated corrosion** (SLS).

## 1.4 Distinction: ULS vs. SLS in Ship Design

| Aspect | Ultimate Limit State (ULS) | Serviceability Limit State (SLS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Governing Concern** | Safety against collapse / yielding | Functional performance under normal use |
| **Load Level** | Maximum (extreme sea state) | Quasi-permanent / frequent (operating sea state) |
| **Load Factor $\gamma_f$** | $\approx 1.2 - 1.3$ (higher) | $\approx 1.0$ (lower, often unfactored) |
| **Partial Safety Factor $\gamma_M$** | Reduced material factor | Material parameters at mean / characteristic |
| **Typical Check** | $\sigma_{von Mises} \leq \sigma_{yield} / \gamma_M$ | $\delta_{actual} \leq \delta_{lim}$ |
| **Failure Consequence** | Loss of life / vessel | Discomfort / operational downtime / repair cost |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Engineering Philosophy Behind Serviceability Design

The reasoning chain for designing ship structures for SLS proceeds in **four ordered steps**:

1. **Identify the serviceability scenario** â€” e.g., a plate panel vibrating at resonance due to main-engine excitation, or a hatch cover deflecting under green-sea loading.
2. **Determine the governing action effect** â€” deflection $\delta$, acceleration $\ddot{z}$, natural frequency $f_n$, or stress range $\Delta\sigma$.
3. **Apply the partial factor / load combination** â€” quasi-permanent SLS combination per IACS / class rules.
4. **Compare against the prescribed limit** â€” e.g., $f_n \geq 5$ Hz for accommodation, or $\delta \leq L/250$ for stiffened panels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SLS governs design more often than ULS in ships:**
> Modern high-tensile steel hulls are rarely strength-critical under normal service; instead, **deflection and vibration of slender plate/stiffener combinations** typically drive scantling decisions for superstructures, deckhouses, and accommodation blocks.

## 2.2 Deflection-Based Serviceability Criteria

### 2.2.1 Stiffened Plate Panels (Bottom, Side Shell, Decks)

For a stiffened panel spanning between primary support members (e.g., between web frames or transverse bulkheads), the **local deflection limit** is universally cited as:

$$\boxed{\delta_{panel} \leq \dfrac{L_{span}}{250}}$$

where $L_{span}$ is the stiffener span between primary supports.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The denominator **250** corresponds to the **"L/250" rule** of DNV, LR, and IACS CSR for plating and ordinary stiffeners under service (quasi-static) loads. For higher-grade accommodation / wheelhouse plating, the limit tightens to **L/360** or even **L/500**.

### 2.2.2 Primary Hull Girder Deflection (Global)

For the hull girder as a whole under still-water bending, the hogging/sagging deflection at amidships is bounded by:

$$\boxed{w_{stillwater} \leq \dfrac{L_{BP}}{500}}$$

where $L_{BP}$ is the length between perpendiculars. This is to prevent:
- Excessive propeller immersion variation
- Bow flare / slamming intensification
- Misalignment of shaft-line bearings

### 2.2.3 Bulkhead Deflection (Plating Between Stiffeners)

For corrugated or flat bulkheads, the plate deflection between face stiffeners is limited to:

$$\boxed{\delta_{plate} \leq \dfrac{s_{stiff}}{150}}$$

where $s_{stiff}$ is the stiffener spacing. (Some class rules permit up to 2 أ— plate thickness in absolute terms; the **whichever is smaller** rule applies.)

## 2.3 Vibration-Based Serviceability Criteria

### 2.3.1 Natural Frequency Requirement (Avoiding Resonance)

A structure must be designed such that its lowest natural frequency $f_n$ lies **outside the excitation frequency band** of machinery and wave loading:

$$\boxed{f_n \geq f_{exc,max} \times \sqrt{2}} \quad \text{or} \quad f_n \leq \dfrac{f_{exc,min}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\sqrt{2}$ creates a **safety margin of $\approx 40\%$** to avoid the resonance build-up zone, which is conventionally the range $0.8\,f_n$ to $1.25\,f_n$ for lightly-damped marine structures.

### 2.3.2 Human Comfort â€” Acceleration Limit (ISO 6954 / DNV)

For crew comfort in accommodation spaces, the **RMS vertical vibration acceleration** must satisfy:

$$\boxed{a_{RMS,vert} \leq 0.315 \, \text{m/s}^2} \quad \text{(1-hour average, comfort threshold)}$$

$$\boxed{a_{RMS,vert} \leq 0.500 \, \text{m/s}^2} \quad \text{(operability / safety threshold, with reduced comfort)}$$

For **horizontal (transverse) vibration**, the limit is approximately **$\frac{2}{3}$** of the vertical threshold due to human-body lateral sensitivity.

### 2.3.3 Local Plate Vibration (Sloshing / Pinging)

For plates subject to slamming or sloshing-induced excitation, the critical natural frequency is:

$$f_{n,plate} = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{2\pi a^2} \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho_s h}}$$

where:
- $D = \dfrac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ is the **flexural rigidity per unit width** (Nآ·m)
- $h$ = plate thickness (m)
- $a$ = shorter plate dimension (m)
- $\rho_s$ = mass density of plate ($\approx 7850$ kg/mآ³ for steel)
- $\lambda$ = 9.87 for simply-supported, 14.10 for clamped
- $E$ = Young's modulus, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio

The **serviceability check** is then $f_{n,plate} \geq 7 - 10$ Hz depending on excitation source.

## 2.4 Local Damage / Durability Serviceability

This covers progressive degradation that impairs function before collapse:

| Degradation Mode | Serviceability Metric | Typical Limit |
|---|---|---|
| **Corrosion** | Thickness loss $t_{corr}$ | $t_{as-built} - t_{corr} \geq t_{net,req}$ |
| **Fatigue cracking** | Crack length $a$ | $a \leq a_{critical,NDT}$ |
| **Abrasion** (bottom plating) | $t_{abraded}$ | $t_{as-built} - t_{abr} \geq t_{min,class}$ |
| **Coating breakdown** | Area % deteriorated | $\leq 20\%$ in critical zones |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion addition (wastage allowance):** IACS CSR mandates minimum **corrosion addition $t_c$** added to the net required thickness. Typical values: $t_c = 0.5$ to $2.0$ mm for ballast tanks, $0.2$ to $0.5$ mm for outer hull. This is a **serviceability-driven** increase, not strength-driven.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Serviceability Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stiffener / panel deflection limit | $\delta \leq L/250$ | m or mm | DNV / LR rule of thumb |
| 2 | Hull girder still-water deflection | $w_{sw} \leq L_{BP}/500$ | m | Global stiffness check |
| 3 | Bulkhead plate deflection | $\delta \leq s/150$ | m | s = stiffener spacing |
| 4 | Plate flexural rigidity | $D = \dfrac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | Nآ·m | $E = 2.06 \times 10^{11}$ Pa for steel |
| 5 | Plate natural frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{2\pi a^2}\sqrt{D/(\rho_s h)}$ | Hz | $\lambda^2 = 9.87$ (SSSS) |
| 6 | Beam natural frequency (SS) | $f_n = \dfrac{(n\pi)^2}{2\pi L^2}\sqrt{EI/(\rho A)}$ | Hz | Euler-Bernoulli |
| 7 | Resonance avoidance | $f_n \geq \sqrt{2} \, f_{exc,max}$ | Hz | 40% margin |
| 8 | Vertical vibration acceleration | $a_{RMS,v} \leq 0.315$ | m/sآ² | ISO 6954 comfort |
| 9 | Transverse vibration acceleration | $a_{RMS,t} \leq 0.210$ | m/sآ² | 2/3 of vertical |
| 10 | Corrosion addition (IACS) | $t_{gross} = t_{net} + t_c$ | mm | Class-specific $t_c$ table |
| 11 | Stress range vs. yield | $\sigma_{ser} \leq 0.6\, \sigma_Y$ | MPa | Yield-margin serviceability |
| 12 | Hatch cover deflection | $\delta \leq L_{span}/400$ | m | DNV / weather-tight limit |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SLS design protects classification society approval** â€” failure to meet deflection limits can reject the design at plan approval.
- **Vibration control** prevents occupational health claims and equipment failure (electronics, gyrocompass malfunction above 0.5 m/sآ²).
- **SLS-Durability** minimizes life-cycle cost (LCC) by extending dry-dock intervals.
- **SLS governs superstructure scantlings** â€” strength rarely critical due to low wave loading aloft.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Plate Natural Frequency (Kirchhoff-Love Plate Theory)
