Starting from the **classical thin-plate equation** for harmonic vibration $w(x,y,t) = W(x,y) e^{i\omega t}$:

$$D \nabla^4 W = \rho_s h \omega^2 W$$

For a simply-supported rectangular plate of dimensions $a \times b$ (with $a \leq b$), the modal solution is:

$$W_{mn}(x,y) = W_0 \sin\!\left(\dfrac{m\pi x}{a}\right) \sin\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

Substituting the Laplacian of the sinusoidal mode:

$$\nabla^4 W_{mn} = \pi^4 \left[\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right\right]^{2} W_{mn}$$

The governing equation then becomes:

$$D \pi^4 \left[\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)\right]^{2} = \rho_s h \omega^2$$

Solving for the natural circular frequency $\omega_{mn}$:

$$\omega_{mn} = \pi^2 \left[\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2\right] \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho_s h}}$$

Converting to **frequency in Hz**, $f_{mn} = \omega_{mn} / (2\pi)$:

$$\boxed{f_{mn} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \left[\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2\right] \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho_s h}}}$$

Setting $m = n = 1$ and defining the combined coefficient $\lambda^2 = \pi^2 \left[ (1/a)^2 + (1/b)^2 \right] \times 2$ (which reduces to $\pi^2 \cdot 2 / a^2 = 2\pi^2/a^2$ for a square plate $a = b$ and then $\lambda^2 = 9.87$ for the square simply-supported plate in the standard form), we obtain the **fundamental mode** expression used in آ§2.3.3.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example â€” Plate Vibration Serviceability Check

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Type Computation â€” Likely to appear in KTU Part B (14 marks) or Part A (3 marks).**

### Problem Statement

A **simply-supported** plating panel on the bridge wing of a naval vessel has dimensions:
- Plate length $a = 0.8$ m (short span)
- Plate length $b = 1.2$ m (long span)
- Thickness $h = 0.006$ m (= **6 mm**)
- Material: **mild steel**, $E = 2.06 \times 10^{11}$ Pa, $\nu = 0.30$, $\rho_s = 7850$ kg/mآ³
- Excitation source: diesel generator at running speed **1500 RPM** with harmonic orders up to **6أ—** running.

### Check whether the plate is serviceable (no resonance).

### Step 1 â€” Compute the Excitation Frequency Band

The fundamental running frequency:

$$f_{run} = \dfrac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ Hz}$$

Maximum excitation frequency (6th harmonic):

$$f_{exc,max} = 6 \times 25 = 150 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 2 â€” Compute the Plate Flexural Rigidity $D$

$$D = \dfrac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \dfrac{2.06 \times 10^{11} \times (0.006)^3}{12(1 - 0.30^2)}$$

Numerator:

$$2.06 \times 10^{11} \times 2.16 \times 10^{-7} = 44{,}496 \text{ Nآ·m}$$

Denominator:

$$12 \times (1 - 0.09) = 12 \times 0.91 = 10.92$$

Therefore:

$$D = \dfrac{44{,}496}{10.92} \approx 4075 \text{ Nآ·m}$$

### Step 3 â€” Compute the Fundamental Plate Natural Frequency

For the **fundamental mode** $m = n = 1$:

$$f_{11} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\left[\left(\dfrac{1}{0.8}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{1}{1.2}\right)^2\right] \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho_s h}}$$

The bracket term:

$$\left(\dfrac{1}{0.64}\right) + \left(\dfrac{1}{1.44}\right) = 1.5625 + 0.6944 = 2.2569 \text{ m}^{-2}$$

The square root (mass per area $\rho_s h$):

$$\rho_s h = 7850 \times 0.006 = 47.1 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

$$\sqrt{\dfrac{4075}{47.1}} = \sqrt{86.52} = 9.302 \text{ (rad/s}^{1/2}\text{)}$$

The full pre-factor:

$$\dfrac{\pi}{2} \times 2.2569 = 1.5708 \times 2.2569 = 3.5454 \text{ m}^{-2}$$

The fundamental natural frequency:

$$f_{11} = 3.5454 \times 9.302 \approx 32.98 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 4 â€” Resonance Avoidance Check

Required minimum natural frequency per آ§2.3.1:

$$f_{n,req} = \sqrt{2} \times f_{exc,max} = 1.414 \times 150 = 212.1 \text{ Hz}$$

**Comparison:**

$$f_{11} = 32.98 \text{ Hz} \;\ll\; f_{n,req} = 212.1 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 5 â€” Serviceability Verdict

> [!WARNING]
> **Result: NOT SERVICEABLE â€” the plate will resonate in a harmonic order of the diesel generator.**
> The plate is within the excitation frequency band; **higher modes** must also be checked: $f_{12}, f_{22}, f_{13}, \dots$ Generally for $h = 6$ mm and a $\sim$1 m plate, **stiffening ribs** at smaller spacing (e.g., 0.4 m) are required to push $f_n$ above 100 Hz.

### Step 6 â€” Remediation Estimate

If stiffener spacing is reduced to $a = 0.4$ m (a halving), the natural frequency scales as $1/a^2$, i.e., a **factor of 4 increase**:

$$f_{11,new} \approx 4 \times 32.98 = 131.9 \text{ Hz}$$

Still below 212 Hz, suggesting a **plate thickness increase to 8 mm or 10 mm** is necessary, or installation of **constrained-layer damping**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix â€” Summary of Checks

| Serviceability Aspect | Governing Equation | Numerical Limit | Failure Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Local deflection | $\delta \leq L/250$ | depends on span | Seal failure, paint cracking |
| Plate vibration | $f_n \geq \sqrt{2}\, f_{exc}$ | $\geq 30$ Hz (accommodation) | Resonance, fatigue, noise |
| Vertical accel. | $a_{RMS} \leq 0.315$ m/sآ² | ISO 6954 | Crew discomfort / sickness |
| Corrosion loss | $t_{gross} \geq t_{net} + t_c$ | class table | Premature renewal |
| Stress under service | $\sigma \leq 0.6\,\sigma_Y$ | MPa | Yielding, permanent set |
| Hatch cover | $\delta \leq L/400$ | mm | Water ingress |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Serviceability Limit State â€” Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Structural Design Input] --> B[Limit State Framework]
    B --> C1[Ultimate Limit State ULS]
    B --> C2[Serviceability Limit State SLS]
    
    C1 --> D1[Strength Check]
    C1 --> D2[Stability Check]
    C1 --> D3[Fatigue ULS]
    
    C2 --> E1[Deflection Check]
    C2 --> E2[Vibration Check]
    C2 --> E3[Local Damage Check]
    C2 --> E4[Human Comfort Check]
    
    E1 --> F1[Delta less than L over 250]
    E1 --> F2[Delta less than L over 500 Hull Girder]
    
    E2 --> G1[Natural Frequency Check]
    E2 --> G2[RMS Acceleration Check]
    
    E3 --> H1[Corrosion Allowance]
    E3 --> H2[Crack Length NDT]
    
    E4 --> I1[Vertical Accel less than 0.315 m per s squared]
    E4 --> I2[Transverse Accel less than 0.210 m per s squared]
    
    F1 --> J[SLS Compliance Verification]
    F2 --> J
    G1 --> J
    G2 --> J
    H1 --> J
    H2 --> J
    I1 --> J
    I2 --> J
    
    J --> K{All Checks Pass?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Classification Approval]
    K -->|No| M[Revise Scantlings or Add Stiffening]
    M --> A
```

## 4.2 Ship Structural Serviceability â€” Component-Level Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HullGirder[Hull Girder Level]
        HG1[Global Stillwater Deflection]
        HG2[Slamming Induced Bow Vibration]
    end
    
    subgraph StiffenedPanels[Stiffened Panel Level]
        SP1[Plating between Stiffeners]
        SP2[Stiffener Span Deflection]
        SP3[Stiffener Web Plate Buckling]
    end
    
    subgraph LocalDetails[Local Detail Level]
        LD1[Hatch Cover Deflection]
        LD2[Bulkhead Plate Vibration]
        LD3[Deckhouse Plate Pinging]
    end
    
    subgraph Excitation[Excitation Sources]
        EX1[Main Engine 25 Hz]
        EX2[Generator 50 to 300 Hz]
        EX3[Wave Slamming 0.5 to 5 Hz]
        EX4[Sloshing 0.1 to 1 Hz]
    end
    
    EX1 --> SP2
    EX1 --> LD2
    EX2 --> LD2
    EX2 --> LD3
    EX3 --> HG2
    EX3 --> SP1
    EX4 --> SP1
    EX4 --> LD1
    
    HG1 --> CHK{SLS Pass}
    HG2 --> CHK
    SP1 --> CHK
    SP2 --> CHK
    SP3 --> CHK
    LD1 --> CHK
    LD2 --> CHK
    LD3 --> CHK
```

## 4.3 Serviceability Decision Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Structural Member Identified]) --> Step1[Step 1: Identify Service Scenario]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Compute Action Effect]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Compare with Serviceability Limit]
    Step3 --> Step4{Within Limit?}
    Step4 -->|Yes| Pass[Member is Serviceable]
    Step4 -->|No| Step5[Step 4: Identify Stiffening or Geometry Change]
    Step5 --> Step2
    Pass --> End([Proceed to ULS Check])
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam â€” May 2024]
**Define the Serviceability Limit State (SLS) as applied to ship structural design. How does it differ from the Ultimate Limit State (ULS)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **SLS Definition:** The state where a structure remains fit for normal use, ensuring functionality, occupant comfort, and durability under expected service loads â€” *without* approaching collapse.
- (1 mark) **Governing criteria:** deflection, vibration, crack width, and corrosion-driven thickness loss within prescribed limits (e.g., $\delta \leq L/250$).
- (1 mark) **Key differences from ULS:**

  | Aspect | ULS | SLS |
  |---|---|---|
  | Concern | Safety / collapse | Functionality / comfort |
  | Load factor | Higher ($1.2-1.3$) | Lower ($\approx 1.0$) |
  | Typical check | $\sigma \leq \sigma_Y / \gamma_M$ | $\delta \leq \delta_{lim}$ |

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam â€” Dec 2023]
**State the IACS-recommended deflection limit for (a) a stiffened plating panel, and (b) a hatch cover plating under green-sea load.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **(a) Stiffened plating panel:** $\delta \leq L_{span}/250$ where $L_{span}$ is the stiffener span between primary supports.
- (1 mark) **(b) Hatch cover plating:** $\delta \leq L/400$ (tighter limit due to weathertightness requirement and seal integrity).
- (1 mark) For **accommodation spaces** the limit is further tightened to $\delta \leq L/360$ to avoid visible sagging and cracking of finishes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) â€” Module 3 Internal Choice

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam â€” Model Paper 2024]

> **"A simply-supported plating panel of an accommodation deckhouse has dimensions 1.0 m أ— 1.5 m, plate thickness 5 mm, and is excited by an auxiliary diesel generator running at 1800 RPM with significant harmonics up to 4أ—. Verify whether the plate meets the SLS vibration requirement. If not, recommend remedial stiffening."**
> *Data: E = 2.06 أ— 10آ¹آ¹ Pa, خ½ = 0.30, دپ_s = 7850 kg/mآ³, limit per DNV: f_n â‰¥ âˆڑ2 أ— f_exc,max.*

**Sub-part (a) â€” 7 marks: Compute fundamental natural frequency**

**Step 1:** Plate flexural rigidity $D$

$$D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{2.06\times 10^{11} \times (0.005)^3}{12(1-0.09)}$$

Numerator: $2.06 \times 10^{11} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-7} = 25{,}750$ Nآ·m

Denominator: $12 \times 0.91 = 10.92$

$$D = \frac{25{,}750}{10.92} \approx 2358 \text{ Nآ·m}$$

**Step 2:** Mass per unit area

$$m'' = \rho_s h = 7850 \times 0.005 = 39.25 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Fundamental natural frequency (mode 1,1)

$$f_{11} = \frac{\pi}{2}\left[\left(\frac{1}{1.0}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{1.5}\right)^2\right]\sqrt{\frac{2358}{39.25}}$$

Bracket: $1.0 + 0.4444 = 1.4444$ mâپ»آ²

Square root: $\sqrt{60.07} = 7.751$

Prefactor: $(\pi/2) \times 1.4444 = 2.269$ mâپ»آ²

$$f_{11} = 2.269 \times 7.751 \approx 17.59 \text{ Hz}$$

**Sub-part (b) â€” 7 marks: Compare with limit and recommend remediation**

**Step 4:** Excitation frequency

$$f_{exc,max} = 4 \times \frac{1800}{60} = 4 \times 30 = 120 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5:** Required natural frequency

$$f_{n,req} = \sqrt{2} \times 120 = 169.7 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 6:** Verdict

$$f_{11} = 17.59 \text{ Hz} \ll 169.7 \text{ Hz} \implies \text{NOT SERVICEABLE}$$

**Step 7:** Remediation â€” add stiffeners at $a = 0.5$ m, $b = 0.75$ m (spacing halved)

Since $f_n \propto 1/a^2$, halving the spacing increases $f_n$ by factor of $4$:

$$f_{11,new} \approx 4 \times 17.59 = 70.4 \text{ Hz}$$

Still insufficient. **Stiffener spacing must be reduced to 0.25 m** (factor 16 increase):

$$f_{11,new} \approx 16 \times 17.59 = 281 \text{ Hz} \;>\; 169.7 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** Install additional stiffeners at **0.25 m spacing** to meet the SLS vibration criterion. Alternatively, increase plate thickness to 8 mm.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) â€” Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam â€” Model Paper 2024]

> **"With the aid of a sketch, explain the various serviceability limit-state criteria applied to ship structures. For a stiffened panel of span 4.0 m, calculate the maximum allowable deflection under service conditions, and discuss the consequences of exceeding this limit."**

**Sub-part (a) â€” 7 marks: Serviceability criteria and allowable deflection**

**Allowable deflection (L/250 rule):**

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{L}{250} = \frac{4000 \text{ mm}}{250} = 16 \text{ mm}$$

**Serviceability criteria classified:**

1. **Deflection-based:** $\delta \leq L/250$ for panels; $\delta \leq L/500$ for hull girder; $\delta \leq L/400$ for hatch covers.
2. **Vibration-based:** natural frequency $f_n \geq \sqrt{2}\, f_{exc}$ and RMS acceleration $a_{RMS} \leq 0.315$ m/sآ².
3. **Local damage / durability:** corrosion addition $t_c$ per IACS; crack length $a \leq a_{crit,NDT}$.

**Sub-part (b) â€” 7 marks: Consequences of exceeding the limit**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often list *only* cosmetic effects (paint cracking, visible sag). Examiners expect both **functional** and **safety** consequences. The list below carries 1 mark per *category* of consequence.

1. **Watertight / weathertight integrity failure:** Excessive plating deflection distorts gasket-bearing surfaces of hatch covers, bulkhead penetrations, and door frames â†’ progressive leakage, corrosion acceleration, possible flooding of compartments.
2. **Equipment malfunction:** Misalignment of machinery, shafts, and bearings. Gyrocompass errors in accommodation blocks due to panel vibration.
3. **Crew discomfort and health:** Motion sickness, reduced operational efficiency, and long-term back injuries (exceeding ISO 6954 limits).
4. **Fatigue damage:** Repeated elastic deflection beyond intended amplitude reduces fatigue life by orders of magnitude (per Palmgren-Miner).
5. **Coating breakdown:** Deflection-induced cracking of paint and corrosion-protection coatings accelerates wastage in ballast tanks and outer hull.
6. **Class non-compliance:** Failure to meet DNV / LR / IACS CSR criteria results in plan approval rejection, requiring redesign and project delay.

**Sketch (described):** A stiffened panel diagram showing the deflection curve $w(x)$ under uniform pressure $p$, with maximum sag $\delta_{max}$ at mid-span between two primary supports, and the line $\delta = L/250$ marked as the serviceability bound.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Serviceability Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the âˆڑ2 factor** in resonance avoidance â†’ design is unsafe (not unconservative enough).
> 2. **Confusing plate flexural rigidity $D$** (Nآ·m, per unit width) with beam $EI$ (Nآ·mآ²) â€” using $EI$ in plate formulas yields a 1,000-fold error in plate natural frequency.
> 3. **Mixing static and dynamic limits** â€” applying L/250 (static deflection) to vibration problems, or applying vibration limits to static loadings.
> 4. **Ignoring corrosion addition $t_c$** in serviceability thickness checks â†’ plan approval rejection at the class society.
> 5. **Forgetting higher modes** in plate vibration â€” fundamental $f_{11}$ may be acceptable while $f_{22}$ or $f_{13}$ is in the excitation band.
> 6. **In 14-mark questions, omitting the verdict statement** ("SLS satisfied / not satisfied") â€” the conclusion carries 1-2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist â€” Serviceability (Module 3)**

- [x] **SLS = Functional + Comfort + Durability**, not collapse.
- [x] Three categories: **deflection, vibration, local damage (corrosion/cracking).**
- [x] **L/250** â€” stiffened panel; **L/500** â€” hull girder; **L/400** â€” hatch cover; **s/150** â€” bulkhead plate between stiffeners.
- [x] **Plate natural frequency** $f_n = (\pi/2)\big[(1/a)^2 + (1/b)^2\big]\sqrt{D/(\rho_s h)}$ for SSSS plate; $D = Eh^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$.
- [x] **Resonance avoidance:** $f_n \geq \sqrt{2}\,f_{exc,max}$ (40 % margin).
- [x] **Human comfort (ISO 6954):** $a_{RMS,vert} \leq 0.315$ m/sآ²; transverse $\leq 0.210$ m/sآ².
- [x] **Stress-based serviceability:** $\sigma_{ser} \leq 0.6\,\sigma_Y$ (avoid yielding / permanent set).
- [x] **Corrosion addition $t_c$** is added to net thickness per IACS CSR / class rules â€” serviceability-driven, not strength.
- [x] SLS partial safety factors are **lower** than ULS ($\gamma_f \approx 1.0$ vs $1.2-1.3$); material factors at mean / characteristic.
- [x] **Higher modes** ($m,n > 1$) must be checked for plate vibration against all excitation harmonics.
- [x] **Remediation options** for failed SLS: increase thickness, reduce stiffener spacing (scales as $1/a^2$), add constrained-layer damping, or mount equipment on vibration isolators.
- [x] **Consequences of SLS exceedance:** watertight failure, fatigue damage, equipment malfunction, crew discomfort, coating breakdown, and class rejection.
- [x] Class references for KTU: **DNV-RU-SHIP Pt. 3 Ch. 4**, **LR SSC Rules Ch. 4**, **IACS CSR S11â€“S13**, **IRS Rules Ch. 4 Sec. 3**.

---

topics.title: Fatigue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fatigue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FATIGUE** is defined as the progressive, localized, and permanent structural damage that occurs in a material subjected to fluctuating loads and stresses (cyclic loading) at values considerably below the ultimate tensile strength or even the yield strength of the material. The failure is characterized by the initiation of a crack, its progressive propagation, and final sudden fracture of the remaining cross-section.

In the context of **Ship Structures II**, fatigue is of paramount importance because a ship hull is continuously subjected to **cyclic wave loading** over its service life (typically **20 to 25 years** or $\approx 10^8$ stress cycles). Every passage through a wave induces alternating tensile and compressive stresses in the hull plating, longitudinal stiffeners, and transverse web frames.

### 1.2 Key Distinctions from Static Failure

| Property | Static Failure | Fatigue Failure |
|----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Stress Level | Near UTS / Yield | Well below yield |
| Loading Type | Monotonic (one direction) | Cyclic (alternating) |
| Warning Signs | Visible yielding | None â€” sudden fracture |
| Crack Behaviour | Not applicable | Initiation + Propagation |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Paperclip Fatigue Test**
> Take a standard metal paperclip and bend it slightly back and forth (say 30آ° each way). It does not break on the first bend. Repeat this cyclic bending 8â€“10 times â€” and suddenly, with a sharp *snap*, it breaks without warning. The maximum stress during each bend was nowhere near the breaking strength of the paperclip in a single pull. Yet, accumulated cyclic damage caused failure.
>
> This is **fatigue**. The same principle applies to ship hull plating: millions of wave cycles slowly initiate microscopic cracks at weld toes and stress concentrations, which then propagate until the plate ruptures.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Fatigue Analysis

- **Endurance Limit ($\sigma_e$ or $\sigma_{-1}$):** The stress amplitude below which a ferrous material can theoretically endure an infinite number of cycles without failure. For **structural steels** (shipbuilding grades like AH36, DH36), typical $\sigma_e \approx 0.4$ to $0.5 \times \sigma_{UTS}$.
- **Fatigue Strength at $N$ cycles ($S_N$):** The maximum alternating stress a specimen can sustain for $N$ cycles before failure.
- **Standard test frequency:** Rotating-bending or axial-loading fatigue tests at $R = -1$ (fully reversed loading).
- **Cycle ratio:** $r = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ (stress ratio).

## 1.5 Classification of Fatigue

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight â€” Three Principal Categories**

1. **High-Cycle Fatigue (HCF):** $N > 10^4$ to $10^5$ cycles, stresses are predominantly *elastic*. Dominant in ship hulls (wave-induced).
2. **Low-Cycle Fatigue (LCF):** $N < 10^4$ cycles, stresses may exceed yield, *plastic strain* governs. Relevant in crash, grounding, or severe slamming events.
3. **Thermal / Corrosion Fatigue:** Combined effect of thermal cycling and/or corrosive seawater environment. Critical in engine room structures and ballast tanks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The S-N Curve (Wأ¶hler Curve)

The **S-N (Stress-Number of cycles)** curve is the foundational diagram of fatigue analysis. It plots the alternating stress amplitude ($S_a$ or $\sigma_a$) on the vertical axis (log scale) against the number of cycles to failure ($N_f$) on the horizontal axis (log scale).

### 2.1.1 Equation of the S-N Curve (Basquin's Law)

For the finite-life region ($10^3 < N < 10^6$), the curve is approximated by a straight line on log-log axes:

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b$$

Where:
- $\sigma_f'$ = **fatigue strength coefficient** (intercept at $2N_f = 1$)
- $b$ = **fatigue strength exponent** (typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$ for steels)
- $\sigma_a$ = alternating stress amplitude

### 2.1.2 Endurance Limit Behaviour

> [!NOTE]
> For **ferrous steels and shipbuilding mild steels**, the S-N curve flattens into a horizontal plateau at $\sigma_a = \sigma_e$ (endurance limit) beyond approximately $10^6$ cycles. Non-ferrous metals (aluminum, copper alloys) and corroding steels in seawater **do not** exhibit a true endurance limit; their S-N curves continue to fall.

## 2.2 Mean Stress Correction Theories

In real ship structures, cyclic stresses rarely alternate symmetrically about zero. There is usually a **mean stress** ($\sigma_m$) component. The following theories predict the allowable alternating stress $\sigma_a$ for a given mean stress and endurance limit.

### 2.2.1 Soderberg Criterion (Most Conservative â€” Used in Critical Ship Components)

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = \frac{1}{n}$$

### 2.2.2 Goodman Criterion (Most Widely Used in Ship Design)

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

### 2.2.3 Gerber Criterion (Parabolic â€” Less Conservative)

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{n}$$

Where $n$ is the **factor of safety**.

## 2.3 Stress Concentration Factor (SCF)

Any geometric discontinuity â€” a hole, a notch, a weld toe, a bracket termination â€” acts as a stress raiser. The **SCF** is defined as:

$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nom}}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{max}$ = peak stress at the discontinuity
- $\sigma_{nom}$ = nominal stress in the un-notched region

In welded ship details, the **hot-spot stress** approach (used in IIW recommendations) replaces $K_t$ with the more practical $K_{hs}$.

## 2.4 Notch Sensitivity ($q$)

Not all materials respond equally to stress concentrations. **Notch sensitivity** $q$ is defined as:

$$q = \frac{K_f - 1}{K_t - 1}$$

Where $K_f$ is the **fatigue stress concentration factor** (the effective SCF under cyclic loading). The fatigue notch factor is then:

$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$$

For $q = 0$ (fully insensitive, e.g., very ductile), $K_f = 1$. For $q = 1$ (fully sensitive, e.g., brittle), $K_f = K_t$.

## 2.5 Cumulative Damage (Palmgren-Miner Rule)

For a ship hull experiencing *variable-amplitude* loading (a real sea state is a stochastic process of varying wave heights), the **Miner's rule** linearly sums the damage from each stress level:

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

Where:
- $n_i$ = number of cycles applied at stress level $S_i$
- $N_i$ = number of cycles to failure at stress level $S_i$ (from S-N curve)
- Failure is predicted when $D \geq 1$ (with $D = 1$ as the classical criterion)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 â€” Essential Fatigue Equations for KTU Board Examination**

| # | Equation | Application / Use | Notation |
|---|----------|-------------------|----------|
| 1 | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N_f)^b$ | Basquin's S-N relation | $\sigma_a$: alt. stress, $N_f$: cycles to fail |
| 2 | $\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Mean stress | $\sigma_{max}$, $\sigma_{min}$ |
| 3 | $\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Stress amplitude | â€” |
| 4 | $R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | Stress ratio | $R = -1$: fully reversed |
| 5 | $\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = \frac{1}{n}$ | Soderberg (conservative) | $n$: FOS |
| 6 | $\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}} = \frac{1}{n}$ | Goodman (common in ships) | â€” |
| 7 | $\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{n}$ | Gerber (parabolic) | â€” |
| 8 | $K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nom}}$ | Theoretical SCF | geometric only |
| 9 | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | Fatigue SCF | $q$: notch sensitivity |
| 10 | $D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$ | Palmgren-Miner cumulative damage | $D \geq 1$ â†’ failure |
| 11 | $\Delta K = Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Paris Law (crack propagation) | $a$: crack length, $Y$: geometry factor |
| 12 | $\frac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m$ | Paris-Erdogan crack growth | $C$, $m$: material constants |

> **Note on Table:** All vertical bars, square roots, and absolute values use `\vert`, `\mid`, or `\sqrt{}` to prevent markdown table syntax conflict.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Classification societies** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV) publish **S-N design curves** specifically for welded ship details (e.g., DNV's FAT-90, FAT-71 classes).
- **Spectral fatigue analysis** is mandated for offshore structures and large container ships using long-term sea state scatter diagrams.
- **Inspection planning** during a ship's service life relies on fatigue crack-growth predictions (Paris Law) to schedule dry-dock surveys.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Stress Amplitude and Mean Stress from Boundary Values

### Given:
A longitudinal stiffener in a ship's bottom plating records the following stress extremes during one wave passage:
- Maximum stress: $\sigma_{max} = +120 \text{ MPa}$
- Minimum stress: $\sigma_{min} = -30 \text{ MPa}$

### Find:
The mean stress $\sigma_m$, the stress amplitude $\sigma_a$, the stress ratio $R$, and the Goodman equivalent fully-reversed stress.

### Step 1: Identify the Formulas

The mean stress is the algebraic average of the maximum and minimum stresses:

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

The alternating stress amplitude is half the difference between the extremes:

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

The stress ratio is:

$$R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$$

### Step 2: Substitute Numerical Values

Substituting $\sigma_{max} = 120$ MPa and $\sigma_{min} = -30$ MPa into the mean stress formula:

$$\sigma_m = \frac{(+120) + (-30)}{2} = \frac{90}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_m = +45 \text{ MPa}}$$

Substituting into the amplitude formula:

$$\sigma_a = \frac{(+120) - (-30)}{2} = \frac{150}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_a = 75 \text{ MPa}}$$

Substituting into the ratio formula:

$$R = \frac{-30}{+120} = -0.25$$

$$\boxed{R = -0.25}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Goodman Correction

For a **shipbuilding steel (AH36)**, take:
- $\sigma_{UTS} = 400$ MPa (typical)
- $\sigma_e = 180$ MPa (endurance limit, $\approx 0.45 \times \sigma_{UTS}$)
- Factor of safety $n = 1.5$

The Goodman line is:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

Solving for the allowable $\sigma_a$:

$$\sigma_a^{allow} = \left(\frac{1}{n} - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right) \cdot \sigma_e$$

Substitute the numbers:

$$\sigma_a^{allow} = \left(\frac{1}{1.5} - \frac{45}{400}\right) \cdot 180$$

Compute the bracketed term:

$$\frac{1}{1.5} = 0.6667, \quad \frac{45}{400} = 0.1125$$

$$0.6667 - 0.1125 = 0.5542$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_a^{allow} = 0.5542 \times 180 = 99.76 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 4: Compare with the Applied Amplitude

$$\sigma_a^{applied} = 75 \text{ MPa} \;<\; \sigma_a^{allow} = 99.76 \text{ MPa}$$

The actual cyclic stress amplitude is **lower** than the Goodman allowable, so the detail is **safe** against high-cycle fatigue.

### Step 5: Margin of Safety (MOS) Calculation

$$\text{MOS} = \frac{\sigma_a^{allow}}{\sigma_a^{applied}} - 1 = \frac{99.76}{75} - 1$$

$$\boxed{\text{MOS} = 0.330 \text{ or } 33.0\% \text{ reserve}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Cycles-to-Failure using Basquin's Equation

### Given:
A welded joint in a hatch corner is subjected to an alternating stress amplitude of $\sigma_a = 90$ MPa. For the joint detail (DNV FAT-71 class), $\sigma_f' = 320$ MPa and $b = -0.10$.

### Step 1: Set Up Basquin's Equation

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b$$

### Step 2: Rearrange for $N_f$

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_f'} = (2N_f)^b$$

Raise both sides to the power $\frac{1}{b}$:

$$\left(\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_f'}\right)^{1/b} = 2N_f$$

$$N_f = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_f'}\right)^{1/b}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_f'} = \frac{90}{320} = 0.28125$$

Since $b = -0.10$, then $\frac{1}{b} = -10$:

$$(0.28125)^{-10} = \frac{1}{(0.28125)^{10}}$$

Compute the intermediate:

$$(0.28125)^2 = 0.07910$$
$$(0.28125)^4 = (0.07910)^2 = 0.006257$$
$$(0.28125)^5 = 0.006257 \times 0.28125 = 0.001760$$
$$(0.28125)^{10} = (0.001760)^2 = 3.097 \times 10^{-6}$$

Therefore:

$$(0.28125)^{-10} = \frac{1}{3.097 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.229 \times 10^{5}$$

Hence:

$$N_f = \frac{1}{2} \times 3.229 \times 10^{5}$$

$$\boxed{N_f \approx 1.61 \times 10^{5} \text{ cycles}}$$

### Step 4: Practical Interpretation

For a ship encountering approximately **300 significant wave cycles per day** (rough North Atlantic), the predicted fatigue life is:

$$\text{Service Life} = \frac{1.61 \times 10^{5}}{300} \approx 537 \text{ days} \approx 1.47 \text{ years}$$

This is critically short and indicates that **detail improvement** (e.g., grinding the weld toe, using a higher FAT class, or increasing plate thickness) is mandatory.

---

## 3.3 Cumulative Damage Calculation (Miner's Rule Worked Example)

### Given:
A transverse frame web experiences three distinct stress amplitudes during one voyage:

| Stress Block | $\sigma_a$ (MPa) | $n_i$ (cycles) | $N_i$ from S-N curve (cycles) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 (calm seas) | 60 | 50,000 | $5 \times 10^{6}$ |
| 2 (moderate seas) | 95 | 12,000 | $2.5 \times 10^{5}$ |
| 3 (storm) | 130 | 800 | $4 \times 10^{4}$ |

### Step 1: Calculate Damage per Block

$$D_1 = \frac{n_1}{N_1} = \frac{50{,}000}{5{,}000{,}000} = 0.010$$

$$D_2 = \frac{n_2}{N_2} = \frac{12{,}000}{250{,}000} = 0.048$$

$$D_3 = \frac{n_3}{N_3} = \frac{800}{40{,}000} = 0.020$$

### Step 2: Sum the Damage

$$D = D_1 + D_2 + D_3 = 0.010 + 0.048 + 0.020$$

$$\boxed{D = 0.078 \text{ per voyage}}$$

### Step 3: Estimate Number of Voyages to Failure

Since failure is at $D = 1$:

$$\text{Number of voyages} = \frac{1}{0.078} \approx 12.8 \text{ voyages}$$

If a ship makes ~50 voyages per year, predicted fatigue life $\approx 0.26$ years â€” **this detail would require immediate redesign**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram â€” Fatigue Failure Mechanism in Ship Hulls

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Wave Loading on Hull"] --> B["Cyclic Stress in Plating"]
    B --> C["Stress Concentration at Weld Toe"]
    C --> D["Stage 1: Crack Initiation"]
    D --> E["Stage 2: Crack Propagation"]
    E --> F["Stage 3: Final Fracture"]
    F --> G["Loss of Structural Integrity"]

    H["Corrosive Seawater"] --> C
    I["Residual Welding Stresses"] --> C

    subgraph Mitigation["Design Mitigation Strategies"]
        M1["Weld Toe Grinding"]
        M2["Improved FAT Class Detail"]
        M3["Increased Plate Thickness"]
        M4["Periodic NDT Inspection"]
    end

    M1 --> B
    M2 --> B
    M3 --> B
    M4 --> E
```

**Reading Guide:** Each rectangular block is an alphanumeric node (e.g., `A`, `B`, `M1`) to comply with Mermaid's reserved-word safeguard. The flow proceeds from wave-induced cyclic stress â†’ crack initiation â†’ propagation â†’ final fracture, with mitigation strategies as parallel interventions.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram â€” S-N Curve Schematic (Block-Level Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Log N axis: 10 to the power 3"] --> A2["LCF Region Plastic Strain"]
    A2 --> A3["Finite Life Region 10 to the power 3 to 10 to the power 6"]
    A3 --> A4["Endurance Plateau for Ferrous Steels"]
    A4 --> A5["Log Sigma axis: Alternating Stress"]

    B1["Log N axis: 10 to the power 3"] --> B2["Finite Life Slope b equals minus 0.10"]
    B2 --> B3["Continuous Decline Non Ferrous"]
    B3 --> B4["No Endurance Limit Aluminum Alloys"]
    B4 --> B5["Log Sigma axis"]

    A1 -.compared with.- B1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram â€” Mean Stress Correction Theories Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Input: Sigma m and Sigma a applied"] --> Q["Choose Failure Theory"]
    Q --> R1["Soderberg Most Conservative Uses Yield"]
    Q --> R2["Goodman Linear Most Used in Ship Design"]
    Q --> R3["Gerber Parabolic Least Conservative"]

    R1 --> S1["Plot straight line from Sigma y on x axis to Sigma e on y axis"]
    R2 --> S2["Plot straight line from Sigma UTS on x axis to Sigma e on y axis"]
    R3 --> S3["Plot parabola from Sigma UTS on x axis to Sigma e on y axis"]

    S1 --> T["Determine Allowable Sigma a"]
    S2 --> T
    S3 --> T
```

## 4.4 Physical Concept Block â€” Anatomy of a Fatigue Fracture Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1["Fatigue Fracture Surface"] --> Z2["Region A: Crack Initiation Site at Notch"]
    Z2 --> Z3["Region B: Beach Marks or Striations"]
    Z3 --> Z4["Region C: Fast Fracture Zone Ductile Dimples"]
    Z4 --> Z5["Final Sudden Separation"]
```

> **Examiner's Tip:** A real fatigue fracture surface, when examined under a microscope, shows three distinct zones â€” origin, propagation (with characteristic *beach marks* or *striations*), and final fast fracture. Beach marks are macroscopic (visible to the naked eye), striations are microscopic (one per load cycle in LCF, multiple in HCF).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A â€” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define fatigue in the context of ship structures. List any three factors that influence the fatigue life of a welded ship hull detail.**

**Model Answer (12â€“15 lines):**

> Fatigue is the progressive, localized structural damage occurring in a material subjected to fluctuating stresses well below the ultimate tensile strength, characterized by crack initiation, propagation, and final fracture. In ship structures, fatigue is caused by repeated wave-induced cyclic loading over the vessel's service life.
>
> **Three factors influencing fatigue life of welded ship hull details:**
> 1. **Stress range ($\Delta\sigma$):** The dominant parameter. Per DNV, fatigue life $N \propto (\Delta\sigma)^{-m}$ where $m \approx 3$ to $5$.
> 2. **Weld geometry and toe condition:** Grinding the weld toe to a smooth profile dramatically improves the FAT class.
> 3. **Corrosive environment:** Seawater contact can reduce fatigue life by factors of 2â€“10 depending on coating integrity.

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the significance of the endurance limit in fatigue design of ship hulls. Why is it applicable only for certain materials?**

**Model Answer:**

> The endurance limit $\sigma_e$ is the alternating stress amplitude below which a material can theoretically withstand an infinite number of cycles without failure. For ship hull design, it allows engineers to size structural members so that the maximum cyclic stress in service remains below $\sigma_e$, theoretically giving an infinite fatigue life.
>
> The endurance limit is a reliable design parameter only for **ferrous metals** (carbon steels, mild steels, AH/DH/EH grades) under normal atmospheric conditions. It arises because strain-aging effects and microstructural dislocation locking create a true threshold for crack propagation.
>
> For **non-ferrous metals** (aluminum alloys used in superstructures, copper-nickel piping) and for **all metals in corrosive seawater**, no true endurance limit exists â€” the S-N curve continues to descend indefinitely. Hence, these materials require **finite-life design** based on an explicit cycle count (e.g., $10^8$ cycles for a 25-year ship service life).

---

## PART B â€” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Instructions:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) â€” Fatigue Life Prediction using S-N Approach

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

A transverse web frame in the side shell of a bulk carrier is welded with a longitudinal stiffener. The nominal stress range in the detail during one voyage is $\Delta\sigma = 110$ MPa, and the mean stress is $\sigma_m = +30$ MPa (tensile).

**Material Properties (Shipbuilding Steel AH36):**
- $\sigma_{UTS} = 400$ MPa
- $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa
- Endurance limit $\sigma_e = 180$ MPa
- Basquin constants for the welded detail: $\sigma_f' = 280$ MPa, $b = -0.09$

**Tasks:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ and check whether the detail is safe against fatigue using the **Goodman criterion** with a factor of safety $n = 1.5$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using Basquin's equation, calculate the **number of cycles to failure** $N_f$ for this welded detail under the applied stress amplitude.

#### Model Solution â€” Part (a)

**Step 1:** Calculate the stress amplitude.

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\Delta\sigma}{2} = \frac{110}{2} = 55 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Goodman criterion.

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

Solving for the allowable alternating stress:

$$\sigma_a^{allow} = \left(\frac{1}{n} - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right) \cdot \sigma_e$$

Substituting values:

$$\sigma_a^{allow} = \left(\frac{1}{1.5} - \frac{30}{400}\right) \cdot 180$$

Compute terms:

$$\frac{1}{1.5} = 0.6667, \quad \frac{30}{400} = 0.0750$$

$$0.6667 - 0.0750 = 0.5917$$

$$\sigma_a^{allow} = 0.5917 \times 180 = 106.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Compare and conclude.

Since $\sigma_a = 55 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_a^{allow} = 106.5 \text{ MPa}$, the detail is **SAFE** against high-cycle fatigue per Goodman.

**Margin of Safety:**

$$\text{MOS} = \frac{106.5}{55} - 1 = 0.936 \text{ or } 93.6\%$$

#### Model Solution â€” Part (b)

**Step 1:** Apply Basquin's equation.

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b$$

Rearrange for $N_f$:

$$N_f = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_f'}\right)^{1/b}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_f'} = \frac{55}{280} = 0.1964$$

$$\frac{1}{b} = \frac{1}{-0.09} = -11.111$$

$$(0.1964)^{-11.111} = \frac{1}{(0.1964)^{11.111}}$$

Compute incrementally:

$$(0.1964)^2 = 0.03858$$
$$(0.1964)^4 = (0.03858)^2 = 0.001488$$
$$(0.1964)^8 = (0.001488)^2 = 2.215 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$(0.1964)^{11} = (0.1964)^8 \cdot (0.1964)^2 \cdot (0.1964)^1$$
$$= 2.215 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.03858 \times 0.1964$$
$$= 1.678 \times 10^{-8}$$

Accounting for the fractional exponent $0.111$:

$$(0.1964)^{0.111} \approx e^{0.111 \cdot \ln(0.1964)} = e^{0.111 \times (-1.627)} = e^{-0.1806} = 0.8348$$

Therefore:

$$(0.1964)^{11.111} = 1.678 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.8348 = 1.401 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$(0.1964)^{-11.111} = \frac{1}{1.401 \times 10^{-8}} = 7.138 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 3:** Final answer.

$$N_f = \frac{1}{2} \times 7.138 \times 10^{7}$$

$$\boxed{N_f \approx 3.57 \times 10^{7} \text{ cycles}}$$

**Practical interpretation:** With ~300 cycles/day, this gives ~$3.18 \times 10^{5}$ days $\approx 871$ years â€” far exceeding design life. Detail is very safe.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) â€” Cumulative Damage using Palmgren-Miner Rule

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A hatch coaming of a container ship experiences variable amplitude loading from a long-term wave scatter diagram. The following stress histogram has been derived from spectral fatigue analysis:

| Stress Block | $\Delta\sigma$ (MPa) | Cycles per year $n_i$ | S-N Curve $N_i$ at this $\Delta\sigma$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 50 | 1,500,000 | $5.0 \times 10^{7}$ |
| 2 | 80 | 350,000 | $3.0 \times 10^{6}$ |
| 3 | 110 | 90,000 | $4.5 \times 10^{5}$ |
| 4 | 140 | 18,000 | $1.2 \times 10^{5}$ |
| 5 | 170 | 3,000 | $4.5 \times 10^{4}$ |

**Tasks:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **annual cumulative damage** using the Palmgren-Miner rule, and determine the **predicted fatigue life in years**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **three engineering measures** that could be adopted to extend the fatigue life of this hatch coaming detail.

#### Model Solution â€” Part (a)

**Step 1:** Apply Miner's rule for each stress block.

$$D_{annual} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

Compute each term:

$$D_1 = \frac{1{,}500{,}000}{50{,}000{,}000} = 0.0300$$

$$D_2 = \frac{350{,}000}{3{,}000{,}000} = 0.1167$$

$$D_3 = \frac{90{,}000}{450{,}000} = 0.2000$$

$$D_4 = \frac{18{,}000}{120{,}000} = 0.1500$$

$$D_5 = \frac{3{,}000}{45{,}000} = 0.0667$$

**Step 2:** Sum the annual damage.

$$D_{annual} = 0.0300 + 0.1167 + 0.2000 + 0.1500 + 0.0667$$

$$\boxed{D_{annual} = 0.5634 \text{ per year}}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate fatigue life in years.

Failure is predicted when total accumulated damage reaches 1:

$$\text{Fatigue Life} = \frac{1.0}{D_{annual}} = \frac{1.0}{0.5634}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Fatigue Life} \approx 1.77 \text{ years}}$$

This is **catastrophically short** for a ship (design life 25 years). The hatch coaming detail is severely under-designed.

#### Model Solution â€” Part (b)

**Three engineering measures to extend fatigue life:**

1. **Weld Profile Improvement (Grinding / TIG Dressing):** The hatch coaming weld toe is a major stress raiser. Burr grinding the weld toe to a smooth concave profile can upgrade the detail from FAT-71 to FAT-90 class, increasing $N_i$ by a factor of ~3, thereby reducing $D$ and extending life.

2. **Increasing Plate Thickness:** The stress amplitude is inversely proportional to the section modulus. Increasing the coaming plate thickness from, say, 12 mm to 16 mm reduces stress by a factor of $(12/16)$ and, since $N \propto (\Delta\sigma)^{-m}$, dramatically multiplies $N_i$ and reduces damage.

3. **Introducing Stress-Relief Features / Better Detailing:** Replacing a sharp bracket termination with a soft-end (sniped) bracket reduces the SCF $K_t$ significantly. Alternatively, using a balanced weld with reduced residual stresses (post-weld heat treatment, low-hydrogen electrodes) lowers mean stress and improves Goodman-based allowable amplitudes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning â€” Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing $\sigma_a$ with $\Delta\sigma$:** Stress amplitude is **half** the stress range. Mixing these will cost 3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Using UTS instead of yield in Soderberg:** Each mean-stress theory has its specific strength term. Substitution errors will cost 2â€“4 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units in Miner's Rule:** The ratio $n_i / N_i$ is **dimensionless** â€” both numerator and denominator must be in cycles.
> 4. **Basquin exponent sign error:** Forgetting the negative sign in $b$ will yield answers off by orders of magnitude.
> 5. **Skipping intermediate steps in Part B:** Examiners award step marks â€” show every substitution and numerical computation explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Fatigue** = progressive damage under cyclic stresses below yield, in three stages: **Initiation â†’ Propagation â†’ Final Fracture**.
- **Basquin's Law** governs the finite-life region of the S-N curve: $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b$ with $b \approx -0.10$ for steels.
- **Endurance limit** $\sigma_e$ exists only for ferrous steels under benign environments; **non-ferrous alloys** and seawater-exposed details have **no true endurance limit**.
- **Stress amplitude** $\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ and **mean stress** $\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$.
- **Mean stress correction theories:** Soderberg (most conservative), **Goodman** (most used in ship design), Gerber (least conservative).
- **Stress Concentration Factor** $K_t$ for geometric discontinuities; **Notch Sensitivity** $q$ accounts for material ductility.
- **Palmgren-Miner cumulative damage rule:** $D = \sum (n_i / N_i)$; failure at $D = 1$.
- **Paris-Erdogan crack growth:** $\frac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$ â€” used for inspection planning.
- **Ship hull fatigue hotspots:** hatch corners, bracket toes, weld terminations, side-shell-transverse intersections.
- **DNV FAT classes:** FAT-71, FAT-90, FAT-112, FAT-125 etc., representing the stress range at $2 \times 10^6$ cycles.
- **Service-life cycle count:** ~$10^7$ to $10^8$ cycles for a 25-year ship operating in harsh seas.
- **Mitigation hierarchy:** better detail â†’ thicker plate â†’ grinding â†’ peening â†’ coating â†’ inspection.
- **Common KTU marks-pitfall:** Using $\Delta\sigma$ where $\sigma_a$ is required, or vice versa.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accidental Limit States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Accidental Limit States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PCNST601 â€“ Ship Structures II**, **Accidental Limit States (ALS)** are defined as the category of *Ultimate Limit States (ULS)* of a ship's hull-girder and local structural components that are reached when the structure is subjected to **abnormal, low-probability, high-consequence load events** such as ship-to-ship **collision, grounding/stranding, fire, flooding, or weapon/missile impact**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Reference):**
> *Accidental Limit States deal with the structural response of the vessel under rare, extreme, and unintended loading conditions where the probability of occurrence is extremely low (typically **10âپ»âپ´ to 10âپ»âپµ per ship-year**), but the consequence on life, environment, and asset value is catastrophic. The design objective shifts from "no damage" to "survival, residual strength, and progressive collapse prevention."*

Mathematically, the **Accidental Limit State condition** is expressed as the inequality:

$$E_d \le R_d$$

Where:
- $E_d$ = design effect of the accidental action (factored load)
- $R_d$ = design resistance of the damaged/affected structure
- This must be satisfied for the *damaged* hull, post-event, with possibly *reduced* partial safety factors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy â€” "The Motorcycle Helmet Principle"**
> Imagine a soldier or a stunt rider. He does not design his *daily life* assuming he will crash every minute (that would be the **Ultimate Limit State** for normal operation). However, he wears a **helmet** â€” a *redundant safety system* â€” because he *acknowledges* that a rare crash (10âپ»âپ´ probability) *could* happen, and when it does, his **head must survive the second impact and not collapse progressively**.
> A ship designed for **Accidental Limit States** is exactly this helmet: it acknowledges the rare, catastrophic event and ensures the vessel **does not sink immediately, does not lose watertight integrity in cascade, and retains enough strength to evacuate the crew and reach a safe harbor**.

| Event Type | Probability per Ship-Year | Consequence Level |
|---|---|---|
| Normal seaway (SLS/ULS) | $10^{0}$ â€“ $10^{-1}$ | Operational, recoverable |
| Severe storm (ULS) | $10^{-2}$ â€“ $10^{-3}$ | Major damage, survival required |
| **Accidental (ALS)** | **$10^{-4}$ â€“ $10^{-5}$** | **Loss of vessel if not designed** |
| Extraordinary (war, terrorism) | $\le 10^{-6}$ | Beyond commercial design |

The **Annual Expected Loss (AEL)** is a key metric used by Classification Societies (DNV, Lloyd's, IACS):

$$\text{AEL} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{Accident}_i) \times C(\text{Accident}_i)$$

Where $P$ is the annual probability and $C$ is the consequence (repair cost, fatality risk, pollution).

> [!TIP]
> The **constant 1.0 (unity) check** for the intact ship under normal seaway is replaced by a **reduced safety factor** (often 1.0 or even lower) for the *damaged* ship under accidental events â€” the philosophy is: *"The event is so rare that the partial safety factor can be relaxed, but the structure must still not collapse."*

---

### 1.3 Governing Codes & KTU Board-Relevant Context

The following classification and regulatory documents govern ALS design and are **frequently referenced in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions**:

- **IACS S21** â€“ Special Service Vessels (Accidental Limit State framework)
- **IMO SOLAS Chapter II-1** â€“ Subdivision and Damage Stability
- **IACS S25** â€“ Strength of Double Hull Oil Tankers (accidental loading)
- **DNV GL DNVGL-OS-C301** â€“ Stability and Watertightness (accidental flooding)
- **Lloyd's Register Ship Rules** â€“ Accidental Loading and Residual Strength
- **Common Structural Rules (CSR)** â€“ IACS harmonized rule set
- **IMO MSC.1/Circ.1455** â€“ Guidelines for approval of alternatives and equivalents (ALS performance-based design)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants (Memorize for KTU Board):**
> - Design life of a ship: **25 years** (standard commercial)
> - Return period for accidental events: **10,000 years** (probability = $10^{-4}$/year)
> - Return period for extreme storm (ULS): **100 years** (probability = $10^{-2}$/year)
> - Double-hull tankers minimum design accidental load (DNV): **25 kN/mآ²** bottom plate impact
> - Collision penetration energy budget (typical design): **80 MJ** for bulk carrier grounding on a soft bottom

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Tier Limit State Hierarchy (Recap for KTU Board)

Every KTU Ship Structures II question on ALS must first be positioned within the **limit state design pyramid**:

```
â”Œâ”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”گ
â”‚  3. ACCIDENTAL LIMIT STATE (ALS)            â”‚  â†گ THIS MODULE
â”‚     P â‰ˆ 10âپ»âپ´ to 10âپ»âپµ / year                â”‚
â”‚     Residual strength, redundancy           â”‚
â”œâ”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¤
â”‚  2. ULTIMATE LIMIT STATE (ULS)              â”‚
â”‚     P â‰ˆ 10âپ»آ² / year (extreme storm)         â”‚
â”‚     No collapse, no buckling                â”‚
â”œâ”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¤
â”‚  1. SERVICEABILITY LIMIT STATE (SLS)        â”‚
â”‚     P â‰ˆ 10âپ»آ¹ / year (operational)           â”‚
â”‚     No yielding, no excessive deflection     â”‚
â””â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”ک
```

### 2.2 The Four Canonical Accidental Scenarios

> [!NOTE]
> The **KTU 2024 syllabus and IMO/IACS framework** recognizes exactly four canonical accidental events. Every 14-mark Part B question on ALS is built around one or more of these.

1. **Collision (Ship-to-Ship or Ship-to-Object)**
2. **Grounding / Stranding (Bottom Impact)**
3. **Fire (Local Compartment Heating)**
4. **Flooding (Progressive Watertightness Failure)**

### 2.3 Detailed Load Modelling for Each Scenario

#### A. Collision Scenario
The collision load is modelled as an **energy-based dynamic impact** between two ships. The kinetic energy of the striking vessel is:

$$E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} M_{eff} V_{rel}^2$$

Where:
- $M_{eff}$ = effective (added-mass corrected) mass of striking ship = $M_{struck} + C_a M_{striking}$
- $C_a$ = added mass coefficient, **typically 0.4 to 0.8** for a struck vessel
- $V_{rel}$ = relative impact velocity in the direction of collision

This energy is partly absorbed by the **striking bow** ($E_{bow}$), partly by the **struck side shell** ($E_{side}$):

$$E_{kin} = E_{bow} + E_{side}$$

> [!TIP]
> A standard KTU Board shortcut: assume **bow absorbs 60% and side absorbs 40%** if the striking bow is reinforced; otherwise **50/50** for an unreinforced nose.

**Side shell indentation depth** under collision (simplified, IACS S21 formulation):

$$h = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{side}}{\sigma_y \, t_{eff}}}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_y$ = yield stress of side shell steel
- $t_{eff}$ = effective plate thickness including stiffeners (transformed section)

**Local panel damage criterion (DNV):** the panel fails if

$$\frac{h}{b} \ge 0.05$$

where $b$ is the panel breadth (spacing of longitudinal stiffeners).

#### B. Grounding Scenario
Grounding is modelled as the **bottom of the ship** striking a rigid rock or seabed protrusion. The classification is:

| Bottom Type | Hardness (kN/mآ²) | Typical Scenario |
|---|---|---|
| Soft (sand, mud) | $\le 2 \times 10^{3}$ | Shallow bank |
| Medium (gravel) | $2 \times 10^{3}$ to $1 \times 10^{4}$ | Continental shelf |
| Hard (rock, coral) | $> 1 \times 10^{4}$ | Reef, pinnacle rock |

The **bottom plate impact pressure** for the damaged condition is:

$$p_{ground} = \frac{F_{ground}}{A_{contact}}$$

with $A_{contact}$ = contact area $\approx 0.5\,L\,B_{touch}$ (length أ— contact breadth).

**Energy balance for grounding** (per IACS S21, simplified):

$$E_{kin} = E_{bottom} + E_{internal}$$

where $E_{internal}$ is absorbed by internal structures (transverse web frames, floors, double bottom).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Double-bottom height** is the primary design parameter against grounding. IACS S21 requires minimum **$h_{DB} = B/20$ or 1.5 m, whichever is greater**, for oil tankers.

#### C. Fire Scenario
Fire is modelled as a **thermal load** that reduces the yield strength of steel and induces thermal stresses. The reduction factor for yield strength is given by an **ISO 834-type curve**:

$$\frac{\sigma_{y,T}}{\sigma_{y,20}} = f(T)$$

Approximate piecewise values (memorize):

| Temperature $T$ (آ°C) | 20 | 200 | 400 | 600 | 800 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\sigma_{y,T}/\sigma_{y,20}$ | 1.00 | 0.95 | 0.75 | 0.45 | 0.10 |

**Thermal expansion strain** (constrained heating, no buckling):

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \Delta T$$

where $\alpha = 11 \times 10^{-6}$ /آ°C for mild steel.

#### D. Flooding Scenario
Flooding is governed by **damage stability** rather than structural strength per se. The key criterion is the **permeability** $\mu$ of each compartment:

$$\mu = \frac{\text{Volume of floodwater}}{\text{Compartment volume}}$$

Typical values: $\mu_{machinery} \approx 0.85$, $\mu_{accommodation} \approx 0.95$, $\mu_{cargo} \approx 0.60$â€“$0.80$.

The **margin line** must not be submerged in the final equilibrium condition after flooding of any one or two adjacent compartments (depending on vessel class per SOLAS).

### 2.4 The Three Design Strategies for ALS

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question (3 marks): "List and explain the three strategies to satisfy Accidental Limit States."**

**Strategy 1 â€” Compartmentalization (Redundancy by Subdivision)**
- Watertight bulkheads divide the hull into compartments
- Failure of one compartment must not lead to progressive flooding
- Index: $N_{compartments} \ge 3$ for cargo length, $N_{factor} \ge 0.5$ for survival

**Strategy 2 â€” Local Reinforcement (Strength-Based Defence)**
- Strengthen side shell, double bottom, and boundaries against the accidental action
- E.g., thicker side shell plating (e.g., $t_{side} \ge t_{bottom} + 2$ mm for tankers)
- Stifferener spacing reduced by 20% in critical zones

**Strategy 3 â€” Energy Absorption (Ductility-Based Defence)**
- Design structural members to deform plastically and absorb kinetic energy
- Use corrugated bulkheads, crash bows, ductile fracture-resistant steel
- Target specific energy absorption: $E_{abs} \ge 0.5 \times E_{kin}$

### 2.5 Probabilistic Basis and Partial Safety Factors

For **ALS design**, the partial safety factors are *reduced* compared to ULS:

| Load Combination | $\gamma_G$ (permanent) | $\gamma_Q$ (variable) | $\gamma_A$ (accidental) | $\gamma_M$ (material) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ULS (normal) | 1.20 | 1.30 | â€” | 1.15 |
| ULS (extreme) | 1.10 | 1.10 | â€” | 1.10 |
| **ALS** | **1.00** | â€” | **1.00** | **1.00** |

The **justification**: since $P(\text{ALS}) \ll P(\text{ULS})$, the *reliability index* $\beta$ can be lower:

$$\beta_{ALS} \ge 2.3 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \beta_{ULS} \ge 3.7$$

(Corresponding to probabilities $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-4}$ respectively.)

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{kin} = \tfrac{1}{2} M_{eff} V^2$ | Collision kinetic energy | Collision ALS |
| 2 | $h = \sqrt{2E/(\sigma_y t_{eff})}$ | Indentation depth | Side shell crash |
| 3 | $h/b \ge 0.05$ | Panel damage criterion | IACS S21 |

| 9 | $\beta = -\Phi^{-1}(P_f)$ | Reliability index | Probabilistic ALS |

> - $R_d$ = design resistance (factored) â†’ keep subscript clear
> - $\Phi^{-1}$ denotes the inverse standard normal CDF â€” **do not** confuse with diameter

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

ALS design principles govern the construction of:
- **Passenger Ships** (SOLAS damage stability â€” 2-compartment subdivision)
- **Oil Tankers** (MARPOL double-hull requirements post-Exxon Valdez)
- **Naval Warships** (battle damage tolerance, shock design)
- **LNG Carriers** (membrane/independent tank integrity under collision)
- **Submarines** (collapse depth + accidental flooding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 1989 *Exxon Valdez* oil spill (Alaska) directly led to the **Oil Pollution Act of 1990 (OPA-90)** and the IMO double-hull mandate. This is a real-world example of an ALS event ($P \approx 10^{-4}$/year) whose consequence justified a *global* structural redesign costing the industry **$ billions**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Side Shell Indentation Depth Under Collision

**Statement:** For a struck ship with side shell of yield stress $\sigma_y$ and effective thickness $t_{eff}$, derive the indentation depth $h$ as a function of the absorbed collision energy $E_{side}$.

**Given:** The side shell acts as a **rigid-plastic membrane** stretching over a fixed boundary (transverse web frame spacing $b$).

**Step 1 â€” Energy Assumption:**
The plastic membrane strain energy per unit width of a stretched plate (large deflection theory) is:

$$U_{plate} = \sigma_y \, t_{eff} \, h^2 \quad \text{(per unit width)}$$

> **Logic:** When a flat plate of thickness $t_{eff}$ is stretched by an indenter to depth $h$, the membrane force is $N = \sigma_y \, t_{eff}$ (full yield). Integrating the work of this constant force over displacement $h$ gives energy $\propto h^2$ (not $h^3$ as in elastic bending â€” plastic regime).

**Step 2 â€” Total Energy of Indented Panel:**
For a panel of width $b$ (stiffener spacing) absorbing energy uniformly:

$$E_{side} = N \cdot \sigma_y \, t_{eff} \cdot h^2$$

**Step 3 â€” Solve for $h$:**

$$h^2 = \frac{2 E_{side}}{\sigma_y \, t_{eff}}$$

$$\boxed{h = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{side}}{\sigma_y \, t_{eff}}}}$$

**Step 4 â€” Apply Damage Criterion (IACS S21):**
The panel is considered *failed* (loss of watertightness) when:

$$\frac{h}{b} \ge 0.05 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h \ge 0.05 \, b$$

**Step 5 â€” Solve for Critical Energy:**

$$E_{crit} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \, t_{eff} \, (0.05\,b)^2 = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \, \sigma_y \, t_{eff} \, b^2$$

> This is the **energy budget** the side shell must absorb *before* breach. The remainder of the collision energy must be absorbed by the bow.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Annual Expected Loss (AEL) and Optimized Reliability

**Statement:** Show that the optimal reliability index for ALS design minimizes the sum of initial construction cost and expected failure cost.

**Step 1 â€” Define Total Cost Function:**

$$C_{total}(\beta) = C_{construct}(\beta) + P_f(\beta) \cdot C_{fail}$$

**Step 2 â€” Construction Cost as a Function of $\beta$:**

$$C_{construct}(\beta) = C_0 + k \cdot \beta$$

(Linear approximation: higher $\beta$ â†’ thicker plates, more material.)

**Step 3 â€” Probability of Failure:**

$$P_f(\beta) = \Phi(-\beta) = \int_{-\infty}^{-\beta} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-u^2/2} \, du$$

**Step 4 â€” Take Derivative and Set to Zero:**

$$\frac{d C_{total}}{d \beta} = k - C_{fail} \cdot \phi(\beta) = 0$$

where $\phi(\beta) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\beta^2/2}$ is the standard normal PDF.

**Step 5 â€” Solve Implicitly:**

$$\phi(\beta_{opt}) = \frac{k}{C_{fail}}$$

$$e^{-\beta_{opt}^2/2} = \frac{k \sqrt{2\pi}}{C_{fail}}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_{opt} = \sqrt{2 \ln\left(\frac{C_{fail}}{k \sqrt{2\pi}}\right)}}$$

**Step 6 â€” Application to Ship Design:**
- For a tanker with $C_{fail} = \$1 \text{ billion}$ (incl. pollution), $k = \$50 \text{ million}$:
- $\beta_{opt} \approx 4.5$ â†’ corresponds to $P_f \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-6}$/year
- This is the **target reliability** for tanker ALS â€” derived, not prescribed.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a *semi-probabilistic* derivation. Classification rules (DNV, IACS) prescribe deterministic equivalents ($\beta = 3.7$ for ULS, $2.3$ for ALS) for ease of design.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Probabilistic Damage Extent via Sub-Probability

**Statement:** For a ship with $N$ watertight compartments, derive the probability that $k$ compartments are simultaneously flooded, assuming independent damage events with probability $p$ per compartment.

**Step 1 â€” Binomial Distribution:**

$$P(\text{exactly } k \text{ flooded}) = \binom{N}{k} p^k (1-p)^{N-k}$$

**Step 2 â€” Expected Number of Flooded Compartments:**

$$E[K] = N p$$

**Step 3 â€” Variance:**

$$\text{Var}(K) = N p (1-p)$$

**Step 4 â€” SOLAS Application (Two-Compartment Subdivision):**
For passenger ships, SOLAS requires survival when **any 2 adjacent compartments** are flooded. Probability of survival:

$$P_{surv} = 1 - P(\text{any 2 adjacent flooded})$$

$$P_{surv} = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} P_i P_{i+1} \approx 1 - (N-1) p^2$$

For $N = 20$, $p = 0.005$:

$$P_{surv} \approx 1 - 19 \times (0.005)^2 = 1 - 4.75 \times 10^{-4} = 0.999525$$

> This corresponds to $\beta \approx 3.3$ â€” the target for passenger ship subdivision.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Thermal Stress in a Constrained Steel Plate Under Fire

**Statement:** A simply-supported plate of side $a$ is heated uniformly from $20آ°\text{C}$ to $Tآ°\text{C}$ while edges are restrained. Derive the induced thermal stress.

**Step 1 â€” Free Thermal Strain (Unrestrained):**

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha (T - 20)$$

**Step 2 â€” Mechanical Strain (Restrained, equal and opposite):**

$$\varepsilon_{mech} = -\varepsilon_{th} = -\alpha (T - 20)$$

**Step 3 â€” Stress (Linear Elastic):**

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon_{mech} = -E \alpha (T - 20)$$

For $E = 210 \text{ GPa}$, $\alpha = 11 \times 10^{-6}$ /آ°C, $T = 500آ°\text{C}$:

$$\sigma = -210 \times 10^9 \times 11 \times 10^{-6} \times 480 = -1.11 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 4 â€” Yielding Check (Steel softens at 500آ°C):**
At $500آ°\text{C}$, $\sigma_{y,500} \approx 0.6 \times \sigma_{y,20} = 0.6 \times 235 = 141 \text{ MPa}$.

Since $|\sigma| = 1110 \text{ MPa} \gg 141 \text{ MPa}$, the plate **yields and enters plastic regime** immediately. In reality, plastic flow relaxes the stress to the yield value:

$$\sigma_{actual} = \sigma_{y,T}$$

**Step 5 â€” Conclusion for Fire ALS:**
Structural members under fire loading must be checked for **reduced yield** at elevated temperature. For ALS, only the **residual (post-fire) strength** matters, since the structure is designed to *survive the event*, not function during it.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A â€” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define Accidental Limit State. List the four canonical accidental scenarios considered in ship design.**

**Model Answer (verbatim board format):**
*Accidental Limit State (ALS) is an Ultimate Limit State of the ship's structure that corresponds to a rare, abnormal loading event with annual probability in the range of **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ per ship-year**. The design objective is to ensure the vessel retains residual strength and does not suffer progressive collapse following such an event.*

*The four canonical accidental scenarios are:*
*1. Ship-to-ship or ship-to-object **collision***
*2. **Grounding / stranding** on seabed*
*3. **Fire** in a compartment*
*4. **Flooding** of one or more compartments (damage stability)*

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for probability range, 1 for four scenarios â€” all three must be present for full marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. State the partial safety factors used in the ALS load combination. Justify why they are lower than those for normal ULS.**

**Model Answer:**
*For ALS, the partial safety factors are typically taken as:*
*$\gamma_G = 1.0$ (permanent loads)*
*$\gamma_A = 1.0$ (accidental action)*
*$\gamma_M = 1.0$ (material resistance)*

*These are **lower than the corresponding ULS values** ($\gamma_G = 1.2$, $\gamma_M = 1.15$) because the probability of an accidental event is **very low** (10âپ»âپ´/year), so the target **reliability index** $\beta_{ALS} \approx 2.3$ is **lower than $\beta_{ULS} \approx 3.7$**. The economic principle is to avoid over-design for a rare event while still preventing catastrophic failure.* (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B â€” 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Instructions:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) Derive an expression for the indentation depth $h$ of a side shell panel under collision energy $E_{side}$, given yield stress $\sigma_y$ and effective thickness $t_{eff}$.** [7 marks]

**(b) A Panamax bulk carrier (L = 200 m, B = 32 m) is struck amidships by a vessel of effective mass 30,000 tonnes at a relative velocity of 6 m/s. The side shell has $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa and effective thickness $t_{eff} = 30$ mm. The transverse stiffener spacing is $b = 0.7$ m. If the striking bow absorbs 60% of the kinetic energy, determine:**
*(i) The energy absorbed by the side shell.* [2 marks]
*(ii) The indentation depth $h$.* [2 marks]
*(iii) Whether the panel fails the IACS S21 damage criterion $h/b \ge 0.05$.* [2 marks]
*(iv) Suggest TWO design modifications to satisfy the ALS check.* [1 mark]

**Model Solution (a):**

*Step 1:* In the rigid-plastic membrane regime, the side shell behaves as a constant-force membrane with plastic force per unit width:
$$N_p = \sigma_y \cdot t_{eff}$$

*Step 2:* Work done by this constant force as the indenter displaces through depth $h$:
$$W = N_p \cdot h^2 = \sigma_y t_{eff} h^2$$

*Step 3:* Equating absorbed energy to collision energy:
$$E_{side} = \sigma_y t_{eff} h^2$$

*Step 4:* Solving for $h$:
$$\boxed{h = \sqrt{\frac{E_{side}}{\sigma_y \, t_{eff}}}}$$

*(Alternative expression with factor 2 accepted if student derives from energy balance $E = N_p \cdot h$ instead of $h^2$. Valuation: 1 mark per step, 7 marks total.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

*(i) Total collision energy:*
$$E_{kin} = \tfrac{1}{2} M_{eff} V^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 30{,}000{,}000 \times 6^2 = 540 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 540 \text{ MJ}$$

*Side shell absorbs 40% (since bow takes 60%):*
$$E_{side} = 0.4 \times 540 = 216 \text{ MJ}$$

*(ii) Indentation depth:*
$$h = \sqrt{\frac{E_{side}}{\sigma_y t_{eff}}} = \sqrt{\frac{216 \times 10^6}{235 \times 10^6 \times 0.030}} = \sqrt{30.64} = 5.54 \text{ m}$$

*(iii) Damage criterion check:*
$$\frac{h}{b} = \frac{5.54}{0.7} = 7.91 \gg 0.05 \quad \text{(FAILS â€” panel breaches)}$$

*(iv) Design modifications:*
*1. **Increase effective thickness** $t_{eff}$ by adding a side stringer plate (corrugated) â€” increases absorption capacity.*
*2. **Reduce stiffener spacing** $b$ to 0.5 m (tighter bays) â€” distributes indentation over more panels.*
*3. Alternative: Add a **crash bulge** in the side shell to divert the bow and absorb energy before contact with the inner hull.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) â€” Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) With neat sketches, explain the three design strategies adopted to satisfy Accidental Limit States in ship structures. Mention the relevant IACS/IMO regulation for each.** [7 marks]

**(b) A double-hull oil tanker has $L = 250$ m, $B = 44$ m, $D = 21$ m. Calculate:**
*(i) The minimum permissible double-bottom height per IACS S25.* [2 marks]
*(ii) The probability that two adjacent compartments are simultaneously flooded, given 18 watertight compartments each with independent damage probability $p = 0.008$ per voyage. State whether this satisfies the SOLAS passenger-ship two-compartment criterion of $P_{survival} \ge 0.999$.** [5 marks]

**Model Solution (a):**

> **Strategy 1 â€” Compartmentalization (Redundancy)**
> *The hull is divided into $N$ watertight compartments separated by transverse bulkheads. The failure of one compartment must not cause progressive flooding of the entire ship.*
> *Relevant Rule: **IMO SOLAS Chapter II-1, Part B** â€” subdivision and damage stability for passenger ships. The required subdivision index $R \ge 0.5$ for a vessel to survive any single compartment flooding, and the "two-compartment" standard requires $R$ such that survival of any two adjacent compartments is assured.*

> **Strategy 2 â€” Local Reinforcement (Strength)**
> *Critical zones (side shell, bottom, connections) are reinforced with extra plate thickness, additional stiffeners, or bracketry. Example: a 2 mm increase in side shell thickness can reduce indentation depth by ~10%.*
> *Relevant Rule: **IACS S21** آ§4.3 â€” minimum local strength requirements for side shell and double bottom in oil tankers and bulk carriers.*

> **Strategy 3 â€” Energy Absorption (Ductility)**
> *Structural members are designed to deform plastically and dissipate collision/grounding kinetic energy. Examples: corrugated transverse bulkheads, crash bow geometry, ductile steel grades (Grade E, F).*
> *Relevant Rule: **IACS S21** آ§4.5 â€” energy-absorption requirements; **DNV GL DNVGL-OS-C301** آ§6 â€” ductility and crashworthiness.*

*Sketch: a cross-section showing watertight bulkheads (Strategy 1), thickened side shell (Strategy 2), and a corrugated bulkhead (Strategy 3).*

*(Valuation: 2 marks per strategy + 1 mark for sketch/regulation. Total 7.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

*(i) IACS S25 minimum double-bottom height:*
$$h_{DB,\min} = \max\left(\frac{B}{20}, 1.5 \text{ m}\right) = \max\left(\frac{44}{20}, 1.5\right) = \max(2.2, 1.5) = \boxed{2.2 \text{ m}}$$

*(ii) Probability of two adjacent compartments flooded:*
With $N = 18$ compartments, the number of *adjacent pairs* is $N - 1 = 17$.

$$P(\text{2 adjacent flooded}) = (N-1) \cdot p^2 = 17 \times (0.008)^2 = 17 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5} = 1.088 \times 10^{-3}$$

*Probability of survival:*
$$P_{surv} = 1 - 1.088 \times 10^{-3} = 0.998912$$

*Comparison with criterion:*
$$P_{surv} = 0.9989 < 0.9990 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FAILS the two-compartment criterion}$$

*Recommended design action:* add **2 additional watertight bulkheads** to reduce $p$ per compartment to $p' = 0.008 \times \sqrt{17/19} \approx 0.0076$, then $P_{surv} \approx 0.999$ (satisfied). Alternative: reduce $N$ to 16 by increasing compartment size, but this **reduces** safety â€” wrong direction.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Read Before Writing Your Exam):**
> 1. **Confusing ULS and ALS partial safety factors.** Writing $\gamma_G = 1.2$ for ALS loses 1 mark. For ALS, **all factors are 1.0**.
> 2. **Forgetting the 2-compartment SOLAS rule for passenger ships.** For cargo ships, it's *1-compartment* (R = 0.5). For tankers, MARPOL double-hull.
> 3. **Unit errors in collision energy.** Convert tonnes to kg ($1 \text{ t} = 1000 \text{ kg}$), velocity in m/s, result in **Joules (J)**.
> 4. **Confusing added mass coefficient $C_a$** with displacement. $M_{eff} = M_{struck} + C_a M_{striking}$ â€” *both* masses appear.
> 5. **Using elastic bending formula for collision** ($W \propto h^3$) instead of plastic membrane ($W \propto h^2$). The *plastic* regime governs ALS.
> 6. **Forgetting to multiply $p^2$ by $(N-1)$** for adjacent compartments â€” students often write only $p^2$ (lone pair), losing 2 marks.
> 7. **Mixing up fire strength reduction with fatigue.** Fire reduces $\sigma_y$ *temporarily* during the event; fatigue is cumulative over the service life. These are **different** limit state categories.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST (Re-read 1 hour before exam):**

- âک‘ **Accidental Limit States (ALS)** address *rare* events with $P \approx 10^{-4}$â€“$10^{-5}$/year.
- âک‘ The **four canonical scenarios** are: Collision, Grounding, Fire, Flooding.
- âک‘ **Partial safety factors for ALS**: $\gamma_G = \gamma_A = \gamma_M = 1.0$ (relaxed from ULS).
- âک‘ **Reliability index** $\beta_{ALS} \approx 2.3$ (vs $\beta_{ULS} \approx 3.7$).
- âک‘ **Collision energy**: $E_{kin} = \tfrac{1}{2} M_{eff} V_{rel}^2$.
- âک‘ **Indentation depth**: $h = \sqrt{E_{side}/(\sigma_y t_{eff})}$.
- âک‘ **Damage criterion (IACS S21)**: $h/b \ge 0.05$ â†’ panel fails.
- âک‘ **Double-bottom height** (IACS S25): $h_{DB} \ge \max(B/20,\; 1.5\text{ m})$.
- âک‘ **Fire**: yield drops to ~60% at 500آ°C, ~10% at 800آ°C.
- âک‘ **Flooding**: SOLAS 1-compartment (cargo) or 2-compartment (passenger) rule.
- âک‘ **Three defence strategies**: Compartmentalization, Local Reinforcement, Energy Absorption.
- âک‘ **Annual Expected Loss**: $\text{AEL} = \sum P_i C_i$ â€” used in risk-based design.
- âک‘ **IACS S21, S25, SOLAS II-1, DNVGL-OS-C301** are the governing references.
- âک‘ **Real-world driver**: *Exxon Valdez* (1989) â†’ OPA-90 â†’ double-hull mandate.
- âک‘ **Key units**: energy in **MJ** (1 MJ = 10âپ¶ J), stress in **MPa**, mass in **tonnes** (1 t = 10آ³ kg), thickness in **mm or m** (be consistent).
- âک‘ Always **state assumptions** explicitly in derivations (e.g., "assume rigid-plastic membrane behaviour", "assume 50/50 energy split").

> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When the question says *"with neat sketches"*, draw at least **one diagram** with **labels** (5+ labels) and a **title**. A diagram without labels gets **0 marks** for the sketch component. When the question says *"derive"*, you must show **all algebraic steps** â€” a single final formula with no steps gets **at most 1 of 7 marks**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultimate Strength Assessment of Hull Girder Using Progressive Collapse Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ultimate Strength Assessment of Hull Girder Using Progressive Collapse Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 â€” Ultimate Strength Assessment of Hull Girder Using Progressive Collapse Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Progressive Collapse Analysis** is a nonlinear, incremental-iterative computational procedure used to determine the **ultimate bending moment capacity** of a ship's hull girder by progressively increasing curvature (خ؛) until structural collapse occurs. It traces the complete **momentâ€“curvature (Mâ€“خ؛) relationship** of the cross-section, accounting for material yielding, plate buckling, stiffener column buckling, lateralâ€“torsional buckling (tripping), and interaction effects. The peak value of this Mâ€“خ؛ curve represents the **Ultimate Hull Girder Moment (Mلµ¤)**.

### 1.1 Why Classical Linear Elastic Analysis Is NOT Sufficient

Traditional ship design relied on **Allowable Stress Design (ASD)** using linear elastic beam theory, where the bending stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

and is compared against an allowable fraction of yield stress. This method has two fatal limitations:

- **It cannot predict failure load** â€” only whether stress at working load exceeds a fraction of yield.
- **It ignores post-buckling reserve strength** and load redistribution.

> [!NOTE]
> Real ship structures possess enormous **post-buckling reserve strength** because thinner plating buckles but continues to carry load through membrane action (tension field / effective width behavior). ASD wastes this capacity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy â€” The "Bamboo Stick Bent Into a U"

Imagine bending a **slotted bamboo stick** (representing a stiffened plating with gaps). The stick does not snap at the first crack â€” instead:

- **Stage 1 (Elastic):** Outer fibres stretch, slight bending.
- **Stage 2 (Plate buckling):** The thin plates between slots wrinkle like corrugated cardboard. They still carry load.
- **Stage 3 (Stiffener buckling):** The "ribs" (stiffeners) themselves start to bow sideways.
- **Stage 4 (Plastic collapse):** The structure sags irreversibly â€” collapse!

The progressive collapse analysis mathematically traces this exact journey from elastic bending to total collapse. The peak moment the stick can carry *just before* it folds completely is the **ultimate strength**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering significance:** The IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR), and modern classification rules (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) require ultimate strength verification via progressive collapse for tankers, bulk carriers, and naval vessels. Progressive collapse analysis typically shows the hull can withstand **1.5 to 2.5 times** the design (rule) bending moment â€” a critical margin for survival in extreme seas.

### 1.3 Historical Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Smith's Method (1977)** â€” Developed by **C.S. Smith** of Det norske Veritas (DNV), published in the *Transactions of SNAME*. This is the foundational and most widely taught procedure for hull girder progressive collapse. Extensions include the **ISUM (Idealized Structural Unit Method)** by Ueda and Rashed, and **ALPS/HULLST** programs.

---

### 1.4 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Ultimate Moment | $M_u$ | Peak moment from Mâ€“خ؛ curve |
| Curvature | $\kappa$ | Bending curvature (1/R) |
| Yield Stress | $\sigma_y$ | Material yield stress |
| Neutral Axis | NA | Axis of zero strain |
| Effective Width | $b_e$ | Reduced width carrying full stress after buckling |
| Plate Slenderness | $\beta$ | Non-dimensional plate parameter |
