**Design Strategy:** Avoid coincidence between excitation frequencies and hull natural frequencies by ensuring a **15â€“20% frequency separation margin**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the single degree of freedom (SDOF) vibration model for a ship hull. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concepts of damping, damping ratio, and logarithmic decrement with mathematical expressions. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a):**

**Schematic Description (Verbal â€” Mermaid Block Representation):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Hull Section Mass m] --- K[Spring Element Stiffness k]
    M --- D[Damper Damping c]
    K --- F[Fixed Reference Base]
    D --- F
    M --> X[Displacement x of t]
```

**Explanation:**

1. The ship hull (or a representative section) is modelled as a **rigid mass** $m$.
2. The **elastic restoring force** of the hull girder is represented by a **linear spring** of stiffness $k$.
3. **Energy dissipation** mechanisms (structural, hydrodynamic) are lumped into a **viscous damper** of coefficient $c$.
4. The **displacement** $x(t)$ is the single degree of freedom.

**Mathematical Model:**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

For free undamped vibration: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and $T = 2\pi/\omega_n$.

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b):**

**1. Damping:**
Damping is the mechanism by which **vibratory energy is dissipated** over time, causing the amplitude to decay. In ships, three forms dominate:
- **Structural (Hysteretic) Damping** â€” internal friction in steel.
- **Hydrodynamic (Radiation) Damping** â€” energy radiated into surrounding water.
- **Viscous (Boundary Layer) Damping** â€” shear at the hull surface.

**2. Damping Ratio:**
The damping ratio $\zeta$ is the ratio of actual damping to critical damping:

$$\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$$

Behaviour:
- $\zeta < 1$: **Underdamped** (oscillatory decay).
- $\zeta = 1$: **Critically damped** (fastest non-oscillatory return).
- $\zeta > 1$: **Overdamped** (slow non-oscillatory return).

**3. Logarithmic Decrement:**
Defined as the natural logarithm of the ratio of two successive peak amplitudes:

$$\delta = \ln\left(\dfrac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$$

**Experimental Use:** In ship trials, $\delta$ is obtained by striking the hull and recording the decay envelope; then:

$$\zeta = \dfrac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^{2}+\delta^{2}}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning â€” Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rad/s to Hz** â€” this costs **2 marks** consistently.
> 2. **Mixing up $\omega_n$ and $f_n$** in the formula sheet â€” clearly distinguish with units.
> 3. **Skipping the free-body diagram step** in derivations â€” KTU examiners award **at least 1 mark** for a clearly stated FBD or its verbal equivalent.
> 4. **Confusing damping ratio with damping coefficient** â€” $\zeta$ is dimensionless, $c$ has units of Nآ·s/m.
> 5. **Omitting the negative sign** in the restoring force equation $F = -kx$ â€” this changes the entire derivation.
> 6. **Not stating the resonance condition** explicitly in conceptual answers â€” must say "when $\omega \approx \omega_n$."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist â€” Introduction to Hull Structure Vibration**

- âœ… Hull vibration = oscillatory motion of elastic hull structure under dynamic excitation.
- âœ… Classification: **Vertical / Horizontal / Torsional**; **Free / Forced / Damped**; **SDOF / Multi-DOF**.
- âœ… **SDOF equation of motion:** $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$.
- âœ… **Natural frequency (undamped):** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ rad/s, $f_n = \omega_n/(2\pi)$ Hz.
- âœ… **Critical damping:** $c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$.
- âœ… **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c/c_c$. Real ship hulls typically have $\zeta \approx 0.01 - 0.05$ (very lightly damped).
- âœ… **Damped natural frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$.
- âœ… **Logarithmic decrement:** $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$; used in sea trials.
- âœ… **Magnification factor** peaks at $r \approx 1$ â†’ **resonance**.
- âœ… **Typical hull natural frequency:** **2 â€“ 12 Hz** (main hull girder modes).
- âœ… **Design rule:** Maintain a **15â€“20% frequency separation margin** between excitation and natural frequencies.
- âœ… **Major excitation sources:** Propeller blade-rate, engine orders, wave encounter frequency, slamming, auxiliary machinery.
- âœ… **Practical implications:** Fatigue, crew comfort (ISO 6954), propeller safety, acoustic stealth.
- âœ… **KTU must-knows:** Derive SDOF equation; explain damping types; calculate $\omega_n$ from given $k$ and $m$; discuss resonance avoidance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources of Vibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST601 - SHIP STRUCTURES II
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sources of Vibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sources of Vibration (KTU Definition)**
> In the context of ship structural dynamics, **Sources of Vibration** are defined as the various physical, mechanical, and hydrodynamic origins of oscillatory excitation forces and moments that induce time-dependent displacements, velocities, and accelerations in the hull, superstructure, and appendages of a marine vessel. These excitations operate across a wide frequency spectrum (typically **0.5 Hz to 1000 Hz**) and are the root cause of all subsequent ship vibration, noise radiation, and structural fatigue phenomena.

In ship structures, vibration is fundamentally classified based on the **energy domain** from which the excitation force is derived. The KTU 2024 Scheme module on vibration analysis categorizes the principal sources into four engineering families:

1. **Propeller-induced hydrodynamic excitation** (e.g., blade-rate forces, shaft-rate forces, surface forces).
2. **Main and auxiliary engine excitation** (e.g., reciprocating inertia forces, torque fluctuations, gear meshing).
3. **Wave-induced and seakeeping excitation** (e.g., regular/irregular wave loads, slamming, whipping).
4. **Cavitation and fluid-structure interaction excitation** (e.g., bubble collapse, vortex shedding).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 â€“ Module 4):**
> The student is expected to identify, classify, and quantify the magnitude-frequency spectrum of each major excitation source, and to predict its first-order interaction with the natural frequencies of the hull girder, local panels, and superstructure elements.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"The Ship as a Concert Hall" Analogy**
> Imagine a large floating concert hall. Every time a musician plays a note, the hall vibrates. The musicians are the **sources of vibration**: the engine drummer (steady, rhythmic), the propeller violins (high-pitched, blade-passing frequency), the wave-dancing ballerinas (slow, irregular), and the cavitation whisperer (broadband hissing). The hall itself â€” the ship hull â€” has its own **resonant notes** (natural frequencies). When any musician's note matches the hall's resonant frequency, the entire building shakes violently. Ship engineers must therefore (a) know the hall's resonant notes, and (b) know exactly which musician plays which note, so they can dampen the troublesome ones.

The key engineering insight is that **a vibration problem has two ingredients**: an excitation source and a resonant structure. The hull, being a complex built-up beam-stiffened plate assembly, has hundreds of natural frequencies. Therefore, the *source* identification is half the solution to the problem â€” once the excitation frequencies are known, the engineer can compare them against the **Campbell diagram** of the ship to identify dangerous resonances.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values for Ship Vibration Analysis:**
> - **Reference acceleration for habitability:** $a_{ref} = 0.01 g$ (where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$)
> - **Sound reference pressure:** $p_0 = 20 \text{ خ¼Pa}$ in air; $p_0 = 1 \text{ خ¼Pa}$ in water
> - **Sea water density:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Ship building steel density:** $\rho_{st} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Young's modulus for ship steel:** $E = 2.1 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$
> - **Standard propeller blade count range:** $Z = 3 \text{ to } 7$
> - **Typical main engine firing frequency range:** $f_e = 5 \text{ to } 20 \text{ Hz}$
> - **Typical blade-rate frequency range:** $f_{BPF} = 20 \text{ to } 120 \text{ Hz}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Vibration Sources (Engineering Logic Breakdown)

The following hierarchical breakdown represents the KTU 2024 taxonomy of ship vibration sources. Each source is analyzed for its **physical origin**, **frequency signature**, and **structural target**.

### 2.1.1 Propeller-Induced Excitation (Hydrodynamic Source)

The rotating propeller is the **dominant high-frequency excitation source** on virtually all merchant and naval vessels. The excitation mechanisms are:

- **Blade-Rate (Blade-Passing) Frequency Forces:** Each blade sweeps past the hull, causing a periodic pressure pulse. With $Z$ blades rotating at $n$ revolutions per second, the blade-rate frequency is:

$$f_{BPF} = Z \cdot n \quad [\text{Hz}]$$

- **Shaft-Rate Frequency Forces:** First-order asymmetric wake fields, misaligned shaft geometry, or non-uniform propeller pitch produce excitation at the shaft rotation rate $n$ itself.

- **Higher-Order Harmonics:** Blade-rate harmonics at $kZ \cdot n$ for $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ (where $k$ is the harmonic number) become significant above $k = 6$ in cavitating propellers.

- **Surface Forces and Bearing Forces:** Asymmetric shaft-bearing forces transmitted through the stern tube and A-bracket.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical "Why":** As the propeller blade passes close to the hull, the local pressure on the hull drops and rises sharply. The non-uniform wake (caused by the hull boundary layer) hitting each blade at a slightly different angle produces a periodic fluctuating thrust component, which is the source of the excitation.

### 2.1.2 Main Engine Excitation (Mechanical Source)

For diesel-driven ships, the reciprocating engine is the **principal low-to-medium frequency source**. The dominant excitation frequencies are:

- **Firing Frequency:** Once per power stroke per cylinder.

$$f_{fire} = \frac{n \cdot N_{cyl}}{2 \cdot \tau} \quad [\text{Hz}]$$

where $\tau$ is the number of strokes per cycle (typically 2 for 2-stroke, 4 for 4-stroke).

- **Cylinder-Order Forces:** Inertial and gas-force unbalance in each cylinder at firing frequency, plus 2أ— firing frequency (secondary forces).

- **Torque Fluctuations:** Multi-cylinder torque variations transmitted through the crankshaft.

### 2.1.3 Wave-Induced Excitation (Environmental Source)

- **Regular Wave Encounter Frequency:** For a ship of length $L$ moving at speed $V$ in a regular wave of length $\lambda$:

$$f_e = \frac{g}{2\pi \lambda} \left( 1 + \frac{4\pi^2 V^2}{g \lambda} \cdot \cos\mu \right) \quad [\text{Hz}]$$

- **Slamming and Whipping:** Transient excitation from bow emergence, stern emergence, or bottom slamming in heavy seas. Whipping is a high-frequency, lightly-damped vibration of the hull girder excited by these transient loads.

### 2.1.4 Cavitation and Fluid-Structure Interaction

- **Sheet/Cloud/Bubble Cavitation Collapse:** Produces broadband high-frequency excitation (typically **1 kHz to 100 kHz**).
- **Vortex Shedding from Appendages:** Rudder, bilge keels, and shaft brackets shed vortices at the Strouhal frequency.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Source | Governing Formula | Variables | Typical Magnitude (Frequency) |
|---|--------|-------------------|-----------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Blade-Passing Frequency | $f_{BPF} = Z \cdot n$ | $Z$ = no. of blades, $n$ = rev/s | **20â€“120 Hz** |
| 2 | Shaft-Rate Frequency | $f_{shaft} = n$ | $n$ = rev/s | **0.5â€“5 Hz** |
| 3 | Engine Firing Frequency | $f_{fire} = \dfrac{N_{cyl} \cdot n}{2\tau}$ | $N_{cyl}$, $n$, $\tau$ | **5â€“20 Hz** |
| 4 | Engine 2nd Order | $f_{2nd} = 2 \cdot f_{fire}$ | â€“ | **10â€“40 Hz** |
| 5 | Wave Encounter Frequency | $f_e = \dfrac{g}{2\pi\lambda}\left(1 + \dfrac{4\pi^2 V^2}{g\lambda}\cos\mu\right)$ | $V$, $\lambda$, $\mu$ | **0.05â€“1 Hz** |
| 6 | Whipping Frequency (Hull Girder 2-node) | $f_{wh} \approx \dfrac{C}{L}\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{m}}$ | $EI$, $L$, $m$ | **4â€“12 Hz** |
| 7 | Vortex Shedding (Strouhal) | $f_v = \dfrac{S_t \cdot U}{D}$ | $S_t \approx 0.2$, $U$, $D$ | **0.5â€“10 Hz** |
| 8 | Cavitation Broadband Peak | $f_{cav} \propto \dfrac{1}{R_c}\sqrt{\dfrac{p_\infty - p_v}{\rho}}$ | $R_c$, $p_\infty$, $p_v$ | **1â€“100 kHz** |
| 9 | Force Harmonic Index | $F_k = F_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{f_k}{f_1}\right)^m$ | $F_1$, $m \in [1.5, 2.5]$ | â€“ |
| 10 | Vibration Level (dB) | $L_v = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{a}{a_{ref}}\right)$ | $a$ in m/sآ², $a_{ref} = 10^{-5}$ m/sآ² | â€“ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The formula sheet above is the entire backbone of vibration prediction during the *design phase* of a ship. The KTU examiner will frequently pose problems where the student must compute $f_{BPF}$ or $f_{fire}$ and compare it against the natural frequency of a local panel or hull-girder mode to identify a **resonance condition**.

## 2.3 Why and How Each Source Matters

- **Why propeller excitation matters:** It is the *only* source that scales with ship size â€” larger ships have larger propellers producing higher blade-rate forces on the afterbody, often coinciding with the 2-node hull-girder mode.
- **Why engine excitation matters:** It is *synchronous* with engine RPM, so it cannot be avoided by speed choice â€” only by engine mounting design (resilient mounts, tuned absorbers).
- **Why wave excitation matters:** It is *broadband and stochastic* â€” the worst-case for fatigue accumulation in the hull-girder.
- **Why cavitation matters:** It is the *only* source capable of exciting local plate modes (above 100 Hz) and is the dominant noise source for sonar signature of submarines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Blade-Passing Frequency Excitation Spectrum

We derive the time-domain and frequency-domain signatures of the propeller-induced hull pressure.

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Establish the Geometric Sweep

A propeller has $Z$ blades equally spaced at angular intervals of $\dfrac{2\pi}{Z}$ radians. The propeller rotates at constant angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi n$ rad/s, where $n$ is the number of revolutions per second.

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Single-Blade Pressure Signature

When a single blade passes a fixed observation point on the hull (e.g., a transducer on the stern), the unsteady pressure pulse $p(t)$ can be modeled as a periodic, localized impulse with a broad frequency content. We represent the pressure-time signal from the $j$-th blade as:

$$p_j(t) = p_0 \cdot \exp\left[-\left(\dfrac{t - t_j}{\tau_p}\right)^2\right]$$

where $t_j = \dfrac{j}{Z n}$ is the arrival time of the $j$-th blade, $\tau_p$ is the pulse width (proportional to blade chord divided by tangential velocity), and $p_0$ is the peak pressure amplitude.

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Sum Over All Blades

The total pressure at the observation point is the sum over all blades in one revolution:

$$p_{total}(t) = \sum_{j=0}^{Z-1} p_0 \cdot \exp\left[-\left(\dfrac{t - j/(Zn)}{\tau_p}\right)^2\right]$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Periodicity Recognition

Since the configuration repeats every $T = 1/(Zn)$ seconds, the signal is strictly periodic with the **blade-passing period** $T_{BPF} = \dfrac{1}{Z n}$. We compute the fundamental frequency:

$$f_{BPF} = \dfrac{1}{T_{BPF}} = Z n$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5: Fourier Series Expansion

Using the complex Fourier series representation for a periodic function:

$$p_{total}(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} c_k \cdot e^{i 2\pi k Z n t}$$

The Fourier coefficient $c_k$ is given by the integral over one period:

$$c_k = Z n \int_{0}^{1/(Zn)} p_{total}(t) \cdot e^{-i 2\pi k Z n t} \, dt$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6: Magnitude of the k-th Harmonic

Because each blade produces a Gaussian pulse of width $\tau_p$, the magnitude of the $k$-th harmonic rolls off approximately as a Gaussian function of frequency:

$$\vert c_k \vert \approx p_0 \cdot \exp\left[-\pi^2 (2 k Z n \tau_p)^2\right]$$

The peak of the spectrum occurs at $f_{BPF} = Zn$, and higher harmonics at $2 Z n, 3 Z n, \ldots$ are present but progressively weaker.

### 3.1.7 Step 7: Force Excitation at the Stern Bearing

The fluctuating pressure on the hull integrates to an oscillating force. The force is the integral of pressure over the wetted surface area $A$ of the afterbody:

$$F_{BPF}(t) = \int_A p_{total}(t, \vec{r}) \, dA$$

The first-harmonic force amplitude scales with the **non-uniform wake** field. If the wake has circumferential variation of amplitude $w_0$ about the mean, the first-harmonic blade-rate force is:

$$F_1 = C_F \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4 \cdot w_0$$

where $C_F$ is a force coefficient (typically 0.3â€“0.8 for merchant ships), $D$ is propeller diameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (Boxed):**
> $$\boxed{f_{BPF} = Z n, \quad F_1 \approx C_F \rho_{sw} n^2 D^4 w_0, \quad L_v = 20\log_{10}(a/a_{ref})}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Source-Frequency-Structure Coupling

The following matrix maps each **source** to its **frequency band** and the **ship structural element** it most likely excites. This is the KTU examiner's favorite chart-style question.

| Source | Frequency Band | Dominant Harmonic Index | Excited Structural Element | Mitigation Strategy |
|--------|----------------|-------------------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| Propeller (BPF) | 20â€“120 Hz | $kZ = 1$ | Afterbody shell, local plates | Wake equalizing ducts, fin stabilizers |
| Propeller (higher) | 60â€“500 Hz | $kZ \geq 2$ | Local plate panels | Cavitation control, skew blades |
| Main Engine Firing | 5â€“20 Hz | $k = 1$ | Hull girder (2-node mode) | Resilient engine mounts |
| Engine 2nd Order | 10â€“40 Hz | $k = 2$ | Hull girder, superstructure | Tandem mounting, dual-stage mounts |
| Shaft-Rate | 0.5â€“5 Hz | $k = 1$ | Stern, shaft bearings | Shaft alignment, bearing stiffness |
| Wave Encounter | 0.05â€“1 Hz | Broadband | Hull girder (overall bending) | Hull form optimization |
| Whipping | 4â€“12 Hz | Transient | Hull girder (2-node) | Section modulus, slamming reserve |
| Cavitation | 1â€“100 kHz | Broadband | Stern plates, sonar dome | Tip-vortex control, anti-cavitation foil |
| Vortex Shedding | 0.5â€“10 Hz | Strouhal peak | Rudder, bilge keel brackets | Fairings, anti-vortex plates |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (KTU-Style):** A container ship is propelled by a 5-bladed propeller rotating at 90 RPM. The ship service generators are 4-stroke, 6-cylinder diesel engines running at 720 RPM. (a) Compute the blade-passing frequency. (b) Compute the engine firing frequency. (c) State which of the two is more likely to coincide with the 2-node hull-girder natural frequency of 4 Hz, and explain.

### Solution:

**Part (a):** Blade-passing frequency.

Convert RPM to rev/s: $n_{prop} = 90/60 = 1.5$ rev/s.

Apply $f_{BPF} = Z \cdot n$:

$$f_{BPF} = 5 \times 1.5 = 7.5 \text{ Hz}$$

**Part (b):** Engine firing frequency.

Convert RPM to rev/s: $n_{eng} = 720/60 = 12$ rev/s.

Apply $f_{fire} = \dfrac{N_{cyl} \cdot n_{eng}}{2\tau}$ with $\tau = 4$ (4-stroke):

$$f_{fire} = \dfrac{6 \times 12}{2 \times 4} = \dfrac{72}{8} = 9 \text{ Hz}$$

**Part (c):** Comparison with 2-node hull-girder mode.

Both 7.5 Hz and 9 Hz are of the same order as the 2-node mode (4 Hz is shown for the example). The **engine firing frequency (9 Hz)** is closer to the 2-node mode and is **synchronous** (locked to RPM), making it the more dangerous source. Propeller BPF (7.5 Hz) is asynchronous and can be detuned by speed change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The student must always state that *synchronous* sources are more dangerous than *asynchronous* ones because they cannot be detuned by speed change. This is a key KTU valuation point.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Vibration Source Architecture for a Ship

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Ship Vibration Sources] --> B[Propeller Hydrodynamic Excitation]
    A --> C[Engine Mechanical Excitation]
    A --> D[Wave Environmental Excitation]
    A --> E[Fluid Structure Interaction Excitation]

    B --> B1[Blade Rate Forces]
    B --> B2[Shaft Rate Forces]
    B --> B3[Higher Harmonics]
    B --> B4[Surface Forces]

    C --> C1[Firing Frequency]
    C --> C2[Second Order Inertia]
    C --> C3[Torque Fluctuations]
    C --> C4[Gear Meshing]

    D --> D1[Regular Wave Encounter]
    D --> D2[Irregular Wave Spectrum]
    D --> D3[Slamming Loads]
    D --> D4[Whipping Response]

    E --> E1[Cavitation Collapse]
    E --> E2[Vortex Shedding]
    E --> E3[Resonance Coupling]

    B1 --> F1[Couples to Afterbody Plates 20 to 120 Hz]
    B2 --> F2[Couples to Stern Structure 0.5 to 5 Hz]
    C1 --> F3[Couples to Hull Girder 5 to 20 Hz]
    D3 --> F4[Couples to Hull Girder Transient]
    E1 --> F5[Couples to Local Plates 1 to 100 kHz]

    F1 --> G1[Radiated Noise 100 to 1000 Hz]
    F3 --> G2[Crew Habitability Issue]
    F4 --> G3[Fatigue Damage]
    F5 --> G4[Sonar Signature]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Frequency Spectrum Allocation Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    S0[Shaft Rate 0.5 to 5 Hz] --> R0[Stern Structure]
    S1[Engine Firing 5 to 20 Hz] --> R1[Hull Girder 2 Node Mode]
    S2[Engine 2nd Order 10 to 40 Hz] --> R2[Superstructure and Masts]
    S3[Propeller BPF 20 to 120 Hz] --> R3[Afterbody and Stern Plates]
    S4[Propeller Higher Harmonics 60 to 500 Hz] --> R4[Local Plate Panels]
    S5[Cavitation 1 to 100 kHz] --> R5[Sonar Dome and Aft Plates]
    S6[Wave Encounter 0.05 to 1 Hz] --> R6[Hull Girder 2 Node Slow Drift]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: Source Identification Methodology

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Observed Vibration Symptom] --> Q1{Frequency Below 5 Hz}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Source RPM Tied}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Engine Firing or Shaft Rate]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Wave Encounter or Whipping]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Frequency 5 to 20 Hz}
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Engine 2nd Order or BPF]
    Q1 -->|No| Q4{Frequency 20 to 120 Hz}
    Q4 -->|Yes| R4[Propeller Blade Rate]
    Q1 -->|No| Q5{Frequency Above 100 Hz}
    Q5 -->|Yes| R5[Cavitation or Vortex Shedding]

    R1 --> Z1[Match with Engine RPM Multiples]
    R3 --> Z2[Match with Engine RPM Multiples]
    R4 --> Z3[Match with Propeller Z times n]
    R5 --> Z4[Match with Cavitation Number]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The block diagrams are the KTU examiner's quick-win tool. A student who can draw this frequency allocation map from memory in a 14-mark question will score 3â€“4 marks instantly on diagram alone.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q:** Define the term *Blade-Passing Frequency* (BPF) as applied to ship vibration. State the formula and indicate the typical frequency range for a merchant vessel.

**[KTU University Exam â€“ June 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Blade-Passing Frequency (BPF)** is the fundamental frequency at which a rotating propeller's blades sweep past a fixed point on the hull, producing a periodic pressure pulse. It is given by:
> $$f_{BPF} = Z \times n$$
> where $Z$ is the number of blades and $n$ is the propeller rotational speed in revolutions per second. For a typical merchant vessel, $Z$ ranges from **3 to 7** and $n$ ranges from **1 to 3 rev/s**, giving $f_{BPF}$ in the range of **20 to 120 Hz**.

### Question A2
**Q:** Differentiate between *synchronous* and *asynchronous* sources of ship vibration. Give one example of each.

**[KTU University Exam â€“ Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

> A **synchronous** source has its excitation frequency locked to the engine/propeller RPM, i.e., the frequency is a fixed multiple of the running speed and *cannot be avoided by speed change*. **Example:** Engine firing frequency or engine second-order inertia forces.
>
> An **asynchronous** source has its excitation frequency not rigidly tied to the running speed, i.e., it depends on hydrodynamics, cavitation, or environmental factors. **Example:** Cavitation-induced vibration or wave-induced whipping.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam â€“ June 2024 Model]** â€” *14 Marks*

> **(a)** Classify and explain the major sources of vibration in a merchant ship, with reference to the typical frequency range of each source. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A 4-stroke, 8-cylinder main engine drives a 4-bladed propeller through a reduction gearbox with ratio 4.5:1. The engine runs at 600 RPM. Compute (i) the engine firing frequency, (ii) the propeller blade-passing frequency, and (iii) identify which of these is more likely to cause a resonance with the hull-girder 2-node mode. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a)

The major sources of vibration in a merchant ship are classified as follows:

1. **Propeller-induced hydrodynamic excitation** (20â€“500 Hz): Includes blade-rate forces, shaft-rate forces, and higher harmonics. These arise from the non-uniform wake field acting on each propeller blade and the cavitation behavior of the blades.

2. **Main engine excitation** (5â€“40 Hz): Includes firing frequency, second-order inertia forces from reciprocating masses, and torque fluctuations. For 4-stroke engines, firing frequency is $f_{fire} = N_{cyl} n / 8$ and 2nd-order is $2 f_{fire}$.

3. **Auxiliary machinery excitation** (10â€“500 Hz): Includes pumps, generators, gearboxes, and HVAC systems. Gear meshing produces high-frequency tonal excitation at the **gear mesh frequency** $f_{GMF} = N_{teeth} \times n_{gear}$.

4. **Wave-induced excitation** (0.05â€“1 Hz): Includes regular and irregular wave encounter frequencies. Whipping from slamming is a transient 4â€“12 Hz excitation.

5. **Cavitation and vortex-induced excitation** (1â€“100 kHz): Broadband high-frequency noise from bubble collapse and vortex shedding from appendages.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**(i) Engine Firing Frequency:**

Engine speed: $n_{eng} = 600/60 = 10$ rev/s.

For 4-stroke, 8-cylinder:

$$f_{fire} = \frac{N_{cyl} \cdot n_{eng}}{2 \tau} = \frac{8 \times 10}{2 \times 4} = \frac{80}{8} = 10 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Propeller Blade-Passing Frequency:**

Propeller speed via reduction ratio: $n_{prop} = n_{eng} / 4.5 = 10/4.5 \approx 2.222$ rev/s.

$$f_{BPF} = Z \cdot n_{prop} = 4 \times 2.222 \approx 8.89 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) Resonance Assessment:**

For a typical merchant ship, the 2-node hull-girder natural frequency lies in the range 4â€“12 Hz. Both $f_{fire} = 10$ Hz and $f_{BPF} \approx 8.89$ Hz fall within this band. The **engine firing frequency (10 Hz)** is the more dangerous source because it is **synchronous** (locked to RPM) and cannot be detuned by speed change, whereas BPF is asynchronous and may move out of resonance with speed adjustment.

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam â€“ Dec 2023 Model]** â€” *14 Marks*

> **(a)** Explain in detail the *wave-induced* and *cavitation-induced* sources of vibration in a ship. State their frequency ranges and the structural elements they excite. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A research vessel operates at 18 knots in regular waves of length 120 m, with wave heading $\mu = 30آ°$ (encounter angle). (i) Compute the wave encounter frequency. (ii) State whether this frequency poses a whipping or hull-girder bending concern, and justify your answer. Take $g = 9.81$ m/sآ² and 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Wave-Induced Vibration:**

The ship moving through ocean waves experiences a time-varying pressure field on its hull. The encounter frequency $f_e$ is determined by the Doppler shift between wave frequency and ship speed:

$$f_e = \frac{g}{2\pi\lambda} \left( 1 + \frac{4\pi^2 V^2}{g\lambda} \cos\mu \right)$$

The frequency range for wave-induced vibration is **0.05 Hz to 1 Hz**, exciting the **overall hull-girder bending modes** (2-node, 3-node vertical and horizontal bending). In severe seas, transient loads from bow or stern slamming excite the **whipping mode** (4â€“12 Hz) as a damped free vibration superimposed on the wave-frequency motion.

**Cavitation-Induced Vibration:**

When local static pressure on the propeller blade drops below vapor pressure, cavities form and subsequently collapse violently. The collapse generates **broadband pressure pulses** with peak energy in the **1 kHz to 100 kHz** range, plus strong **discrete cavitation tones** at harmonics of BPF. These pulses excite:

- **Local plate modes** in the stern and afterbody.
- **Shell-plate and sonar dome** vibration, contributing to the vessel's acoustic signature.

Cavitation is especially severe in ships with high propeller loading, in shallow water, and in rough seas.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**(i) Wave Encounter Frequency:**

Convert ship speed: $V = 18 \times 0.5144 = 9.259$ m/s.

Compute the first term: $g / (2\pi\lambda) = 9.81 / (2\pi \times 120) = 9.81 / 753.98 \approx 0.01301$ Hz.

Compute the second term: $4\pi^2 V^2 / (g \lambda) = 4\pi^2 (9.259)^2 / (9.81 \times 120) = 4\pi^2 \times 85.73 / 1177.2$.

First compute numerator: $4 \pi^2 \times 85.73 \approx 39.478 \times 85.73 \approx 3384.6$.

Then divide: $3384.6 / 1177.2 \approx 2.875$.

Multiply by $\cos(30آ°) = 0.866$: $2.875 \times 0.866 \approx 2.490$.

Therefore the bracket term is $1 + 2.490 = 3.490$.

Finally:

$$f_e = 0.01301 \times 3.490 \approx 0.0454 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Whipping or Bending Concern:**

The encounter frequency $f_e \approx 0.045$ Hz is well below the 4â€“12 Hz whipping range, and the **encounter period** is $T_e = 1 / 0.0454 \approx 22$ seconds. This is a **slow, low-frequency** excitation of the order of one cycle every 22 seconds.

Therefore, the concern is **NOT whipping**, but **overall hull-girder 2-node and 3-node bending** with possible springing resonance if the encounter frequency coincides with a higher harmonic. At this low frequency, the concern is **fatigue accumulation** in the deck and bottom plating, not the high-frequency whipping phenomenon.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall â€“ Common Mark Losses:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in BPF:** Many students write $f_{BPF} = Z \times N$ using $N$ in RPM without converting to rev/s. This gives a factor-of-60 error. Always convert to Hz first.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the firing frequency factor $\tau$:** In the engine firing formula, students often write $f_{fire} = N_{cyl} \cdot n$ without the $2\tau$ denominator, which doubles or quadruples the answer.
>
> 3. **Confusing synchronous vs. asynchronous:** A 14-mark question on "which source is more dangerous" requires the student to *explain* the synchronous-asynchronous distinction, not just identify the higher frequency. Many students lose 2 marks by omitting this.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the angle $\mu$ in the wave encounter formula:** The Doppler factor $\cos\mu$ is essential. Setting $\mu = 0$ for all problems loses 1 mark in oblique wave cases.
>
> 5. **Not drawing the frequency allocation diagram:** A 14-mark question on "classification of sources" is incomplete without a labeled diagram. Examiners award at least 3 marks for a clean frequency-source map.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist â€” Sources of Vibration (KTU Module 4)**
>
> - **The Four Source Families:** Propeller (hydrodynamic), Engine (mechanical), Wave (environmental), Cavitation/FSI (fluid-structure).
>
> - **BPF Formula:** $f_{BPF} = Z \cdot n$, with $n$ in rev/s. Convert RPM by dividing by 60.
>
> - **Firing Frequency Formula:** $f_{fire} = N_{cyl} \cdot n / (2\tau)$, with $\tau = 2$ for 2-stroke and $\tau = 4$ for 4-stroke.
>
> - **Wave Encounter Doppler Formula:** $f_e = \dfrac{g}{2\pi\lambda}\left(1 + \dfrac{4\pi^2 V^2}{g\lambda}\cos\mu\right)$. Remember the $\cos\mu$ term for oblique seas.
>
> - **Synchronous vs. Asynchronous:** Synchronous = engine firing, shaft rate, gear mesh. Asynchronous = BPF (in some sense), cavitation, vortex shedding. Synchronous sources are more dangerous because they cannot be detuned.
>
> - **Whipping vs. Springing:** Whipping is a transient high-frequency hull-girder response (4â€“12 Hz) excited by slamming. Springing is a continuous resonance at higher bending modes. KTU exam questions on whipping should mention "slamming" and "transient."
>
> - **Vibration Level in dB:** $L_v = 20 \log_{10}(a / a_{ref})$ with $a_{ref} = 10^{-5}$ m/sآ².
>
> - **Habitability Threshold:** Total weighted RMS acceleration must stay below $0.01 g$ in accommodation spaces (ISO 6954).
>
> - **Always draw a frequency-source map** in 14-mark classification questions â€” it is a guaranteed 3-mark pickup.
>
> - **Match the source to the structural target:** BPF â†’ afterbody plates; Firing â†’ hull girder; Cavitation â†’ sonar dome; Wave â†’ overall bending.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measures for Control of Vibration
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vibration Control Measures** are the engineered interventions â€” passive, semi-active, or active â€” designed to reduce, suppress, or isolate the amplitude, transmissibility, or harmful effects of mechanical vibrations in ship structures, propulsion systems, and onboard machinery, ensuring compliance with classification society comfort criteria (ISO 6954, ISO 2631, DNV, ABS Rules) and crew/passenger well-being.

In the context of **PCNST601 â€“ Ship Structures II**, Module 4, vibration control refers to the systematic reduction of the dynamic response of hull plates, superstructures, propeller-induced vibrations, and engine orders in marine vessels. The four primary control philosophies addressed in the KTU syllabus are:

1. **Vibration Isolation** (decoupling source from receiver)
2. **Vibration Damping** (dissipating vibrational energy)
3. **Vibration Absorption** (tuned/auxiliary mass dampers)
4. **Detuning** (shifting natural frequencies away from excitation)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy â€” "The Wobbly Washing Machine"**
> Imagine a washing machine spinning unbalanced on a tiled floor. The floor shakes, the noise travels through the apartment, and the machine itself walks across the room. How would you stop it?
> - Put **rubber pads** under its feet â†’ *Vibration Isolation* (decouples source from floor).
> - Add a **heavy concrete base** with rubber inside â†’ *Damping* (absorbs energy).
> - Hang a **small counterweight on a spring** from the chassis â†’ *Tuned Mass Damper* (absorber).
> - Make the drum spin at a *different speed* so it doesn't match the floor's natural rhythm â†’ *Detuning*.
>
> A ship hull behaves exactly the same way under propeller forces, engine orders, and wave impacts. The "machine" is the engine/propeller, the "floor" is the hull, and the "apartment" is the superstructure and accommodation blocks.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard human comfort limits (ISO 2631):** RMS acceleration $\leq 0.315 \text{ m/s}^2$ (fatigue-decreased proficiency boundary).
> - **Shipboard vibration limit (ISO 6954):** Peak velocity $V_{rms} \leq 4 \text{ mm/s}$ for crew comfort on merchant vessels.
> - **Transmissibility ratio:** $T = \frac{F_T}{F_0}$ (dimensionless), target $T < 1$ for isolation, $T \ll 1$ for high-quality isolation.
> - **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ (dimensionless).
> - **Acceptable practical range:** $\zeta = 0.05$ to $0.30$ for marine mountings; $T < 0.25$ above the isolation region.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Philosophy of Vibration Control

The governing equation for a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) forced system is:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

where $m$ = mass, $c$ = damping coefficient, $k$ = stiffness, $F_0$ = excitation amplitude, $\omega$ = forcing frequency.

The **steady-state amplitude** is:

$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}} = \frac{X_{st}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

where:
- $X_{st} = F_0 / k$ = static deflection
- $r = \omega / \omega_n$ = frequency ratio
- $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ = natural frequency
- $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km})$ = damping ratio

> [!NOTE]
> **Three control levers emerge from this equation:**
> 1. Increase $m$ (mass addition / ballast) â€” reduces $r$.
> 2. Increase $c$ (damping) â€” reduces peak amplitude near resonance.
> 3. Modify $k$ (stiffness change via supports) â€” shifts $\omega_n$ away from $\omega$.

### 2.2 Measure 1 â€” Vibration Isolation (Resilient Mountings)

**Operational principle:** Introduce a low-stiffness elastic medium between the vibrating source (engine, generator) and the receiving structure (hull, foundation), so that the **transmissibility** $T$ is less than unity.

The **transmissibility** for base-excited / force-isolated SDOF system is:

$$T = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Key isolation rule:** Isolation is effective **only when** $r > \sqrt{2}$, i.e., the excitation frequency must exceed $\sqrt{2}$ times the natural frequency of the mounting system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design heuristic:** Choose mount stiffness so that $\omega_n \leq \omega / \sqrt{2}$. For a marine diesel engine running at 900 rpm (15 Hz) with blade-passing frequency of 60 Hz, the mount natural frequency should be $\leq 60/\sqrt{2} \approx 42.4$ Hz â€” practically achieved as 8â€“15 Hz with rubber or steel-spring mounts.

**Marine mounting types:**
- **Elastomeric (rubber) mounts:** $\zeta \approx 0.05$â€“$0.15$, low cost, used for auxiliary machinery.
- **Steel-spring mounts:** $\zeta \approx 0.005$â€“$0.02$ (very low), need added damping (viscous dampers or rubber-in-shear).
- **Air mounts (pneumatic):** Very low $\omega_n$ (1â€“3 Hz), used for precision equipment and shipboard inertial navigation systems.

### 2.3 Measure 2 â€” Vibration Damping

**Operational principle:** Increase the energy dissipation capacity of the structure so that vibrational amplitude decays rapidly when excitation stops, and resonance peaks are suppressed.

The **logarithmic decrement** for free vibration decay is:

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_1}{x_2}\right) = \frac{2\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \approx 2\pi\zeta \quad (\zeta \ll 1)$$

The **quality factor** $Q = 1/(2\zeta)$ measures sharpness of resonance.

**Marine damping techniques:**
- **Constrained-layer damping (CLD):** Viscoelastic (e.g., 3M ISD110) sandwiched between hull plate and constraining layer â€” widely used in superstructure panels of naval vessels.
- **Free-layer damping:** Viscoelastic sheet bonded directly to plate; effective in audio-frequency range (50â€“500 Hz) where ship panel resonances dominate.
- **Tuned viscoelastic dampers:** VEDs combining high stiffness and high loss factor; used for damping plate vibration in the 100â€“1000 Hz range.
- **Damping treatments in stiffened panels:** Adding damping material at the panel level increases modal loss factor by 3â€“5أ— in critical bands.

**Material loss factor** $\eta$ is the critical parameter. For ship steels $\eta \approx 10^{-3}$; with damping treatment, $\eta$ can reach $0.1$â€“$0.3$.

### 2.4 Measure 3 â€” Vibration Absorption (Tuned Mass Dampers)

**Operational principle:** Attach a secondary massâ€“springâ€“damper system to the primary structure, tuned so that the absorber mass oscillates out of phase with the primary motion, effectively cancelling the vibration at the design frequency.

For a **tuned mass damper (TMD)** on a primary system with mass $M$, stiffness $K$:

- Absorber mass: $m_a$
- Absorber stiffness: $k_a$
- Tuned frequency: $\omega_a = \sqrt{k_a / m_a}$

**Optimal tuning (Den Hartog, 1947):**

$$\omega_a^{opt} = \frac{\omega_n}{1 + \mu}$$

$$\zeta_a^{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{3\mu}{8(1+\mu)^3}}$$

where $\mu = m_a / M$ is the mass ratio.

For $\mu = 0.05$ (typical marine TMD): $\omega_a \approx 0.93 \omega_n$, $\zeta_a \approx 0.12$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine applications of TMDs:**
> - **AEGIR / QUEEN MARY 2 fin stabilizers:** actively controlled TMDs to suppress roll.
> - **Naval vessel masts:** TMDs to reduce radar mast vibration in the 5â€“15 Hz range.
> - **Propeller-shaft TMDs:** circumferential absorbers around shaft to suppress torsional vibration at engine order.

### 2.5 Measure 4 â€” Detuning (Frequency Shifting)

**Operational principle:** Modify the natural frequency of a structural component so it does not coincide with any excitation frequency (engine order, blade frequency, wave encounter).

**Detuning methods in ships:**
- **Stiffener spacing optimization:** Altering frame or longitudinal spacing shifts panel natural frequencies.
- **Local mass addition:** Adding or removing plate or stiffener material.
- **Opening placement:** Resizing/resiting openings in decks and bulkheads.
- **Propulsion detuning:** Choosing number of propeller blades so that blade-frequency excitation does not excite superstructure modes (typical 4 or 5 blades selected to avoid 12â€“25 Hz hull-girder modes).

The **coincidence condition to avoid:**

$$f_{excitation} = f_n \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{no detuning; resonance occurs}$$

Recommended separation:

$$\left| f_{excitation} - f_n \right| \geq 0.10 \, f_n \quad (\text{10\% margin, KTU/class rule of thumb})$$

### 2.6 Measure 5 â€” Active and Semi-Active Control

| Method | Sensor | Actuator | Controller | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive isolation** | None | Rubber/spring mount | None | Aux machinery |
| **Tuned mass damper** | None | Inertial mass + spring | None | Masts, fin stabilizers |
| **Active mass damper (AMD)** | Accelerometer | Electromagnetic actuator | PID/LQR | Warship masts |
| **Active constrained-layer damping** | Strain gauge | Piezoelectric patches | Feedback | Hull plates |
| **Semi-active (skyhook)** | Accelerometer | Variable-orifice damper | On-off logic | Ship suspension |
| **Active structural acoustic control** | Microphones | Piezoelectric speakers | Adaptive filter | Submarine hulls |

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency | rad/s | Mounting design |
| 2 | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | Damping ratio | â€” | All SDOF systems |
| 3 | $r = \omega / \omega_n$ | Frequency ratio | â€” | Isolation/detuning |
| 4 | $X = \dfrac{X_{st}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Forced amplitude | m | Resonance analysis |
| 5 | $T = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Transmissibility | â€” | Mounting design |
| 6 | $T < 1$ iff $r > \sqrt{2}$ | Isolation criterion | â€” | Resilient mount sizing |
| 7 | $\delta = 2\pi\zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | Log decrement | â€” | Damping measurement |
| 8 | $Q = 1/(2\zeta)$ | Quality factor | â€” | Resonance sharpness |
| 9 | $\omega_a^{opt} = \omega_n/(1+\mu)$ | TMD optimum tune | rad/s | Absorber design |
| 10 | $\zeta_a^{opt} = \sqrt{3\mu/[8(1+\mu)^3]}$ | TMD optimum damping | â€” | Absorber design |
| 11 | $f_{bp} = N_{blade} \cdot f_{shaft}$ | Blade-pass frequency | Hz | Excitation source |
| 12 | $\eta_{composite} \approx \eta_{viscoelastic} \cdot V_f$ | Composite loss factor | â€” | Damping treatment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility of these formulas in ship design:**
> - Naval architects use (1)â€“(6) to specify resilient mounts for engine rooms, avoiding costly post-delivery noise complaints.
> - Equation (5) determines whether the existing steel-spring mount on a 25 MW marine diesel will meet the 0.25 transmissibility goal at the 9th engine order.
> - Equations (9)â€“(10) are directly applied when retrofitting a TMD to a frigate's radar mast to reduce Line-of-Sight jitter.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Transmissibility Equation $T(r, \zeta)$

**Starting point:** Consider a rigid mass $m$ on a spring $k$ and damper $c$, mounted on a base that experiences a harmonic displacement $y(t) = Y\sin(\omega t)$. We want the force $F_T$ transmitted to the foundation.

**Step 1 â€” Equation of motion of the absolute mass displacement $x$:**

The spring/damper force depends on the *relative* displacement $(x - y)$. So:

$$m\ddot{x} + c(\dot{x} - \dot{y}) + k(x - y) = 0$$

**Step 2 â€” Substitute $z = x - y$ (relative displacement):**

Since $y = Y\sin\omega t$, we have $\ddot{y} = -\omega^2 Y\sin\omega t$. Also $z = x - y$, so $\ddot{x} = \ddot{z} + \ddot{y} = \ddot{z} - \omega^2 Y\sin\omega t$.

$$m(\ddot{z} - \omega^2 Y\sin\omega t) + c\dot{z} + kz = 0$$

**Step 3 â€” Rearrange into standard SDOF form:**

$$m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = m\omega^2 Y\sin\omega t$$

This is forced vibration of $z$ with effective forcing $F_0 = m\omega^2 Y$.

**Step 4 â€” Steady-state solution for $z$:**

$$z(t) = Z\sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

where

$$Z = \frac{m\omega^2 Y}{\sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 5 â€” Force transmitted to base:**

The transmitted force is the sum of spring and damper reactions on the base:

$$F_T(t) = kz(t) + c\dot{z}(t)$$

**Step 6 â€” Magnitude of $F_T$:**

The amplitude is

$$F_{T,0} = \sqrt{(kZ)^2 + (c\omega Z)^2} = Z\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$

**Step 7 â€” Substitute $Z$ from Step 4:**

$$F_{T,0} = \frac{m\omega^2 Y \sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{\sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 8 â€” Express in non-dimensional form using $r = \omega/\omega_n$, $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$, and $m\omega^2 = kr^2$:**

$$F_{T,0} = \frac{kr^2 Y \sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Step 9 â€” Transmissibility as ratio to the inertial force $m\omega^2 Y$:**

The "input" force amplitude is taken as $m\omega^2 Y = kr^2 Y$. So:

$$T = \frac{F_{T,0}}{m\omega^2 Y} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

$$\boxed{\;T(r,\zeta) = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}\;}$$

**Step 10 â€” At very high frequency ($r \to \infty$):**

$$T \to \frac{2\zeta r}{r^2} = \frac{2\zeta}{r} \to 0$$

Confirms that high frequencies are well isolated. Conversely, at $r = 0$ (static), $T = 1$ (no isolation, as expected).

**Step 11 â€” Minimum transmissibility condition (differentiate numerator/denominator):**

Set $\partial T / \partial r = 0$. After algebra, the optimum damping for minimum peak transmissibility is:

$$\zeta_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{3}{8}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 2/\mu}} \quad \text{(for TMD on primary mass)}$$

For simple base isolation, the minimum $T$ at high $r$ is achieved with the smallest practical $\zeta$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Logarithmic Decrement $\delta$

**Step 1 â€” Free vibration solution of underdamped SDOF:**

For $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$ with $\zeta < 1$:

$$x(t) = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t} \cos(\omega_d t + \phi)$$

where $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ is the damped natural frequency.

**Step 2 â€” Successive peak amplitudes separated by one period $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$:**

$$x_n = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_n}, \quad x_{n+1} = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n (t_n + T_d)}$$

**Step 3 â€” Ratio:**

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d} = e^{\zeta\omega_n \cdot 2\pi/\omega_d}$$

**Step 4 â€” Take natural log and simplify:**

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \frac{2\pi\zeta\omega_n}{\omega_d} = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

For $\zeta \ll 1$: $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$.

**Step 5 â€” Engineering use:** Measure two consecutive peaks $x_n$ and $x_{n+1}$ on a decay trace and compute $\delta = \ln(x_n/x_{n+1})$; from that obtain $\zeta \approx \delta / (2\pi)$.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example â€” Selecting a Resilient Mount for a Marine Generator

**Problem (KTU-style):** A 1200 kg marine diesel generator operates at 1800 rpm. The dominant excitation frequency is the 6th engine order. Choose a rubber mount stiffness $k$ such that transmissibility $T \leq 0.20$ and verify the isolation region.

**Step 1 â€” Compute excitation frequency:**

$$f_{exc} = \frac{N_{order} \cdot RPM}{60} = \frac{6 \times 1800}{60} = 180 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 180 = 1130.97 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 â€” Required frequency ratio for $T \leq 0.20$:**

For high $r$, $T \approx 2\zeta/r$. To get $T \leq 0.20$ with typical rubber $\zeta = 0.10$:

$$r \geq \frac{2\zeta}{T} = \frac{2 \times 0.10}{0.20} = 1.0$$

But isolation requires $r > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. So enforce $r = 1.414$ as minimum.

**Step 3 â€” Required natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \frac{\omega}{r} = \frac{1130.97}{1.414} = 800 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{800}{2\pi} \approx 127.3 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 â€” Required mount stiffness:**

$$k = m\omega_n^2 = 1200 \times (800)^2 = 7.68 \times 10^{8} \text{ N/m}$$

Wait â€” that is far too stiff. Let me re-check: the **6th engine order at 1800 rpm** is unusual (typically dominant orders are 1â€“4). Let me redo for the **fundamental** running frequency.

**Revised â€” using 1st engine order:**

$$f_{exc} = \frac{1 \times 1800}{60} = 30 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\omega = 2\pi \times 30 = 188.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

For $r = 1.5$ (safe margin above $\sqrt{2}$):

$$\omega_n = \frac{188.5}{1.5} = 125.7 \text{ rad/s}, \quad f_n = 20 \text{ Hz}$$

$$k = 1200 \times (125.7)^2 = 1.895 \times 10^{7} \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 5 â€” Static deflection check:**

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{mg}{k} = \frac{1200 \times 9.81}{1.895 \times 10^7} = 6.21 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.62 \text{ mm}$$

This is a practical, achievable rubber-mount deflection. âœ“

**Step 6 â€” Verify transmissibility at $r = 1.5$, $\zeta = 0.10$:**

$$T = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2 \times 0.10 \times 1.5)^2}{(1 - 1.5^2)^2 + (2 \times 0.10 \times 1.5)^2}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{1 + (0.30)^2}{(1 - 2.25)^2 + (0.30)^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 0.09}{1.5625 + 0.09}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{1.09}{1.6525}} = \sqrt{0.6596} = 0.812$$

Hmm â€” $T = 0.81$, not below 0.20. This shows the limitation: at $r = 1.5$ with $\zeta = 0.10$, transmissibility is still 0.81. To reach $T \leq 0.20$, we need a higher $r$.

**Step 7 â€” Solve for $r$ that gives $T = 0.20$ with $\zeta = 0.10$:**

Numerically:

$$0.20^2 = \frac{1 + 0.04 r^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + 0.04 r^2}$$

$$0.04 [(1-r^2)^2 + 0.04 r^2] = 1 + 0.04 r^2$$

For large $r$, $(1 - r^2)^2 \approx r^4$, so $0.04 r^4 \approx 1 \Rightarrow r \approx 2.24$.

**Step 8 â€” Required $\omega_n$ for $r = 2.24$:**

$$\omega_n = \frac{188.5}{2.24} = 84.1 \text{ rad/s}, \quad f_n = 13.4 \text{ Hz}$$

$$k = 1200 \times 84.1^2 = 8.49 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{1200 \times 9.81}{8.49 \times 10^6} = 1.39 \text{ mm}$$

Achievable with rubber-metal mounts. âœ“

**Conclusion:** Choose a rubber mounting system with total stiffness $\approx 8.5 \text{ MN/m}$ and $\zeta \approx 0.10$, yielding $T \approx 0.20$ at the 30 Hz fundamental.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix â€” When to Use Each Control Measure

| Symptom / Problem | Root Cause | Recommended Measure | KTU High-Yield Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hull-girder vibration at engine order | Coincidence of $f_n$ & $f_{exc}$ | **Detuning** (frame spacing, plate thickness) | $f_{exc} = N_{order} \cdot f_{shaft}$ |
| Crew comfort in E/R | High force transmission from engine | **Isolation** (resilient mounts) | $T < 1$ when $r > \sqrt{2}$ |
| Superstructure panel rattle | Audio-frequency panel resonance | **Damping** (CLD treatment) | $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ |
| Radar mast jitter in head seas | Vortex shedding / wave excitation | **Tuned mass damper** | $\omega_a^{opt} = \omega_n/(1+\mu)$ |
| Propeller-shaft torsional vibration | Torsional resonance at engine order | **Shaft TMD / detuner** | $T_a \approx 2\pi\zeta_a f_n$ |
| Localized fin/rudder buzz | Hydro-elastic coupling | **Damping + isolation** | Combined formula |
